  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-01 — GRO AND PGR IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Section 1 — What NR-01 is and why it matters for sound technicians

Regulatory Standard No. 1 — General Provisions and Occupational Risk Management — is the foundational standard for the entire occupational health and safety framework in Brazil. Updated by MTE Ordinance No. 1,419/2024, it establishes the Occupational Risk Management (GRO) system and the Risk Management Program (PGR), which must address all the risks to which workers are exposed — including, explicitly from May 26, 2026, the Work-Related Psychosocial Risk Factors (FRPRT).

For the sound technician at live events, NR-01 is particularly relevant because the occupational risks of this role are simultaneously physical (exposure to noise above 85 dB for 10–14 hour shifts), ergonomic (forced postures at consoles, lifting heavy equipment), psychosocial (deadline pressure, abusive shifts, harassment by production) and accident-related (working at height on rigging, handling electrical equipment). All of these vectors must appear in the Risk Inventory of the employer's PGR.


⚠️  IMPORTANT — GRO IS MANDATORY FOR ALL

GRO is not optional and has no minimum company size. Every organization that has CLT (formally employed) workers in roles involving occupational risks — including events companies, production companies, and sound and lighting rental firms — must implement the PGR with an up-to-date risk inventory.

  02    FUNDAMENTAL ACOUSTICS — THE PHYSICS OF SOUND AND SAFETY

Section 2 — What sound is: from physics to occupational risk

Sound is a mechanical pressure wave that propagates through material media (air, water, solids) via periodic pressure variations. For the sound technician at events, understanding the physics of sound is not merely technical mastery of sound quality — it is the foundation for understanding why the work environment at a concert can cause permanent hearing damage and what can be done to prevent it.

2.1 Fundamental quantities of sound

Quantity

Symbol/Unit

Definition

Relevance to safety and quality at events

Frequency

f — Hz (Hertz)

Number of pressure-variation cycles per second. Defines the timbre of the sound.

Human hearing: 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Frequencies below 100 Hz (subwoofers) travel far and penetrate structures — risk of vibration in temporary stage structures

Wavelength

λ — meters

λ = c / f (where c = 340 m/s in air). Distance between two consecutive pressure peaks.

At 100 Hz, λ = 3.4 m. At 10 kHz, λ = 3.4 cm. Determines interference and reflection patterns in the event environment

Sound Pressure

Pa (Pascal)

Variation of air pressure relative to atmospheric pressure. The physical parameter the ear detects.

0.00002 Pa = hearing threshold. 20 Pa = pain threshold. The scale is vast — that is why we use dB

Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

dB SPL

SPL = 20 × log₁₀(P/P₀) where P₀ = 20 μPa (hearing threshold). Logarithmic scale.

0 dB SPL = hearing threshold. 85 dB = NR-15 limit (8h). 110–120 dB = typical live-show level. 140 dB = pain threshold

Speed of sound

c — m/s

~340 m/s in air at 20°C. Varies with temperature and humidity.

Critical for delay calculation in distributed PA systems — 1 ms = 34 cm of distance. Alignment error causes phase cancellation


💡  TECHNICAL TIP — THE dB SCALE IS LOGARITHMIC

Each 10 dB increase represents a doubling of perceived loudness and 10 times the acoustic power. Therefore: 80 dB + 80 dB ≠ 160 dB. Two 80 dB systems together produce ~83 dB (not 160 dB). This is fundamental for sizing PA systems and for understanding why doubling the number of loudspeakers does not double the sound level.


2.2 The audible frequency range and musical instruments

Human hearing nominally covers the range from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz), although in practice sensitivity varies significantly with age and history of noise exposure. At live events, the sound technician works with this full range:

  • Sub-bass (20–80 Hz): a physical sensation of pressure more than tonal hearing. Large-format subwoofers. Risk: structural vibration and pain threshold at high sound pressure

  • Bass (80–250 Hz): bass, kick drum, fundamental of low voices. The region that "thickens" or "thins" a mix

  • Low-mids (250–800 Hz): fundamental of most instruments. Excess causes a "boxy" sound

  • Mids (800 Hz–2 kHz): vocal intelligibility, presence of guitar and piano. Region of greatest human hearing sensitivity

  • High-mids (2–5 kHz): presence, articulation, the "attack" of instruments. The most aggressive region for prolonged hearing

  • Highs (5–10 kHz): brightness, air, vocal sibilance. Instrument harmonics

  • Ultra-highs (10–20 kHz): air and sense of ambience. First to be lost with hearing aging

  03    SOUND LEVEL — dB SPL, NR-15 AND NIHL RISK

Section 3 — Decibels, Legal Limits and Noise-Induced Hearing Loss

Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL) is the most prevalent occupational disease among sound technicians at live events — and one of the most underestimated, because it is silent, progressive and irreversible. The worker does not notice the gradual loss of hearing acuity until it already seriously compromises both their quality of life and their professional ability. NR-01, together with NR-15 (Unhealthy Activities and Operations) and NR-09 (LTCAT), establishes the regulatory framework for managing noise risk.

⚠️  IMPORTANT — NR-15: MAXIMUM EXPOSURE TABLE BY LEVEL

NR-15 (Annex I) establishes the maximum daily noise exposure limits without protection: 85 dB = 8h | 88 dB = 4h | 91 dB = 2h | 94 dB = 1h | 97 dB = 30 min | 100 dB = 15 min | 105 dB = 7 min | 110 dB = 3 min | above 115 dB: no exposure permitted. In a typical 2-hour show with FOH at 105 dB, the technician has already exceeded the legal limit without adequate protection.


3.2 NIHL — Noise-Induced Hearing Loss

NIHL is an occupational disease characterized by the progressive and irreversible destruction of the cochlea's hair cells due to prolonged exposure to noise above safe limits. Its main characteristics are:

  • Irreversible: destroyed hair cells do not regenerate — the loss is permanent

  • Progressive: it begins with loss at the high frequencies (4 kHz), then advances to the high-mids, progressively compromising speech intelligibility

  • Silent: the worker does not perceive the gradual loss — by the time they do, it is already significant

  • Bilateral: it occurs in both ears simultaneously

  • Disabling for sound professionals: loss in the 4–8 kHz frequencies directly compromises the ability to perceive mix details that are the professional's competitive edge

  • Aggravated by recreational noise: a worker who also frequents clubs, attends shows as an audience member, and uses headphones at high volume outside work exponentially accelerates the loss


💡  PRACTICAL TIP — IN-EAR MONITORS ARE PROFESSIONAL HEARING PROTECTION

The use of in-ear monitors (IEMs) by musicians and technicians who need a stage reference is not merely a comfort preference — it is a hearing-protection measure that, when configured correctly (reference volume between 80–85 dB, passive attenuation of 25–30 dB from the environment), dramatically reduces exposure to stage noise. A well-configured IEM is the hearing protector with the greatest acceptance and effectiveness in the sector.


  04    MICROPHONES — TYPES, USES AND APPLICATION AT EVENTS

Section 4 — Microphones: the Entry Point of the Sound System

The microphone is the transducer that converts acoustic energy (sound pressure) into electrical energy (audio signal). Choosing the correct type of microphone for each application at events is a fundamental technical decision that directly affects sound quality, voice intelligibility, feedback rejection and the sound technician's operational stress level.

4.1 Types of Microphones and Applications at Live Events

Type

Operating principle

Characteristics

Typical applications at events

Dynamic (moving-coil)

A moving coil attached to the diaphragm oscillates within a magnetic field, generating current by electromagnetic induction

Rugged, needs no phantom power, excellent response to high SPL, less sensitive at high frequency

Live vocals (SM58), kick drum (Beta 52), snare, guitar cab, presenters in adverse conditions

Condenser (capacitor)

A conductive diaphragm forms a capacitor with a backplate; variation in capacitance generates the signal — requires phantom power (+48V)

High sensitivity, excellent frequency and transient response, more fragile, sensitive to humidity and extreme SPL

Drum overheads, acoustic piano, acoustic guitar, studio vocals, side-fill pickup, rehearsal room

Ribbon

A thin metal ribbon (aluminum) suspended in a magnetic field oscillates with the air, generating current

Natural figure-8, smooth response, vintage, fragile — beware of phantom power on vintage mics

Guitar amplifiers (modern ribbon mics), brass, vocals with a vintage character, special setups

Electret (permanent condenser)

Condenser with a permanent electrical charge on the diaphragm or backplate — needs no phantom power in most versions

Compact, inexpensive, good sensitivity — quality varies enormously by manufacturer

Presenter headsets, lavaliers, boundary mics, frontman headbands


4.2 Polar patterns and their importance at events

A microphone's polar pattern describes its directional sensitivity — from where the microphone "hears" best and from where it rejects sound. At live events, the polar pattern is the primary tool for feedback control and source isolation:

  • Cardioid: picks up in front, rejects behind (~180°). Most common at events. A good balance between capture and feedback rejection. Suitable for most live situations.

  • Supercardioid / Hypercardioid: narrower pickup in front, some pickup behind (~135°). Greater side rejection. Used to isolate sources on crowded stages.

  • Figure-8 (bidirectional): picks up in front and behind, rejects at the sides. Standard for ribbons. Rare in conventional live use.

  • Omnidirectional: picks up equally in all directions. Lowest gain before feedback. Used in lavaliers for greater voice naturalness.



  05    IMPEDANCE — WHAT IT IS AND WHY IT MATTERS

Section 5 — Impedance: the Concept that Defines Equipment Compatibility

Impedance (Z), measured in Ohms (Ω), is the opposition that an electrical circuit offers to the flow of alternating current. In professional audio systems, impedance determines how equipment connects to one another and directly affects signal level, noise and sound quality. Understanding impedance is essential for the sound technician at events because impedance-matching errors cause loss of level, distortion, noise and, in extreme cases, equipment damage.

5.1 The golden rule of impedance matching in audio

In professional audio systems, the rule of thumb is: the input impedance must be at least 10 times greater than the output impedance of the preceding equipment. Example: a dynamic microphone has an output impedance of ~150Ω → the preamplifier must have a minimum input impedance of 1,500Ω (in practice, professional consoles have 1,200Ω–5,000Ω microphone input impedance, which is correct).

Equipment

If connected to a Hi-Z instrument input: loss of ~6–10 dB and possible feedback

If phantom is absent: no signal. If +48V on a vintage ribbon: destroys the ribbon

💡  TIP — THE DI BOX IS AN IMPEDANCE SOLUTION

The DI Box (Direct Injection Box) is a transformer that converts a Hi-Z signal (instrument) into Lo-Z (balanced line) with the correct impedance matching, eliminating ground-loop noise and allowing long stage cables to the stage rack or snake. It is the device that allows guitar, keyboard and bass to be connected directly to the console without going through an instrument amplifier.


  06    EQUALIZATION — A FUNDAMENTAL OPERATOR TOOL

Section 6 — Equalization: Shaping the Sound and Controlling the System

Equalization (EQ) is the process of adjusting the frequency response of an audio signal — boosting, attenuating or filtering specific bands of the spectrum. In the live-events context, equalization has two fundamental purposes that the sound technician must master: creative equalization (shaping the timbre of each source for the desired mix) and system equalization (linearizing the PA system's response in the event environment to maximize gain before feedback and ensure sonic fidelity).

6.1 Types of equalization filters

  • High Pass Filter (HPF): eliminates frequencies below a cutoff point. Practical application: activate the HPF on all channels that carry no low-frequency information (vocals: HPF at 100 Hz; guitars: HPF at 80 Hz; percussion: HPF at 60 Hz). Eliminates stage noise, structural vibration and phase problems in the lows.

  • Low Pass Filter (LPF): eliminates frequencies above a cutoff point. Application: subwoofer crossover (cutting above 80–100 Hz on the sub signal), removing hiss on low-frequency instrument microphones.

  • Parametric EQ (Peak/Dip / Bell): boosts or attenuates a band of frequencies centered on a specific frequency (f), with adjustable bandwidth (Q). Q determines how narrow or wide the curve is: low Q (0.3–0.7) = a wide, musical curve; high Q (5–10) = a surgical curve for a feedback notch.

  • Shelf EQ: boosts or attenuates all frequencies above (high shelf) or below (low shelf) a starting frequency. Smoothing of lows and highs in blocks.

  • Notch filter (surgical cut): a very narrow and deep attenuation (high Q, 30 dB of cut) at a specific problem frequency — a room resonance frequency, a feedback frequency, a 60/120/240 Hz hum.

6.2 System equalization — practical procedure at events

Equalizing an event's PA system begins before the audience arrives and consists of adjusting the system's frequency response for the venue's specific acoustic environment — compensating for the audience's absorption of high frequencies, the room's resonances and irregularities in the system's response. The standard procedure:

  1. Set up the system with the space empty and measure the response with a reference mic (RTA or FFT)

  2. Identify peaks (resonances) and dips (phase nulls or absorption) in the response

  3. Apply cuts to the peaks — never boost dips initially (boosting nulls is ineffective and reduces available gain)

  4. With the audience present, the space changes (human absorption increases, especially in the highs) — readjust the main EQ during the sound check

  5. Set the crossover frequencies between sub and full-range according to the system's cabinets and the measured response


⚠️  IMPORTANT — MONITOR EQ IS CRITICAL FOR THE ARTIST'S HEARING SAFETY

Poorly equalized stage monitors that "feed back" lead the technician to raise the monitor volume to compensate for the loss of definition — creating a level-escalation cycle that ends with monitors at 115–120 dB and accelerated NIHL in all musicians. Correct monitor EQ, with notches at the problem frequencies and an HPF applied, allows a lower working volume with more clarity and definition.


  07    FEEDBACK — CAUSE, PREVENTION AND CONTROL

Section 7 — Feedback (Howling): the Most Common Problem at Live Events

Acoustic feedback — popularly called howling or howlround — is the phenomenon that occurs when the signal captured by a microphone is amplified, emitted by the PA system, captured again by the same microphone and re-amplified in a cycle that grows exponentially until it reaches the system's maximum level, producing the characteristic squeal or howl. It is the most common, most frustrating and most avoidable problem at live events.

7.1 Conditions for feedback — understanding the cause

Feedback occurs when the total gain of the path microphone → console → amplifier → loudspeaker → air → microphone is equal to or greater than 1 (zero dB). Mathematically, the Gain Before Feedback (GBF) is the maximum amount of amplification applicable to the system before feedback occurs. Factors that reduce the GBF:

  • A microphone too close to the loudspeaker or monitor — short distance between source and pickup

  • A microphone pointed directly at the monitor's high-frequency driver — the pickup axis aligned with the source

  • Monitor or PA volume unnecessarily high for the artist's needs

  • A highly reverberant environment — reflections reach the microphone via multiple paths

  • System equalization with uncorrected peaks at the feedback frequencies

  • A microphone with an omnidirectional polar pattern in a monitor situation — it picks up equally from all directions

7.2 Feedback prevention techniques

  1. Correct microphone positioning: keep it in the monitor's acoustic "shadow" — slightly behind the monitor's null axis (for a cardioid monitor pattern, the null axis is the central rear line)

  2. Notch equalization: identify the system's feedback frequencies with real-time RTA, apply surgical cuts (narrow notch, high Q) at those frequencies before raising the volume to the working level

  3. Adequate stage gain: use the minimum stage gain necessary — microphones close to the source reduce the need for gain

  4. Subgroups and VCAs: reduce the gain of groups of microphone channels not in use (automatic offset of choir channels that are not singing)

  5. Automatic feedback processors (AFS — Automatic Feedback Suppression): in small and medium systems, dedicated processors automatically identify and notch the feedback frequencies

  6. Use supercardioid microphones in intense monitoring situations — greater side rejection


  08    DELAY AND TIME ALIGNMENT IN DISTRIBUTED PA SYSTEMS

Section 8 — Delay: Time, Phase and System Coherence

In a distributed PA system — with main clusters, stage fills, delay towers and fills of fills — all the loudspeakers emit the same signal, but it reaches the listener at different times, because they are at different distances. When the sound from the nearest system reaches the listener before the sound from the farthest system, the result is a "double slap" echo effect, degradation of speech intelligibility and unwanted sonic coloration from phase interference.

8.1 Basic delay calculation

The speed of sound in air at 20°C is approximately 343 m/s, equivalent to 1 ms for every 34.3 cm. Therefore, to calculate the delay needed for a fill system positioned 10 meters in front of the main cluster:

  • Distance from the main cluster to the listener at the fill position: 25 meters → Arrival time: 25 / 343 = 72.9 ms

  • Distance from the fill to the listener at the fill position: 3 meters → Arrival time: 3 / 343 = 8.7 ms

  • Delay needed on the fill = 72.9 ms – 8.7 ms = 64.2 ms

  • In practice, an extra 5–10 ms of delay ("grace time") is added so that the brain associates the sound with the main cluster (precedence law / Haas effect)


💡  TIP — THE HAAS EFFECT (PRECEDENCE LAW) AND ITS PRACTICAL USE

The human auditory system locates the origin of sound in the direction of the first wavefront that reaches the ear. If two systems emit the same signal and the first arrives up to 35 ms earlier, the listener perceives the sound as coming from the direction of the first — even if the second is up to 10 dB louder. Use this principle: the fill must always arrive slightly after the main cluster (5–15 ms of grace time). If the fill arrives first, the listener will hear the stage as being behind them — an unpleasant spatial disorientation.


8.2 Phase and coherence — why alignment matters beyond timing

Beyond the time delay, the phase alignment between system elements must be verified. Two systems that emit the same signal but with a 180° phase difference cancel each other out — producing an acoustic null in the overlap of their fields. Correct delay processing and phase testing with measurement (analysis software such as Smaart or ARTA) are essential professional practices that distinguish a well-aligned system from one that is "working but sounding bad."


  09    OCCUPATIONAL RISKS OF THE SOUND TECHNICIAN — GRO INVENTORY

Section 9 — Applying NR-01: the Sound Technician's Risk Inventory

With mastery of the technical topics from the previous sections, the sound technician is equipped to actively participate in NR-01's Occupational Risk Management (GRO) — not only as an object of protection, but as a primary source of risk identification in their role. This section presents the risk inventory specific to the sound technician at live events, structured according to the risk groups of NR-01.

9.1 Risk inventory — sound technician at live events

Risk Group

Specific Hazard

Assessment (Prob × Sev)

Control Measures

Physical — Noise

Exposure to 95–115 dB SPL for 8–14 hours at the FOH of live shows

HIGH — high probability × serious severity (irreversible NIHL)

PPE: hearing protector NRR 25+ (plug or earmuff) | IEM calibrated ≤ 85 dB | Mandatory hearing breaks | Monthly SPL measurement | Updated LTCAT

Physical — Noise

Exposure to 110–125 dB SPL at a stage side-fill monitor during sound check

VERY HIGH — extreme severity

IEM mandatory for monitor technicians. Limit setting on the stage monitor drive.

Ergonomic — Biomechanical

Manual lifting of loudspeakers, amplifiers and racks > 23 kg

HIGH — high frequency × medium-high severity (low back pain, hernia)

Pallet jack for moving loads. Two technicians for items > 25 kg. Lifting technique. Back belt as a complement.

Ergonomic — Posture

Sedentary posture for 8–14 hours at the FOH console with the neck flexed toward the reference monitor

MEDIUM-HIGH — high time × progressive severity (RSI, cervical WRMSD)

Console at ergonomic height. Adjustable chair with lumbar support. Monitor at eye level. Breaks every 2 hours.

Psychosocial — FRPRT

Extreme deadline pressure (the show starts on time). Habitual shift of 16–22h. Continuous night work.

HIGH — high probability × high severity (burnout, NIHL accelerated by fatigue)

Shift planning with a buffer. Technician rotation. Overload communication channel. Minimum 11h rest between shifts (CLT).

Connecting power cables to an energized distribution board (plug/socket)

NR-10 training. 30 mA RCD on all circuits. Connection procedure with the system powered off.

Accident — Fall

Access to the rigging grid to position monitors or make adjustments during setup

HIGH — NR-35 requires training

NR-35 training. Full-body harness + double lanyard. Suitable work platform.


  10    PSYCHOSOCIAL RISKS — NR-01 AND THE SOUND TECHNICIAN'S MENTAL HEALTH

Section 10 — Psychosocial Risk Factors (FRPRT): the New Frontier of NR-01

MTE Ordinance No. 1,419/2024 expressly included the Work-Related Psychosocial Risk Factors (FRPRT) in NR-01's Occupational Risk Management, taking effect on May 26, 2026. For the live-events sector, this inclusion represents the regulatory recognition of a reality that professionals already know in practice: the combination of extreme shifts, deadline pressure and income instability constitutes one of the most aggressive environments for mental health among the professions of the cultural sector.

10.1 FRPRT specific to the sound technician at events

  • Quantitative overload: 3–5 shows per week in high-season periods, with shifts of 14–20 hours each — accumulating far above the CLT (8h + 2h overtime = 10h maximum per day)

  • Time pressure with a fixed deadline and public consequences: the show starts on time, in front of thousands of people — there is no room to "come back tomorrow and finish"

  • Exposure to extreme noise as a cultural norm: NIHL is normalized as "part of the trade" — which prevents the worker from claiming protective conditions without being seen as an amateur

  • Employment instability: most sound technicians are self-employed (as a company or sole proprietor) — with no access to PCMSO, PPRA, health insurance or occupational-disease leave

  • Systematic night work: post-show teardown in the early hours, multiple consecutive nights, with chronic sleep deprivation

  • Lack of recognition and invisibility: the sound technician is a co-creator of the audience's musical experience, but is rarely recognized — an effort-reward imbalance


⚠️  IMPORTANT — BURNOUT HAS BEEN AN OCCUPATIONAL DISEASE SINCE 2022

Since January 1, 2022, Burnout has been included as an occupational disease by the WHO in the ICD-11 (QD85: Occupational Burnout Syndrome). With the updated NR-01, an employer who does not include the FRPRT in the PGR, where a worker develops Burnout, may be held liable through a causal link. For the events sector, where Burnout is endemic and silent, this regulatory change carries significant practical weight.


  11    CASE STUDY — THE TECHNICIAN WHO LOST HIS HEARING

Section 11 — Real Case Study: NIHL in a Sound Technician for National Shows

11.1 Case profile

FOH technician, 38 years old, 14-year career in large national shows (stadiums, arenas and festivals). Worked regularly with PA systems of 150 kW to 400 kW, in a FOH position 30–50 meters from the stage, with exposure of 8–12 hours per day including soundcheck. Hearing protection used sporadically and inconsistently — "only when the system was very loud." No personal IEM; used an open-loudspeaker reference at the desk.

11.2 Development

After 9 years of intense work, the first symptoms appeared insidiously: difficulty understanding speech in noisy environments, persistent ringing (tinnitus) after shows, a "plugged" ear sensation that lasted days after large events. When seeking an audiological evaluation for the first time, the audiometry revealed a bilateral 4 kHz-notch loss of 40 dB HL (a moderate loss in the high-definition sound frequencies).

11.3 Professional and personal impact

The hearing loss in the 3–6 kHz frequencies compromised exactly the region of the spectrum most critical to the quality of mixing work: the presence of vocals (2–5 kHz), the attack of percussive instruments and the brightness of stringed instruments. The technician reported that his mixes became "full-bodied but without air" — and that he could no longer clearly perceive incipient feedback in those frequencies. Within 2 years of the diagnosis, he had to give up large-scale shows.

11.4 Lessons and prevention applied to NR-01

This case illustrates the importance of three practices that NR-01 (via the GRO) and NR-09/NR-15 require:

  • Regular SPL measurement at the FOH position and documentation in the LTCAT — a correct LTCAT would have classified the position as unhealthy and mandated the use of PPE from the start of the career

  • Annual occupational audiometry with comparison between consecutive audiograms — the progression of NIHL would have been detected 4–5 years earlier, allowing a change in behavior

  • An organizational culture of hearing protection — normalizing the use of IEMs and hearing protectors backstage reduces stigma and increases compliance


💡  TIP — FIELD dB MEASUREMENT TOOLS

Calibrated SPL measurement apps (NIOSH Sound Level Meter, Decibel X) are practical tools for the sound technician to measure the SPL at their position before and during events and make informed decisions about hearing protection. Although they do not replace the calibrated sound level meter required by the LTCAT, they are valuable resources for real-time awareness of the exposure level.

  • Bartlett, B.; Bartlett, J. Practical Recording Techniques. 7th edition. Focal Press, 2017. — Microphone, acoustics and recording techniques

SMU PRODUÇÕES

Professional Training in Occupational Safety

TECHNICAL HANDBOOK

NR-06

Personal Protective Equipment — PPE

SELECTION · CERTIFICATE OF APPROVAL · SUPPLY · USE · CONTROL


Module focused on the Live Events Sector

Workload: 4 hours  |  Level: Advanced Operational

Updated in accordance with MTE Ordinance No. 2,175/2022 and MTE Ordinance No. 57/2025

"PPE is the last line of defense — not the first solution."

  01    TECHNICAL INTRODUCTION — WHAT IS NR-06

NR-06: The Standard That Regulates the Last Line of Defense

NR-06 establishes the requirements for the approval, sale, supply and use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) in Brazil. Published in 1978 and deeply updated by MTE Ordinance No. 2,175/2022 and by MTE Ordinance No. 57/2025, it is one of the most visible standards in day-to-day operations — and also one of the most frequently disregarded in the live events sector.

Understanding NR-06 goes far beyond knowing which PPE to use. Event professionals must master: how to select the correct PPE for each risk identified in the PGR, how to check the validity of the Certificate of Approval (CA), what their legal responsibilities are as employer or worker, and how to integrate PPE management into the GRO/PGR system of NR-01.


Position of NR-06 in the OSH System

NR-06 does not operate on its own. It is part of the management system defined by NR-01 and complements the specific standards for each risk activity. This integration is mandatory and must be reflected in the PGR:


NR-01 (GRO/PGR)

Defines the residual risks that require PPE; PPE selection must be recorded in the Risk Inventory and in the PGR Action Plan

NR-06

Defines how PPE must be approved (CA), supplied, trained on, recorded, maintained and controlled

NR-35 (Height)

Specifies the mandatory PPE for work at height: helmet, class III full-body harness, double lanyard, fall arrester, anchorage

NR-10 (Electrical)

Defines specific PPE for temporary electrical installations: insulating rubber gloves, insulating footwear, goggles

NR-07 (PCMSO)

Health monitoring may indicate ineffectiveness of PPE in use — e.g., audiometric exams that reveal progressive hearing loss

NR-33 (Confined Spaces)

Specific PPE for stage pits and under-stage spaces: air-line respirator, portable gas detector


⚠️  FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLE OF NR-06

PPE must be supplied only after the possibilities of collective protection (EPC) and improvement in work organization have been exhausted — in accordance with the hierarchy of controls of NR-01. Supplying PPE without first assessing whether the risk could be eliminated or controlled collectively is a practice contrary to current legislation.


Ordinance of creation

MTb No. 3,214/1978

Major structural revision

MTP Ordinance No. 2,175/2022 — complete new wording of NR-06

Most recent update

MTE Ordinance No. 57/2025 — prohibition of assignment/loan of CA between manufacturing units

Technical standards update

MTE Ordinance No. 1,369/2024 — new table of technical standards with a risk category column

Scope of application

Organizations that acquire PPE + user workers + manufacturers and importers

CA requirement

All PPE manufactured in Brazil or imported may only be sold and used with a valid CA issued by the MTE


  02    DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION OF PPE

What is PPE? Legal Definition and Classification

Official Definition

According to NR-06 in its current wording: PPE is any device or product, for individual use worn by the worker, designed and manufactured to provide protection against the occupational risks present in the work environment, which is listed in Annex I of NR-06.

The 2022 revision broadened the concept to also cover systems or sets of individual-use devices. This covers the complete fall protection set — helmet + full-body harness + lanyard + fall arrester + anchorage point — treated as an integrated system of individual protection.


Classification by Body Region — Events Sector

Annex I of NR-06 organizes PPE by the protected body region. The table below maps the most relevant PPE for the live events sector:


Body Region

PPE

Risk at the Event

When to Use

HEAD

Class B safety helmet

Falling objects, electric shock at height

All setup and teardown involving work at height or load hoisting

HEARING

Earmuff or earplug type hearing protector

Noise above 85 dB (action level) and 90 dB (tolerance limit)

Proximity to PA systems, diesel generators, sound checks

EYES AND FACE

Safety goggles, face shield

Flying particles, welding, chemical splashes

Fixing metal structures, grinder, chemical floor cleaning

BREATHING

PFF2 respirator, half-face with filter

Dust, paint vapors, generator emissions, stage fog

Enclosed environments with a generator, stage painting, poorly ventilated spaces

HANDS AND ARMS

Suede leather gloves, insulating gloves

Cutting, abrasion, electric shock

Handling cables and sharp materials; temporary electrical connections

LEGS AND FEET

Safety footwear with steel toe cap

Crushing, puncture, electric shock

Entire setup area and backstage during event operation

TORSO

Class 2 or 3 high-visibility vest

Being struck by a vehicle, night work

Vehicle movement in the service area, nighttime operations on public roads

FALL-ARREST SYSTEM

Class III full-body harness + double lanyard + fall arrester

Fall from height above 2 meters

Rigging, fixing trusses, work on roofs, lighting towers


💡  FALL-ARREST SYSTEM — MANDATORY SET

The fall protection system is always an integrated set and never an isolated component: Class III Full-Body Harness + Lanyard with double locking + Certified anchorage point. The use of any isolated element does not provide adequate protection and does not comply with NR-35. The CA must cover the complete system.


  03    CERTIFICATE OF APPROVAL — CA

The CA: Legal Guarantee That the PPE Works

The Certificate of Approval (CA) is the central element of NR-06. Without a valid CA, the product cannot be sold as PPE, and the company that supplies it to the worker will be in violation — and may be held civilly and criminally liable in the event of an accident.

The CA is issued by the Ministry of Labor and Employment (MTE) after the equipment has been subjected to laboratory tests carried out by accredited laboratories, which prove the effectiveness of the equipment in the protection declared by the manufacturer.


How the CA System Works

Who issues it

MTE — General Coordination of OSH, upon request from the manufacturer/importer with approved laboratory tests

Where to verify

CAEPI System — public search on the MTE website by CA number, PPE type or manufacturer's corporate name

Where it appears on the product

Inscribed indelibly, legibly and visibly on the PPE itself, together with the batch number and date of manufacture

CA validity

Linked to the term of the conformity assessment — the manufacturer must request renewal before expiry

Renewal

The manufacturer requests renewal with new laboratory tests — without renewal, the CA is automatically canceled

Product change

Any technical modification to the PPE requires a new certification process — the previous CA loses validity for the altered product

2025 novelty

MTE Ordinance No. 57/2025 prohibited the sharing of the CA between headquarters and branches — each manufacturing unit needs its own CA


MTE Ordinance No. 1,369/2024 — New Table of Technical Standards

Published in August 2024 with immediate effect, this ordinance updated the reference technical standards for PPE certification, inserting a new Risk Category column. It directly affects the CA issuance and renewal processes and the technical selection of PPE for each type of risk.


🔴  ATTENTION — PURCHASING PPE WITHOUT A VALID CA

Before any PPE purchase, check the CA number printed on the product and consult the CAEPI system to confirm that it is active and within its validity period. PPE with an expired or canceled CA is equivalent to having no PPE — it does not legally protect the employer and does not physically protect the worker.


  04    SELECTING THE CORRECT PPE

How to Select PPE: Technical and Legal Criteria

Selecting the correct PPE is a technical and legal responsibility of the employer, advised by the SESMT (when there is one) or by the CIPA, always in consultation with the user workers. It is not enough to acquire just any helmet or any glove — the equipment must be suited to the specific risk identified in the Risk Inventory of the PGR.


Mandatory Selection Criteria — NR-06

The standard requires that the selection of PPE mandatorily take into account:


  • The prevention measures based on the hazards identified and the risks assessed in the PGR of NR-01

  • The effectiveness needed to control the residual risk — the PPE must in fact protect against the specific risk identified

  • The suitability for the worker and the comfort provided — it must be assessed together with the group of user workers

  • The compatibility between PPE items when it is necessary to use more than one at the same time — e.g., a helmet and an earmuff-type hearing protector must be compatible models

  • A valid CA at the moment of acquisition — not only at the moment of supply to the worker

  • The shelf life of the product itself — beyond the CA, the PPE has a service life that depends on the conditions of use and storage


⚠️  PRACTICAL ERROR — INCOMPATIBILITY BETWEEN PPE

In the events sector it is common to see workers using earmuff-type hearing protectors that do not seal properly when combined with the helmet. NR-06 requires that the combination of PPE be assessed for compatibility and joint effectiveness. The OSH technician must select models that work correctly in simultaneous use.


Practical Selection Table — Live Events


Activity at the Event

Main Risk

Minimum Mandatory PPE

Regulatory Basis

Rigging and trusses

Fall from height above 2m

Class B helmet + Class III full-body harness + Double lanyard + Fall arrester

NR-06 + NR-35

Operation of the PA system

Noise above 85 dB

Hearing protector with NRR compatible with the measured noise level

NR-06 + NHO-01 FUNDACENTRO

NR-06 + NR-10

Assembly of metal structures

Helmet + Suede leather gloves + Boot with steel toe cap

NR-06 + NR-18

Operation of a diesel generator

NR-06

NR-06

NR-06 + NR-33

Nitrile gloves + Sealing safety goggles + Waterproof apron

NR-06


  05    EMPLOYER OBLIGATIONS

What the Law Requires of the Employer

The employer's obligations under NR-06 are extensive, detailed and enforced. Failure to comply with any item may lead to a notice of violation, a fine and — in the event of an accident — civil and criminal liability. In the events sector, where work is intense and dynamic with frequently outsourced teams, these obligations are even more critical.

Complete List of Employer Obligations

  1. Acquire only PPE with a valid CA issued by the MTE — supplying PPE without a CA is expressly prohibited

  2. Provide the risk-appropriate PPE free of charge, in perfect condition of preservation and operation — charging or deducting from the worker's salary is prohibited

  3. Record the supply of PPE to the worker — by book, form, electronic system or biometric system

  4. Instruct and train the worker on correct use, storage, preservation, sanitizing and limitations of the PPE, in accordance with the manufacturer's manual

  5. Immediately replace the PPE when: damaged, lost, past its expiration date, or when it no longer provides adequate protection against the risk

  6. Be responsible for the periodic sanitizing and maintenance of the PPE, in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions — including the maximum number of sanitizing cycles

  7. Require and oversee the effective use of PPE by workers throughout the performance of the risk activities

  8. Report to the MTE any irregularity observed in PPE (improper CA, product not compliant with the specifications)

  9. Select the risk-appropriate PPE with the support of the SESMT or CIPA, record the selection and integrate it into the PGR

  10. Ensure the availability of disposable PPE in sufficient quantity, with immediate replacement, preserving the product's identification information


🔴  CRITICAL POINT — SUPPLY RECORD

The PPE delivery record is the employer's main — and often only — evidence in the event of an accident with a labor lawsuit. If the worker claims they did not receive adequate PPE and there is no record, the company has no defense. Electronic systems with biometrics or a digital signature are the best practice for proof.


PPE Management with Outsourced Teams

The multi-supplier model typical of events (rigging, sound, lighting, structure, security, catering) requires special attention in PPE management:

  • Each contracted company is responsible for the PPE of its own workers

  • The contracting company must verify whether the contractors are complying with NR-06 within its premises

  • In the event of an accident involving an outsourced worker without adequate PPE, liability may be shared between the contractor and the contracting party

  • The contracting party's PGR must include the PPE requirements for all contractors working at the event


  06    WORKER OBLIGATIONS

Worker Responsibilities Regarding PPE

NR-06 does not impose obligations only on the employer. The worker also has explicit legal responsibilities — and non-compliance may result in disciplinary measures, including dismissal for just cause under the terms of Article 482 of the CLT.



Legal Obligations of the Worker — NR-06

  1. Use the PPE only for the purpose for which it is intended — modifying, altering or replacing it with non-certified alternatives is prohibited

  2. Be responsible for the storage and preservation of the PPE received — take care of the equipment between uses and store it properly

  3. Immediately report to the employer any damage, defect, alteration, loss or situation in which the PPE is not providing adequate protection

  4. Undergo the training provided by the employer on correct use, sanitizing and limitations of each PPE

  5. Use the PPE correctly and effectively — not merely carry it, but use it properly throughout the exposure to the risk


⚠️  REFUSAL TO USE PPE

A worker who refuses to use mandatory PPE may face progressive disciplinary measures: warning, suspension and dismissal for just cause. However, the company may only apply these measures after proving that it: supplied the correct PPE, trained the worker and oversaw its use consistently. The refusal must be documented.


Can the Worker Refuse Inadequate PPE?

Yes. The worker has the right to refuse to use PPE that is: inadequate for the identified risk, uncomfortable to the point of compromising the effectiveness of the protection, with an expired CA or without a CA. In this case, they must formally notify the employer and the OSH manager. This right is aligned with the right to refuse a serious and imminent risk activity provided for in NR-01.


Culture of Correct Use — The Sector's Greatest Challenge

In the events sector, the greatest challenge is not the supply of PPE — it is ensuring consistent use throughout the entire operation. Common phrases that preceded serious accidents:

  • "I'll just go up there real quick" — an accident does not choose the duration of the exposure

  • "It's hot, I'll take off the helmet for just a minute" — falling objects do not wait

  • "The hearing protector gets in the way of hearing the radio" — PPE compatible with communication exists; removing the hearing protector is not the answer

  • "I've done this a thousand times without PPE" — the risk does not decrease with repetition; the perception of the risk decreases


  07    OBLIGATIONS OF THE MANUFACTURER AND IMPORTER

The Chain of Responsibility: Manufacturers and Importers

NR-06 creates a chain of responsibility that begins with the manufacturer and ends with the user worker. The safety professional in events needs to understand that, when acquiring PPE, they are relying on this chain — and that checking the CA before purchase is the only way to guarantee that it worked correctly.


Obligations of the Manufacturer / Importer

  • Sell only PPE with a valid CA issued by the MTE — selling a product without certification is prohibited

  • Inscribe indelibly on the product: the CA number, the manufacturing batch number and the date of manufacture

  • Provide an instruction manual in Brazilian Portuguese with: correct use, restrictions, cleaning processes, maintenance and the maximum number of sanitizing cycles before mandatory replacement

  • The manual may be in electronic format, but the packaging must mandatorily contain: composition materials, instructions for use, protection offered, restrictions and the CA number

  • Provide adaptations of the PPE for workers with physical disabilities, when technically applicable

  • Renew the CA periodically and report to the MTE any change in the product before marketing it

  • Register with the MTE to operate as a manufacturer or importer of PPE within the national territory

  • Since MTE Ordinance No. 57/2025: each manufacturing unit must have its own CA, with the assignment or loan of the CA between headquarters and branch being prohibited


💡  ELECTRONIC MANUAL — PRACTICAL ATTENTION

When the PPE comes without a printed manual, only with a QR code or electronic link, the original packaging must contain all the minimum mandatory information. Keep the original packaging or download the manual for the company's safety file. During an inspection, the inspector may request the PPE manual.


  08    SUPPLY, RECORDING AND CONTROL

Operational Management of PPE: From Purchase to Disposal

Compliance with NR-06 requires a management system covering the entire PPE life cycle: acquisition with CA verification, proper storage, distribution with recording, training, maintenance, inspection, replacement and disposal. In the events sector, where teams are formed temporarily and equipment circulates between many hands, this control is especially critical.


PPE Supply Recording System

The delivery record is a legal obligation and the employer's main documentary evidence. NR-06 accepts:

  • Registration book with the worker's signature

  • Individual PPE form with equipment data and the recipient's signature

  • Electronic system with login and digital acceptance

  • Biometric system with fingerprint or facial recognition

The record must contain at least: the worker's name, position/function, type of PPE, CA number, delivery date and the recipient's signature.


Validity and Replacement Control

PPE CA

Check in the CAEPI system before purchase and keep a record of the CA of each PPE in stock

Product validity

Respect the expiration date printed on the packaging/product by the manufacturer

Service life in use

Depends on the conditions of use, frequency, exposure to the risk and storage — inspect after each intense use

Immediate replacement

PPE that is damaged, deformed, cracked, with a broken seam, a fatigued elastic ring or any compromise of integrity

Sanitizing

Follow the manufacturer's protocol — maximum number of washing cycles before mandatory replacement

Disposable PPE

Ensure availability in sufficient quantity for all workers, with immediate replacement


🔴  HIDDEN RISK — PPE PAST ITS VALIDITY

In the events sector, it is common to find full-body harnesses with worn seams, helmets dented from previous falls, rubber gloves with micro-holes and hearing protectors with deteriorated foams — all being used normally. PPE with compromised integrity does not protect and exposes the employer to full liability in the event of an accident.


  09    TRAINING ON PPE

Training: How to Use, Preserve and Know the Limitations

NR-06 requires the employer to train the worker on the correct use of the PPE supplied. This training is not a one-time event — it must be carried out before the first use and whenever there is a change of function, of risk or of type of PPE. In the events sector, the pre-operation safety briefing is the ideal moment for this reinforcement.


Minimum Content of PPE Training — NR-06

  1. What the PPE is for — which specific risk it protects against and which risks it does NOT cover (limitations)

  2. How to put on, adjust and remove the PPE correctly — individual practical demonstration

  3. How to inspect the PPE before each use — what to check and which signs indicate that the PPE must be replaced

  4. How to store and preserve the PPE after use — suitable location, protection against sunlight, humidity and chemical products

  5. How to carry out sanitizing in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions — permitted and prohibited products

  6. What to do when the PPE presents a defect, damage or loss — communication channel and replacement process

  7. The legal obligations of the worker regarding the PPE supplied


💡  BEST PRACTICES — DDS AND SIPAT

The pre-operation Daily Safety Dialogue (DDS) is the best moment to reinforce the correct use of the PPE specific to that activity. At the annual SIPAT, include practical demonstrations of PPE inspection — especially full-body harnesses and helmets, which accumulate damage invisible in everyday use.


  10    INSPECTION AND PENALTIES

What the MTE Inspector Checks in NR-06

The inspection by the Ministry of Labor at live events has intensified after high-profile accidents. The labor audit inspector has the power to issue notices of violation, fines and to shut down operations in the event of non-compliance with NR-06.


What the Inspector Checks in NR-06

  • Existence and validity of the CA of each PPE in use — on-site verification on the product and in the CAEPI system

  • Suitability of the PPE for the activity risk — cross-referenced with the Risk Inventory of the PGR

  • Supply records — forms or electronic system with the worker's signature

  • Evidence of training on the use, preservation and limitations of the PPE

  • Condition of preservation of the PPE in use — damaged PPE is an immediate irregularity

  • Effective use by the workers during the operation — PPE hanging from the belt or stored in the backpack during the activity is a violation

  • Availability of PPE for all workers, including contractor teams


Penalties for Non-Compliance

Minor violation

Incomplete supply record, lack of documented training, PPE without a manual in Portuguese

Moderate violation

Supply of PPE without a valid CA, failure to replace damaged PPE, lack of records

Serious violation

Worker performing a risk activity without adequate PPE — risk of an accident with injury

Very serious violation

Accident with serious injury or death due to the absence or inadequacy of mandatory PPE

Immediate shutdown

Activity with serious and imminent risk due to the absence of PPE — the inspector may halt the operation on the spot

Civil liability

If it is proven that the accident occurred due to the lack of adequate PPE, the company is liable for material and moral damages


  11    OPERATIONAL PROCEDURE AND CHECKLIST

NR-06 Operational Protocol for Events

Efficient PPE management requires structured routines that cover each phase of the operation. Compliance with NR-06 must be verified before, during and after each event — and this process must be documented.


BEFORE THE EVENT — Preparation Checklist

  1. Check the PPE stock — sufficient quantity for all workers of all teams, including contractors

  2. Confirm a valid CA for each type of PPE in the CAEPI system — record it in the compliance file

  3. Inspect the condition of preservation of all PPE — immediately replace those that are damaged or past their validity

  4. Confirm valid PPE training for all workers — onboarding or periodic in accordance with the applicable NR

  5. Prepare the PPE delivery form for the pre-operation briefing

  6. Check the compatibility between PPE items that will be used simultaneously — e.g., helmet + hearing protector


DURING THE OPERATION — Monitoring

  1. Distribute PPE at the pre-operation briefing with a delivery record and use guidance

  2. Oversee the effective use of PPE throughout the setup — not only at the start

  3. Immediately replace any PPE damaged during the operation

  4. Reinforce use at the start of teardown — the phase of greatest fatigue and least attention to PPE


AFTER THE EVENT — Maintenance and Recording

  1. Collect and inspect all returned PPE — record the condition of each item

  2. Sanitize the reusable PPE in accordance with the manufacturer's protocol

  3. Dispose of PPE with compromised integrity — never reintroduce damaged PPE into the stock

  4. Update the control forms with the date of use, condition and forecast for replacement


Final NR-06 Compliance Checklist


✅  VALID CA

All PPE in use have an active CA verified in the MTE's CAEPI system

✅  FREE SUPPLY

No worker paid or will have a deduction for supplied PPE

✅  DELIVERY RECORD

Forms or electronic system with the signature of all workers

✅  DOCUMENTED TRAINING

Evidence of training by function — content, date, workload and signatures

✅  PRESERVATION

PPE in perfect condition — without damage, deformation or expired term

✅  COMPATIBILITY

Combined PPE verified for effectiveness in simultaneous use

✅  CONTRACTORS

PPE of outsourced teams verified — CA and condition of preservation

✅  INTEGRATED PGR

PPE selection documented and referenced in the Risk Inventory


  12    INTEGRATION OF NR-06 WITH THE OSH SYSTEM

NR-06 and the PGR: A Mandatory Integration

NR-06 does not operate as an isolated standard. It is the practical unfolding of the hierarchy of controls defined in NR-01. The PGR defines the residual risks; NR-06 defines how the PPE must be managed to control those risks. This integration is required by the standard itself — the selection of PPE must be recorded in the PGR.



NR-06 Integrated into the Risk Inventory

Each residual risk identified in the Risk Inventory that requires PPE as a control measure must contain in the PGR:

  • Type of PPE selected for that specific risk

  • CA number and manufacturer of the selected PPE

  • Justification for the selection — why this PPE is suitable for this risk

  • Person responsible for control and supply

  • Inspection frequency and replacement criterion


PPE and eSocial — Mandatory Connection

The Risk Inventory of the PGR, which includes PPE as control measures, directly feeds the S-2240 event of eSocial (Environmental Conditions of Work). This information is audited by the MTE and by the Federal Revenue Service. PPE not recorded in the PGR and in eSocial is equivalent to PPE not supplied for the purposes of digital auditing.


  13    CONCLUSION AND TECHNICAL SUMMARY

Closing: PPE as a Management System, not as a Bureaucratic Rule

🎨  IMAGE PROMPT (Nanobanana)

Professional panoramic photograph showing a complete live event production team in Brazil correctly using PPE during the assembly of a large stage, riggers with complete full-body harnesses working at height on the trusses, stage crew with helmets and safety boots organizing the metal structure, sound technicians with hearing protectors working near the PA system, everyone with high-visibility vests, an OSH supervisor with a checklist clipboard in Portuguese moving around the area, safety signage plates in Portuguese visible, a sense of genuine safety culture and high-level professional operation, golden afternoon light, cinematic panoramic 16:9 composition


NR-06 transforms PPE from a bureaucratic object into a technical risk management tool. A professional live event operation does not ask "do you need to use PPE?" — it asks "which is the correct PPE for this specific risk, which is the valid CA, who was trained and is the supply recorded?"


FROM: PPE as bureaucracy

TO: PPE as the last line of defense in the risk control system

FROM: Buying just any glove or helmet

TO: Selecting PPE with a valid CA suited to the risk identified in the PGR

FROM: Deliver and forget

TO: Train, record, oversee, maintain and replace systematically

FROM: Isolated PPE management

TO: PPE integrated into the GRO/PGR of NR-01 and into eSocial

FROM: Inspection as a threat

TO: Documentation as legal protection for the employer and the worker


The Six Pillars of PPE Management in Events

  1. TECHNICAL SELECTION: Valid CA + suitability for the PGR risk + compatibility between PPE items

  2. FREE SUPPLY: Every worker, without exception — including contractor teams

  3. ROBUST RECORDING: Signed form or electronic system — it is your legal defense

  4. EFFECTIVE TRAINING: Not just deliver — demonstrate, verify understanding, document

  5. ACTIVE MAINTENANCE: Inspect, sanitize, replace — deteriorated PPE is nonexistent PPE

  6. INTEGRATION WITH THE PGR: PPE as a control measure documented in the Risk Inventory and in eSocial


"PPE is the last line of defense — not the first solution."

PRODUCTIONS

Professional Training in Occupational Safety

TECHNICAL HANDBOOK

NR-17

Ergonomics

BIOMECHANICS · WORK ORGANIZATION · PSYCHOSOCIAL RISKS · AEP · AET


Module focused on the Live Events Sector

Workload: 4 hours  |  Level: Advanced Operational

In accordance with NR-17 (Ordinance MTP No. 423/2021) · NR-01 (Ordinance MTE No. 1,419/2024) · NBR ISO 9241

"The body does not forget what work imposes — but ergonomics can change what work demands."

  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-17 IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 1 — Ergonomics as the Science of Adapting Work to the Human Being

Regulatory Standard No. 17 — Ergonomics — establishes the guidelines and requirements that allow working conditions to be adapted to the psychophysiological characteristics of workers, so as to provide comfort, safety, health and efficient performance at work. Updated by Ordinance MTP No. 423/2021, it is the only Brazilian OSH standard that explicitly recognizes the worker's psychophysiological dimension as an object of protection — not only the physical body, but also the mind, the cognitive functions and the organizational conditions that shape the work experience.

The fundamental principle of ergonomics — and of NR-17 — is the inversion of the conventional logic of work: instead of adapting the worker to the demands of the workstation, ergonomics adapts the workstation to the characteristics of the worker. This inversion has profound and practical consequences: it is not the sound technician who must "make do" with an inadequate chair at the FOH desk over a 12-hour operation; it is the desk and the chair that must be dimensioned to the technician's bodily characteristics and to the task.

In the live events sector, the application of NR-17 faces a characteristic operational paradox: the industry is built on intensity, improvisation and time compression — values that are inherently contrary to the ergonomic principles of sustainable pace, adequate breaks and stable working conditions. Uninterrupted 20-hour setups, double shifts between load-in and load-out, console operators seated for 10 hours without a regulated break, and roadies carrying 60 kg flight cases without mechanical aid are endemic practices that constitute systemic violations of NR-17 — and that generate, as a consequence, a silent and progressive musculoskeletal and psychosocial illness among the sector's workers.




Legal basis

NR-17 — Ordinance MTP No. 423/2021 | Complemented by Ordinance MTE No. 1,419/2024 (Psychosocial Risks)

Objective

Adapt working conditions to the PSYCHOPHYSIOLOGICAL characteristics of workers

Scope of application

All work situations of organizations with CLT employees — without exception by sector or size

Working conditions

Primary instrument

AEP — Preliminary Ergonomic Assessment (mandatory for all at-risk workstations)

In-depth instrument

AET report retention period

Integration with NR-01

🔴  CRITICAL UPDATE — ORDINANCE MTE No. 1,419/2024

Ordinance MTE No. 1,419/2024 expressly included Work-Related Psychosocial Risk Factors (FRPRT) in the GRO of NR-01, effective as of May 26, 2026. The management of the FRPRT must be carried out together with NR-17: starting with the AEP and integrating psychosocial factors into the PGR Risk Inventory. In the events sector, where abusive shifts, production harassment and deadline pressure are endemic, this update is of special importance.


  02    AEP AND AET — THE TECHNICAL INSTRUMENTS OF NR-17

Module 2 — From Screening to In-Depth Diagnosis: AEP and AET

NR-17 structures the ergonomic assessment into two hierarchical and complementary levels: the Preliminary Ergonomic Assessment (AEP) and the Ergonomic Work Analysis (AET). Understanding the distinction between these two instruments — and the triggers that activate each one — is a fundamental competency of the OSH professional in any sector, including live events.

2.1 AEP — Preliminary Ergonomic Assessment

The AEP is the mandatory ergonomic screening: every workstation that presents conditions requiring adaptation to the psychophysiological characteristics of workers must be the object of an AEP. It may be carried out through qualitative methods (observation, interview, checklist), semi-quantitative methods (rating scales) or quantitative methods (biomechanical measurements), according to the risk and the legal requirements. The result of the AEP feeds directly into the PGR Risk Inventory — the identified ergonomic risks must be documented together with the planned control measures.

In the practice of the events sector, the AEP of a stage PA technician role would immediately identify: a shift exceeding 12 hours without regulated breaks, manual lifting of loads above the biomechanical limit appropriate to the task, forced postures during the assembly of equipment in confined spaces, and exposure to noise above 85 dB without hearing protection. Each of these items must be recorded in the Risk Inventory and have an associated control measure in the PGR Action Plan.

2.2 AET — Ergonomic Work Analysis

The AET is the in-depth diagnosis carried out when the AEP indicates the need for a more detailed study, or in the specific situations set out in item 17.3.2 of NR-17:

  • When the need for a more in-depth assessment of the work situation is observed

  • When inadequacies or insufficiency of the actions adopted based on the AEP are identified

  • When suggested by the PCMSO — when there is an association between injuries and health impairments and the risks identified at the workstations

  • When a cause related to working conditions is indicated in the analysis of an accident or occupational disease recorded in the PGR

The AET is an in-depth study that includes: analysis of the demand (complaint, problem or situation to be investigated); analysis of the prescribed task versus the actual activity (how the work is really done, not how it appears in the manual); analysis of the working conditions in accordance with NR-17; direct participation of workers in building the diagnosis; formulation of technical and organizational recommendations; and feedback of the results with validation by the parties involved. The AET report must be kept available at the organization for a period of 20 years.

When to Apply

  03    WORK ORGANIZATION — THE HEART OF ERGONOMICS

Module 3 — Work Organization: Pace, Breaks and Overload

Work organization is the aspect of NR-17 most relevant to the live events sector — and the most systematically ignored. The standard defines that work organization must consider: production standards; the operating method; the time requirement; the work pace; the content of the tasks and the technical instruments and means available; and the cognitive aspects that may compromise the worker's safety and health.

In the events sector, the "production standard" is implicit and tyrannically simple: the show starts at the scheduled time. This absolute deadline requirement creates a time pressure that reorganizes the entire hierarchy of the team's priorities — worker safety is frequently sacrificed on the altar of the show's punctuality. NR-17 does not eliminate the show's deadline, but it requires that work organization — the planning of the setup, staff allocation, time management — be structured in a way that does not generate psychophysiological overload on workers in order to meet the deadline.

3.1 Work Shifts in Events: the Central Problem

The work shift in the live events sector is structurally incompatible with the ergonomic parameters of NR-17 in its usual configuration. Uninterrupted 18-to-24-hour setups, post-show teardown from midnight to 6 a.m. (accumulating on top of the previous day's workload), and weeks with multiple consecutive shows without adequate rest create a level of accumulated fatigue that compromises the safety, health and efficiency of workers in documentable ways.

Accumulated fatigue has immediate operational consequences beyond the health ones: electrical connection errors, forgetting of safety procedures, reduced reaction time, impaired decision-making and a marked increase in the risk of accidents. Occupational medicine studies document that after 17 hours of wakefulness, cognitive performance is equivalent to that of a person with 0.05% blood alcohol — above the legal limit for driving in Brazil.

3.2 Breaks as a Right and as a Performance Strategy

NR-17 is explicit: when it is not possible to eliminate extreme postures or continuous repetitive movements, breaks and alternation of activities must mandatorily be adopted. Breaks must: not be accompanied by an increase in the individual's cadence (the worker cannot be required to "make up for" the break time by speeding up the pace); and must be taken away from the workstations.

In the context of a large-scale show, implementing breaks requires advance staff planning — it is impossible to give a PA console operator a break during the performance without a qualified substitute. The ergonomically correct solution is to schedule enough workers to cover the regulated breaks, including the break time in the event's staff planning — not as an additional cost, but as part of the correct operational cost of the operation.


⚠️  WORK ORGANIZATION — THE EMPLOYER'S RESPONSIBILITY

NR-17 is unequivocal: work organization is the employer's responsibility. The claim that "the events sector just works this way" does not constitute a legal defense in the event of an occupational disease or accident caused by fatigue. The event producer who demands 22-hour shifts without breaks and without team rotation is in breach of NR-17 and exposed to labor, civil and social-security liability.

  04    BIOMECHANICS AND MANUAL LIFTING OF LOADS

Module 4 — Body in Motion: Biomechanics Applied to Work in Events

Occupational biomechanics is the subfield of ergonomics that studies the forces that work imposes on the human musculoskeletal system — bones, muscles, tendons, ligaments, cartilage and intervertebral discs. In the live events sector, where the manual lifting, transport and positioning of heavy equipment is a daily activity, understanding the biomechanical limits of the human body is operational knowledge directly related to the prevention of serious and permanent injuries.

NR-17 addresses the individual lifting, transport and unloading of loads based on biomechanical principles, eliminating the concept of a "fixed weight limit" and replacing it with a contextual analysis that considers: the weight of the load, the frequency of lifting, the horizontal and vertical distance of the movement, the posture adopted, the characteristics of the worker and the conditions of the environment. This contextual approach is more protective than the fixed limit, because it recognizes that a 20 kg lift may be safe once a day but dangerous when repeated 50 times in the same shift.

4.1 Reach Zones and Posture in Lifting

NR-17 establishes that the pick-up and deposit locations for loads must be organized so as not to force the worker to perform excessive trunk flexion, extension and rotation and other forced positioning of the body segments. The lifting of loads must not exceed 60 centimeters of horizontal reach relative to the body — a principle that in events is frequently violated when flight cases are pulled out of trucks with the arms fully extended.

The optimal working zone for manual lifting is the functional reach zone: between elbow height (approximately 1.0-1.1 m) and the height of the lower third of the thigh (0.6-0.8 m), with a maximum horizontal distance of 30 to 40 cm from the body. Outside this zone, the muscular force required increases exponentially and the risk of lumbar injury grows in a non-linear fashion. An L4-L5 intervertebral disc safely withstands compressions of up to 3,400 N (Newtons); a 23 kg lift with a 60 cm reach and the trunk inclined 45° can generate compression of up to 6,500 N in that same disc.



4.2 Factors that Amplify the Risk in Manual Lifting

  05    WORKSTATIONS — FURNITURE AND PHYSICAL CONDITIONS

Module 5 — The Workstation as an Ergonomic Instrument

NR-17 dedicates a specific section to workstation furniture, establishing that the set of furniture must present adjustments in one or more elements that allow it to be adapted to the anthropometric characteristics that meet the needs of the group of workers involved and the nature of the work. The principle of adjustability — the workstation's ability to be adapted to different heights and body types — is the central requirement that distinguishes an ergonomic workstation from a generic one.

In the live events sector, workstations are frequently improvised, temporary and standardized for the logistics of the event — not for the characteristics of the workers who will occupy them. A professional audio console placed on a generic fixed-height riser, a plastic chair without adjustment or lumbar support, an equipment rack whose operating height forces the operator to keep the shoulders raised for hours — these are workstations that are non-compliant with NR-17, regardless of their technological sophistication.

5.1 Furniture Requirements for Event Workstations

  • Tables and consoles: height compatible with the type of activity, with the required distance from the eyes to the work field and with the operator's height — adjustable or dimensioned for the assigned worker

  • Seats for prolonged work: adjustable height; adequate support for the back, especially in the lumbar region; seat surface with a depth compatible with the length of the operator's thighs; rounded edges (no pressure on the back of the thighs)

  • Footrest: mandatory when the height of the seat does not allow natural support of the feet on the floor — common at elevated audio consoles

  • Posture alternation: whenever the work can be performed alternating standing with sitting, the workstation must be designed to favor this alternation — a standing vs. seated operation schedule for long-shift operators

  • Optimal reach zone: frequently used materials and controls within the normal reach zone (without elbow extension above 90°); occasionally used materials may be in the maximum reach zone

  • Workstation lighting: appropriate to the visual task — lighting consoles operate in a darkened environment but require reading light for the cue sheets; a minimum work area of 200 lux for precision tasks




  06    ENVIRONMENTAL COMFORT — NOISE, LIGHTING AND TEMPERATURE

Module 6 — The Environment as an Ergonomic Factor

NR-17 addresses environmental comfort conditions as integral components of working conditions. In the live events sector, three environmental vectors present a systematic ergonomic risk: noise (which in the show environment routinely exceeds 100 dB), lighting (which in technical operation areas is frequently inadequate for the precise tasks performed) and thermal conditions (which at outdoor events or in warehouses without climate control can create conditions of significant thermal stress).

6.1 Noise — The Most Prevalent Environmental Risk in Events

Noise in live events operates on two distinct risk scales: the risk to hearing (regulated by NR-09, with an exposure limit of 85 dB for 8 hours) and the ergonomic risk of cognitive and psychophysiological overload (addressed by NR-17). NR-17 establishes that, for purposes of acoustic comfort in indoor work environments, the background noise level must respect the reference values of the technical standards — for effective verbal communication, the reference limit is up to 65 dB(A).

In event practice, technicians who operate in areas near the main stage during the performance are exposed to levels of 100 to 110 dB — values that, beyond the hearing risk (NR-09), compromise verbal communication among the team, increase the cognitive load of all tasks involving auditory processing, and contribute to accelerated mental fatigue. The ergonomic solution is not only to provide a hearing protector (an NR-06 and NR-09 measure) — it is to restructure team communication toward systems that do not depend on the voice in a noisy environment (radio, digital display, coded hand signals).

6.2 Lighting — Visibility for Precision Tasks

Live events create lighting paradoxes: the stage is intensely lit while the technical work areas (backstage, sub-stage, rack area, LX console) frequently operate in dimness or darkness. For the precision tasks performed by technicians in these areas — connecting cables and connectors, reading cue sheets, inspecting equipment — inadequate lighting is an ergonomic risk factor that causes visual fatigue, operational errors and forced postures (leaning toward the task to see better).

6.3 Thermal Conditions — Heat Stress at Outdoor Events

Outdoor events in the Brazilian tropical climate create conditions of significant thermal stress for setup workers. NR-17 requires that thermal comfort be ensured in work situations — in climate-controlled environments, the range between 18°C and 25°C. For outdoor work, the control measure involves: scheduling heavy loading during the cooler hours, providing drinking water in adequate quantity, shaded rest areas, and PPE appropriate to the weather conditions. These measures are not optional — they are working conditions that the employer is obligated to provide.


  07    PSYCHOSOCIAL RISKS — NR-01 AND NR-17 INTEGRATED

Module 7 — Psychosocial Risk Factors in the Events Sector

The inclusion of Work-Related Psychosocial Risk Factors (FRPRT) in the Occupational Risk Management of NR-01 by Ordinance MTE No. 1,419/2024, effective as of May 26, 2026, represents the most significant regulatory change for the live events sector in recent years. This sector concentrates, in a structural and endemic way, precisely the psychosocial risk factors that the standard aims to identify and control: abusive shifts, deadline pressure, production harassment, lack of recognition, continuous night work and the absence of any boundary between personal and professional life.

Psychosocial factors are situations that involve the way activities are planned, organized and carried out. When they are not well managed, these situations harm the mental, physical and social health of workers. The WHO estimates that 12 billion working days are lost annually worldwide due to depression and anxiety — conditions with a documented causal link to work-related psychosocial risk factors.
