🔴  EFFECTIVE DATE — 05/26/2026

As of May 26, 2026, events-sector companies that keep CLT employees are required to include Work-Related Psychosocial Risk Factors in the PGR Risk Inventory. The management of the FRPRT must be carried out together with NR-17, starting with the AEP (Preliminary Ergonomic Assessment). Non-compliance subjects the company to citation by the MTE and to judicial liability for harm to workers' mental health.


  08    RSI/WMSD — OCCUPATIONAL INJURIES IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 8 — RSI/WMSD: the Diseases the Sector Does Not Talk About

Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSI) and Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WMSD) are the set of musculoskeletal diseases with an established causal link to working conditions — especially to prolonged exposure to repetitive effort, forced postures, static muscular overload and excessive lifting of loads. In the live events sector, these conditions are endemic but rarely reported — in part because a large share of workers is freelance, without access to the occupational health system that detects and records these impairments.

The most frequent RSI/WMSD in live events workers include: occupational low back pain (chronic lumbar pain from excessive lifting and forced postures during setup); shoulder and wrist tendinitis (lighting technicians who connect and reconnect hundreds of cables per event); carpal tunnel syndrome (audio technicians who operate console faders and buttons for long periods); and rotator cuff syndrome (roadies who throw flight cases onto high shelves or hoist equipment overhead repeatedly).

8.1 RSI/WMSD Prevention Measures in accordance with NR-17

NR-17 (item 17.4.2) determines that in activities requiring static or dynamic muscular overload of the trunk, neck, head, upper and lower limbs, technical engineering, organizational and/or administrative measures must be adopted to eliminate or reduce these overloads. The priority hierarchy is:

  1. ELIMINATE (engineering): replace manual lifting with mechanical equipment (pallet jack, forklift, hoist); redesign the workstation to eliminate the forced posture

  2. REDUCE (engineering): reduce the unit weight of the loads (smaller packages); adjust the workstation height to reduce the reach; install conveyors or ramps to reduce the transport effort

  3. CONTROL (organizational): rotation of workers between high- and low-ergonomic-risk tasks; alternation of activities; regulated breaks

  4. PROTECT (administrative): training in safe lifting techniques; ergonomic PPE (a back belt in specific and temporary cases); awareness about postures

The back belt (abdominal belt) is frequently used as a primary protective measure in events — a practice that NR-17 does not support as a main measure. The back belt does not protect the intervertebral disc from compression overload; it merely reduces trunk movement. The correct approach, in accordance with the NR-17 hierarchy, is first to eliminate or reduce the need for manual lifting — the belt is a complementary measure, not a substitute for engineering and organizational measures.


  09    COGNITIVE ERGONOMICS — THE MIND ALSO WORKS

Module 9 — Cognitive Load: the Invisible Risk of Technical Operation in Events

NR-17 explicitly incorporated cognitive aspects as an object of ergonomics, determining that work organization must consider the cognitive aspects that may compromise the worker's safety and health. This inclusion recognizes that intellectual work and work of concentrated attention — the processing of information, real-time decision-making, the simultaneous monitoring of multiple systems — generates real and measurable overload on the human organism.

In the live events sector, the cognitive load is especially high in real-time operation functions: the FOH technician who mixes the show live simultaneously monitors dozens of audio channels, responds to cues from the artist on stage, anticipates the scene changes of the technical script and communicates with the monitor engineer — all in real time, without the possibility of "undoing" an error that has already gone through the speakers to 20,000 people. This intense cognitive concentration over prolonged periods, without adequate breaks, is an ergonomic risk factor that NR-17 requires to be assessed and controlled.

9.1 Cognitive Overload Risk Factors in Events

  • Simultaneous monitoring of multiple systems without auxiliary technical support (one operator for multiple simultaneous responsibilities)

  • Real-time decision-making under immediate and public consequence — with no margin for error and no "second opinion" support

  • Extensive technical scripts with a high density of information and frequent cues without periods of lower demand

  • Fragmented communication in a high-noise environment — the operator processes the radio communication simultaneously with the primary operating task

  • Night work in a state of sleep deprivation — which reduces the capacity for sustained attention and increases the error rate by up to 300%

  • Deadline pressure without the possibility of interruption for cognitive recalibration

9.2 Cognitive Overload Control Measures

  • Quality technical documentation: detailed show script, numbered cue lists, setup checklists — reduces dependence on working memory and frees cognitive capacity for monitoring

  • Division of responsibilities: a schedule of specific functions that prevents a single operator from monitoring more systems than the human attention capacity can handle

  • Mandatory cognitive breaks: a minimum of 10 minutes every 2 hours of cognitively intense operation, away from the workstation, without access to digital devices

  • Pre-show technical briefing: an organized presentation of all critical information before operation — reduces the ad hoc processing load during the show

  • Sleep management: do not schedule workers in critical cognitive operation (FOH console, automated system operation) after a shift already exceeding 14 hours


  10    NR-17 IN THE PGR — INTEGRATION AND DOCUMENTATION

Module 10 — Ergonomics in the OSH Management System

The integration of NR-17 into the OSH management system of NR-01 is the greatest practical change brought by the regulatory update for the events sector. Ergonomics ceases to be a standalone document (the company's "AET") and becomes an integral and dynamic part of the PGR — with its risks in the Inventory, its control measures in the Action Plan, its indicators in continuous monitoring and its revisions linked to the PGR triggers.

10.1 NR-17 Integration into the PGR Risk Inventory

The PGR Risk Inventory must include, for each workstation in the events sector that presents exposure to ergonomic factors:

  • Identification of the ergonomic factor (biomechanical, cognitive, organizational or psychosocial)

  • Description of the work situation that generates the risk — not just the name of the risk

  • Exposed workers — function, number and shift

  • Risk assessment — severity × probability (result of the AEP)

  • Existing control measures — what is already implemented

  • Proposed control measures — what needs to be implemented

  • Responsible party and Action Plan deadline


10.2 NR-17 Integration with Other NRs in the Context of Events

NR-01 (GRO/PGR)

Ergonomic risks and FRPRT in the Risk Inventory; AEP and AET as instruments of the PGR; ergonomic measures in the Action Plan

NR-07 (PCMSO)

The PCMSO must contain protocols for screening RSI/WMSD and psychosocial impairments; examinations targeted at high-ergonomic-risk functions; documented causal link

NR-11 (Materials Handling)

Complements NR-17 in the lifting and transport of loads — NR-11 governs the equipment, NR-17 governs the worker's biomechanical capacity and the organization of the task

NR-09 (LTCAT/Agents)

Noise as a physical agent measured by NR-09; acoustic comfort as an ergonomic requirement of NR-17 — complementary approaches that result in distinct actions

NR-15 (Unhealthy Work)

Heat as an unhealthiness agent measured by NR-15 (WBGT); thermal comfort as an ergonomic requirement of NR-17 — combined interventions

NR-18 (Construction)

Ergonomics of assembling stages, bleachers and tents — NR-18 governs the structures, NR-17 governs the conditions of the worker who performs the assembly

CLT art. 59 (Overtime)

Legal limit of 2h overtime per day (total 10h); NR-17 grounds the need for staff planning that avoids habitual exceedance of this limit in the events sector


  11    CASE STUDIES — ERGONOMIC ILLNESS IN EVENTS

Module 11 — Real Cases: When Ergonomics Was Not Considered

Case 1 — Herniated Disc in a Load-in/Load-out Technician

Profile: PA equipment technician, 34 years old, 8 years of experience in the sector. He performed truck load-in and load-out operations on average 3 times a week, handling flight cases of 40 to 80 kg without auxiliary equipment and with an extended-reach posture (removing cases from the back of the truck with arms fully extended). After 6 years under these conditions, he presented an acute low back pain crisis with radiation to the left lower limb (sciatica) during a teardown. Diagnosis: L4-L5 disc herniation with nerve-root compression. Leave: 8 months. Surgery required.

Post-event ergonomic analysis: an AEP had never been carried out. The role did not appear in the PGR as exposed to ergonomic risk. No auxiliary material-handling equipment was available. The technicians used back belts as "protection" — an inadequate control measure as a substitute for mechanization.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

A mandatory AEP for every role with manual lifting of loads above the biomechanical limit. An electric pallet jack and an unloading ramp are engineering measures that eliminate the risk — the back belt does not eliminate it, it only masks it. Document it in the PGR and implement it before illness occurs.


Case 2 — Mental Exhaustion (Burnout) in an FOH Technician

Profile: FOH audio technician, 29 years old, 5 years operating large-scale shows. Average shifts of 14h per event; 3 to 4 events per week during the high season; frequent night work during teardown; no regular rest between events. After 2 years under these conditions, she presented severe insomnia, generalized anxiety, an inability to concentrate during operations, and finally a panic attack during a show. Diagnosis: Burnout Syndrome (ICD-11: QD85). Leave: 4 months.

Post-event ergonomic analysis: the FRPRT had never been assessed. The usual shift systematically violated the CLT overtime limit. There was no communication channel available for the worker to report overload. The company's management model normalized excessive shifts as "the culture of the sector".

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

FRPRT are mandatory in the PGR as of 05/26/2026 — but prevention cannot wait. Limiting shifts, ensuring rest and creating safe communication channels are measures that must be implemented immediately. Burnout is an occupational disease with a causal link — it generates employer liability.


Case 3 — Bilateral Tendinitis in a Lighting Technician

Profile: stage lighting technician, 41 years old, 15 years in the sector. His duties included assembling fixtures (connecting and disconnecting hundreds of XLR, DMX and electrical cables per event), focusing luminaires (wrist movements bearing the weight of the instrument) and console programming. On average 4 events per week. After 10 years, he developed bilateral tendinitis of the wrist flexors (bilateral medial epicondylitis). The pain progressively limited his working capacity to the point of being unable to perform normal professional activity. Partial permanent leave. Recognized as an occupational disease by the INSS after a medical review.

Post-event ergonomic analysis: the frequency analysis of repetitive wrist movements was never carried out. No job rotation between high- and low-wrist-demand tasks had been implemented. Tools that would reduce the twisting force needed for the connections (connector extractors, tools with an ergonomic handle) were never assessed or provided.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 3

Repetitive wrist and hand movements are a documented ergonomic risk in lighting technicians. The AEP must include an analysis of the frequency of connections/disconnections per shift. Ergonomic tools, job rotation and active upper-limb breaks are viable and low-cost preventive measures.

  CO    CONCLUSION — ERGONOMICS AS CULTURE, NOT AS A STANDARD

Closing: The Worker's Body as a Non-Negotiable Limit

NR-17 applied to the live events sector challenges the culture of a sector that prides itself on its resilience, its adaptability and its ability to "make it happen" against all odds. This culture has genuine value — the creativity and commitment of events professionals are unmatched. But it carries an invisible human cost that ergonomics makes visible: lumbar hernias in 35-year-old technicians, permanent tendinitis in lighting technicians, mental burnout in sound operators, and an industry that discards its most experienced professionals when their bodies can no longer keep up the pace.

The SMU PRO professional who completes this module understands that NR-17 is not a standard that "gets in the way of the show" — it is the instrument that ensures the professionals who put on the show can keep putting on shows for decades. Regulated breaks do not delay the setup when there is adequate staff. Material-handling equipment does not make the event more expensive when the cost of a leave due to RSI is calculated. Humane shifts do not reduce technical quality when the team is rested and cognitively capable.

Ergonomics in the events sector is neither a luxury nor bureaucracy: it is the recognition that the human body and mind have limits — and that respecting those limits is, at the same time, a legal obligation, an ethical responsibility and a condition for the sustainability of the business.



"The body does not forget what work imposes — but ergonomics can change what work demands."

⚠️  CONTEXT — NR-15 AND THE EVENTS SECTOR

The live events sector systematically concentrates multiple unhealthiness agents that are rarely recognized and managed: noise of 95 to 115 dB at FOH and stage monitor, extreme heat on covered and open-air stages during the Brazilian summer, structural vibration from subwoofers, cold in service chambers of refrigerated warehouses at certain venues, in addition to chemical agents from special effect fluids. NR-15 is the legal basis that sets the tolerance limit for each of these agents — and the LTCAT is the document that proves the exposure and grounds the unhealthiness allowance.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-15 — UNHEALTHINESS IN LABOR LAW

Module 1 — What Unhealthiness Is: Legal Basis and Structure of NR-15

Regulatory Standard No. 15 — Unhealthy Activities and Operations — regulates articles 189 to 196 of the Consolidation of Labor Laws (CLT) and establishes the criteria to identify and classify work activities considered unhealthy, defining the tolerance limits of agents harmful to health and determining the degrees of unhealthiness that generate the right to the allowance. Originally published by MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978, it went through several updates over almost five decades, with the most recent amendment by MTE Ordinance No. 2,021, of December 3, 2025.

The legal concept of an unhealthy activity is precise: they are activities that, by their nature, conditions or work methods, expose employees to agents harmful to health, above the tolerance limits set on the grounds of the nature and intensity of the agent and the time of exposure to its effects (CLT, art. 189). The tolerance limit is the central technical concept of NR-15: the maximum or minimum concentration or intensity, related to the nature and the time of exposure to the agent, that will not cause harm to the worker's health throughout their working life.

In the live events sector, unhealthiness is endemic and structural — but rarely documented, recognized or correctly compensated. FOH technicians who operate shows at 105 dB for 8 hours are above the legal tolerance limit from the second hour of work onward. Riggers who assemble stages under 40°C sun are exposed to thermal stress that can characterize unhealthiness. Generator operators live with vibration, noise and diesel fumes. NR-15 is the standard that turns this reality into a legal right.


Legal basis

NR-15 + CLT arts. 189–196 | MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Last amendment: MTE Ordinance No. 2,021/2025

Definition

Activities that expose the worker to harmful agents ABOVE the tolerance limits defined in the annexes

Tolerance Limit

Maximum concentration/intensity that does not cause harm to health throughout the worker's entire working life

Physical agents

Continuous/impact noise | Heat | Cold | Vibration | Ionizing and non-ionizing radiation | Hyperbaric pressure | Humidity

Chemical agents

Substances with tolerance limits (Annex 11) | Agents without a TL assessed by inspection (Annex 13) | Benzene (Annex 13-A)

Biological agents

Viruses, bacteria, fungi, parasites — exposure in specific activities (Annex 14)

Degrees and allowances

Minimum (10%) | Medium (20%) | Maximum (40%) — calculated on the national minimum wage (Binding Precedent No. 4/STF)

Non-cumulativity rule

When exposed to more than one unhealthiness agent: the HIGHEST DEGREE prevails — percentages are not added together

Elimination of the allowance

If the company adopts measures that neutralize or eliminate the unhealthiness (proven by report), the allowance ceases

Assessment instrument

LTCAT — Technical Report on Environmental Working Conditions | prepared by an engineer or occupational physician


  02    NOISE — ANNEX 1 AND 2 OF NR-15: THE MOST CRITICAL AGENT IN EVENTS

Module 2 — Noise: the Number 1 Unhealthiness Agent in the Events Sector

Noise is the most prevalent unhealthiness agent in the live events sector and, at the same time, the most chronically underestimated and under-negotiated. The routine operation of professional PA systems exposes technicians to sound levels that, in any other industrial sector, would immediately imply the characterization of unhealthiness, mandatory supply of hearing PPE and half-yearly audiometric monitoring. In the events sector, NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) is normalized as an "occupational deformity" — and NR-15 is the standard that shows this normalization is illegal.

2.1 Continuous and Intermittent Noise — Annex 1 of NR-15

Annex 1 of NR-15 defines the tolerance limits for continuous or intermittent noise (all noise that is not impact noise). The assessment is done in dB(A) — decibels weighted on the A curve, which reproduces the sensitivity of the human ear to the different frequencies. The measuring instrument must be an integrating dosimeter or sound level meter, positioned close to the level of the worker's ear.

🔴  NR-15 ANNEX 1 TABLE — TOLERANCE LIMITS FOR CONTINUOUS NOISE

The LTCAT (Technical Report on Environmental Working Conditions) must document noise measurements by role and by shift, using a sound level meter or dosimeter calibrated in accordance with Fundacentro's NHO-01. The measurements must be carried out during actual work activity — in the period of highest noise exposure (soundcheck or show), not under rehearsal conditions or setup without operation of the PA system.

2.3 Impact Noise — Annex 2 of NR-15

Annex 2 of NR-15 defines the tolerance limit for impact noise — that whose duration is less than one second, with intervals greater than one second. In the events sector, the most relevant impact noises are: firing of scenic pyrotechnics (sparkle, confetti cannons, scenic artillery fireworks), bass drum percussion in large PA systems and operation of percussion tools during the assembly of structures.

  • NR-15 Annex 2 limit for impact noise: 130 dB (linear) — no exposure permitted above this level

  • Professional pyrotechnics can generate peaks above 130 dB linear at short distance — use of hearing PPE with adequate NRR is mandatory for all technical personnel during pyrotechnics operation

  • Assessment with a sound level meter on an impact response circuit — or FAST circuit with C weighting (result accepted by NR-15 at 120 dB C as an alternative)


  03    HEAT — ANNEX 3 OF NR-15: WBGT AND THERMAL STRESS IN EVENTS

Module 3 — Heat: the Invisible Environmental Risk of Outdoor Events in Brazil

Brazil is a tropical country with open-air events held throughout the year, including in the hottest months (October to March, with temperatures above 35°C across much of the territory). Outdoor events with stages covered by low tents, setup warehouses without ventilation, understages with generators and amplification systems, and loading and unloading zones without shade are environments that systematically exceed the heat tolerance limits of NR-15 — without this being assessed, documented or compensated.

3.1 WBGT — Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature Index

NR-15 uses WBGT as the assessment parameter for occupational thermal stress. WBGT integrates three measurements: natural wet-bulb temperature (nwb) — considers relative air humidity; globe temperature (gt) — considers thermal radiation; and dry-bulb temperature (dbt) — air temperature. The calculation formulas are:

  • Indoor or outdoor environments WITHOUT solar load: WBGT = 0.7 × nwb + 0.3 × gt

  • Outdoor environments WITH direct solar load: WBGT = 0.7 × nwb + 0.1 × dbt + 0.2 × gt

The WBGT tolerance limit varies according to the metabolic rate of the activity — the heavier the physical work, the lower the tolerated WBGT limit. The loading and unloading activities for event equipment (roadies) have a metabolic rate in the "heavy" category (>290 W) — which implies more restrictive WBGT limits than sedentary work.

⚠️  ATTENTION — OUTDOOR HEAT AND NR-15

Annex 3 of NR-15 (after the 2021 update) does NOT characterize heat unhealthiness in open-air activities without an artificial heat source — unhealthiness from solar heat is assessed under NR-9 (a health risk to be managed) but does not generate an allowance under NR-15. However: when there is an ARTIFICIAL SOURCE of heat (generator, covered stage with dimmer racks and amplifiers, closed tent with a thermal load from electrical equipment), assessment under Annex 3 of NR-15 is fully applicable — and in the understage of a large show, temperatures frequently exceed the limits even for light work.


Heat assessment for LTCAT purposes must follow the methodology of Fundacentro's NHO-06, analyzing the 60 most critical minutes of exposure in the work shift. The report must be prepared by an occupational safety engineer or a qualified occupational physician, with an ART or the equivalent.


  04    VIBRATION — ANNEX 8: SUBWOOFERS, GENERATORS AND STAGE STRUCTURES

Module 4 — Vibration: the Risk the Body Feels but the Ear Does Not Process

Annex 8 of NR-15 — Vibration — establishes the criteria for characterizing unhealthiness from exposure to vibration in the work environment. In the live events sector, vibration is an agent frequently ignored for three reasons: it is invisible (you cannot see vibration, you only feel it), it is often confused with the normal perception of the "intensity of the show" by the accustomed professional, and it is rarely measured technically for LTCAT purposes.

Vibration in the live events context occurs in two main modes: whole-body vibration (WBV), which occurs when the entire body structure is subjected to vibration transmitted through the support surface — such as the FOH technician who stands for hours on the venue floor with 18" subwoofers pulsing at 40-60 Hz at 105 dB, or the generator operator who spends the shift beside the engine-generator set; and hand-arm vibration (HAV), which occurs when the vibrating tool is held with the hands, such as impact drivers, electric hammers and saws used in the assembly of stage structures and scenography.

4.1 Whole-Body Vibration (WBV) — in live events

  • Large-format subwoofers (21", 18") operated at high sound pressure levels transmit structural vibration to the venue floor and to adjacent surfaces

  • Technicians who remain in a static position for hours in locations of high structural vibration (near sub stacks, on stages with large infra systems) are candidates for WBV characterization

  • Generator operators: the diesel generator produces continuous vibration in the engine — workers who operate and monitor the generator for long periods are exposed to WBV

  • WBV assessment uses the parameter of weighted equivalent acceleration (aw) in m/s² for the axis of worst exposure — in accordance with ISO 2631 and Fundacentro's NHO-09

4.2 Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV) — in event assembly

  • Use of pneumatic or electric impact drivers in the assembly of stage structures, trusses and scenic equipment

  • Electric percussion tools for fixing scenography elements

  • Saws and rotary tools in carpentry and set construction

  • HAV assessment uses the daily equivalent acceleration (A(8)) in m/s² — in accordance with ISO 5349 and Fundacentro's NHO-10

💡  VIBRATION AND HEALTH — LONG-TERM CONSEQUENCES

Chronic exposure to WBV is associated with: low back pain, herniated disc, degenerative spine diseases, compression of abdominal organs, circulatory disorders. Chronic exposure to HAV is associated with Raynaud's Syndrome (whitening of the fingers from vascular spasm) and upper-limb WRMD. In the events sector, these disorders are common among long-career professionals but are rarely linked to the occupational nexus.


  05    ADDITIONAL PHYSICAL AGENTS — COLD, HUMIDITY, RADIATION

Module 5 — Cold, Humidity and Radiation: Specific Risks by Type of Event

Beyond noise, heat and vibration — the most prevalent physical agents in the live events sector —, NR-15 recognizes other physical agents that can generate unhealthiness in specific situations of the sector: cold, humidity and non-ionizing radiation. Each has a specific Annex and an applicability that varies according to the type of event and the worker's function.

5.1 Cold — Annex 9 of NR-15

Annex 9 characterizes unhealthiness from cold for work performed inside cold-storage chambers or in places with an artificial source of cold, with effective temperatures below 12°C. In the events sector, this Annex is applicable to:

  • Professionals who work in indoor arenas with high-power industrial refrigeration systems — some arenas keep an internal temperature of 8-14°C for hockey and skating events adapted for shows

  • Refrigerated service chambers at venues that store food for large-scale events — production workers who access these chambers regularly

  • Degree of unhealthiness: Medium (20%) — for continuous work at temperatures below 12°C

5.2 Humidity — Annex 10 of NR-15

Annex 10 characterizes unhealthiness from humidity for permanent work in flooded or soaked places. In the events sector, the applicability scenarios are:

  • Open-air events with persistent rain where workers remain operating PA and lighting systems on soaked flooring for prolonged periods

  • Work in understages of venues with drainage problems, where soil humidity keeps the environment soaked throughout the entire setup

  • Degree: Medium (20%) — requires an inspection report proving the condition of permanent, not occasional, humidity

5.3 Non-Ionizing Radiation — Annex 7 of NR-15

Annex 7 lists non-ionizing radiation as unhealthy when there is exposure to microwaves, ultraviolet and laser at intensities above the tolerance limits. In the events sector:

  • Ultraviolet radiation (UV): black light (UV-A) used in theater shows and special performances — professionals who work exposed directly to the UV beam for prolonged periods may characterize unhealthiness. NR-15 refers to the ACGIH TLVs for UV

  • Laser: shows with high-power laser (class 3B and 4) — laser operators who work near the equipment outputs are subject to ocular and cutaneous risk. Assessment by inspection of the site according to the powers and distances involved

  • Microwaves: high-power radiofrequency signal transmission systems (live video links, large-scale IEM transmitters) can generate microwave exposure at very close distances to the antennas

  • Annex 7 does not establish its own numerical limits — it refers to assessment by inspection of the site, with reference to the technical standards of ANATEL and ACGIH for each type of radiation


  06    CHEMICAL AGENTS — UNHEALTHINESS FROM PRODUCTS USED IN EVENTS

Module 6 — Chemical Agents: Fluids, Fuels and Vapors in Events

NR-15 addresses unhealthiness from chemical agents in three distinct Annexes: Annex 11 (substances with numerical tolerance limits — quantitative assessment), Annex 13 (substances whose unhealthiness is proven by inspection, without a defined numerical limit) and Annex 13-A (benzene — special regulation). In the events sector, the most relevant chemical agents are those generated by the operation of diesel generators, special effects and equipment maintenance products.

6.1 Relevant Chemical Agents in the Events Sector

Chemical Agent

⚠️  UNDERSTAGE AND GENERATOR — THE ENVIRONMENT OF GREATEST CHEMICAL RISK IN EVENTS

The understage of a large-scale show with a generator positioned externally but with its exhaust close to the understage entrance creates an environment of CO and hydrocarbon accumulation that can quickly exceed the tolerance limits of NR-15. Technicians who work in the understage during the setup and operation of the show are among the most exposed. Forced ventilation, a CO detector and continuous monitoring of the environment are measures required by NR-09 and ground the report of unhealthiness from chemical agents.


  07    LTCAT — TECHNICAL REPORT ON ENVIRONMENTAL WORKING CONDITIONS

Module 7 — LTCAT: the Document that Proves Unhealthiness

The Technical Report on Environmental Working Conditions (LTCAT) is the central technical-legal document of NR-15. It is what proves, in a technical and methodologically grounded way, the presence of unhealthiness agents in the work environment above the tolerance limits defined by the standard — generating or ruling out the right to the unhealthiness allowance. Without the LTCAT, the company can neither state that the environment is not unhealthy nor pay (or not pay) the allowance with legal certainty.

7.1 Who can prepare the LTCAT

  • Occupational Safety Engineer — qualified at the CREA with a specialization in occupational safety/hygiene

  • Occupational Physician — qualified at the CRM with a specialization in occupational medicine

  • The report must be signed and identified with an ART (Engineer) or an equivalent act (Physician)

  • An Occupational Hygienist certified by the ABHO may carry out the technical measurements, but the unhealthiness report must be signed by a qualified engineer or physician

7.2 Mandatory content of the LTCAT for events

  1. Identification of the company and the work sites assessed — name of the event, venue, date of the assessment

  2. Detailed description of the activities of the assessed roles — what the worker does, for how long, under what conditions

  3. Identification and description of all physical, chemical and biological agents present in the environment

  4. Assessment methodology used — NHO-01 (noise), NHO-06 (heat), ISO 2631 (WBV), the specific standard for each agent

  5. Quantitative results of the measurements — with the measurement uncertainty stated

  6. Comparison of the results with the tolerance limits of NR-15 and reference standards

  7. Technical conclusion: characterization or not of unhealthiness, by agent and by role, with the corresponding degree

  8. Existing and recommended control measures — PPE supplied, CPE installed, organizational measures

  9. Date of issue, validity (recommended: 1 year or when there is a change in working conditions) and signature of the technical officer


💡  LTCAT IN EVENTS — FREQUENCY AND VALIDITY

For the events sector, where working conditions vary according to the venue, the type of show and the scale of the production, the LTCAT must be reviewed periodically. The technical recommendation is annual review or whenever there is a significant change in the equipment used, in the work sites or in the organization of activities. A sound rental company that does shows of 50 to 500 people needs an LTCAT that covers the exposure spectrum of its technicians — not just the typical scenario, but also the extremes of exposure.


  08    UNHEALTHINESS ALLOWANCE — RIGHTS AND OBLIGATIONS

Module 8 — Unhealthiness Allowance: the Right that Exists but is Rarely Exercised

The unhealthiness allowance is the compulsory payment that the employer must make to the worker exposed to unhealthiness agents above the tolerance limits of NR-15. Established by the CLT (arts. 192-193) and regulated by NR-15, it is calculated on the national minimum wage — in accordance with Binding Precedent No. 4 of the STF, which prohibited the use of the contractual salary or the base salary as the basis of calculation.

In the live events sector, the unhealthiness allowance is systematically withheld — whether due to workers' lack of knowledge about their rights, due to the informality of labor relations (many technicians are hired as legal-entity/self-employed, without a CLT bond and without the corresponding labor rights), or due to the absence of technical reports at the sector's companies. Awareness of this right is part of the OSH training that SMU PRO sets out to offer.

8.1 Degrees of unhealthiness and corresponding allowances

Degree

Allowance

Basis of Calculation

Examples in Events

🔴  NR-15 RULE — CUMULATIVITY PROHIBITED

When the worker is exposed to more than one unhealthiness agent simultaneously (e.g., a stage monitor technician exposed to noise + heat + vibration), the allowance paid is ONLY that of the highest degree — percentages are not added together. A technician exposed to maximum-degree noise (40%) + medium-degree heat (20%) receives only 40%, not 60%. This rule is stated in the CLT (art. 192) and confirmed by consolidated labor case law.


8.2 Elimination and neutralization of unhealthiness — when the allowance ceases

NR-15 provides that the elimination or neutralization of unhealthiness may cease payment of the allowance. Elimination occurs when engineering or organizational measures reduce exposure below the tolerance limits. Neutralization occurs when the PPE supplied verifiably reduces exposure below the tolerance limit. In both cases, the technical report must prove the effectiveness of the measure.

  • Noise: a hearing protector that reduces effective exposure below 85 dB(A) — proven by dosimetry with and without the PPE — can neutralize the unhealthiness

  • Heat: installation of a ventilation/air-conditioning system that reduces the WBGT below the limit — proven by a new measurement — can eliminate the unhealthiness

  • Important: the mere supply of PPE is not enough — it is necessary to prove technically that it neutralizes exposure below the legal limit


  09    CONTROL AND PREVENTION MEASURES — NR-09 AND NR-15 HIERARCHY

Module 9 — Controlling Unhealthiness: Hierarchy of Measures

NR-15 is not only the standard of the unhealthiness allowance — it is, fundamentally, the standard that requires the control of harmful agents. The hierarchy of control measures for unhealthiness risks is established by NR-09 (which deals with the assessment and control of occupational exposures to physical, chemical and biological agents) and complemented by NR-01 (GRO/PGR). Whenever possible, eliminating or controlling the agent at the source takes priority over PPE.

9.1 Hierarchy of measures for noise unhealthiness in events

  1. ELIMINATE (engineering control at the source): replace higher-acoustic-power equipment with more efficient equipment — less actual power for the same level perceived by the audience; use line array systems with directional coverage that reduces spill in the technical areas

  2. REDUCE AT THE SOURCE (engineering control): install acoustic barriers between PA stacks and the FOH position; use distributed PA systems that operate at lower volumes; apply IEM monitoring solutions for musicians and stage technicians — eliminating floor monitors

  3. CONTROL THE EXPOSURE (administrative/organizational): rotation of technicians at the FOH position during long soundchecks; regulated hearing breaks in accordance with NR-17; a shift limit in high noise-exposure areas

  4. PROTECT WITH PPE (last barrier): a hearing protector with an NRR adequate to the exposure level, supplied free of charge by the employer, with training on use and periodic replacement; an IEM calibrated with a volume limit programmed by the OSH professional

9.2 Heat unhealthiness controls in events

  • Engineering: thermal insulation of tent and stage roofs; high-flow fans in understages; positioning of generators with exhaust directed away from work areas; mandatory shade in loading and unloading areas

  • Organizational: heavy work schedules (loading/unloading) during the coolest hours of the day; rotation of assembly teams with rest breaks in a cool place; frequent hydration available at all stations

  • PPE: reflective clothing for work under direct sun; high-performance moisture-wicking fabric; a cap or helmet with sun protection

9.3 Health monitoring — PCMSO integrated with the LTCAT

The Occupational Health Medical Control Program (PCMSO — NR-07) must be planned considering the unhealthiness agents documented in the LTCAT. For sound technicians exposed to noise above the limits of NR-15, the PCMSO must include:

  • Baseline pure-tone audiometry on admission — recording the baseline hearing condition before exposure to occupational noise

  • Periodic audiometry: half-yearly in the first two years of employment; yearly from the third year onward (as recommended by NHO-01)

  • Comparison of consecutive audiograms: early identification of NIHL progression — intervention before permanent loss

  • Tinnitus examination: workers with persistent ringing after a show must be assessed by an otolaryngologist with a specialization in NIHL


  10    INTEGRATION OF NR-15 WITH OTHER NRs AND CASE STUDIES

Module 10 — NR-15 in the OSH Ecosystem and Practical Cases

10.1 Integration of NR-15 with other NRs applicable to the events sector

NR-01 (GRO/PGR)

The unhealthiness agents documented in the LTCAT must appear in the PGR Risk Inventory. The control measures defined in the LTCAT ground the PGR Action Plan. The risk classification (probability × severity) must consider the measured exposure level.

NR-06 (PPE)

The PPE for controlling unhealthiness (hearing protectors, thermal clothing, chemical PPE) must be selected based on the agents and levels documented in the LTCAT. The Certificate of Approval (CA) must be verified. Training on use and periodic inspection are mandatory.

NR-07 (PCMSO)

The PCMSO must be prepared considering the unhealthiness agents of the LTCAT. Specific medical exams by agent: audiometry (noise), liver function test (solvents), pulmonary assessment (dusts and vapors). The PCMSO coordinating physician must have access to the LTCAT.

NR-09 (Environmental risks)

NR-09 defines the methodology for the quantitative and qualitative assessment of environmental risks — which is the technical basis of the LTCAT. The measurements of noise (NHO-01), heat (NHO-06), vibration (NHO-09/10) and chemical agents are carried out in accordance with NR-09.

NR-17 (Ergonomics)

Ergonomic risks (posture, lifting, work organization) and unhealthiness risks (noise, heat) coexist at the same workstations in events. Integrated assessment prevents the ergonomic intervention from worsening exposure to the unhealthiness agent and vice versa.

NR-26 (Signage)

Workstations with documented unhealthiness must have NR-26 signage adequate to the agent: "HEARING PROTECTOR MANDATORY" (blue) in noise areas above 85 dB; "CAUTION — EXCESSIVE HEAT" (yellow) in hot understages; GHS identification of unhealthy chemical products.


10.2 Case Study 1 — FOH technician without an LTCAT and with documented NIHL

Situation: FOH technician, 39 years old, 12 years of career in medium- and large-scale shows. Hired under the CLT by a medium-scale events production company 4 years ago. The company never prepared the LTCAT. A hearing protector was never offered. Occupational audiometry was never done. Diagnosed with moderate bilateral NIHL (4 kHz notch, 45 dB HL bilateral) after a spontaneous complaint to the occupational physician during a renewal ASO. Requests recognition of an occupational disease and retroactive payment of the unhealthiness allowance.

NR-15 Analysis: The absence of an LTCAT is a direct breach of NR-15. The absence of occupational audiometry is a breach of the PCMSO (NR-07). The causal nexus between the FOH activity (noise above 95 dB for 8-12h/day for 12 years) and the NIHL is technically established. The company is liable for the retroactive allowance, for the occupational disease and for all the labor and social-security consequences of the NIHL — costs that would be drastically lower with adequate prevention.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

The LTCAT and PCMSO are not bureaucratic paperwork — they are the company's legal protection and the protection of the worker's health. The absence of these documents turns unrecognized unhealthiness into a high-cost labor liability. An up-to-date LTCAT and periodic audiometry cost a fraction of the cost of a labor lawsuit for NIHL with a retroactive allowance.


10.3 Case Study 2 — Understage with generator and CO poisoning

Situation: 3-day festival with a 250 kVA generator positioned to the side of the understage, with its exhaust partially directed toward the understage access opening. PA technicians made adjustments in the understage during the shows — staying 20 to 40 minutes each. On the second day of the festival, a technician presented with an intense headache, nausea and dizziness after returning from the understage. Evacuated and assessed by the on-duty physician, with suspected CO poisoning. No CO detector had been installed.

NR-15 Analysis: Carbon monoxide has a TL of 39 ppm in NR-15 Annex 11. In understages with a nearby generator, the concentration can easily exceed this limit. The environment was a confined space (NR-33) with an unhealthy chemical agent (NR-15) — without measurement, without respiratory PPE and without a CO detector. The sequence of regulatory failures (NR-09, NR-15, NR-33, NR-26) turned a foreseeable risk into an accident with a victim.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

A fixed CO detector is a mandatory device in every understage or confined area where there is a generator with a nearby exhaust. Cost: R$200-500. Cost of a hospitalization for moderate to severe CO poisoning: R$15,000-80,000 — plus leave, labor liability and damage to the production's image.

☀️  WHY NR-21 IS CRITICAL FOR THE OUTDOOR EVENTS SECTOR

Festivals, open-air concerts, external corporate events and any outdoor production place dozens to hundreds of workers under intense sun for shifts of 10 to 20 hours. Brazil has one of the highest skin cancer rates in the world — 30% of all cancers recorded by INCA are skin cancers. Roadies working load-in under 38°C sun from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m., technicians climbing metal structures that absorb solar heat, stagehands working with no shade available: all are directly within the scope of NR-21. This standard is not bureaucracy — it is the difference between a workplace that cares and one that makes people sick.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-21 — OPEN-AIR WORK

Module 1 — What NR-21 Is and Why It Exists for Those Who Produce Open-Air Shows

Regulatory Standard No. 21 — Open-Air Work — establishes the minimum safety and health conditions for workers who carry out their activities in the open air, directly exposed to environmental agents such as solar radiation, heat, cold, humidity, rain and wind. Enacted by MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 and still in its original wording (one of the oldest and least-updated standards in the Brazilian OSH framework), it is surprisingly simple in its letter — but profoundly relevant in its application to the live events sector.

In June 2025, the Ministry of Labor and Employment opened a public consultation to update NR-21, recognizing that the 1978 standard does not reflect new climatic, scientific and operational realities. The proposal includes a technical definition of open-air work, a requirement for regular breaks for thermal recovery, the mandatory provision of shaded areas and hydration, and formal integration with NR-01 (ORM/RMP). Until the update is published, the current 6-item text is what applies — and this module expands the practical application of that text to the specific context of live events.

The six items of the current NR-21 are: 21.1 (mandatory shelters), 21.2 (measures against sunstroke, heat, cold, humidity and winds), 21.3 (lodgings with sanitary conditions), 21.4 (prophylaxis in swampy regions), 21.5 (sanitary conditions of workplaces) and 21.6 (housing provided by the employer). For the open-air events sector, items 21.1 and 21.2 are the ones with the most immediate and universal application.


Legal basis

NR-21 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Update under public consultation: MTE, June/2025

Definition

Open-air work: any labor activity carried out in the open air, without a roof or structure that controls the climatic environment

Scope of application

All sectors with outdoor activities — expressly including open-air events (festivals, concerts, outdoor corporate events)

Item 21.1

Mandatory shelters, even if rustic, to protect workers against inclement weather

Item 21.2

Special measures against excessive sunstroke, heat, cold, humidity and inconvenient winds

Item 21.3

Lodgings with adequate sanitary conditions when workers reside on site

Item 21.4

Prophylaxis of endemic diseases in swampy or flood-prone regions (relevant to festivals in rural areas)

Item 21.5

Workplaces kept in sanitary conditions compatible with the activity

Item 21.6

Housing provided by the employer with adequate sanitary conditions

2025 Update

Public consultation proposes: mandatory breaks for thermal recovery, shade and hydration, integration with RMP/NR-01, and a technical definition of open-air work


  02    SOLAR RADIATION — UV-A, UV-B AND THE RISKS TO WORKER HEALTH

Module 2 — Understanding the Invisible Enemy: the Physics and Biology of Solar Radiation

Solar radiation is the main risk agent for open-air workers — and also the most systematically underestimated. It does not hurt, make noise or have a smell. Its effects are cumulative and progressive, appearing decades after exposure. In the open-air events sector, where shifts of 12 to 20 hours under the tropical sun are routine, especially during setup, the worker accumulates occupational sun exposure equivalent to entire weeks of exposure for an ordinary person.

Brazil leads the global rankings for skin cancer incidence: according to the National Cancer Institute (INCA), non-melanoma skin cancer accounts for approximately 30% of all malignant tumors recorded in the country, with more than 186,000 new cases estimated for 2024. The vast majority of these cases have a direct causal link with accumulated sun exposure — and professionals who systematically work in the open air are the highest-risk group.

2.1 The two types of UV radiation and their effects

Type of Radiation

☀️  CRITICAL FACT — UV RADIATION ON CLOUDY DAYS

UV-A rays pass through clouds and glass — sun protection is necessary even on cloudy days. Many event workers do not apply sunscreen when the sky is overcast or when there is "no strong sun." This perception is wrong: UV-B burns may occur slightly reduced, but UV-A exposure is practically the same as on a sunny day. In terms of accumulated damage to skin DNA — the mechanism of carcinogenesis — exposure on a cloudy day is almost equivalent to that of a clear day.


2.2 UV Index — the yardstick of solar radiation in Brazil

The UV Index (UVI) measures the intensity of ultraviolet radiation at a given location, time and date. The National Institute of Meteorology (INMET) publishes the daily UVI by city. For tropical Brazil, especially in the Southeast, Northeast and Central-West regions during the summer (October to March), the UVI can reach "Extreme" values (> 11) at peak hours — representing the maximum risk of skin damage within a few minutes of unprotected exposure.

  03    HEATSTROKE AND ILLNESSES RELATED TO SOLAR HEAT

Module 3 — From Cramp to Heat Stroke: Recognizing and Acting in Time

Solar heat combined with intense physical work creates thermal-stress conditions that can rapidly progress from discomfort to a medical emergency. The open-air event worker is at especially high risk because multiple aggravating factors combine simultaneously: high ambient temperature, direct solar radiation, high relative humidity (especially on the coast and in humid tropical regions), work clothing that covers more of the skin (vests, work shirts), intense physical effort (lifting and transporting equipment) and long shifts without adequate breaks.

The progression of heat-related illnesses follows a predictable sequence: heat cramps → heat exhaustion → heatstroke (heat stroke). Each stage represents a deterioration in the body's ability to maintain thermal homeostasis. The event safety professional must be able to identify each stage and act before the progression occurs — because untreated heatstroke is a life-threatening medical emergency.

3.1 Progression of heat-related illnesses — identification and response

Stage

Signs and Symptoms

Body temperature

Immediate Action

Medical response

Heat cramps

Painful muscle contractions in the legs, arms or abdomen | Heavy sweating | Intense thirst

Normal (36–37°C)

Remove from the sun | Rest in the shade | Oral rehydration with water + electrolytes | Massage the affected muscles

Not urgent — if it persists for more than 1 hour, refer to the event's on-call physician

Heat exhaustion

Intense weakness | Paleness | Cold, clammy skin with heavy sweating | Nausea | Dizziness | Headache | Rapid heartbeat (> 100 bpm) | Possible fainting

Elevated (37–40°C)

Remove from the sun immediately | Lie down in a cool, ventilated place | Elevate the lower limbs | Oral rehydration if conscious | Damp cloth on the face, nape and armpits | MONITOR continuously

Refer to the on-call physician — if there is no improvement within 15 minutes or if consciousness is lost: SAMU 192 immediately

Heatstroke — Heat Stroke (EMERGENCY)

Hot, dry and reddened skin (no sweat — a sign of cooling-system failure) | Mental confusion or unconsciousness | Very high body temperature | Seizures | Very rapid and irregular heartbeat | Fast, shallow breathing

Above 40°C (critical at 41°C+)

IMMEDIATE ACTION: call SAMU 192 | Cool the worker URGENTLY: cold cloths on the neck, armpits and groin | Ventilate with a fan | DO NOT give fluids if unconscious | DO NOT leave alone

EMERGENCY — SAMU 192 immediately. Heatstroke without immediate treatment can cause permanent brain damage and death within minutes to hours


🔴  HEAT STROKE — MEDICAL EMERGENCY: DRY SKIN IS THE CRITICAL SIGN

The difference between heat exhaustion and heat stroke is the presence or absence of sweating. In exhaustion, the worker is still sweating (the cooling system works). In heat stroke, the skin becomes DRY AND HOT — the body has stopped sweating because the thermoregulation system has collapsed. This is a medical emergency that can cause death within hours. At events, the on-call physician or SAMU (192) must be called immediately once it is identified.


3.2 Groups of greatest vulnerability at live events

  • Non-acclimatized workers — those who came from colder regions or who return to outdoor work after a period of leave (the body takes 7 to 14 days to acclimatize to heat)

  • Workers over 45 — thermoregulation becomes progressively less efficient with age

  • Workers with pre-existing health conditions — hypertension, diabetes, obesity, heart problems — have reduced heat tolerance

  • Workers who take medications affecting sweating or vasodilation — some antihypertensives, antihistamines and antidepressants reduce thermoregulation capacity

  • Dehydrated workers — dehydration equivalent to 2% of body weight already significantly reduces sweating capacity and heat tolerance

  • Workers on their first outdoor shift — the first day of work in the sun after a period in an air-conditioned environment carries the highest risk of thermal exhaustion


  04    ITEM 21.1 — MANDATORY SHELTERS: THE STANDARD AND THE PRACTICE

Module 4 — Shelters: the Right to Have Shade at Work

Item 21.1 of NR-21 states: "In work carried out in the open air, the existence of shelters, even if rustic, capable of protecting workers against inclement weather, is mandatory." This is one of the simplest and most frequently breached requirements at open-air events. The phrase "even if rustic" reveals the legislative intent: a sophisticated structure is not required — what is required is that shade and rain protection be available to all workers.

At a large festival with 500 workers on the ground, NR-21 requires that there be enough shelter for all of them — not just for the production team, the artists and the audience. The roadies carrying cables, the stagehands, the lighting technicians: all are entitled to the shelter provided for in item 21.1. The lack of adequate shelter for field technical workers at outdoor events is one of the most prevalent and least-cited non-conformities in the sector.

💡  SHELTER POSITIONING — MANDATORY PRACTICE

The shelter must be CLOSE to the workplace — not 500 meters away in a service area. NR-21 does not specify the maximum distance, but the principle of effective protection dictates that the worker must be able to reach the shelter in a useful time in the event of sudden rain or the need for a heat break. The practical recommendation for events is: a shelter no more than 200 m from any open-air workstation. At large events, distribute multiple shelter points along the perimeter of the technical area.


  05    ITEM 21.2 — SPECIAL MEASURES AGAINST SUNSTROKE AND INCLEMENT WEATHER

Module 5 — Active Protection: Solar PPE, Hydration and Work Organization

Item 21.2 of NR-21 states: "Special measures shall be required to protect workers against excessive sunstroke, heat, cold, humidity and inconvenient winds." This item is the most operationally rich in the standard — because "special measures" is a broad concept covering PPE, CPE, work organization, breaks, hydration and weather protocols. The employer's responsibility is to adopt the set of measures necessary to ensure that the worker does not suffer health harm from the weather conditions — and this responsibility is proportional to the intensity of the climatic agent.

5.1 Sunscreen — the most forgotten PPE at events

Sunscreen is not formally classified as PPE by NR-06 (it does not have an MTE Approval Certificate), but it is widely recognized by the OSH system as a mandatory personal protective device for open-air workers. NR-21 (item 21.2) grounds its obligatory nature as a "special measure" of protection against excessive sunstroke. The employer's responsibility is to provide sunscreen free of charge and to ensure the conditions for its application and reapplication during the shift.

  • Minimum Sun Protection Factor (SPF): SPF 30 for basic protection | SPF 50 to 70 for intense exposure and fair skin | SPF 70+ for Very High or Extreme UVI

  • Type of sunscreen: broad spectrum (protection against UV-A and UV-B) | preferably water/sweat resistant — important for workers who sweat heavily

  • Application: at least 20 minutes before sun exposure begins — not immediately upon going out | full coverage of all exposed skin (face, neck, nape, ears, hands, arms)

  • Reapplication: every 2 hours of continuous exposure, or immediately after heavy sweating, contact with water or cleaning the face — sweat dilutes and removes the sunscreen

  • Provision by the employer: the event producer must make sunscreen available in the work areas — not merely rely on the worker bringing their own

5.2 Solar protection PPE — complete set for outdoor events

Reduces the risk of sunburn and skin cancer | Protects the DNA of epidermal cells

Whenever UVI ≥ 3 (moderate) | Provided by the employer per NR-21

Mandatory per item 21.1 of NR-21 — complements the individual PPE


  06    HYDRATION — THE MAIN ALLY AGAINST HEAT

Module 6 — Water: the Simplest and Most Neglected Protection Measure

Dehydration is the central biological vector in the progression from thermal stress to heat exhaustion and heat stroke. When the body loses more water through sweating than it replenishes through intake, blood viscosity increases, plasma volume drops, the heart has to work harder to maintain pressure, and sweating capacity — the human body's primary cooling mechanism — begins to decline. In intense physical work under 38°C sun, a worker can lose 1 to 2 liters of sweat per hour.

NR-21 and the update proposal under public consultation in 2025 reinforce the employer's obligation to ensure easy access to drinking water in sufficient quantity. For open-air events, this means not only making water available at the central catering (usually 300 meters from the workstation) — but positioning water fountains or water bottles at points near each open-air work area.

6.1 Hydration protocol for outdoor work — evidence-based guidance

  1. Pre-shift: drink 500 ml of water before starting work in the sun — especially important on the hottest days

  2. During work: drink 200 to 300 ml of water every 20 minutes of active physical work in the sun — equivalent to approximately 600 ml to 900 ml per hour of intense work

  3. Hydration monitoring: monitor the color of the urine — pale yellow = hydrated; dark yellow or amber = dehydrated; no urination for more than 4 hours = a sign of severe dehydration

  4. Electrolytes: on shifts of more than 2 hours with heavy sweating, replace electrolytes with coconut water, sports drinks or oral rehydration solutions — water alone can cause hyponatremia (dilution of sodium) in extreme cases

  5. Restrictions: avoid alcoholic beverages during the shift (they increase dehydration) | limit caffeine (a diuretic) | avoid very cold drinks (they can cause gastric cramps)


⚠️  EMPLOYER OBLIGATION — WATER NEAR THE WORKSTATION

The provision of drinking water "in hygienic conditions and in sufficient quantity" is a requirement of NR-21. At events, the artists' catering and the production's dining area do not meet this requirement for field technical workers, because they are far from the workstations. Water bottles or mobile drinking fountains must be positioned in the stage-setup, load-in/load-out, rigging and open-air equipment-operation areas. Cost: minimal. Impact: critical.


  07    WEATHER PROTOCOL — WHEN TO SUSPEND OUTDOOR WORK

Module 7 — Weather Protocol: Rules for Stopping before the Accident Happens

A formal weather protocol is a technical document that defines the meteorological conditions that trigger the suspension or modification of open-air work activities at events. It is the practical application of item 21.2 of NR-21 combined with the risk-management requirements of the RMP (NR-01). In the events sector, the absence of a weather protocol is the rule — and events that take place during electrical storms, winds above safety limits or extreme heat waves are the result of this absence.

The weather protocol must be defined before the event, communicated to all workers at the pre-setup briefing, and have a designated person responsible for the decision to stop work. Production pressure — "the show must go on" — is the main antagonist of an effective weather protocol. That is why it is essential that the protocol be documented, signed, and that the person responsible for the stop-work decision have authority independent of the event's artistic direction.

7.1 Weather-protocol parameters for outdoor events

  08    SANITARY CONDITIONS — ITEMS 21.3 TO 21.6 AT EVENTS

Module 8 — Sanitary Conditions: Toilets, Water and Lodging at Multi-Day Events

Items 21.3 to 21.6 of NR-21 address the sanitary conditions of lodgings and workplaces — most directly applicable to construction works, sites and long-duration activities in remote locations. At live events, these items gain relevance especially at multi-day festivals (such as Lollapalooza, Rock in Rio, Tomorrowland Brasil) where part or all of the technical crew stays overnight at the event site.

8.1 Sanitary facilities for workers at events — NR-21 + NR-24

NR-21 (item 21.5) requires that workplaces be kept in sanitary conditions compatible with the activity. Complemented by NR-24 (Sanitary and Comfort Conditions in Workplaces), it establishes the minimum standard for toilets, washbasins and the supply of drinking water. At open-air events, the practical obligations are:

  • Chemical toilets or fixed sanitary facilities: available in adequate numbers for the workers — not just for the audience. The minimum ratio is 1 toilet for every 40 workers of the same sex (NR-24)

  • Toilets separated by sex: when there are more than 10 female workers at the event, separate female sanitary facilities are mandatory

  • Washbasin with running water and soap: near any location where workers handle chemicals or food — and before the crew's dining area

  • Maximum distance to the toilet: NR-24 establishes that toilets must be no more than 150 meters from the workstation — at large events, distribute toilets along the perimeter of the technical area

  • Drinking water: available at easily accessible points throughout the open-air work area — drinking fountains, water bottles or filters

8.2 Lodgings at multi-day festivals — items 21.3 and 21.6

When the producer provides housing to workers during multi-day festivals (permanent dressing room, habitable container, crew camping tent), the lodgings must meet minimum sanitary-condition standards under items 21.3 and 21.6 of NR-21:

  • Adequate ventilation — at summer festivals, unventilated lodgings can reach temperatures of 45–50°C inside tents and containers without cooling

  • Direct lighting — natural or artificial — for safety and comfort during nighttime use

  • Waterproofing — protection against rain and ground moisture, especially at festivals with grass or dirt ground

  • Minimum distance from garbage deposits, waste facilities and cesspits — prevention of contamination and vector proliferation

  • Sealing against pests (mosquitoes, cockroaches, rodents) — essential at events in rural and swampy areas

  • Prohibition of collective housing with the worker's family (item 21.6.1) — only the worker in the lodging provided by the employer


💡  WORKER TOILETS ≠ AUDIENCE TOILETS

A common non-conformity at events: organizers provide abundant toilets for the audience but forget to ensure adequate toilets for the field technical workers — often far from the work areas or shared with the audience without segregation. Event workers have the same sanitary rights as any Brazilian worker, regulated by NR-24, regardless of the "special" context of the event.


  09    RAIN, COLD AND WIND — PROTECTION IN ADVERSE CONDITIONS

Module 9 — Beyond the Sun: Protection against Rain, Cold and Wind at Events

Item 21.2 of NR-21 is not limited to protection against heat and sunstroke — it covers protection against "cold, humidity and inconvenient winds." In Brazil's open-air events sector, which takes place across all regions and seasons of the year, the safety professional must be prepared for conditions ranging from summer festivals in the tropical zone to winter events in the south of the country, where sub-zero temperatures are possible.

9.1 Protection against rain at outdoor events

  • Waterproof rain cape: mandatory for workers who remain in open-air work during rain — not just available, but provided before the shift when rain is forecast

  • Waterproof boots or water-protective footwear: essential at events with grass or dirt terrain that turns muddy during rain — a risk of falls and of contact with water contaminated by waste

  • Protection of electrical equipment: a priority even before the protection of the workers — electrical installations without adequate IP protection + rain = electrical emergency. Electrical protection in the rain is an obligation of NR-10, which must be integrated into the NR-21 rain protocol

  • Suspension of work at height: rain with wind creates slippery surfaces on trusses and grids — work at height must be suspended per the NR-35 protocol

9.2 Protection against cold at winter events and in cold regions

  • Layered thermal clothing: base layer (thermal underwear that absorbs sweat), intermediate layer (insulation) and outer layer (wind- and rain-proof)

  • Cold-resistant gloves: especially for rigging crews and work at height, where loss of sensation in the hands is a safety risk in addition to a health risk

  • Head and neck protection: beanie and balaclava — a large part of body-heat loss occurs through the head

  • Heating of the rest area: a heated shelter (electric or gas heater with ventilation) for breaks — exposure to cold without the possibility of warming up is a non-conformity with item 21.2

  • Hot drinks: provision of tea or coffee on site — NR-21 does not specify this, but it is a caring practice compatible with the protection required against cold

  • Crew rotation: shorter periods of cold exposure with more frequent rotation — reduces the risk of hypothermia and of decreased performance and attention due to cold

9.3 Protection against wind — comfort and safety simultaneously

Wind affects event workers in two dimensions: comfort and safety. From the standpoint of comfort and health (the scope of NR-21), cold wind significantly increases the wind-chill sensation and the risk of hypothermia, and hot, dry wind accelerates dehydration. From the standpoint of operational safety (the scope of NR-35), wind above the established limits makes work at height unsafe. In both cases, the solution begins with measurement — an anemometer is basic equipment for any sizable outdoor production.


  10    INTEGRATION OF NR-21 WITH OTHER NRs AND CASE STUDIES

Module 10 — NR-21 in the OSH Ecosystem and Lessons from Real Cases

10.1 Integration of NR-21 with other standards applicable to outdoor events

NR-01 (ORM/RMP)

The climatic risks identified by NR-21 (solar radiation, heat, rain, wind) must appear in the RMP Risk Inventory. The weather protocol must be the Emergency Action Plan for climatic risks. The NR-21 update proposal under public consultation in 2025 proposes formal integration with the RMP.

NR-06 (PPE)

Sunscreen, UV glasses, protective hat, rain cape, thermal clothing — all are PPE or personal protective devices that the employer must provide free of charge according to the risks identified by NR-21.

NR-09 (Environmental Risks)

NR-09 defines the methodology for assessing environmental risks — including heat (NHO-06) and non-ionizing radiation (UV) — which underpins the "special measures" required by item 21.2 of NR-21.

NR-15 (Unhealthy Work)

Heat from an artificial source can give rise to a hazard-pay premium (Annex 3 of NR-15). NR-21 regulates working conditions — NR-15 regulates the financial compensation for unhealthy exposure. The two standards complement each other without excluding one another.

NR-17 (Ergonomics)

The ergonomics of thermal comfort (NR-17, item 8) and the climatic protection of NR-21 converge on the prevention of thermal stress. The breaks for physical and thermal recovery provided for in the 2025 NR-21 update are also ergonomic measures of NR-17.

NR-23 (Fire)

At outdoor events with a risk of atmospheric discharge (lightning), the NR-21 weather protocol works together with the NR-23 emergency plan — the storm evacuation routes must be the same as, or complementary to, the fire evacuation routes.

NR-24 (Sanitary Conditions)

NR-24 details the minimum standards for sanitary facilities that complement the requirement of item 21.5 of NR-21 — number of toilets per worker, maximum distance, separation by sex, washbasins.

NR-35 (Work at Height)

Suspension of work at height due to adverse weather conditions (wind > 46 km/h, storm, fog, heavy rain) is an explicit requirement of NR-35 that works together with the weather protocol provided for by NR-21.


10.2 Case Study 1 — Heat Stroke in a Roadie during a Festival

Scenario: Multi-day festival in a coastal region in February. Temperature of 38°C, relative humidity of 82%, UVI 12 (extreme). A 28-year-old roadie, physically active, transporting subwoofer boxes from the truck to the stage — a distance of 400 meters, several trips. No sunscreen applied. No hat. No regular breaks. No easy access to water (drinking fountain 200 meters from the route). After 4 hours of uninterrupted work, the worker shows mental confusion, hot and dry skin, and a body temperature measured at 40.8°C. Severe heatstroke — heat stroke. Evacuated by ambulance to the emergency care unit. Two-day hospitalization for observation.

NR-21 analysis: multiple non-conformities. Absence of shelter near the work route (item 21.1). Absence of special measures against sunstroke (item 21.2) — no sunscreen, no hat, no thermal breaks. Absence of accessible drinking water along the route. Absence of a weather protocol that would have suspended or restructured the heavy work at the peak of the heat.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

Heavy physical work (loading and unloading equipment) is the activity with the HIGHEST risk of heat stroke at outdoor events. The solutions are straightforward: schedule loading and unloading for cooler hours (before 10 a.m. or after 5 p.m.), provide an electric pallet truck to reduce physical effort, and ensure water and shade near the route. None of these measures has a prohibitive cost — and all are required by NR-21.


10.3 Case Study 2 — Storm without a Protocol and Workers on a Structure

Scenario: Open-air concert in a temporary arena. The weather forecast indicated the possibility of a storm from 6 p.m. There was no formal weather protocol. At 5:40 p.m., a storm front advanced rapidly with winds of 70 km/h and heavy rain. Two lighting technicians were still on the grid at a height of 8 meters making last-minute adjustments before the show. The storm arrived faster than forecast — the technicians had to climb down hastily in the rain with strong wind and slippery trusses. One of them slipped and was held by his lanyard, suffering a shoulder contusion when he struck the structure. The show was canceled by force majeure.

NR-21 + NR-35 analysis: The absence of a formal weather protocol (an obligation of item 21.2 of NR-21 and an impeding condition of NR-35) was the root cause. With a protocol, the technicians would have descended from the structures 30 minutes before the storm arrived, at the first alert from the weather radar. The show would have been delayed or canceled safely — instead of nearly having a fall-from-height victim due to exposure to extreme wind.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

A documented weather protocol + an anemometer + access to real-time weather radar are low-cost investments that prevent extremely high-cost emergencies. Designating a person responsible for the stop-work decision with authority independent of the artistic direction is the most critical organizational step — because the pressure of the show is the greatest antagonist of safety at outdoor events.

⚠️  NR-24 AND EVENTS — THE STANDARD THAT CARES FOR WHAT NO ONE SEES

NR-24 is the standard that protects workers' most basic human needs: having a clean and sufficient restroom, being able to eat in dignified conditions, having somewhere to store their belongings, and drinking potable water. In the live events sector, these conditions are systematically precarious — especially for field technical crews who spend 12 to 20 hours at the event site. The law sets the minimum standards. This module teaches how to apply them in the sector's practice.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-24 — THE RIGHT TO DIGNITY IN THE WORKPLACE

Module 1 — What NR-24 Is and How It Applies to Events

Regulatory Standard No. 24 — Sanitary and Comfort Conditions in the Workplace — establishes the minimum requirements that every employer must provide to ensure that workers have dignified conditions of hygiene, food and comfort while performing their duties. Updated by MTP Ordinance No. 2,772, of September 5, 2022, it is organized into seven technical requirements: sanitary facilities, sanitary components, changing rooms, meal areas, kitchens, lodging and work clothing.

NR-24 applies to all workplaces, without restriction as to line of business or company size. For the live events sector, the most relevant point of application is in Annex I of the standard, which deals specifically with the sanitary and comfort conditions applicable to workers in external and temporary work — a definition that covers exactly the technicians, roadies, riggers and support crews who work in loaned, rented or public spaces for the staging of events.

The core objective of NR-24 is not to impose luxury or superfluous comfort — it is to prevent inadequate conditions of toilets, food and rest from causing occupational diseases, accidents and deterioration of the worker's health. An insufficient or unsanitary restroom at a 3-day event with 500 workers is not merely an inconvenience: it is a real vector for the transmission of infectious diseases and an environment of biological risk (one of the risk groups in NR-15, Annex 14).


Legal basis

NR-24 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Update: MTP Ordinance No. 2,772/2022

Scope of application

Every workplace with CLT employees — no restriction as to line of business or size | Applicable to events via Annex I (External and temporary work)

Seven requirements

(1) Sanitary facilities | (2) Sanitary components | (3) Changing rooms | (4) Meal areas | (5) Kitchens | (6) Lodging | (7) Work clothing

Annex I — External work

Applies to work performed at the client's establishment or on public grounds — directly relevant to events

Responsibility

Employer always responsible — when the event takes place at the client's establishment, the client must guarantee the conditions; when it takes place on public grounds, the employer must provide them

Relationship with NR-21

NR-21 requires shelters and protection against the weather | NR-24 sets the hygiene and comfort standards for those shelters

Relationship with NR-18

For assembly work that constitutes a construction site, NR-18 takes precedence and specificity over NR-24

Penalties

Violation characterized by an MTE labor inspector | Administrative fines | Shutdown of activities in serious cases of health risk


  02    SANITARY FACILITIES — THE 20-WORKER RULE

Module 2 — How Many Restrooms? The Ratio the Law Requires

Sanitary facilities are the most immediately practical and most frequently violated requirement of NR-24 in the context of live events. The standard is clear and numerical: the minimum ratio, the construction conditions and the hygiene requirements are precisely defined. The challenge for the events sector is that the sanitary facilities of the venue or event site are usually sized for the audience — not for the workers. The responsibility to ensure adequate toilets for the workers lies with the employer, not the venue.

2.1 Minimum ratio of sanitary facilities — NR-24

📏  FUNDAMENTAL RULE OF NR-24 — RATIO OF TOILETS

1 toilet bowl + 1 washbasin for each group of 20 workers or fraction thereof, SEPARATED BY SEX. In activities with exposure to or handling of infectious material, toxic or irritant substances, or that cause soiling: 1 sanitary set (bowl + washbasin + shower) for each group of 10 workers. Men's facilities must have a urinal: 1 for each 20 workers up to 100; 1 for each 50 beyond that. The use of communal cups and towels is prohibited.


In practice, for an event with 100 field workers (50 men and 50 women), NR-24 requires at a minimum: Women's area: 3 toilet bowls + 3 washbasins. Men's area: 3 toilet bowls + 3 urinals + 3 washbasins. Total: 12 sanitary units — more than most small and mid-sized events provide for their technical crew.
