💡  CHEMICAL TOILETS — ARE PERMITTED BY NR-24

Annex I of NR-24 (external and temporary work) expressly permits the use of chemical toilets equipped with a flushing mechanism or waste-isolation system, with venting and ventilation, and material for washing and drying hands. This is the practical solution for events on public grounds and sites without permanent sanitary infrastructure. Additional requirement: mandatory DAILY SANITIZATION of the units, and prohibition of communal towels (individual paper towels or hand dryers).


2.2 Technical requirements for sanitary facilities

  • Floor and walls of impermeable and washable material — to facilitate periodic cleaning and disinfection

  • Adequate ventilation — natural or mechanical — to prevent the buildup of gases and odors

  • Sufficient lighting — minimum 100 lux in permanent restrooms

  • Piped water and sewage connected to the general network or to a system that poses no health risk

  • When located outside the main body of the establishment: connected to work areas by a covered passageway

  • Maintenance in a condition of upkeep, cleanliness and hygiene — mandatory and documented

  • Toilet paper available at all bowls — communal toilet paper prohibited (individual only)

  • Soap or liquid soap and a means of drying hands — individual paper towels, never a communal towel


  03    SANITARY COMPONENTS — THE QUALITY THE LAW REQUIRES

Module 3 — Sanitary Components: Materials, Showers and Privacy

Beyond the numerical ratio, NR-24 defines the quality and privacy requirements of sanitary components — the bowls, washbasins, showers and their enclosures. For the events sector, where chemical toilets are the most frequent solution, these requirements must be verified in the specification and contracting of the sanitary units.

3.1 Toilet bowl

  • Resistant and easy-to-sanitize material — vitrified porcelain, high-density polyethylene or similar

  • Seat (lid) — mandatory | impermeable and washable material | no sharp edges

  • Efficient trap and flush — flush of at least 6 liters | or waste-sealing system in chemical toilets

  • Individual stall with a door that ensures privacy and emergency opening — minimum stall height: 2.10m | minimum depth: 80cm

3.2 Showers — when they are mandatory

Showers are mandatory in sanitary facilities when the nature of the work requires cleaning of the body at the end of the shift — especially in activities with exposure to infectious material, toxic substances, intense dust and soiling that cannot be removed by handwashing alone. The ratio is 1 shower for each 10 workers in these activities, or 1 shower for each 10 workers lodged in accommodations.

For the events sector, showers are especially relevant in: multi-day festivals where workers are lodged on site (mandatory in the lodging); venue cleaning and sanitization activities; generator operators exposed to fuel vapors; and workers in contact with concentrated cleaning products.

  • Hot and cold water — mandatory in restrooms with showers

  • Metal tap at mid-height on the wall — to avoid accidental contact with workers

  • Stall with partition or individual cabin — privacy guaranteed

  • Non-slip floor in the stall — prevention of falls on a wet surface

3.3 Maximum distance from toilets to the workstation

NR-24 and NR-21 together determine that sanitary facilities must be within a distance that allows access within a reasonable time during the shift. The practical technical reference is a maximum of 150 meters from the workstation — a distance that allows the worker to step away briefly without significant impact on productivity and without excessive exposure to adverse conditions along the way.

At large events with an extensive technical area (a 300m line check, loading and unloading areas far from the covered area), distributing chemical toilets at multiple points along the technical perimeter is the appropriate solution — not a single cluster of toilets at the center of services.

  04    CHANGING ROOMS — WHEN THEY ARE MANDATORY AND HOW TO SIZE THEM

Module 4 — Changing Rooms: the Transition Space between the Worker and the Professional

The changing room is the space where the worker transitions between their personal clothing and their work clothing — and vice versa at the end of the shift. NR-24 requires a changing room whenever: the activity requires the use of a uniform or specific work clothing; the activity is carried out in an industrial establishment; or the company imposes the use of a uniform whose change must take place at the workplace.

In the live events sector, changing rooms are especially relevant in: multi-day festivals with crews lodged on site (the changing room is part of the lodging complex); large productions where the technical crew wears identification vests and work uniforms provided by the employer that must be returned at the end; and productions with catering teams that handle food (mandatory food-handler-specific clothing).

4.1 Technical requirements for the changing room

  • Separation by sex — mandatory | a mixed changing room is not accepted by NR-24

  • Minimum area: 1.50 m² per worker — calculated based on the shift with the largest headcount

  • Individual locker per worker — with lock and key | double locker (two compartments) when there is unhealthy activity (one for personal clothing, one for work clothing)

  • Walls of masonry or concrete with impermeable and washable coating

  • Impermeable, washable floors with a smooth finish, sloped toward trapped drains

  • Minimum lighting: 100 lux (a 100W lamp per 8m² with a 3m ceiling height)

  • Ventilation through windows with a minimum area of 1/8 of the floor area

  • Impermeable and washable seats to make changing clothes easier

  • Prohibition of using the changing room for any other purpose — including on a provisional basis (storage, office, etc.)

4.2 Double locker — unhealthy activities and exposure to harmful agents

When the worker performs activities with exposure to dust, greasy or oily products, toxic, irritant or infectious material — which at events includes generator operators (diesel), special-effects technicians (chemical fluids) and cleaning crews — the locker must be of the double type (two separate compartments): one for personal (clean) clothing and another for work clothing (possibly contaminated). This segregation prevents cross-contamination between work clothing and the worker's personal clothes.

  05    MEAL AREAS — CANTEEN AND ALTERNATIVES

Module 5 — Eating with Dignity: Canteen, Food and Their Requirements

The meal area is the space where the worker eats during the breaks of the shift. NR-24 requires this space to be adequate, clean and comfortable — because eating in inadequate conditions is not merely a comfort problem: it is a health matter. Eating in a work area with exposure to chemical agents, dust or intense noise is a route of contamination and a compromise of the rest that the meal break is meant to provide.

5.1 When the canteen is mandatory and what the standards are

According to NR-24 (item 24.5), employers must provide places in conditions of comfort and hygiene for taking meals during the breaks granted throughout the shift. The rule is divided according to the number of workers served:

Number of workers

NR-24 requirement

Requirements for the area

Typical situation at events

Up to 30 workers

Meal area — not necessarily a formal canteen | may be adapted

Protected against the weather | Hygienic conditions | Sufficient tables and seats | Drinking water | A place to heat food

Covered catering area with tables — suitable for small teams at corporate events

31 to 300 workers

Formal canteen — outside the work area, dedicated to this purpose

Floor coated with washable material | Adequate ventilation and lighting | Tables and seats sufficient for 1/3 of the shift simultaneously | Area of 1m² per user | Minimum circulation of 0.75m | Equipment to preserve and heat food | Drinking water

Catering structure of a mid-sized event — requires specific area and infrastructure planning

Over 300 workers

Mandatory canteen — with complete structure | eating in another location of the establishment is not allowed for workers of companies over 300

All the previous requirements + must not connect to sanitary facilities or unhealthy areas | Dedicated changing room and toilets for the kitchen team

Large festivals with teams of 400+ workers — need for a complete and segregated food structure


⚠️  EXTERNAL WORK — SPECIAL RULE OF ANNEX I OF NR-24

For external, mobile or temporary work on public grounds — which is exactly the situation of most live events — the employer must ensure: (a) sanitary facilities at a ratio of 1 set per 20 workers (which may be a chemical toilet); AND (b) a meal area protected against the weather and in hygienic conditions for all workers, OR provision of a means of funding meals at nearby commercial establishments. The funding option (meal voucher or equivalent subsidy) is the most practical for most events.


5.2 Express prohibitions related to meals — NR-24

  • Prohibited to take meals in the work area — especially in places with exposure to chemical agents, dust, intense noise or heat

  • Prohibited for the canteen to connect directly to sanitary facilities or to unhealthy areas

  • Prohibited use of communal cups — provision of individual disposable or personal cups is mandatory

  • Prohibited to split the meal break into more than two groups — to ensure everyone has the minimum time for rest and food (minimum 1h under the CLT for shifts over 6h)


  06    DRINKING WATER — A UNIVERSAL AND IMMEDIATE OBLIGATION

Module 6 — Drinking Water: A Basic Right That Admits No Exception

NR-24 establishes that potable water must be provided to workers in all workplaces, with the use of communal cups being prohibited. Water supply must be provided by means of drinking fountains at a ratio of at least 1 fountain for each group of 50 workers or fraction thereof, or another system that offers the same hygienic conditions.

For the live events sector, this requirement links directly to NR-21 (open-air work, which requires continuous hydration as a measure of protection against heat) and to NR-17 (ergonomics, which requires adequate environmental comfort conditions). Providing drinking water near the workstation is not merely a legal obligation — it is a public health measure that prevents dehydration, reduces the risk of heat stroke and maintains the professionals' capacity for safe work.

6.1 Requirements for the supply of drinking water at events

  • Minimum ratio: 1 drinking fountain for each 50 workers — or water dispensers, filters or another system with equivalent hygienic conditions

  • Placement: accessible at all workstations — maximum recommended distance of 50 meters for events with intense heat (NR-21 supplements this with hydration every 20-30 minutes)

  • Prohibition of communal cups: provision of individual disposable cups or guidance to use personal bottles

  • Temperature: cool water — a refrigerated fountain is preferable, especially at events with extreme heat

  • Equipment hygiene: fountains and water dispensers must be sanitized regularly — record the sanitization frequency

  • Unrestricted access during the shift: no worker may be prevented from drinking water during work — NR-24 guarantees this access at any time during the shift

💡  WATER FOR WORKERS ≠ WATER FOR THE EVENT

A recurring failure at events: water available at the artists' catering and at the audience's food court is plentiful, but the field technical crew has limited access or has to travel long distances to hydrate. NR-24 and NR-21 combined require that drinking water be available NEAR the workstation of each technical team — not just at a central point of the event.


  07    LODGING AT MULTI-DAY EVENTS

Module 7 — Lodging: When the Event Becomes Home for a Few Days

NR-24 defines lodging as the set of spaces or buildings, comprising a dormitory, sanitary facilities, canteen, living areas and a place for washing and drying clothes, under the responsibility of the employer, for the temporary accommodation of workers. For the events sector, lodging is most relevant at large multi-day festivals (such as 3- to 5-day festivals) where part or all of the technical crew remains on site throughout the production.

7.1 Lodging requirements at festivals — NR-24

  • Dormitory: maximum capacity of 100 workers per dormitory | maximum of 2 beds on the same vertical (bunk beds)

  • Sanitary facilities in the lodging: minimum ratio of 1 shower for each 10 lodged workers or fraction thereof

  • Canteen or meal area: available within or immediately adjacent to the lodging

  • Living area: a space for socializing and resting outside the dormitory

  • A place for washing and drying clothes: especially relevant at 3+ day festivals where the worker needs to wash and dry work clothing

7.2 Physical conditions of the lodging

  • Adequate ventilation — mandatory | tents without ventilation at summer events can reach internal temperatures of 45-50°C — a health risk

  • Adequate natural or artificial lighting for use during the day and at night

  • Waterproofing of the roof — complete protection against rain over all beds

  • Minimum distance from garbage deposits, septic tanks and waste facilities — prevention of vectors and contamination

  • Sealing against insects and rodents — especially at rural events and areas with risk of endemic diseases (dengue, zika, yellow fever)

  • Prohibition of workers' family members living in the lodging — only the worker may use the lodging provided by the employer (NR-21, item 21.6.1)

7.3 Lodging in tents — common solutions at festivals

Type of lodging

  08    WORK CLOTHING — WHAT NR-24 REQUIRES

Module 8 — Work Clothing: Who Provides It and What the Standards Are

The work-clothing requirement of NR-24 sets out the employer's obligations when the activity requires the use of a uniform or specific protective clothing. In the events sector, work clothing spans from technical-crew identification vests (production, security, OSH) to food-handler uniforms and protective clothing for generator operators and effects technicians.

8.1 Employer responsibilities for work clothing

  • Free provision of all work clothing required by the employer — the worker must not bear the cost of a uniform they are required to wear

  • Periodic replacement and maintenance in a good state of preservation and hygiene — torn, faded or deteriorated clothing must be replaced

  • Adequate quantity for use and maintenance — especially at multi-day festivals where the worker needs to change clothing daily

  • Suitability to the nature of the activity and to climatic conditions — work clothing at an outdoor summer event must be of light, breathable fabric, preferably with UV protection

  • Uniform customization — event or company branding is optional, but it cannot replace or reduce the required protection conditions

8.2 Special clothing — PPE and work clothing

NR-24 addresses work clothing in the sense of a uniform and functional garment. Clothing that constitutes PPE (FR clothing for electricians under NR-10, reflective vests for teams on roadways, chemical protective clothing for handlers of hazardous substances) is governed by NR-06 regarding the CA (Approval Certificate) and free provision. In practice, at events, both obligations coexist: the employer provides the team uniform (NR-24) AND the PPE necessary for the role (NR-06).


💡  COLOR-CODED VESTS BY SECTOR — GOOD PRACTICE BEYOND THE NR

At events with teams from different companies working simultaneously (production company, sound company, lighting company, rigging company, security, cleaning crew), the use of different-colored vests by sector — besides being good organizational practice — makes it easier to identify who is authorized in each area and to locate those responsible in an emergency. NR-24 does not require differentiation by color, but it indirectly implements the safety and organization principles set out in NR-01 (ORM).


  09    PRACTICAL APPLICATION — NR-24 IN EVENT PLANNING

Module 9 — From the Standard to the Event: Sanitary Sizing in Real Situations

The sanitary sizing of an event must be done in the planning phase — not on the day of the load-in. The responsible producer or the OSH coordinator must calculate the number of facilities needed based on the projected staffing, considering the shift with the largest headcount (usually show day, with the full crew).

  10    NR-24 INTEGRATION AND CASE STUDIES

Module 10 — NR-24 in the OSH Ecosystem and Lessons from Real Cases

10.1 Integration of NR-24 with other NRs applicable to the events sector

NR-01 (ORM/RMP)

Health risks related to inadequate sanitary conditions (biological risk from the absence of adequate toilets, food contamination) must be included in the Risk Inventory of the RMP. The action plan for sanitary compliance underpins the Action Plan of the RMP.

NR-06 (PPE)

Work clothing that constitutes PPE is provided in accordance with NR-06 (with a CA). The other work clothing (uniform, identification vest) is provided in accordance with NR-24. The employer has an obligation under both standards.

NR-15 (Unhealthy conditions)

Inadequate sanitary conditions can characterize a biological risk (Annex 14 of NR-15) — especially at events with worker exposure to waste or organic residues (cleaning crew, event waste management). The LTCAT (technical report on environmental working conditions) must assess this exposure.

NR-17 (Ergonomics)

NR-17 addresses thermal comfort, access to sanitary facilities without embarrassment, and food and rest areas. The requirements of NR-24 and NR-17 converge in the objective of ensuring dignified working conditions — the absence of dignified sanitary conditions is also a psychosocial risk factor (WROPRF — NR-01).

NR-21 (Open-air work)

NR-21 requires shelters and adequate sanitary conditions for open-air workers (item 21.5). NR-24 defines the technical standards for those sanitary conditions. The two standards are complementary and must be applied together at any outdoor event.

NR-18 (Construction)

When the assembly of stages and temporary structures constitutes a construction site, NR-18 prevails over NR-24 in its specificities. The living area of NR-18 is the equivalent of the set of sanitary facilities + changing rooms + canteen of NR-24.

ANVISA / Health Surveillance

Food handled in event kitchens must meet ANVISA requirements (Resolution RDC 216/2004 for food services) in addition to NR-24 requirements for kitchens. The food-handler certificate is an ANVISA requirement independent of NR-24.


10.2 Case Study 1 — Gastroenteritis outbreak at a 3-day festival

Scenario: A festival with 280 workers over 3 days. Catering served in an improvised area near the stage (5 meters from the generator area). Insufficient chemical toilets (14 units for 280 workers — a 50% shortfall). Sanitization of the units: once a day (insufficient for the demand). By the second day of the festival, 32 workers presented with vomiting and diarrhea. The health service identified a gastroenteritis outbreak — likely caused by Salmonella contaminating food at the catering. Analysis: food preparation area without adequate ventilation and near sources of heat and contamination. Absence of a washbasin for handwashing at the entrance to the catering.

NR-24 analysis: multiple violations. Insufficient ratio of toilets (14 units for 280 workers — the minimum would be 28). Insufficient sanitization (1x a day vs. the recommended minimum of 3x). Inadequate meal area — near the generator area and without adequate ventilation. Absence of a washbasin for handwashing at the access to the food area.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

Insufficient toilets + catering in inadequate conditions = an outbreak of foodborne illnesses. The cost of 14 additional sanitary units is trivial compared to the cost of an outbreak: 32 workers off duty, partial cancellation of activities, legal liability of the production for harm to workers' health. Plan the correct sizing in the event's budgeting phase.


10.3 Case Study 2 — Worker without access to a restroom during a 12-hour shift

Scenario: A rigging technician works on the grid at 12 meters high from 8am to 8pm (12 hours). The event's restrooms are in the service area, 400 meters from the grid. To come down from the structure, the technician must disconnect all safety equipment, use the access ladder, walk the 400 meters to the restroom and return — a process that takes 25 to 30 minutes. The production coordinator discourages frequent descents ("we'll fall behind on setup"). The technician reports that they restricted fluid intake throughout the day to reduce the need to go to the restroom. Result: mild dehydration at the end of the shift, with dizziness that caused a minor safety incident while coming down from the structure.

NR-24 + NR-21 analysis: the worker restricted hydration to avoid using the distant restroom — a combined violation of NR-24 (toilets must be accessible during the shift) and NR-21 (hydration is a heat-protection measure). Restricting fluids for a worker doing physical work at height is an immediate safety risk (dehydration → dizziness → fall).

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

The distance between the restroom and the workstation is not merely an inconvenience — it is a health and safety risk factor when it leads the worker to restrict water intake. At workstations at height or in hard-to-reach locations, the logistics of breaks for physiological needs must be explicitly planned in the work procedure.

🏭  CIPA AND THE EVENTS SECTOR — WHY THIS MATTERS

Production and equipment rental companies for live events are, for the most part, classified as Risk Grade 3 or 4 by CNAE — which entails the mandatory requirement of a CIPA with 8h or 20h of training. The sector concentrates multiple serious risks (work at height, electrical, intense physical effort, noise, heat, chemical products) and has a history of fatal accidents. The CIPA is the organizational structure that turns compliance with the NRs into living practice within each company in the sector.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS — WHAT THE CIPA IS AND WHY IT EXISTS

Module 1 — CIPA: From the Acronym to Practice in the Events Sector

Regulatory Standard No. 05 — Internal Commission for the Prevention of Accidents and Harassment (CIPA) — establishes the parameters and requirements for the constitution, organization and operation of the internal commission whose objective is the prevention of work-related accidents and diseases, so as to permanently make work compatible with the preservation of life and the promotion of the worker's health. Published by MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978, it was significantly updated in December 2022 by MTP Ordinance No. 4,219, in force since March 20, 2023 — an amendment that formally included combating harassment in the CIPA's scope and changed its name.

The name change is more than semantic: it reflects a substantive expansion of the scope of action. The CIPA became the Internal Commission for the Prevention of Accidents AND Harassment — incorporating as an explicit mandate the prevention and combating of sexual harassment and other forms of violence at work. This expansion originates in Law No. 14,457/2022, which established the Emprega + Mulheres Program and recognized that harassment — especially sexual harassment — is a real occupational risk that affects the health, safety and dignity of workers, with a particular impact on women.

In the live events sector, where mixed teams work under high-pressure conditions, in temporary locations, often at night and with asymmetrical power relations among production, artists and technical crew, both workplace accidents and harassment are real and documented risks. The CIPA is the legal mechanism that NR-05 provides so that workers and the company act together, in a structured way, to prevent both.


Full name

Internal Commission for the Prevention of Accidents and Harassment (CIPA)

Legal basis

NR-05 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Latest update: MTP Ordinance No. 4,219/2022 (in force since 03/20/2023)

Complementary law

Law No. 14,457/2022 — Emprega + Mulheres Program — included combating harassment in the CIPA's scope

Objective

Prevent work-related accidents and diseases, and combat sexual harassment and other forms of violence

Mandatory requirement

All organizations and public bodies that have employees governed by the CLT — with no exception by sector

Composition

Joint: employer representatives (appointed) + employee representatives (elected by secret ballot)

Who presides

President: appointed by the employer | Vice-President: elected by the full employee representatives

Term

1 year — re-election allowed for 1 additional consecutive term

Job security of the elected member

From the registration of the candidacy until 1 year after the end of the term — arbitrary dismissal prohibited

Meetings

Minimum of once a month during normal working hours | Allowed remotely (2022 update) | Minutes mandatory

Documentation

Kept for a minimum of 5 years at the establishment, available to labor inspection


  02    MANDATORY REQUIREMENT AND SIZING — WHO NEEDS A CIPA

Module 2 — Who Is Required, Who Is Exempt and How to Size It

NR-05 is universal: it applies to all organizations and public bodies of the direct and indirect administration, of the Legislative and Judicial branches and the Public Prosecutor's Office, that have employees governed by the CLT. However, the obligation to set up the CIPA as a commission depends on the number of employees and the Risk Grade (RG) of the activity, in accordance with Table I of NR-04 (based on the company's CNAE).

2.1 Risk Grade and CNAE — how events companies are classified

The Risk Grade (RG) is determined by the company's CNAE (National Classification of Economic Activities), in accordance with Table I of NR-04. Live events companies are classified under different CNAEs according to their main activity:

⚠️  COMPANIES WITH UP TO 19 EMPLOYEES — NR-05 REPRESENTATIVE

Companies with up to 19 CLT employees are exempt from setting up a CIPA — but are NOT exempt from every obligation. NR-05 requires them to appoint an NR-05 Representative: a trained employee who takes on the CIPA's preventive functions at the establishment. This representative must receive the same training as CIPA members (4h, 8h or 20h according to the Risk Grade) and enjoys the same job-security guarantees as an elected member.


2.2 How to read Table I of NR-05 — sizing the CIPA

Table I of NR-05 cross-references the Risk Grade with the number of employees at the establishment to determine the minimum number of full and alternate representatives of each party (employer and employees). The sizing considers only the establishment's CLT employees — it does not include service providers or self-employed workers.

The composition must represent the sectors with the highest risk or the highest accident history.


  03    ELECTORAL PROCESS — HOW THE CIPA IS ELECTED

Module 3 — CIPA Election: Step by Step of the Electoral Process

The CIPA's electoral process is regulated in detail by NR-05 because the legitimacy of workers' representation depends on a transparent, free and democratic process. The CIPA's joint composition — with representatives appointed by the employer and elected by the workers — is what gives it the power to be an effective voice for both sides of the employment relationship. A flawed or poorly conducted electoral process invalidates this legitimacy and weakens the CIPA's entire preventive structure.

🔴  LEGAL GUARANTEE FOR THE ELECTED MEMBER — JOB SECURITY FROM THE CANDIDACY

Job security begins with the REGISTRATION OF THE CANDIDACY — not with the election. From the moment the worker registers their candidacy until 1 year after the end of the term, they cannot be arbitrarily dismissed (without just cause). This guarantee is in the CLT (art. 165) and is one of the most important protections of NR-05: it ensures that employee representatives can act without fear of retaliation. In the events sector, where labor relations are frequently informal or short-term, this guarantee is especially relevant.


  04    DUTIES OF THE CIPA — WHAT EACH MEMBER DOES IN PRACTICE

Module 4 — The 10 Duties of the CIPA in the Daily Routine of an Events Company

The CIPA's duties are defined in NR-05 and determine what the commission must actively do during its term. It is not enough to be elected and attend the monthly meetings — the CIPA has continuous operational responsibilities of observation, recording, discussion and forwarding of OHS matters. In an events company, these duties translate into practical and specific actions for the sector's risk profile.

4.1 The CIPA's legal duties — NR-05 and its application at events

Duty (NR-05)

Concrete application in the events sector

Identify risks of the work process and prepare the Risk Map

Map all risks present in the equipment warehouses, maintenance workshops, transport vehicles and at the event sites (stages, assembly, rigging)

Prepare a work plan to make prevention actions effective

Annual CIPA plan with a schedule of inspections, DSD topics, SIPAT, RMP review and prioritized corrective actions

Participate in the implementation and quality control of prevention measures

Monitor the adoption of PPE, implementation of guards on equipment, adequacy of electrical and health installations at the company's workplaces

Assess work processes and environments aiming to identify conditions that may cause accidents and occupational diseases

Periodic inspections at the warehouse, workshop, vehicles, and at the event sites during assembly

Request measures to reduce, eliminate or neutralize risks

Formalize requests to management and the SESMT to correct unsafe conditions — with a deadline for response (the employer has 8 days to respond)

Request the employer to shut down a machine or sector with serious and imminent risk

At an event, this includes halting a lift with wind above the limit, suspending work at height during a storm, or stopping the operation of a generator with a fuel leak

Collaborate in the development and implementation of the PCMSO and the RMP

Actively participate in the meetings to update the Risk Management Program — provide field data and validate the proposed measures

Disseminate and ensure compliance with safety standards

Monthly DSDs, notices, posters, SIPAT — promote the safety culture throughout the technical crew

Participate in the analysis of accidents together with the SESMT

Investigate each accident (including commuting accidents) with the SESMT, identify root causes and propose measures to prevent recurrence

Prevent and combat sexual harassment and other forms of violence at work — including during the SIPAT

Give talks, promote the whistleblowing channel, disseminate the company's Code of Conduct, especially in a sector with a documented history of harassment



  05    CIPA TRAINING — CONTENT, WORKLOAD AND EXECUTION

Module 5 — Training: What the CIPA Member Needs to Know

Training is a mandatory condition for the investiture of CIPA members. NR-05 requires the employer to provide training for all members (full and alternate, elected and appointed), for the secretary and their substitute, and for the NR-05 Representative (companies with up to 19 employees). The training must take place during normal working hours, be fully funded by the employer and have a workload that varies according to the establishment's Risk Grade.

5.1 Minimum workload by Risk Grade

Risk Grade

Minimum Workload (in person)

Format

Typical events companies

RG 2

4 hours

In person or distance learning (in accordance with NR-01)

Support services companies (catering, cleaning, reception) linked to events

RG 3

8 hours

In person (practical part) + possibility of distance learning for the theoretical part

Event producers, sound and lighting rental companies, private security companies

RG 4

20 hours

Spread over a maximum of 8h/day | In person mandatory for the practical part

Metal structure assembly companies, rigging services, temporary electrical installations


💡  TRAINING CARRIED OVER — NR-05 RULE

Training carried out less than 2 years ago may be carried over within the same organization, subject to the provisions of NR-01. This means that a CIPA member who was trained in 2024 and is re-elected for the 2025-2026 term does not need to redo the full training if the content is the same and the interval is less than 2 years. The company must check the NR-01 criteria for carrying over training.


5.2 Mandatory minimum content of NR-05 training

  • Study of the environment, working conditions and existing or generated risks — with emphasis on the company's specific activities

  • Methodology for investigating and analyzing work-related accidents and diseases — root-cause analysis techniques (Ishikawa diagram, cause tree)

  • Notions of labor and social-security legislation regarding occupational safety and health — applicable NRs, CAT, LTCAT, PCMSO, RMP

  • General principles of occupational hygiene and of risk-prevention measures — physical, chemical and biological agents

  • Organization of the CIPA and other matters necessary for exercising the Commission's duties — sizing, election, term, job security, meetings, minutes

  • Prevention and combating of sexual harassment and other forms of violence at work — mandatory content included by MTP Ordinance No. 4,219/2022

5.3 Who may deliver the training

  • The company's SESMT (when mandatory in accordance with NR-04)

  • The sector's employer entity (associations of producers, employer union)

  • Workers' entity (unions)

  • Contracted external qualified professional — occupational safety engineer, safety technician with qualification and registration

  • Vocational training institution with a recognized course in the OHS field


  06    MEETINGS — FREQUENCY, AGENDA AND DOCUMENTATION

Module 6 — CIPA Meetings: How to Conduct Them, What to Record

Meetings are the formal space where the CIPA exercises its duties collectively. NR-05 establishes that the CIPA will meet with all its members at least once a month, in an appropriate place and during the company's normal working hours, following the annual calendar drawn up at the first meeting of each term. The 2022 update allowed ordinary meetings to be held remotely (videoconference), which is especially relevant for companies with itinerant teams — the typical profile of the events sector.

6.1 Types of meeting and their triggers

Type of Meeting

Frequency / Trigger

Mandatory Content

Deadline

Ordinary

Minimum of once a month according to the annual calendar

Analysis of the period's accidents | Inspection report | Ongoing actions | Workers' demands | Open agenda item

According to the calendar | May be remote

Extraordinary — Serious accident

Whenever an accident occurs with death, loss of a limb or of an organic function, or major damage

Analysis of the accident | Identification of causes | Preventive measures | Forwarding to the SESMT and the employer

Within a maximum of 48 hours after the accident

Extraordinary — Urgent demand

When a serious and imminent risk requiring immediate action is identified

Description of the risk | Formal request for shutdown or corrective measure | Forwarding to the employer

Immediately — a deadline of 8 days for the employer's response

Immediately after the investiture

Preparation of the annual calendar of meetings | Definition of the standard minutes | Distribution of responsibilities

First month of the term


6.2 The Meeting Minutes — a mandatory and legal document

Every CIPA meeting must be recorded in minutes — a formal document that records: date, time and place; members present and absent; agenda discussed; decisions taken; deadlines and those responsible for each action; and signatures of all those present. The minutes are the legal instrument that proves the CIPA's regular operation and are part of the documentation that must be kept for 5 years at the establishment.

  • Minutes numbered sequentially — e.g., Minutes No. 001/2026, Minutes No. 002/2026

  • Record all members present and justify the absences

  • Describe the resolutions clearly — vague decisions have no legal effect

  • Record the requests made to the employer and the responses received (8-day deadline)

  • Signature of all those present or a record of a justified refusal

  • Filing for 5 years — paper or digital with guaranteed backup


  07    RISK MAP — A TOOL FOR IDENTIFICATION AND COMMUNICATION

Module 7 — Risk Map: Making the Invisible Hazards Visible

The Risk Map is the graphic representation of the risks present in the work environments, prepared by the CIPA based on a participatory survey. It is posted at the workplaces so that all workers — including new and temporary ones — can visually identify the risks of each area. In the context of the events sector, the Risk Map must cover the company's permanent environments (warehouse, workshop, administrative area) and must be complemented by specific instruments for the temporary environments of each event (PRA — Preliminary Risk Analysis, in accordance with NR-01).

The 2022 update of NR-05 brought an important flexibility: the CIPA may adopt other risk-identification tools and techniques in addition to the traditional Map — including risk matrices, RMP inventories, worker risk-perception questionnaires and digital recording tools. The Risk Map is no longer the only required form, but it remains the most visual and accessible for the shop floor or the warehouse of an equipment rental company.

7.1 Risk Map symbols — groups and colors

Group

Color

Type of Agent

Examples in the events sector

Group 1 — Physical

Green

Noise | Vibration | Extreme temperatures | Ionizing and non-ionizing radiation | Abnormal pressures | Humidity

Noise from PA systems at soundcheck and shows | Solar and generator heat | Vibration of subwoofers | UV radiation in outdoor work

Group 2 — Chemical

Red

Dusts | Fumes | Mists | Fogs | Gases | Vapors | Substances and compounds with an exposure limit or by inspection

Diesel vapors in the generator area | Fog-machine fluid (propylene glycol) | Contact cleaner spray (IPA) | CO₂ in enclosed sub-stages

Work at height without adequate protection | Electrical risk in temporary distribution boards | Fall of suspended equipment without a safety cable | Fire due to a short circuit in provisional installations


7.2 How to prepare the Risk Map — a participatory process

  1. Get to know the work process of each area — visit the site with CIPA members and workers from each sector

  2. Identify the risks present in each area — using the table of groups and colors

  3. Identify the preventive measures already in place and assess their effectiveness

  4. Represent them graphically on the floor plan or sketch of the area: circles with the group's color, size proportional to the severity and number of exposed workers

  5. Post the Map in a place visible to all workers in the area

  6. Review the Map whenever there is a change in the work process, the equipment or the conditions of the area


  08    HARASSMENT — THE CIPA'S NEW FRONT AFTER LAW 14,457/2022

Module 8 — CIPA Against Harassment: the Newest and Most Urgent Duty

The inclusion of combating harassment in the CIPA's scope by Law No. 14,457/2022 and by MTP Ordinance No. 4,219/2022 responded to a documented reality: sexual harassment and moral harassment are forms of violence that cause harm to workers' mental and physical health, and that occur with particular frequency in work sectors with informal hierarchies, concentration of power in leadership figures and high team turnover — characteristics that precisely describe the live events sector.

The events sector has structural characteristics that favor harassment: teams that form quickly for each production and dissolve at the end; power hierarchies that include artists, managers, executive producers and technical crews with direct financial-dependence relations; night work and intense socializing environments; and a culture of normalizing extreme pressure that sometimes extends to the normalization of abusive behaviors.

8.1 Definitions — sexual and moral harassment

📋  LEGAL DEFINITION — SEXUAL HARASSMENT

Sexual harassment is the coercion of someone with the intent to obtain a sexual advantage or favor, with the agent taking advantage of their status as a hierarchical superior or of ascendancy inherent to the exercise of an employment, position or role — Penal Code, art. 216-A. It is characterized by the imposition of acts or insinuations of a sexual nature against the victim's will, using power or influence. In the events sector, it may involve artists, producers, managers or any person in a position of power over the technical crew.


📋  DEFINITION — MORAL HARASSMENT

Moral harassment is the exposure of workers to humiliating and embarrassing situations, repetitive and prolonged during the working day, that destabilize the victim's relationship with their work environment, often forcing them to give up the job — it causes harm to physical and mental health. In the events sector, moral harassment frequently appears as: shouting and public humiliation during assembly, deadline pressure with constant threats of dismissal, discrimination by gender, race or sexual orientation in the distribution of tasks.


8.2 The CIPA's obligations regarding harassment

  • Include topics of prevention and combating of sexual harassment and other forms of violence in ALL CIPA activities — including the SIPAT

  • Disseminate the company's Whistleblowing Channel — ensuring that all workers know how and where to report, and that the report is confidential and protected from retaliation

  • Participate in the preparation or review of the company's Code of Conduct — which must include a clear zero-tolerance policy toward harassment

  • Carry out annual awareness campaigns on harassment for all hierarchical levels — including leaders and managers

  • Monitor the procedures for investigating harassment reports — not as an investigator (the role of HR and Legal), but as a guarantor that the process is taking place

  • Record in the minutes the actions taken regarding combating harassment — to evidence compliance with legal obligations

8.3 What the CIPA does NOT do regarding harassment

The CIPA has a preventive and awareness-raising role — it is not the body for investigating or judging harassment reports. When a harassment report reaches the CIPA, it must forward it to the company's Whistleblowing Channel or to HR/Legal for formal investigation. The CIPA may monitor the process to ensure it takes place, but it does not conduct the investigation.


  09    SIPAT — INTERNAL WORK ACCIDENT PREVENTION WEEK

Module 9 — SIPAT: the Week That Turns Obligation into Culture

The SIPAT — Internal Work Accident Prevention Week — is the annual event organized by the CIPA with the aim of spreading the safety culture to all the company's workers. NR-05 establishes that the CIPA must hold the SIPAT annually, integrating it with the company's other preventive activities. With the 2022 update, the SIPAT became mandatorily a space to address topics of combating sexual harassment and other forms of violence, in addition to the traditional accident-prevention topics.

For the live events sector, the SIPAT has a special meaning: it is the opportunity to bring into the company the same care for safety that is required at events. It is common for equipment rental companies and producers to prioritize safety at events — under the pressure of the NRs and of clients — but to neglect safety in the daily routine of the warehouse, the workshop and the internal processes. The SIPAT breaks this contradiction.

9.1 SIPAT planning — a practical guide

  1. Define the central theme of the SIPAT — a participatory choice with all CIPA members | Integrate the risks identified in the Risk Map

  2. Put together the program with a variety of formats: talks, activities, practical demonstrations, emergency drills, films and debates

  3. Mandatorily include a topic on combating harassment — a talk, debate or specific activity

  4. Invite external specialists: occupational physician, safety engineer, psychologist, union representatives

  5. Organize a Risk Map or safety-slogan contest — it increases engagement

  6. Record the attendance of all participants — an attendance list per activity for documentation

  7. Prepare a SIPAT report to file alongside the CIPA's documentation

  8. Evaluate the SIPAT results with a satisfaction survey — use the feedback to improve the next edition

9.2 Suggested topics for the SIPAT at a live events company

Topic

Suggested format

Relevance for the sector

Work at Height and NR-35

Practical demonstration with real PPE | Inspection of the harness and lanyard

High — rigging, stage assembly and structures are the activities with the greatest fatal risk in the sector

Electrical Risks and NR-10

Talk with real cases + LOTO demonstration

High — temporary electrical installations, generators and distribution boards are daily risks at events

Ergonomics and RSI Prevention

Practical activity on the correct lifting of loads (flight cases, speaker boxes)

High — the loading and unloading of heavy equipment is the main cause of RSI in the sector

Prevention and Combating of Harassment

Talk with a psychologist + open debate | Dissemination of the Whistleblowing Channel

MANDATORY under NR-05 after 2022 | Sector with a high documented incidence of moral and sexual harassment

High — sound technicians exposed to 95-115 dB are the most exposed to NIHL in the sector

Mental Health and Deadline Pressure

Very relevant — 20h shifts, extreme pressure and the sector's unpredictability are psychosocial risk factors


  10    CIPA AND RMP — INTEGRATION WITH THE RISK MANAGEMENT SYSTEM

Module 10 — CIPA in the OHS Ecosystem: from NR-05 to NR-01

The CIPA does not operate in isolation — it is one of the pillars of the integrated occupational-risk management system required by NR-01 (Occupational Risk Management — ORM) and materialized in the Risk Management Program (RMP). The 2022 update of NR-05 explicitly reinforced this integration: the CIPA actively participates in the hazard identification and risk assessment of the RMP, proposes control actions, monitors execution and keeps records of evidence in the Program.

10.1 Integration of NR-05 with the other NRs of the events sector

NR-01 (ORM/RMP)

The CIPA actively participates in the preparation and review of the RMP — providing field data on identified risks, investigated accidents and workers' perception. The CIPA's Risk Map feeds the RMP's Risk Inventory. The RMP's Action Plan incorporates the CIPA's recommendations.

NR-04 (SESMT)

The CIPA and the SESMT (when mandatory) are complementary bodies — the CIPA represents the workers and the employer in its preventive action; the SESMT provides specialized technical expertise. Accidents must be analyzed jointly. The CIPA may request technical reports and opinions from the SESMT.

NR-06 (PPE)

The CIPA verifies and disseminates the correct use of PPE throughout the company. In the periodic inspections, it records the presence and state of conservation of the PPE at the workstations. It requests the employer to replace deteriorated or inadequate PPE.

NR-07 (PCMSO)

The CIPA collaborates in the development of the PCMSO — informing the coordinating physician of the risks identified in the Risk Map and the activities with potential harm to health. It monitors the results of the audiometric and general-health exams — data that feed back into the RMP.

NR-10 (Electrical)

At events, the CIPA must verify whether the temporary electrical installations (distribution boards, generators, dimmer systems) are being carried out by qualified professionals in accordance with NR-10 and whether the safety conditions are being observed.

NR-35 (Height)

In companies with rigging, stage assembly and maintenance-at-height activities, the CIPA verifies whether the NR-35 training is valid, whether the height PPE is being provided and used correctly, and whether the PRAs and work permits are being issued as required.

NR-15 (Unhealthy work)

The CIPA monitors the LTCAT and verifies whether the control measures for the unhealthy agents (especially noise, for sound technicians) are being implemented. It requests the SESMT and the employer to update the LTCAT when there is a change in working conditions.


  11    CASE STUDIES — CIPA IN ACTION IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 11 — When the CIPA Works and When It Does Not: Real Cases

11.1 Case 1 — A CIPA that identified a serious risk and prevented a fatal accident

Situation: Sound and lighting equipment rental company, 65 employees, RG 3. The CIPA member representing the employees, during a monthly inspection of the warehouse, identified that the metal shelving system where the heavy flight cases are stored (averaging 80 kg per case) was installed without adequate fixing to the wall — a risk of the shelf collapsing onto workers during the handling of the cases. He formally recorded it in the meeting minutes and forwarded it to the employer with a request for action and a 15-day deadline. The employer responded within the 8 legal days with a fixing schedule, completed in 12 days. No accident occurred.

Analysis: The CIPA worked exactly as NR-05 provides: proactive inspection, formal recording, forwarding with a deadline, employer response, monitoring of execution. The CIPA member had the legal authority to request the shutdown of the use of the storage area until the correction was made — which was agreed voluntarily by the company.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

The CIPA's effectiveness depends on the regular and documented periodic inspection of the work environments — not just on responding to accidents after they occur. In a rental company, the warehouse, the workshop and the vehicles are the permanent environments of greatest risk — and often the most neglected because they "don't look like events."


11.2 Case 2 — A "paper" CIPA that did not prevent a serious accident

Situation: Mid-sized event producer, 45 employees, RG 3. CIPA set up in accordance with the law — elected members, registered with the MTE, with monthly minutes. In practice: 20-minute meetings with no real risk analysis; no inspection carried out in the 8 months of the term; Risk Map prepared at the investiture and never updated again; CIPA members with no real authority (informally inhibited by the manager from "creating problems"). A maintenance technician suffers a serious accident while working on equipment with no LOTO applied — a wrist fracture and a deep cut. The investigation reveals: absence of a lockout procedure, absence of NR-12 training for maintenance, and a CIPA that had never identified nor forwarded this risk.

Analysis: The CIPA existed on paper and in the bureaucracy, but did not exercise its real duties. The organizational cause: CIPA members without adequate training, without a structured inspection schedule and without real support from leadership. A CIPA that does not inspect, does not record and does not forward protects no one — but still generates a false sense of compliance.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

A "paper" CIPA is worse than a nonexistent CIPA in some respects: it creates the illusion that the risks are being managed when they are not. The trained and committed CIPA member, with a monthly inspection schedule, an updated Risk Map and direct access to leadership for forwarding requests, is what distinguishes the effective CIPA from the bureaucratic CIPA.

⚡  NR-16 IN THE EVENTS SECTOR — WHY IT MATTERS

The live events sector concentrates multiple activities classified as hazardous by NR-16: electricians operating temporary distribution boards (QDTs) and high-power generators (electrical energy — Annex IV); special-effects technicians working with LPG, pyrotechnics and flame machines (flammables/explosives — Annexes I and II); private security teams at events (property security — Annex III). In all these situations, the 30% hazard premium is a legitimate labor right that frequently goes unpaid — due to lack of awareness, informal hiring or the absence of a technical report.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS — HAZARD × UNHEALTHINESS: THE DIFFERENCE THAT DEFINES THE RIGHT

Module 1 — What Hazard Is and How It Differs from Unhealthiness

Regulatory Standard No. 16 — Hazardous Activities and Operations — regulates articles 193 to 196 of the Consolidation of Labor Laws (CLT) and defines which activities and operations are considered hazardous, establishing the right to the hazard premium for workers who perform them. Originally published by MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978, it was last amended by MTE Ordinance No. 2,021, of December 3, 2025, with partial effect from April 3, 2026.

The fundamental distinction between hazard and unhealthiness defines which standard applies to each situation and what the corresponding premium is. Hazard (NR-16) refers to immediate risks that can cause death or serious injury suddenly — explosion, electric shock, instantaneous fire. Unhealthiness (NR-15) refers to risks that develop gradually over the working life — noise-induced hearing loss, chronic poisoning, pneumoconioses. Confusion between the two concepts is recurrent in the events sector and results in classification errors and payment of incorrect premiums.

📊  HAZARD × UNHEALTHINESS — COMPARATIVE TABLE

HAZARD (NR-16): IMMEDIATE and IMMINENT risk | Can kill or seriously injure suddenly | Premium: 30% of the BASE SALARY (without bonuses) | Technical report by a Safety Eng. or Occupational Physician | Reports kept for 20 years | Examples: electric shock at a QDT, LPG explosion, pyrotechnic detonation. /// UNHEALTHINESS (NR-15): GRADUAL and CUMULATIVE risk | Causes harm to health over years of exposure | Premium: 10, 20 or 40% of the NATIONAL MINIMUM WAGE | Technical report (LTCAT) | Examples: noise-induced hearing loss, skin cancer from UV, chronic CO poisoning.


The same activity can simultaneously generate hazard and unhealthiness. The electrician who works at high voltage has a hazard risk (immediate electric shock) and may have an unhealthiness risk (industrial-frequency electromagnetic fields — assessed separately). In this case, the worker is entitled to the hazard premium (NR-16) AND may be entitled to the unhealthiness premium (NR-15) — but the accumulation of the two premiums is controversial in labor case law, so it is necessary to check the applicable TST Precedents (Súmulas).


Legal basis

NR-16 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Last amendment: MTE Ordinance No. 2,021/2025 (partial effect: 04/03/2026)

CLT — Art. 193

Hazardous activities: permanent exposure to flammables, explosives, electrical energy, robbery/physical violence, ionizing radiation and radioactive substances

Premium

30% of the base salary — without the addition of bonuses, awards or profit sharing

Intermittent work

Treated as permanent exposure in the MONTHS in which there is exposure — the intermittency is not deducted

6 categories (Annexes)

I: Explosives | II: Flammables | III: Personal/property security | IV: Electrical energy | V: Ionizing radiation | VI: Motorcycle

Characterization instrument

Technical Hazard Report — Safety Eng. (CREA) or Occupational Physician (CRM) with specialization | ART mandatory

Report retention

Minimum of 20 years — unlike the unhealthiness LTCAT (NR-15), which has no express deadline beyond the limitation period

Elimination of the premium

The company may eliminate/neutralize the hazard through engineering or organizational measures — proven by a de-characterization report

Special retirement

Workers in hazardous conditions are entitled to special retirement after 25 years of contribution (INSS)


  02    ANNEX I — EXPLOSIVES: PYROTECHNICS AND STORAGE AT EVENTS

Module 2 — Explosives: Professional Pyrotechnics and NR-16

Annex I of NR-16 lists the hazardous activities and operations involving explosives, granting the hazard premium to all workers engaged in these activities, as well as to those operating in the corresponding risk area. Explosives are substances or mixtures of substances that, under certain conditions of heat, impact, friction or electric shock, react rapidly, producing gases, heat and pressure in proportions that cause destruction.

In the live events sector, the explosives most relevant to the application of NR-16 are: professional pyrotechnics (scenic fireworks, confetti cannons, flamethrowers and detonation effects used in live shows), sparkulars (cold-spark systems that use metallic powder under pressure), and effect generators with an explosive charge. The professional pyrotechnician and any worker operating in the risk area of pyrotechnic storage or detonation are entitled to the hazard premium.

2.1 Activities with explosives that generate hazard at events

  • Storage of professional pyrotechnics at the event site — any worker operating in the risk area of the depot

  • Transport of pyrotechnics from the depot to the event site — loading and unloading, inventory verification

  • Handling and preparation of pyrotechnic effects before firing — connecting fuses, positioning mortars, programming electric firing systems

  • Detonation and firing of pyrotechnics during the show — pyrotechnician and firing-system operator

  • Destruction of undetonated explosives — recovery and disposal of defective pyrotechnics

  • Any activity carried out within the delimited risk area — including sound, lighting and security technicians who remain in the zone

⚠️  BRAZILIAN ARMY LICENSE — A REQUIREMENT PRIOR TO HAZARD

Before discussing hazard, it is important to remember that operating professional pyrotechnics in Brazil requires a license from the Brazilian Army (DFPC — Directorate for the Inspection of Controlled Products). The pyrotechnician must be a certified professional holding a valid authorization. NR-16 defines the RIGHT to the premium — the Army defines who may PERFORM the activity. Both requirements coexist.


2.2 Risk areas for explosives — NR-16 Annex I

NR-16 defines risk areas around explosive-storage sites, within which any worker is entitled to the premium. The distances range from 45 meters (minimum quantities) to 1,350 meters (large-scale depots) depending on the quantity and type of explosive. For event pyrotechnics, the risk areas are defined according to the Brazilian Army's tables and the specifications of the company's technical hazard report.


  03    ANNEX II — FLAMMABLES: DIESEL, LPG AND EFFECT FLUIDS AT EVENTS

Module 3 — Flammables: the Fuels of the Show and Their Hazard Risks

Annex II of NR-16 defines the operations of transporting, storing and handling flammable liquids and gases that characterize hazardous conditions. For the live events sector, the most relevant flammables are: diesel and gasoline for electrical generators, LPG and propane for flame effects and high-power fog machines, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) in large quantities for cleaning equipment, and special fuels for pyrotechnic effects.

3.1 Hazard threshold — the quantity rule of NR-16

📏  THRESHOLDS THAT DETERMINE HAZARD — FLAMMABLES

FLAMMABLE LIQUIDS: transport or handling of MORE than 200 liters = HAZARDOUS | Up to 200 liters = does not characterize hazard under NR-16. LIQUEFIED FLAMMABLE GASES (LPG): transport or handling of MORE than 135 kg = HAZARDOUS | Up to 135 kg = does not characterize hazard. IMPORTANT: flammables contained in the vehicles' factory-original fuel tanks are expressly EXCLUDED — the diesel in the tank of the generator or the truck does not generate hazard merely because it is in the original tank.


In practice for the events sector: the person responsible for refueling a 250 kVA generator who performs multiple refuelings of 200 liters of diesel over a multi-day event accumulates handling above the threshold — characterizing hazard. The technician who operates fog machines with industrial LPG (45 kg cylinders) and changes multiple cylinders over the event may exceed the 135 kg limit handled — also characterizing hazard.

3.3 Risk areas for flammables — NR-16 Annex II

NR-16 defines risk areas around installations that store flammables. Any worker who performs their activities within these areas is entitled to the hazard premium — even if they do not directly handle the flammable. The main risk-radius references are:

  • Containment basin of flammable-liquid tanks: the entire internal area of the basin

  • Fueling stations: a circle with a radius of 7.5 meters from the pumps

  • LPG cylinder storage area: radius to be defined according to quantity and a specific technical report

  • Flammable depot at an event: area defined by the hazard report — a minimum radius of 10 m around the event's fuel depot is recommended

  04    ANNEX III — PERSONAL AND PROPERTY SECURITY AT EVENTS

Module 4 — Event Security: Physical Violence and the Hazard Premium

Annex III of NR-16 defines as hazardous the activities or operations that involve exposing personal or property security professionals to robbery or other kinds of physical violence. In the live events sector, this Annex applies directly to private security teams hired for events — entrance security, access-control teams, armed fire wardens and artist security personnel.

4.1 Who is entitled to the premium for property security at events

NR-16 defines as personal or property security professionals the workers who cumulatively meet the following conditions:

  • They are employees of companies providing security and surveillance services — registered and authorized by the Ministry of Justice and Public Security

  • Or they are part of an in-house private security service of a non-specialized company — duly authorized under the relevant legislation

  • They are exposed to robbery or other kinds of physical violence in the performance of their duties

In the events sector, the private security companies hired for access control, perimeter monitoring and artist security have their workers covered by this Annex. Security staff who work at events such as concerts, festivals and high-profile corporate events are exposed to the risk of physical violence — especially at events with large crowds, alcohol consumption and potential for conflict.

  05    ANNEX IV — ELECTRICAL ENERGY: THE MOST RELEVANT FOR THE SECTOR

Module 5 — Electrical Energy: the Greatest Hazard Vector in Live Events

Annex IV of NR-16 — Hazardous Activities and Operations with Electrical Energy — is the Annex with the broadest and most direct application to the live events sector. The combination of high-power temporary electrical installations, large generators, dimmer and amplifier systems, and electrician teams that are not permanent employees of the company (they are hired per event) creates a scenario of systemic electrical hazard in any medium- or large-scale production.

NR-16 Annex IV must be read together with NR-10 (Safety in Electrical Installations and Services), which defines the conditions for a worker's qualification to work with electricity and the mandatory safety measures. Hazard (NR-16) defines the right to the premium; NR-10 defines the conditions for safe work.

5.1 Who is entitled to the premium for electrical energy

  • Workers who perform activities or operations on electrical installations or equipment energized at HIGH VOLTAGE (above 1,000 V alternating current or above 1,500 V direct current)

  • Workers who perform work in PROXIMITY to electrical energy as defined in NR-10 — including controlled-risk zones

  • Workers on electrical installations or equipment energized at LOW VOLTAGE in the consumption electrical system (SEC) when the requirements of NR-10 (training, procedures, PPE) are NOT met

  • Workers in electrical power systems (SEP) — construction, operation and maintenance

5.2 What does NOT generate the premium for electrical energy

🔴  IMPORTANT EXCLUSION — ELEMENTARY LOW-VOLTAGE ACTIVITIES

Elementary low-voltage activities are NOT considered hazardous, such as the use of energized electrical equipment and the procedures of switching electrical circuits on and off, PROVIDED THAT the electrical materials and equipment comply with the official technical standards. At events: switching amplifiers, mixing consoles and controlled lighting equipment on and off — even if energized at 220 V — do NOT generate hazard if the equipment complies with ABNT/IEC standards and the NR-10 procedures are followed.

Intermittent work in a hazardous zone is treated as permanent exposure for payment purposes: in the months in which there is exposure to the electrical risk that characterizes hazard, the premium is fully owed — even if the exposure does not occur every day of the month. This rule is particularly relevant for electricians hired per event, who work intermittently but in conditions of real hazard during operations.

  06    TECHNICAL HAZARD REPORT — HOW IT WORKS IN PRACTICE

Module 6 — Hazard Report: the Document That Guarantees the Right

NR-16 is explicit: the characterization or de-characterization of the hazard of an activity must be carried out by means of a technical report prepared by an Occupational Physician or Safety Engineer registered with the Ministry of Labor. Without the report, there is no technical basis for paying the premium — nor for the company to deny payment. The report is the instrument that transforms the technical assessment into an enforceable labor right.

6.1 Who may prepare the report

  • Safety Engineer — qualified with CREA and specialized in occupational safety | must issue an ART (Technical Responsibility Note) for the report

  • Occupational Physician — qualified with CRM and specialized in occupational medicine | must issue a Medical ART or equivalent document

  • The report cannot be prepared by an Occupational Safety Technician — this professional may perform measurements and data collection, but not sign the conclusive technical report

6.2 Minimum content of the Technical Hazard Report at events

  1. Identification of the company — corporate name, CNPJ, address, CNAE and Risk Grade

  2. Identification of the responsible professional — name, professional registration, ART

  3. Detailed description of the activities and operations assessed — including positions, roles and workplaces

  4. Identification of the hazard agents present — explosives, flammables, electrical energy, physical violence — with quantitative data where applicable

  5. Assessment methodology — standards, equipment, measurements and references used

  6. Assessment result — characterization or de-characterization of the hazard, by position and by agent

  7. Existing preventive measures and their effectiveness — PPE, collective protection, operating procedures

  8. Conclusion and recommendations — with the report's validity period and conditions for review

  9. Signature and ART of the responsible professional

6.3 Retention and updating of the report

  • The hazard report must be kept by the employer for a minimum period of 20 years

  • It must be updated whenever there is a change in working conditions — new equipment, new processes, new event locations

  • Recommended review frequency: annual — or each time there is a relevant change in the nature of the exposure

  • The report must be available for consultation by the workers and by the labor inspectorate

💡  DE-CHARACTERIZATION REPORT — WHEN THE COMPANY ELIMINATES THE HAZARD

If the company adopts engineering or organizational measures that eliminate or neutralize the hazard (e.g., automation that removes the human presence from the electrical risk area, replacing flammables with non-flammable substances), a DE-CHARACTERIZATION report may be issued — and the premium ceases to be owed. De-characterization requires the same professional qualifications as characterization and the same ART. Without the de-characterization report, the company cannot simply stop paying the premium.


  07    HAZARD PREMIUM — CALCULATION, KNOCK-ON EFFECTS AND RIGHTS

Module 7 — Hazard Premium: 30% of What, for Whom and When

The hazard premium corresponds to 30% of the worker's base salary — without the additions resulting from bonuses, awards or company profit sharing. This percentage is fixed and does not vary according to the degree of hazard — unlike unhealthiness, which has three degrees (10%, 20% and 40%). NR-16 does not establish a gradation of hazard: either the activity is hazardous and generates 30%, or it is not hazardous and generates no premium.

7.1 Correct calculation base for the hazard premium

📊  HAZARD PREMIUM FORMULA

PREMIUM = 30% × BASE SALARY | The BASE SALARY is only the fixed contractual salary — it does NOT include: overtime | bonuses | production awards | 13th salary (calculated separately) | night-shift premium | unhealthiness premium | profit sharing. EXAMPLE: a sound technician with a base salary of R$ 3,500 who works as an event electrician under hazardous conditions receives: R$ 3,500 + R$ 1,050 (30% × R$ 3,500) = R$ 4,550/month in the months of exposure.


7.2 Knock-on effects of the hazard premium on other rights

7.3 Intermittent work and hazard — special rule of NR-16

Intermittent work under hazardous conditions is treated as permanent exposure for purposes of full payment of the premium in the months in which there is exposure — excluding occasional exposure, understood as a chance occurrence or something that is not part of the routine. This is a fundamental rule for the events sector:

  • An electrician hired per event who works 3 days a month on a temporary QDT: receives the full 30% premium in those months — not just proportional to the 3 days

  • An effects technician who operates LPG above 135 kg at sporadic events: if the operation is part of their professional routine (even if not monthly), it characterizes regular — not occasional — exposure

  • "Occasional exposure": is the chance occurrence or that which is NOT part of the routine — a sound technician who exceptionally and without planning helps change an LPG cylinder a single time does not characterize hazard by that isolated act


  08    NR-16 × NR-10 — INTEGRATION FOR THE EVENT ELECTRICIAN

Module 8 — From Hazard to Safety: NR-10 as Protection, NR-16 as Right

NR-16 (Annex IV) and NR-10 have an essential complementary relationship that the event safety professional needs to understand: NR-10 defines the conditions for safe work with electricity — qualifications, procedures, PPE; NR-16 defines the right to the hazard premium when work with electricity falls within the defined risk categories. The two standards do not exclude each other — they complement each other.

A specific situation of great relevance to events is the relationship between NR-10 compliance and the characterization of hazard: if the electrician performs low-voltage work WITH all the NR-10 requirements met (qualified, with procedures, with adequate PPE, in the consumption electrical system), the activity may not characterize hazard under Annex IV of NR-16. If the NR-10 requirements are NOT met, hazard is characterized. Paradoxically, compliance with NR-10 may de-characterize hazard in some situations — which does not mean that safety should be neglected in order to keep the premium.

💡  KEY MESSAGE — SAFETY FIRST, THE PREMIUM IS A CONSEQUENCE

NR-10 exists so that the electrician SURVIVES their work. The hazard premium of NR-16 exists because, even with all precautions, the risk is real and the worker must be compensated for assuming it. Never trade safety for the premium — and never neglect payment of the premium out of an unwillingness to recognize the risk.


  09    NR-16 × NR-20 — FLAMMABLES: HAZARD AND SAFETY INTEGRATED

Module 9 — Flammables at Events: NR-20 Protects, NR-16 Compensates

Just as NR-16 and NR-10 are complementary on the topic of electricity, NR-16 and NR-20 are complementary on the topic of flammables. NR-20 (Safety and Health at Work with Flammables and Fuels) defines the technical requirements for the safe storage, handling and operation of flammables — including the PEAM (Emergency Response Plan), classification of environments, safety distances and PPE. NR-16 defines who is entitled to the hazard premium for working with flammables.

9.1 When NR-20 is mandatory at events

  • Storage of fuel for generators above 250 liters of diesel or above 100 liters of gasoline — classification as a hazardous area class I or II (NR-20 / NBR 17505)

  • Use of LPG in volumes that require a fixed or semi-fixed installation — flame effects, high-power fog with a permanent gas line

  • Operation of fog machines and effect machines with flammable fluids (propane, butane) in quantities exceeding the exemption limits

  • Any fuel-based combustion or heating system in an enclosed or semi-covered area of the event

9.2 Practical integration of NR-16 + NR-20 at events

Large diesel generator (> 100 kVA)

NR-20: PEAM mandatory | Demarcated hazardous area | CO₂ extinguisher nearby | Spill-containment basin | Safety distances per NBR 5410. NR-16: Hazard report for the electrician and the generator operator | 30% premium in the months of operation | NR-10 for electrical operation.

LPG for scenic flame effects

NR-20: Operator training | Product SDS | Gas detector | Dry-chemical extinguisher | Audience distances per a specific report. NR-16: Hazard report for the pyrotechnician / operator | 30% premium if handling above 135 kg.

Diesel for refueling a generator (> 200 L per operation)

NR-20: Safe refueling procedure | Adequate PPE (nitrile gloves, goggles) | Prohibition of refueling with the generator running. NR-16: Hazard report for the refueling operator | 30% premium in the months of operation above the threshold.

Fog machines with propylene glycol fluid (non-flammable at low concentration)

NR-20: Check the flash point of the fluid — pure propylene glycol is not classified as flammable by NR-20. NR-16: No classification as a hazardous flammable for conventional propylene glycol fluid — check the specific composition of the product.


  10    CASE STUDIES AND INTEGRATION WITH OTHER NRs

Module 10 — NR-16 in Practice: Real Cases and the Ecosystem of Standards

10.1 Integration of NR-16 with other NRs of the events sector

NR-01 (GRO/PGR)

The hazard agents identified in the Technical Report must appear in the Risk Inventory of the PGR. The control measures for eliminating or neutralizing the hazard are part of the PGR Action Plan. The PGR and the Hazard Report are complementary documents.

NR-06 (PPE)

The specific PPE for hazardous activities — insulating gloves for the electrician, fire-resistant clothing for work with flammables — is defined in the Hazard Report and must be provided in accordance with NR-06 (with a valid CA and free of charge). Providing PPE does not de-characterize the hazard — it only reduces the risk.

NR-10 (Electrical)

Complementary to Annex IV of NR-16 — defines the qualification conditions, procedures and PPE for safe work with electricity. Compliance with NR-10 may influence the characterization of hazard at low voltage (SEC) as described in Module 8.

NR-20 (Flammables)

Complementary to Annex II of NR-16 — defines the safety conditions for the storage, handling and operation of flammables. The Hazard Report for flammables must consider the PEAM and the other requirements of NR-20.

NR-23 (Fire)

In risk areas involving flammables or explosives, the firefighting plan of NR-23 is an integral part of the prevention system that underpins the Hazard Report — the fire-control measures influence the assessment of the residual risk.

NR-26 (Signage)

The risk areas defined in the Hazard Report must be marked in accordance with NR-26 — "DANGER — HAZARDOUS AREA" in red, "RESTRICTED ACCESS" in blue, flammable signage with the GHS02 pictogram (flame).

NR-07 (PCMSO)

Workers in hazardous activities have specific medical examinations in the PCMSO — especially those exposed to electrical energy (cardiovascular assessment, audiometry for electricians in high-power systems) and to flammables (pulmonary function, liver tests for exposure to vapors).


10.2 Case Study 1 — Electrician without the premium on an event QDT

Scenario: A sound and lighting equipment rental company hires electricians per event — without a permanent employment relationship. The electricians connect temporary 380 V three-phase QDTs, manage 200 kVA generators and carry out the event's entire electrical installation. The company has never issued a Hazard Report — it argues that the electricians are self-employed (PJ) and that the work is at low voltage. After a tax inspection, a report is issued that characterizes the hazard for all electricians who work on the temporary QDTs. The company is ordered to pay the premium retroactively for the last 5 years + fines.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

The absence of a Hazard Report does not exempt the company from paying the premium — it only prevents the company from defending itself technically in the event of a labor lawsuit or tax assessment. Worse still: without a report, the company cannot pay the premium correctly even if it wants to. The report is the instrument that regularizes, not just that proves.


10.3 Case Study 2 — Pyrotechnician without the premium at a festival

Scenario: A professional pyrotechnician licensed by the Brazilian Army works at 12 festivals a year, hired as a PJ/MEI by each producer. He has never received the hazard premium because he is hired as self-employed. Legal advice reveals: when there is a bond of subordination, habituality and economic dependence on a main producer (even with a CNPJ), the Labor Court may recognize the employment relationship — with all labor rights, including the hazard premium retroactive for the last 5 years.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

PJ/MEI informality does not automatically remove labor rights when there is habituality and subordination. Pyrotechnicians, electricians and security staff who regularly work for the same event companies may have an employment relationship recognized in court — with the hazard premium forming part of the retroactive liability.
