⚖️  WHY NR-28 MATTERS TO THE EVENTS SECTOR

Labor inspection can take place at any event — during load-in, on show day or during load-out. Production companies, equipment rental firms and technical service companies are subject to citation for NR violations at any time. Knowing how the inspection system works, what the penalties are, how to defend against improper citations and, above all, how to prevent violations is an essential competency of every manager and OSH coordinator in the sector.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS — THE LABOR INSPECTION SYSTEM IN BRAZIL

Module 1 — Who Inspects, How It Works and What Can Happen

Regulatory Standard No. 28 — Inspection and Penalties — regulates the system for inspecting occupational safety and health conditions and the penalties applicable to companies and employers that fail to comply with the Regulatory Standards and the legal provisions of OSH. Updated by MTP Ordinance No. 2,773 of September 5, 2022, it is the standard that closes the cycle of the OSH regulatory system: while the other NRs define what must be done, NR-28 defines what happens when it is not done.

Labor inspection in Brazil is carried out by Labor Inspectors (AFT) — public servants linked to the Ministry of Labor and Employment (MTE), with powers defined by Decree No. 4,552/2002 (Labor Inspection Regulation). Labor inspectors have broad legal authority to access any workplace, at any time, without the need for prior notice or a court warrant — provided they present their official identification. This prerogative is constitutional and derives from ILO Convention No. 81 (ratified by Brazil).

For the live events sector, inspection can occur at any stage of the production: during load-in, when there are workers at height without NR-35 training; during the show, when there are electricians operating temporary distribution boards (QDTs) without NR-10 qualification; during load-out, when there is loading and unloading without adequate sanitary conditions (NR-24). The unpredictability of when the inspection will take place is an additional argument for maintaining permanent compliance — not only when "it looks like they are coming".

  02    THE INSPECTION INSTRUMENTS — WHAT THE INSPECTOR CAN DO

Module 2 — Infraction Notice, Work Stoppage and Shutdown: Practical Differences

NR-28 defines the legal instruments that the Labor Inspector uses to document and sanction violations of OSH standards. Each instrument has a different nature, deadline and consequences — and the events professional needs to know them in order to understand what happens in each situation and what their rights and obligations are once each document is drawn up.

2.1 Infraction Notice (AI)

The Infraction Notice is the instrument by which the Labor Inspector formally records the observed violation and applies the corresponding fine. It is drawn up at the site of the violation or later, after the inspection is concluded. The AI must contain: identification of the company, a precise description of the violation, the legal provision infringed (NR and specific item), date and place of the violation, name and signature of the labor inspector.

  • After the AI is drawn up: the company has 20 business days to file an administrative defense

  • No defense within the deadline: fine consolidated and forwarded for administrative collection (registration as active government debt)

  • With a defense: an administrative proceeding is opened — the company may submit documents, technical reports and evidence of compliance

  • First-instance decision: issued by the Regional Labor Office (GRT) | Appeal: 10 business days to the higher instance

  • Final administrative decision: fine upheld, reduced or annulled | If upheld: registration as active federal government debt

2.2 Notification for Compliance (NC)

The Notification for Compliance is used when the violation can be corrected — the inspector grants a deadline for the company to regularize the situation before applying the fine. It is applied when the violation does not involve serious and imminent risk and when regularization is technically feasible within the deadline granted.

  • Regularization deadline: set by the inspector according to the complexity of the required adaptation — from 30 to 360 days

  • If regularized within the deadline: no fine — the violation is considered remedied

  • If not regularized within the deadline: citation with an AI and the corresponding fine — with no further notification

  • In events: the NC is more common for documentation violations (outdated PGR, expired training, technical reports without an ART) than for operational violations of immediate risk

2.3 Work Stoppage Notice (AE) — suspension of construction or service

The Work Stoppage Notice is drawn up when the inspector finds serious and imminent risk to the safety and health of workers in a construction project or service. The work stoppage immediately suspends the execution of the affected construction or service until the risk is eliminated. It is most frequent in construction projects, but it can be applied to the assembly of event structures that constitute a construction site (NR-18).

  • Immediate effect: workers must be evacuated from the affected area immediately

  • Maintenance: the employer may perform only the services necessary to eliminate the risk — it may not resume normal production

  • Lifting of the stoppage: after the elimination of the risk is proven, the labor inspector lifts the stoppage of the construction | inspection deadline: up to 10 days after the employer's notification

  • Work stoppage fine: calculated on the value of the construction or service + fine for the original violation | non-compliance with the stoppage: crime of disobedience

2.4 Shutdown Notice (AI2) — immediate stoppage

🔴  SHUTDOWN — THE MOST SEVERE MEASURE OF NR-28

The Shutdown Notice is drawn up when the inspector finds a working condition or situation that constitutes serious and imminent risk to the life or health of the worker. The shutdown may affect: a specific sector | a machine or piece of equipment | the entire establishment. Effect: IMMEDIATE STOPPAGE of all activity at the shut-down location | Workers must be removed without loss of wages (a right guaranteed even during the stoppage). A shutdown of a show on the day of the event is the scenario with the greatest operational and financial impact for a production — and it is entirely avoidable with prior compliance.

  03    FINE AMOUNTS — TABLE OF VIOLATIONS AND SEVERITY

Module 3 — How Much Non-Compliance Costs: Table of Fines and Aggravations

NR-28 (together with MTP Ordinance No. 2,773/2022 and the ordinances updating the fine amounts) defines a table of violations classified by severity, with the corresponding fine amounts. The fines are updated periodically by the MTE through specific ordinances and indexed to the UFIR or an equivalent index. The amounts presented here are references in force in 2025 — always check the most recent updating ordinance.

💰  REFERENCE FINE AMOUNTS — NR-28 (Basis: MTE Ordinance)

MINOR SEVERITY: R$ 938.78 per violation. MEDIUM SEVERITY: R$ 1,877.57 per violation. SERIOUS SEVERITY: R$ 3,755.16 per violation. VERY SERIOUS SEVERITY: R$ 7,510.41 per violation. REPEAT OFFENSE: DOUBLE any of the amounts above. PER AFFECTED WORKER: fine multiplied by the number of workers affected by the violation. Example: a company with 50 workers without a properly constituted CIPA (NR-05) = R$ 3,755.16 × 50 = R$ 187,758.00 of potential fine.

  05    RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF THE EMPLOYER DURING INSPECTION

Module 5 — How to Conduct Yourself During a Labor Inspection

Labor inspection is an act of public authority — the Labor Inspector acts in the regular exercise of their legal function, with powers defined by Decree No. 4,552/2002 and by ILO Convention No. 81. The employer and its representatives have duties to cooperate with the inspection, but they also have rights that guarantee the right to contest and to a full defense. Knowing both sides of this relationship is essential to navigating an inspection professionally, without aggravating the situation through improper conduct.

5.1 Duties of the employer during the inspection

  • Allow the labor inspector access to any area of the workplace, at any time, upon presentation of official identification — refusing access constitutes resistance and doubles the fine

  • Provide all documents requested by the inspector: PGR, PCMSO, LTCAT, technical reports, training records, PPE records, CIPA minutes, compliance certificates

  • Provide truthful information to the inspector — false information constitutes the crime of ideological falsehood

  • Sign the Infraction Notice or expressly document the refusal — refusal to sign does not annul the citation; the inspector documents the refusal and the AI remains fully valid

  • Comply with the measures determined: evacuation of areas subject to a work stoppage or shutdown immediately after the respective notices are drawn up

5.2 Rights of the employer during and after the inspection

  • Require the inspector's official identification before any action — verify the name, registration number and Regional Labor Office of origin

  • Accompany the entire inspection personally or through a designated representative — note down all areas visited and documents consulted

  • Receive a copy of all notices drawn up — the AI, AE and AI2 must be delivered to the employer at the time they are drawn up

  • File an administrative defense within 20 business days — with documents, technical reports, photographs, testimonies and any other evidence of compliance

  • Appeal the first-instance decision within 10 business days to the higher instance of the MTE

  • Receive specialized technical support during the administrative proceeding — labor attorney, occupational safety engineer, occupational physician

5.3 What to NEVER do during an inspection

🔴  CONDUCT THAT AGGRAVATES THE SITUATION — ALWAYS AVOID

NEVER: block the inspector's access to the site | argue aggressively with the inspector | provide false or altered documents | move workers from their posts to "hide" irregularities | pressure workers not to talk to the inspector | sign documents without reading them completely | pay the fine without assessing the possibility of a defense. ALWAYS: stay calm | cooperate professionally | write everything down | consult a specialist before making any relevant decision.


  06    ADMINISTRATIVE DEFENSE — HOW TO CONTEST A CITATION

Module 6 — Administrative Defense: Your Rights after the Infraction Notice

Not every citation results in an actual fine — the administrative proceeding guarantees the employer the right to contest and to a full defense before the penalty is consolidated. A well-prepared defense, with solid technical documentation, can result in the reduction or annulment of the fine. Knowing the procedure of the labor administrative proceeding is therefore a management tool as important as the prevention of violations.

6.1 Procedure of the labor administrative proceeding — NR-28

  1. Drawing up of the Infraction Notice: the labor inspector draws up the AI on site or later | the business owner signs or registers the refusal | a copy is delivered to the employer

  2. Notification of the AI: the date on which the employer is formally notified (signature or summons) — starts the 20-business-day deadline for defense

  3. First-instance defense (Challenge): filed with the Regional Labor Office (GRT) of the jurisdiction | may include: compliance documents, technical reports, photographs of the site on the date of the inspection, workers' statements, certificates

  4. First-instance ruling: the GRT reviews the defense and decides: to uphold, reduce or annul the fine | a deadline not legally defined but usually 30 to 180 days

  5. Second-instance appeal: if the decision is unfavorable, an appeal to the Social Security Appeals Council (CRPS) or to the Undersecretariat of Labor Inspection | deadline: 10 business days from notification of the decision

  6. Final administrative decision: if the fine is upheld: registration as active federal government debt (PGFN) | if annulled: filing away | if reduced: payment of the reduced amount

  7. Tax enforcement: if the fine is not paid voluntarily after registration as active government debt: judicial enforcement | protest at a notary's office | account freezing (via SISBAJUD)

6.2 Most effective defense arguments in event citations

  • Compliance demonstrated by documents: present the PGR, technical reports, training certificates, PPE records and other documents proving that compliance existed on the date of the inspection — even if the inspector did not verify them on site

  • Factual discrepancy: demonstrate that the violation described in the AI does not correspond to the actual situation observed — with photos, videos, plans, witness statements

  • Formal nullity of the AI: an Infraction Notice with formal defects (wrong legal provision, imprecise description of the violation, incorrect identification of the company) may be annulled on grounds of formal nullity

  • Proportionality: in some situations, demonstrate that the measure applied was disproportionate to the violation — especially for small companies on a first citation

  • Regularization before the ruling: demonstrate that the irregularity was remedied immediately after the citation — this may influence the decision to reduce or cancel it in cases of non-serious violations


  07    CIVIL AND CRIMINAL LIABILITY — BEYOND THE ADMINISTRATIVE FINE

Module 7 — When the Fine Is the Least of Your Problems: Criminal and Civil Liability

The NR-28 administrative fine is just one of the possible consequences of non-compliance with the NRs. In cases of a workplace accident — especially serious ones, with permanent or fatal consequences — the employer and the responsible managers may face civil liability (compensation to victims and their families) and criminal liability (prosecution for the crimes of negligent bodily harm or involuntary manslaughter). These liabilities are independent of one another and of administrative liability: the company can pay the fine, be civilly ordered to compensate the victim AND have its partner or director prosecuted criminally — all at the same time.

7.1 Civil liability — compensation for workplace accidents

The employer is civilly liable for the harm caused to workers when the workplace accident results from negligence, recklessness or malpractice in adopting the OSH measures required by law. Civil liability may be:

  • Strict: when the employer takes on a risk-bearing activity — regardless of fault, it is liable for the harm caused (Civil Code, art. 927, sole paragraph). In events, activities such as rigging, work at height and temporary electrical installations may be classified as risk-bearing activities

  • Fault-based: when it is demonstrated that the employer acted with fault (negligence, recklessness or malpractice) — absence of PPE, lack of training, absence of collective protection

  • Quantification: material damages (medical treatment, rehabilitation, loss of income) + moral damages (suffering, lasting consequences) + lost profits + a lifelong monthly pension in the event of death or permanent disability

  • Typical amounts: in a fatal accident, compensation can exceed R$ 500,000 to R$ 1,500,000 depending on the worker's income, their age and the degree of the employer's fault

⚠️  CAUSAL NEXUS — THE KEY TO CIVIL LIABILITY

The employer's civil liability for a workplace accident requires the demonstration of the causal nexus between the employer's conduct (action or omission) and the harm suffered by the worker. Non-compliance with the NRs is one of the most powerful pieces of evidence of a causal nexus: if the worker fell from height without PPE and without NR-35 training — both required by law and not provided — the causal nexus between the employer's omission and the accident is practically irrefutable.


7.2 Criminal liability — the OSH crimes

Non-compliance with OSH standards that results in serious bodily harm or the death of a worker may constitute the crimes provided for in the Penal Code:

Crime (Penal Code)

Description

Sentence

Typical Situation in Events

Negligent bodily harm (PC art. 129 §6)

Bodily harm caused by negligence, recklessness or malpractice — without intent to cause harm

Detention of 2 months to 1 year (simple negligence) | Detention of 2 to 8 years if the injury is serious or very serious (fault + serious result)

A worker who fractures a vertebra falling from a stage structure without PPE | An electrician with serious burns from a shock in an irregular installation

Involuntary manslaughter (PC art. 121 §3)

Death caused by negligence, recklessness or malpractice — without intent to kill

Detention of 1 to 3 years | Sentence increased by 1/3 to 2/3 if the crime results from the failure to observe a technical rule of the profession (art. 121 §4 — especially relevant to OSH)

A rigger who dies falling from a grid without protection | An electrician electrocuted at a QDT without NR-10 qualification | A worker crushed by a structure that collapses due to incorrect assembly

Endangerment of life (PC art. 132)

Exposing the life or health of another to direct and imminent danger through the violation of a safety rule

Detention of 3 months to 1 year

Keeping workers at height with a recognized serious and imminent risk without taking any protective measure


Important: the sentence is applied to the PERSON RESPONSIBLE for the non-compliance with the OSH standards — which may be the managing partner, the operations director, the OSH coordinator or any individual who had decision-making power over the working conditions that led to the accident. The company as a legal entity may be held civilly liable; the individual who decided on non-compliance is held criminally liable.


  08    LIABILITY IN THE CONTRACTING CHAIN — THE SERVICE RECIPIENT

Module 8 — Who Is Responsible at the Event: Producer, Contractor and Venue

The live events sector has a chain contracting structure that is particularly relevant to NR-28: the production company hires the sound rental company, which hires the technicians; the production company hires the rigging company, which hires the riggers; the venue hires the security company, which hires the guards. Who is responsible for compliance with the NRs when a worker from a contracted company suffers an accident?

NR-01 (GRO/PGR) and consolidated labor case law establish that the contracting company (the service recipient) has joint or subsidiary liability for compliance with the NRs in relation to the workers who provide services on its premises or under its direction — regardless of whether the formal employment relationship is with the contracted company. For the events sector, this means that the executive production company is responsible for the safety of ALL workers at the event site — including those of outsourced companies.

💡  RECOMMENDED PRACTICE — AN OSH PACKAGE AS A HIRING REQUIREMENT

The most effective way to protect the production company from liability for the OSH violations of its contractors is to make OSH documentation a mandatory part of the proposal and the contract. Before hiring any company for an event: require an updated PGR, a PCMSO, a list of valid training for the workers who will work at the event, and a workplace accident insurance policy. Include in the contract an OSH liability clause and the right to terminate should the contractor fail to comply with the NRs during the event.


  09    WORK STOPPAGE AND SHUTDOWN IN EVENTS — HOW TO AVOID THE WORST-CASE SCENARIO

Module 9 — Work Stoppage and Shutdown: the Nightmare That Is Entirely Avoidable

A work stoppage and a shutdown on event day represent the scenario with the greatest financial and operational impact that a production can face over OSH issues. A labor inspector who arrives at the festival load-in and finds: workers at height without NR-35 training, a temporary electrical installation without an NR-10-qualified electrician, a stage structure without an engineer's ART — has the legal authority to stop the load-in or shut down the sector, preventing the event from taking place.

This scenario, although rare, occurs in Brazil frequently enough to be treated as a real planning risk — especially after serious, high-profile accidents that increase the inspection focus on the events sector. The Kiss case (2013) dramatically accelerated the MTE's inspection action over live events — and Law 13,425/2017 (the Kiss Law) created additional legal obligations that are the subject of specific inspection.

9.1 Situations that typically trigger a work stoppage or shutdown in events

  • Workers doing work at height (rigging, grid assembly, PA installation) with NO NR-35 training — immediate serious and imminent risk

  • High-power temporary electrical installation performed by workers not qualified under NR-10 — risk of electric shock and fire

  • Temporary structure (stage, grandstand, PA tower) without an engineer's ART — impossibility of verifying structural safety

  • Absence of emergency exits adequate for the expected number of people — risk of a stampede and panic in an emergency

  • A flagrant situation of imminent risk: a compromised structure, an electrical installation with a short-circuit risk in a public area

  10    CASE STUDIES AND FINAL INTEGRATION

Module 10 — Real Cases and the Integrated View of the Inspection System

10.1 Case Study 1 — Citation during a festival load-in

Scenario: An MTE labor inspector arrives at the load-in of a large festival at 8 a.m., before the start of the show (which would take place at 2 p.m.). He identifies: 12 workers on the lighting grid at 12 meters of height — 7 of them without a valid NR-35 certificate; an electrician without NR-10 qualification connecting a 380V QDT; the absence of an engineer's ART for the stage structure. He draws up: 3 Infraction Notices (one for each violation); a Shutdown Notice for the work-at-height area due to serious and imminent risk; a Work Stoppage Notice for the electrical services.

Consequences: load-in halted for 6 hours while the production company sought: 5 new riggers with a valid NR-35 to replace the irregular ones, an NR-10-qualified electrician to take over the work, an emergency engineer's ART for the structure. Show delayed by 2 hours. Total fine: R$ 3,755.16 × 3 violations × 12 affected workers = approximately R$ 135,186. Estimated operational loss: R$ 80,000 in extra contracts + loss of audience due to the delay.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

R$ 135,186 in fines + R$ 80,000 in operational loss = R$ 215,186. Cost of prior compliance: NR-35 and NR-10 training for the entire team = approximately R$ 8,000 to R$ 15,000 per year. Compliance is exponentially cheaper than non-compliance — even considering only the financial aspect, without counting the lives.


10.2 Case Study 2 — Death of a worker and the producer's criminal liability

Scenario: A lighting technician falls from a grid at 8 meters during the load-in of an indoor show. The investigation reveals: a worker without NR-35 training | without fall-protection PPE | without an APR and PT issued | the executive producer knew of the irregularities but had chosen not to invest in the training to reduce costs. The worker dies in the hospital 3 days after the accident.

Consequences: (1) MTE Infraction Notice — very serious fine of R$ 7,510.41 × aggravating factors = approximately R$ 75,000. (2) Civil action: the worker's family (wife + 2 minor children) obtains compensation of R$ 850,000 (material + moral damages + a monthly pension for the children until age 25). (3) Criminal proceeding: the executive producer indicted for involuntary manslaughter with a factor of increase (failure to observe a technical rule — PC art. 121 §3 and §4) — a sentence of 1 year and 4 months to 4 years, in an open regime, with the possibility of substitution by penalties restricting rights.

🔴  LESSON FROM CASE 2

The decision to save R$ 800 on training and PPE resulted in: R$ 75,000 in fines + R$ 850,000 in civil compensation + a criminal proceeding against the producer. No contract, no show and no cost reduction justifies the risk of being held criminally liable for the death of a worker.

TECHNICAL COURSEBOOK

NR-26

Safety Signaling and Identification

COLORS · NBR 7195 · GHS · LABELING · SDS · CHEMICAL PRODUCTS AT EVENTS


Module focused on the Live Events Sector

Workload: 4 hours  |  Level: Operational

In accordance with NR-26 (MTP Ordinance No. 2,770/2022) · NBR 7195 · ABNT NBR 14725 · GHS/UN

A color in the right place saves those who do not know where the danger is.

  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-26 IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 1 — What NR-26 is and why it matters to those who set up events

Regulatory Standard No. 26 — Safety Signaling and Identification — establishes the measures relating to safety signaling and identification to be adopted in workplaces. Originally published in 1978 and updated by MTP Ordinance No. 2,770/2022, it is the standard that defines the visual language of workplace safety: the colors, labels, and data sheets that communicate risks to any worker, regardless of their level of education or previous experience.

NR-26 operates along two complementary axes: (I) color signaling, which indicates risks, delimits areas, and identifies safety equipment according to the NBR 7195 standard; and (II) the identification of chemical products, which classifies, labels, and documents the risks of hazardous substances according to the UN GHS. Both axes have direct application in the events sector — which uses diesel, CO2, pyrotechnics, fog/haze fluids, isopropyl alcohol, propane for fire machines, and scenography solvents.

The fundamental principle of NR-26 is that color signaling does not replace other forms of accident prevention — PPE, training, engineering measures. It works as an additional layer of protection: a worker who has never been at that location can instantly identify where the fire extinguishers are (red), where the use of PPE is mandatory (blue), where there is a fall risk (yellow/black), and where the emergency exit is (green).


Legal basis

NR-26 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Latest update: MTP Ordinance No. 2,770/2022

Structure (2022)

3 chapters: (I) Color signaling | (II) Chemical product identification | (III) Information and training

Technical color standard

ABNT NBR 7195 — Safety colors | ABNT NBR 6493 — Colors for piping

Chemical product system

GHS — UN Globally Harmonized System | ABNT NBR 14725 (SDS/MSDS)

Number of colors

8 safety colors: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple, white, and black

Mandatory training

Preventive labeling, SDS, specific hazards, and emergency procedures with chemical products

SDS / MSDS

Safety Data Sheet — mandatory for every hazardous chemical product | available to all workers

Fundamental principle

Color signaling does NOT waive PPE, training, and engineering measures


ATTENTION — NR-26, ITEM 26.1.4

The use of colors must be as REDUCED as possible, in order not to cause distraction, confusion, and fatigue to the worker. At events, where the artistic production creates a profusion of colors and light effects, safety signaling must be especially clear and standardized — never confused with decorative or scenography elements.


  02    THE 8 SAFETY COLORS — NBR 7195 APPLIED TO EVENTS

Module 2 — The Language of Colors: what each one Communicates

NBR 7195 — Safety Colors — is the technical standard that details how to technically implement what NR-26 requires. It defines 8 safety colors with standardized meanings and specific applications. Standardization is what makes signaling effective: a rigger who works in São Paulo and goes to an event in Manaus finds the fire extinguisher in red, the emergency exit in green, and the PPE obligation in blue — in every location, always.


COLOR

MEANING

GENERAL APPLICATIONS - NBR 7195

APPLICATION AT LIVE EVENTS

RED

Fire fighting and prohibition

Extinguishers and their location; hydrants; alarm boxes; sprinklers; emergency exit doors; emergency stop buttons; barricades; acetylene hose

CO2 extinguisher near the PDB and generator; extinguisher location in the dimmer rack area; stop button of the lifting platform; backstage emergency exit

ORANGE

Danger — moving parts

Moving parts of machines and equipment; inner faces of electrical device boxes; aquatic rescue equipment (buoys, vests)

Interior panel of the PDB (electrical distribution) — faces with energized components; danger zone of the stage lifting platform; signaling of electric hoists in motion

YELLOW

Caution / Attention

Handrails, parapets, floors with risk; edges of elevated stages; forklift and tractor areas; portable ladders; areas around fire equipment

Yellow/black striped bands on the stage edge (fall risk); signaling of level differences in recesses; forklift traffic areas at the loading dock; steps of a metal staircase

GREEN

First-aid boxes; stretchers; emergency exits; assembly point; location guides; water piping

Event assembly point; first-aid post; backstage emergency exit signaling; location of the first-aid box

BLUE

ATTENTION — RED DOES NOT MEAN DANGER IN NBR 7195

This is the most common error in signaling: using red to indicate generic danger. The color red in NBR 7195 is EXCLUSIVE to fire fighting and prohibition/emergency stop. To indicate danger and risk alerts, the correct color is YELLOW (caution) or ORANGE (danger of moving parts). Using red for everything dilutes its meaning and can confuse workers in real emergencies.


  03    INTEGRATED SIGNALING — NR-26 AND THE OTHER NRs AT EVENTS

Module 3 — How NR-26 Integrates with the Other NRs at the Event

NR-26 does not operate in isolation within an event’s safety management system — it is the visual layer that materializes the signaling requirements of all the other NRs. NR-12 requires signaling of danger zones on machines and equipment: NR-26 defines which colors to use. NR-35 requires isolation of the area below work at height: NR-26 defines that this signaling must use yellow/black. NR-10 requires signaling of electrical distribution boards: NR-26 defines the colors for each risk.

3.1 NR-26 as the communication layer of the other NRs

Related NR

NR-10 (Electrical)

Signal electrical risk zones, energized panels, and parts with dangerous voltage

Orange: interior of PDBs and dimmers | Red: emergency stop | Yellow: electrical attention areas

  04    CHEMICAL PRODUCTS AT EVENTS — GHS CLASSIFICATION

Module 4 — Chemical Products: Classify to Protect

The live events sector uses a variety of chemical products that are rarely recognized as such by workers. Diesel for generators, CO2 in cylinders for stage effects, fog machine fluid, haze fluid, isopropyl alcohol for cleaning, propane for fire machines, and pyrotechnics are all chemical products that NR-26 requires to be correctly classified, labeled, and accompanied by a Safety Data Sheet (SDS).

The Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals (GHS) — developed by the UN and adopted in Brazil by NR-26 and by ABNT NBR 14725 — standardizes worldwide how the risks of chemical products are communicated. The GHS uses 9 standardized pictograms that communicate the hazard class regardless of the manufacturer’s language — especially relevant for imported products used at events.

4.1 The 9 GHS pictograms and application at events

GHS Pictogram

Hazard Class

Examples at Live Events

GHS01 — Explosive

Unstable explosives, pyrotechnic mixtures, organic peroxides

Show pyrotechnics: pyrotechnic confetti shots, sparkular, low-altitude fireworks

GHS02 — Flammable

Flammable liquids and solids, flammable gases, self-reactives

Generator diesel, gasoline, isopropyl alcohol, cleaning sprays, propane for fire machines

GHS03 — Oxidizer

Oxidizing substances that intensify fires

Pyrotechnic materials with oxidizers; certain concentrated cleaning products

GHS04 — Pressurized Gas

Gases under pressure, refrigerated liquids, dissolved gases

CO2 cylinders for fog effect and sparkular; nitrogen cylinders for pneumatic effects

GHS05 — Corrosive

Substances that cause corrosion in metals or burns on the skin

Concentrated cleaning solutions; UPS batteries (sulfuric acid)

GHS06 — Acute Toxicity

Substances with high acute toxicity — fatal by inhalation or contact

Pyrotechnics with heavy-metal compounds; generator maintenance products

GHS07 — Harmful / Irritant

Harmful, irritant, skin-sensitizing substances

Fog machine fluids (glycol); haze fluids; projector lens cleaning sprays

GHS08 — Health Hazard

Carcinogens, mutagens, target-organ toxicants, respiratory sensitizers

Scenography solvents in prolonged use; diesel generator emissions in enclosed spaces

GHS09 — Environmental Hazard

Substances hazardous to the aquatic or terrestrial environment

Hydraulic fluids of stage equipment; generator oil; UPS batteries disposed of improperly


  05    PREVENTIVE LABELING AND SDS — NR-26 OBLIGATIONS

Module 5 — Label and SDS: the Safety Identity of the Chemical Product

Preventive labeling is the set of visual and textual elements on the packaging of a chemical product classified as hazardous. It is the first source of safety information available to the worker who handles the product. The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is the technical document structured into 16 sections standardized by the GHS that consolidates all the safety information of a chemical product.

5.1 Mandatory elements of the GHS/NR-26 label

Element

Description

Example in an event product

Product identification

Name, composition, manufacturer with emergency contact

Fog fluid: SMOKE MACHINE FLUID — Propylene glycol 60% / Glycerin 40% — Manufacturer: [name] — Tel: 0800-XXX

Hazard pictogram(s)

GHS symbol in a red and white diamond indicating the hazard class(es)

GHS02 (flammable) + GHS07 (harmful) in a heated glycol-based fog fluid

Signal word

DANGER (most serious categories) or CAUTION (less serious) — never both

CO2 cylinder: CAUTION (gas under pressure, risk of oxygen displacement in enclosed spaces)

Hazard statements (H)

Standardized codes and statements describing the nature and degree of the hazard

Cleaning spray: H222 Extremely flammable aerosol + H319 Causes serious eye irritation

Precautionary statements (P)

Recommended measures to minimize or prevent harmful effects

P210 Keep away from heat/spark/flame + P260 Do not inhale vapors + P501 Dispose of according to local regulations

Supplementary information

Additional manufacturer information not covered by the GHS elements

Storage temperature; shelf life after opening; compatibility with surfaces


PRODUCT TRANSFERRED TO A SMALLER CONTAINER — LABEL MANDATORY

Common practice at events: transferring diesel into smaller cans to refuel the generator; diluting fog fluid into small bottles. Any product transferred to a container different from the original packaging must have a label with: product name, applicable GHS pictograms, signal word, and main hazard statement. A chemical product container without identification is a direct violation of NR-26.


5.2 The 16 mandatory sections of the SDS according to GHS

  1. Identification of the product and the company/supplier

  2. Hazard identification — pictograms, signal word, H statements

  3. Composition and information on the components

  4. First-aid measures

  5. Fire-fighting measures — suitable extinguishers and procedures

  6. Control measures for spillage or leakage

  7. Handling and storage

  8. Exposure controls and personal protection — recommended PPE

  9. Physical-chemical properties — flash point, vapor pressure, etc.

  10. Stability and reactivity — conditions to avoid, incompatibilities

  11. Toxicological information

  12. Ecological information

  13. Disposal considerations

  14. Transport information

  15. Regulatory information

  16. Other information — date of preparation, revision


  06    TRAINING AND INTEGRATION WITH THE RMP

Module 6 — Training, Case Study, and Integration with the RMP

NR-26 (item 26.2.4) determines that workers who handle chemical products must receive mandatory specific training. The employer is responsible for ensuring that the training takes place and for documenting that the workers received it.

6.1 Minimum content of NR-26 training

  • Understanding of GHS preventive labeling: pictograms, signal words (DANGER vs CAUTION), H statements, and P statements

  • How to locate and interpret the SDS of each product used at the event — which sections to consult in each situation (emergency, storage, disposal)

  • The specific hazards of the products the worker handles: fire risk of diesel, asphyxiation risk of CO2 in an enclosed space, respiratory risk of fog fluid

  • Preventive measures for safe use: adequate ventilation, storage distances, product-specific PPE

  • Procedures for acting in emergencies: spillage, leakage, accidental inhalation, fire with a chemical product


SDS ACCESS IS MANDATORY AT THE WORKPLACE

Item 26.2.3 of NR-26 determines that the organization must ensure that workers have access to the SDS of the chemical products used. At events, where workers handle diesel, CO2, fog fluid, and other products without ever seeing the safety documentation, the producer or company responsible for the setup is in direct non-compliance with NR-26. The SDS must be available on site — not only at the company office.


6.2 Case Study — Accident Due to Absent Signaling at an Event

Scenario: Large-scale festival, outdoor area. Diesel refueling is done from a 200-liter drum stored near the generator, with no GHS identification on the container and no SDS available on site. A sound technician, during the nighttime teardown, mistakes the diesel drum for a drum of demineralized water (both in metal drums without a label). He uses the diesel to clean the face of a loudspeaker near a heated dimmer rack. Start of a fire — controlled by the CO2 extinguisher, but with damage to the equipment and a minor burn to the technician.

Analysis: The root cause of the accident is the absence of GHS labeling on the diesel drum — a direct violation of NR-26. If the product had been correctly identified with the GHS02 pictogram (flammable) and the word DANGER, the technician would not have mistaken it for demineralized water. The diesel SDS (section 7 — handling) has an explicit instruction to keep away from heat sources.

LESSON FROM THE CASE

Every chemical product at an event — diesel, CO2, fog fluid, alcohol, propane — must be in its original packaging with an intact and legible GHS label, or in a secondary container identified with at least the product name, GHS pictogram, and signal word. The SDS must be available in the work area in physical form or in a digital format accessible without internet.


6.3 Integration of NR-26 into the RMP

  • Risk inventory: the identified risks must have the NR-26 signaling measures listed as an administrative control

  • Chemical product inventory: every chemical product registered with GHS classification, required PPE, and SDS location

  • Action plan: implementation or update of the event signaling with a responsible party and a deadline

  • Training: NR-26 training recorded in the RMP with an attendance list and programmatic content

📋  MANDATORY REQUIREMENT — WHO NEEDS NR-10?

NR-10 is mandatory for ANY worker who interacts directly or indirectly with electrical installations — including sound technicians who connect cables into distribution boards, lighting technicians who plug in dimmers and fixtures, generator operators, roadies who run power cables, and all stage builders who work on structures with electrical equipment. In the events sector, the list is vast — and the absence of training is a direct violation of NR-10.

  01    INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL SAFETY — NR-10

Module 1 — Why Electricity Kills and Why NR-10 Exists

Regulatory Standard No. 10 — Safety in Electrical Installations and Services — establishes the minimum requirements and conditions for implementing control measures and preventive systems, ensuring the safety and health of workers who, directly or indirectly, interact with electrical installations and services involving electricity. Originally published by MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 and completely overhauled by GM Ordinance No. 598 of December 7, 2004, it is one of the standards with the greatest impact on preventing fatal accidents in Brazil.

NR-10 applies to all phases of the generation, transmission, distribution and consumption of electric power — including the design, construction, assembly, operation and maintenance stages, and any work carried out in the vicinity of electrical installations. In the live events sector, where temporary electrical installations are set up and dismantled within hours, often under production pressure, often by workers without formal training, NR-10 is the standard that separates a safe operation from a foreseeable tragedy.

The events sector relies heavily on temporary electrical installations: sound systems that draw from 50kW to 500kW, lighting systems of 100kW to 300kW at large shows, video and LED systems, generators from 100kVA to 500kVA, temporary distribution boards, and cabling running hundreds of meters. These installations are set up by crews with limited time, in locations ranging from industrial warehouses to open squares, in unpredictable weather conditions — and often by workers who have never received formal training in electrical safety.

Legal basis

NR-10 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978 | Overhaul: GM Ordinance No. 598/2004

Scope of application

All phases of the generation, transmission, distribution and consumption of electric power — with no exception by sector

Basic Course (NR-10)

40 hours (24h theoretical + 16h practical) | Validity: 2 years | For low voltage (up to 1,000V AC)

SEP Course

Additional 40 hours | For work on electric power systems (medium and high voltage)

Authorized Worker

Qualified/licensed with the company's formal consent after NR-10 training — categorization required by the standard

Trained Worker

Received training under the guidance of a licensed professional — works under supervision

Without training

The worker may NOT interact with electrical installations — a direct violation of NR-10

Complementary standard

NBR 5410 (LV installations) + NBR 14039 (MV installations) + NBR 5419 (LPS) + NBR IEC 61439 (temporary distribution boards)


  02    FUNDAMENTALS OF ELECTRICITY — WHAT KILLS AND WHY

Module 2 — Electricity: Fundamental Concepts Applied to Safety

Understanding the basic concepts of electricity is not an academic exercise — it is survival knowledge for the events worker. Knowing why it is current that kills, not voltage; understanding why a wet worker is at ten times greater risk than a dry worker; and grasping why a short circuit in an undersized cable can start a silent fire are the kinds of knowledge that drive safe behavior in the field.

2.1 The Three Pillars of Electricity — Ohm's Law

The relationship between voltage, current and resistance is described by Ohm's Law: I = V / R (Current = Voltage / Resistance). This equation has direct implications for electrical safety: the current that flows through the human body during a shock is determined by the voltage the body is exposed to divided by the body's resistance.

Quantity

Symbol/Unit

Technical Definition

Relevance in Events

Electric Voltage

V — Volt

Potential difference between two points — the "force" that pushes the electrons

Voltages at events: 127V (residual single-phase) | 220V (industrial single-phase) | 380V/440V (three-phase for large systems)

Electric Current (Amperage)

A — Ampere

Flow of electrons per unit of time. It is the current — not the voltage — that kills

1mA: tingling | 10mA: muscle contraction | 50mA: fibrillation | 100mA+: cardiac arrest

Electric Resistance

Ω — Ohm

Opposition to the flow of current. Human body: 100,000Ω dry → 1,000Ω wet

Wet skin (rain, sweat) reduces resistance by 100x — multiplying the current by 100x for the same voltage potential

Electric Power

W — Watt

P = V × I. Rate of energy consumption

Critical sizing: 200kW PA system + 150kW lighting + 50kW video = 400kW total demand — undersizing causes fire


🔴  OHM'S LAW APPLIED TO ELECTRIC SHOCK

Practical example: A technician with sweat-soaked skin (R = 1,000 Ω) touches a 220V terminal. Current = 220V / 1,000 Ω = 0.22 A = 220 mA. This current is more than 4 times the cardiac-arrest threshold (50 mA). Conclusion: in humid conditions, even 220V can be instantly lethal. Outdoor events in the rain represent an electrical risk of the critical category.


2.2 Effects of Electric Current on the Human Body

Current (mA)

Effect on the Human Body

Risk to Life

Typical Situation at Events

  03    ELECTRICAL HAZARDS AT EVENTS — COMPLETE TYPOLOGY

Module 3 — The Six Electrical Hazards the Events Professional Must Know

NR-10 identifies the main electrical hazards that must be prevented in installations and services involving electricity. In the live events sector, these hazards present specific characteristics that amplify their danger: the temporary nature of the installations, deadline pressure, the variety of venues and the multiplicity of simultaneous crews.

3.1 Electric Shock

Electric shock is the passage of electric current through the human body, resulting from contact with energized parts. It can occur through direct contact (touching an energized conductor) or indirect contact (touching a metal surface that has become energized due to an insulation fault). At events, the most frequent shock situations are: touching industrial plugs with an exposed energized pin, contact with cables whose insulation is compromised (cracked, cut, worn), touching the casings of equipment with insulation faults and absent grounding, and handling connectors in the rain.

3.2 Electric Arc

An electric arc is a high-intensity electrical discharge through the air, between two conductors or between a conductor and the ground. It produces temperatures that can exceed 20,000°C (four times the temperature of the sun's surface), intense ultraviolet light (which causes instant blindness), an acoustic pressure wave (an explosion that can throw the worker meters away), and the projection of molten metal particles that cause severe burns even at distances of several meters.

At events, an electric arc can occur when: connecting or disconnecting power cables under load ("hot" connection); when a short circuit occurs in poorly sized distribution boards; or when metal tools fall onto energized busbars. It is the most serious, most unexpected and most frequently underestimated hazard in the sector.

🔴  ELECTRIC ARC — THE MOST UNDERESTIMATED HAZARD AT EVENTS

At events, the practice of "connecting hot" (plugging or unplugging power cables with the circuit energized) is routine and normalized. This practice exposes the worker to the risk of an electric arc every time it is performed. The correct solution is: always switch off the circuit breaker before any connection or disconnection of a power cable. This operation takes 10 seconds. The electric arc takes milliseconds to cause permanent injuries.


3.3 Electrical Burns

Electrical burns occur in two distinct modes: contact burns (at the entry and exit points of the current through the body, caused by the heating of tissue along the path of the electric current) and electric-arc burns (caused by thermal radiation and the ionized gases of the arc, which can reach large areas even without direct contact with the conductor). Internal contact burns are frequently underestimated because the surface injury may be small while the deep tissues — muscles, nerves, blood vessels — are extensively damaged.

3.4 Falls Caused by Shock

An electric shock can cause involuntary muscle contraction or loss of consciousness — in either case, the result can be a fall of the worker. At events, where work at height is routine (rigging assembly at 12 meters, installation of lighting fixtures on elevated structures, positioning of speaker cabinets on PA towers), the combination of electrical risk and fall risk is particularly dangerous. NR-10 and NR-35 must be applied simultaneously in these situations.

3.5 Fire and Explosion of Electrical Origin

Short circuits, overloads and poor contact are the three main causes of fires of electrical origin. At events, the risk is amplified by: undersized cables (which heat up progressively until the insulation melts), makeshift splices with insulating tape (which create high-resistance points that generate heat), overloading of residential extension cords with industrial loads, and proximity to combustible materials (stage curtains, carpets, wooden scenery, tent tarps).

3.6 Electromagnetic Field

Electromagnetic fields are generated by high-power electrical equipment and by radio-frequency transmission systems. At events with large PA systems (amplifiers of 50kW+) and RF transmitters for microphone and wireless in-ear systems, exposure can be relevant for workers with cardiac pacemakers or metal implants. The technical producer must identify workers with implants before positioning them in zones of high electromagnetic exposure.


  04    ELECTRICAL RISK CONTROL MEASURES — NR-10 HIERARCHY

Module 4 — Control Before You Fight: the Hierarchy of Protection

NR-10 establishes a hierarchy of electrical-risk control measures that must be followed in the order of priority indicated. The logic is the same as that of the risk management system of NR-01: eliminate or control the risk at the source before resorting to individual protection. De-energization is always the first choice — and is frequently possible in the events sector with a minimum of planning.

4.1 De-energization — The Priority Measure

De-energization is the process of switching off the electrical installation and ensuring it remains without power throughout the entire period of the work. NR-10 is clear: whenever possible, work must be carried out with the installation de-energized. In the events sector, de-energization is always possible during setup — the electrical installation can be built, tested and inspected with the circuits switched off before any general energization.

Mandatory de-energization sequence (6 NR-10 steps):

  1. SECTIONING: Switch off the circuit at the switching devices (circuit breakers, disconnect switches) — not just at the equipment's button

  2. PREVENTION OF RE-ENERGIZATION: Physically lock the switching device with a padlock (LOTO) and an identification tag — each worker applies their own padlock

  3. VERIFICATION OF ABSENCE OF VOLTAGE: Use a calibrated voltage detector on ALL phases and at all work points — never assume it is switched off

  4. TEMPORARY GROUNDING with equipotential bonding of the conductors — drains residual energy and capacitive charges

  5. PROTECTION of the energized elements existing in the work zone — barriers, insulating covers, signaling tapes

  6. SIGNALING to prevent re-energization — visible sign: "DO NOT SWITCH ON — WORK IN PROGRESS — [name/date]"


4.2 Protective Grounding — The Invisible Foundation of Electrical Safety

Protective grounding is the intentional connection of the casings and metal parts of equipment and structures to the earth, in order to drain leakage currents and limit the touch voltage to safe values (below 50V). At events, grounding is the most critical collective protection measure and the most frequently absent or incorrectly executed.

The rule is absolute: every metal event structure (stages, trusses, PA towers, barriers, walkways) that receives electrical equipment MUST be grounded to the event's earthing system. And every earthing system must have its resistance measured — the maximum value allowed by NBR 5410 for protection systems is 10 Ohms.

4.3 Residual Current Device (RCD) — The Device That Saves Lives

The Residual Current Device (RCD) continuously monitors the difference in current between the line and neutral conductors of a circuit. When this difference exceeds the device's sensitivity (typically 30 mA for protection of people), the RCD switches off the circuit in less than 40 milliseconds — before the current can cause ventricular fibrillation. The RCD is the only protective device that acts specifically against the electric shock that occurs when a person comes into contact with an energized conductor.

🔴  ABSOLUTE RULE — RCD AT EVENTS

Every circuit that supplies equipment accessible to workers or the public at events MUST have RCD protection of no more than 30mA. This requirement comes from NBR 5410 and is reinforced by NR-10. There is no technical, operational or financial justification that authorizes the absence of an RCD at events with workers. The absence of an RCD on a circuit that causes death by electric shock is evidence of criminal negligence.


4.4 Extra-Low Voltage (ELV) — Eliminating the Risk Through Voltage

Extra-low voltage (ELV) — up to 50V in alternating current (AC) or 120V in direct current (DC) — reduces the electrical risk to manageable levels for most exposures. At events, the use of low-voltage LED lighting systems (12V or 24V DC), isolated power supplies for stage equipment and battery-powered communication systems are examples of applying the ELV principle. Where technically feasible, designing installations in ELV is the most robust protection measure.


  05    TECHNICAL STANDARDS — NBR 5410, NBR 14039 AND RELATED STANDARDS

Module 5 — The Technical Framework of a Quality Electrical Installation

The ABNT technical standards complement and technically detail the legal requirements of NR-10. NR-10 establishes the principles and minimum safety requirements; the technical standards define how to technically implement those principles in specific situations. For the events sector, the most relevant set of standards forms a complete technical framework that defines the minimum quality standard of a safe temporary electrical installation.

NBR 5410

Low-voltage electrical installations (up to 1,000V AC / 1,500V DC). Defines requirements for design, execution, verification and maintenance. Every temporary low-voltage event installation must comply with NBR 5410 in full.

NBR 14039

Medium-voltage electrical installations (1kV to 36.2kV). Applicable to large events with direct medium-voltage supply from the utility. Requires the complementary 40-hour SEP course for work at this voltage level.

NBR 5419

Protection of structures against lightning (LPS — lightning protection system). Mandatory for outdoor events with tall metal structures (stages, towers, roofs). The LPS protects the structure and the workers in the event of a direct strike.

NBR IEC 61439

Low-voltage switchgear and controlgear assemblies — temporary distribution boards. Defines requirements for the construction, verification and marking of the electrical boards used at events.

NBR IEC 60079

Electrical equipment for potentially explosive atmospheres. Relevant when there is pyrotechnics, special effects with gas, or when generators operate near flammable gases.

NBR ISO/IEC 80079

Explosion protection — classification of areas and selection of equipment. Defines the explosion-risk zones and the requirements for equipment used in those areas.


💡  CABLE SIZING — THE MOST COMMON MISTAKE AT EVENTS

Undersizing of conductors is the number-one cause of fires of electrical origin at events. A cable with a cross-section of 2.5mm² supports up to 21A continuous; used with a 40A load, it heats up progressively until the insulation melts — which can take hours before the fire starts. The correct sizing per NBR 5410 is: calculate the real current of the circuit + temperature correction factor + cable grouping factor. At events, always err on the side of the larger cross-section.


  06    CLASSIFICATION OF WORKERS AND RISK ZONES

Module 6 — Who Can Do What: Qualification and Authorization in NR-10

NR-10 establishes a formal classification of workers according to their level of competence and authorization to interact with electrical installations. This classification is not merely administrative — it legally defines each worker's responsibility in the event of an accident and determines which activities each level of competence is authorized to perform.

6.1 Classification of Workers Under NR-10

⚠️  MOST EVENT TECHNICIANS — CURRENT SITUATION

Most sound and lighting technicians who connect cables into distribution boards, plug in dimmers and operate generators at live events qualify as a trained worker at most — or, more often, hold no valid categorization at all. NR-10 is explicit: ANY interaction with an electrical installation requires at least the categorization of Trained Worker with documented training. The 40-hour NR-10 Basic Course is the minimum for the categorization of Authorized Worker.

  07    CPE AND PPE FOR ELECTRICAL SERVICES AT EVENTS

Modules 7 and 8 — Collective and Individual Protection: the Electrician's Shield

The protection hierarchy of NR-10 places CPE before PPE: collective protections that eliminate or reduce the risk for all workers in the area take priority over individual protections that protect only the worker who uses them. At events, the correct implementation of this hierarchy requires planning the electrical installation with a safety mindset from the design stage.

7.1 CPE — Collective Protective Equipment

  • Safety barriers and signaling to isolate areas with exposed electrical installations — cones, tapes, trestles

  • Insulating rubber mats and blankets to be placed in front of open electrical boards during maintenance

  • Insulating covers to protect exposed energized parts during work in controlled zones

  • Temporary grounding systems with clamps and copper cables — sized for the short-circuit current of the circuit

  • Voltage detectors — proximity and contact — for verifying the absence of voltage before any intervention

  • Lockout and tagout systems (LOTO) — padlocks, lockout hasps, identification tags and lockout boxes

  • 30mA residual current devices (RCDs) — installed in the distribution board — collective protection for all users of the circuit

  • Safety signaling per NR-26 — signs reading "DANGER — ELECTRICAL HAZARD", "DO NOT SWITCH ON — WORKING", "RESTRICTED ACCESS"

8.1 Electrical PPE — Specific Requirements

Test before each use: inflate to 1/3 of capacity, check for the absence of holes. Never use if damaged. Store protected from sun and heat.

Check the integrity of the insulation before each use. Never use common ferrous tools for electrical work.


⚠️  ABSOLUTE PROHIBITION — METAL JEWELRY

NR-10 prohibits the use of watches, rings, bracelets, chains, earrings or any metal jewelry during electrical work. Metal objects create a low-resistance path for short-circuit currents, and can cause deep burns at the points of contact with the skin and high-energy electric arcs. This prohibition is absolute — it admits no exceptions for habit or convenience.


  09    WORK ROUTINES AND OPERATIONAL PROCEDURES

Module 9 — When and How to Work: Procedures That Protect

NR-10 distinguishes two work regimes for electrical installations, with specific requirements for each: work on de-energized installations (the normal and preferred situation) and work on energized installations (an exceptional situation that requires substantial additional protections).

9.1 Work on De-energized Installations

Work on de-energized installations is the mandatory standard: whenever technically possible, the work must be carried out with the installation without power. At events, this is feasible for: the assembly and connection of the entire electrical installation (distribution boards, cabling, equipment) before general energization; maintenance and repairs on equipment during dismantling; and the replacement of damaged components during setup.

The complete de-energization procedure (the six steps described in Module 4) must be followed rigorously, with a record in the Work Order and the signature of the person responsible before the start of any intervention.

9.2 Work on Energized Installations — The Exception, Not the Rule

When it is not possible to de-energize — which at events may occur during the soundcheck (with equipment on to check for electrical noise) or in live fault diagnosis — work on energized installations requires specific additional conditions:

  • Authorized Worker trained and documented under NR-10 — without exception

  • Use of ALL insulating PPE appropriate to the working voltage — gloves, footwear, goggles, Class B helmet

  • Specific Risk Analysis (APR) for the task to be performed — documented and signed

  • NEVER work alone — the presence of a second trained worker is mandatory

  • Work Order (WO) issued and signed by the technical officer before the start

  • Insulated tools certified to IEC 60900

9.3 Preliminary Risk Analysis (APR) at Events

The APR is the technical document that identifies the hazards, assesses the risks and defines the control measures for each stage of the electrical activity. It must be specific to each event, taking into account: type of venue (indoor/outdoor), weather conditions, total power of the installation, quality of the supply point, proximity to overhead lines and the presence of the public in the vicinity.


  10    DOCUMENTATION OF INSTALLATIONS — PIE AND RECORDS

Module 10 — Document to Control: the Electrical Installations Dossier

NR-10 requires companies to keep structured documentation of their electrical installations. For installations with a load greater than 75 kW, the Electrical Installations Dossier (PIE) is mandatory. At medium- and large-scale events — where a 100kW PA system + 150kW lighting + 50kW video result in 300kW of total demand — the total power systematically exceeds this threshold, making the PIE mandatory.

Single-line diagram

Graphic representation of the entire electrical installation of the event: supply point, distribution boards, circuits, load of each circuit, type of protection installed. Must be prepared by a licensed professional with an ART.

Specification of PPE and CPE

Complete list of the available PPE and CPE, with the CA number, expiry date and the person responsible for periodic verification

Training certificates

Copies of the valid NR-10 certificates of all workers who interact with electricity at the event

Inspection and measurement records

Grounding resistance measurement report (issued by a licensed professional); records of visual inspection of cables and connections

Procedures and technical instructions

APR, WO, de-energization/re-energization procedure, electrical emergency procedure — all documented and signed

Electrical testing report

Measurements of conductor insulation, grounding continuity, RCD operation — carried out before general energization

ART of the technical officer

Technical Responsibility Note (ART) of the engineer or technician responsible for the electrical installation of the event


💡  PIE — 75kW POWER: A THRESHOLD EASILY EXCEEDED AT EVENTS

A typical medium-scale show has: 30kW main PA + 15kW monitors + 80kW lighting + 25kW LED video + 10kW dressing room and support = 160kW total. The PIE is mandatory from 75kW — that is, any show with a professional PA and stage lighting is already above the threshold. The technical producer must ensure that the PIE is prepared and made available before energization.


  11    ADDITIONAL HAZARDS — WHEN ELECTRICAL RISK COMBINES

Module 11 — Compound Hazards: When Electricity Meets Other Dangers

NR-10 warns of the additional hazards that coexist with the electrical risk in the work environment. In the events sector, the combination of electrical risk with other risk vectors creates situations of compound danger that require integrated management — applying NR-10 and the specific standard of the identified additional hazard simultaneously.

  12    FIRE OF ELECTRICAL ORIGIN — PREVENTION AND RESPONSE

Module 12 — Electrical Fire: Class C and the Rules of Firefighting

Fires of electrical origin are classified as Class C fires as long as the energy source is present — because the electric current is an active component of the fire and must be taken into account in firefighting. After the circuit is de-energized, the fire is reclassified according to the combustible material involved (Class A for solids, Class B for flammable liquids).

This distinction is operationally critical: the mistake of using water on a Class C fire — with the installation still energized — results in fatal electric shock for the firefighter. The electric current conducts along the water jet up to the hand of the extinguisher operator. In the events sector, where the pressure to quickly extinguish a flare-up without shutting down the show is enormous, this mistake is plausible and must be prevented through training and signaling.

12.1 Correct Extinguishers by Class of Electrical Fire

Situation

Classification

Correct Extinguisher

NEVER use

Electrical equipment on fire with the circuit ENERGIZED

Class C

CO₂ (carbon dioxide) — preferred: leaves no residue on the equipment

Water — fatal shock. Foam — shock + damage. ABC powder — can be used but permanently damages the equipment

Distribution board with sparks/smoke, circuit ENERGIZED

Class C

CO₂ (6 kg minimum) or Dry Chemical Powder BC/ABC

Water or foam under any circumstance with an active circuit

Electrical cable on fire after DE-ENERGIZATION of the circuit

Class A (plastic insulation)

Pressurized water (after ensuring complete de-energization) or ABC powder

No restriction after confirmed de-energization — but verify the absence of voltage first

Generator on fire

Class B + C

Dry Chemical Powder BC or ABC for the fuel; CO₂ for electrical parts — after switching off the generator

Water on electrical parts even with the generator switched off (risk of contact through deficient grounding)


12.2 Prevention of Fires of Electrical Origin at Events

  • Correctly size the cross-section of the cables — use the NBR 5410 table, never undersize

  • Check the tightness of all connections — poor contact creates contact resistance that produces progressive heat

  • Never overload extension cords and distribution boards — measure the real demand before connecting loads

  • Keep combustible materials away from dimmers, amplifiers and transformers — minimum 0.5m distance

  • Never run electrical cables under carpets, tarps or any covering that prevents heat dissipation

  • Visually inspect all cables before each event — immediately replace cables with compromised insulation

  • Position a CO₂ extinguisher near each distribution board, dimmer rack and generator — with unobstructed access


  13    ACCIDENTS OF ELECTRICAL ORIGIN — ANALYSIS AND PREVENTION

Module 13 — Causes, Patterns and Lessons of Electrical Accidents at Events

The analysis of accidents of electrical origin reveals recurring patterns that, when understood, allow the implementation of effective preventive measures. In the events sector, electrical accidents rarely happen by chance — they are almost always the foreseeable consequence of a combination of systemic failures: lack of training, absence of procedures, deficient maintenance, and productivity pressure that leads to the suppression of safety measures.

13.1 Most Frequent Causes of Electrical Accidents at Events

  • Contact with exposed energized conductors — bare wires, splices without insulation, plugs with exposed pins

  • Indirect contact due to an insulation fault on equipment without grounding — what would be a simple RCD trip becomes a shock

  • Accidental re-energization during maintenance — absence of LOTO lockout or LOTO system ignored by another worker

  • Use of damaged electrical equipment — cables with cracked or cut insulation, connectors with charring

  • Improvisations — residential extension cords with industrial loads, cascaded "T" adapters, splices with common insulating tape

  • Approaching overhead lines during structure assembly — crane or stage truss touching the utility line

  • Work on energized installations without adequate PPE — "I'll just check it real quick"

  • Progressive overload of circuits — heating of cables and connections that precedes the fire

  14    FIRST AID IN ELECTRICAL ACCIDENTS

Module 14 — Help Without Becoming a Victim: Electrical Emergency Protocol

The first-aid protocol for electrical accidents begins with a rule that goes against the natural human instinct to immediately help the victim: never touch the victim while they are still in contact with the electrical source. The untrained rescuer who touches the victim without first interrupting the flow of current becomes a second victim — and one shock accident turns into two.

14.1 Electrical Emergency Protocol — Mandatory Sequence

  1. ASSESS YOUR SAFETY: Before any action, check that you are safe — do not step in water, do not touch nearby metal structures, move people away from the area

  2. SWITCH OFF THE POWER SOURCE: Trip the main circuit breaker, the generator switch or the disconnect — this is the first and most critical action. If you cannot, use DRY insulating material (wood, rubber, dry cloth) to move the victim away

  3. NEVER TOUCH DIRECTLY: Do not touch the victim with your hands until you are certain they are no longer in contact with the power source

  4. CALL SAMU (192): Call immediately — state that it is an electrical accident, describe the victim's apparent condition and the exact address

  5. ASSESS THE VICTIM: After ensuring safety — check for consciousness, breathing and pulse

  6. CPR IF NECESSARY: If there is no breathing and no pulse — begin Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (30 compressions + 2 breaths) and use an AED (Automated External Defibrillator) if one is available at the event

  7. TREAT THE BURNS: Only after ensuring electrical safety — cool with clean running water for 10-20 minutes. Do not apply creams, ointments or any substance

  8. KEEP THE VICTIM WARM: Cover with a blanket, keep them at rest, do not give fluids — wait for medical help


🔴  EVERY SHOCK VICTIM MUST GO TO THE HOSPITAL — WITHOUT EXCEPTION

Victims of electric shock can present serious internal injuries — myocardial injury, delayed cardiac arrhythmias (which can occur hours after the shock), renal injury from rhabdomyolysis (destruction of muscle by the heat of the current), neurological injury — with no visible external sign at the moment of the accident. EVERY victim of electric shock, even if conscious and apparently well, MUST be transported to the hospital for a complete medical evaluation with cardiac monitoring of at least 6 hours.


  15    LEGAL RESPONSIBILITIES AND PENALTIES

Module 15 — Who Answers for What: Legal Responsibility in NR-10

The responsibility structure of NR-10 is shared among the employer, the worker and the licensed technical officer. The absence of any one of the elements of this chain — training of the workers, safe conditions of the installations, or a licensed professional in charge — constitutes a violation that generates civil liability and, in the event of an accident, potentially criminal liability.

Employer / Producer

Keep electrical installations in safe conditions and in compliance with the technical standards; ensure valid NR-10 training for all workers who interact with electricity; provide adequate PPE in perfect condition; implement and maintain the PIE when the power exceeds 75kW; designate a licensed professional as technical officer for the event's electrical installation

Worker

Rigorously comply with the safety procedures and standards; correctly use all the PPE provided; immediately report to the supervisor any identified electrical-risk situation; exercise the right to refuse an activity with serious and imminent risk; participate in the training and keep the certification valid

Technical Officer (licensed Engineer/Technician with ART)

Technical responsibility for the compliance of the electrical installation; preparation and signing of the single-line diagram and the PIE; issuance of the ART for the event; presence or formal representation during energization of the installation; formal authorization of the workers who will act on the installation

Electrical assembly company

Ensure that all its workers assigned to the event have a valid NR-10; provide adequate electrical PPE; comply with the approved electrical design; not carry out improvisations without authorization from the technical officer


15.1 Penalties for Non-Compliance with NR-10

  • Administrative fines from the MTE — graded according to the severity of the violation and the number of exposed workers (NR-28)

  • Embargo of the electrical installation — the labor inspector may shut down the entire installation until the non-conformities are remedied

  • Civil liability — compensation for material, moral and aesthetic damages in the event of an accident with a victim

  • Criminal liability — negligent bodily injury (art. 129 of the Penal Code) or negligent homicide (art. 121) in the event of death; the technical officer and the employer may be indicted

  • Liability before CREA/CFT — the licensed professional responsible for the installation may have their registration suspended or revoked by the Council


  16    SPECIFIC PRACTICES — ELECTRICITY AT LIVE EVENTS

Module 16 — From the Distribution Board to the Cabling: Technical Guide for the Event Installation

This module integrates all the concepts from the previous modules into a practical technical guide for the electrical installation of a live event. It is the application module — where theoretical knowledge is transformed into an operational procedure specific to the reality of the sector.

16.1 Temporary Distribution Board — The Heart of the Installation

The temporary distribution board is the central element of every temporary electrical installation at an event. It must be sized and assembled by a licensed professional with an ART specific to the event. A well-designed distribution board is the difference between a safe installation and a constant risk throughout the entire operation of the event.

16.2 Minimum Requirements of the Event Distribution Board

  • Construction in resistant material with a minimum IP rating of IP44 for indoor use and IP54 for outdoor (protection against rain)

  • Three-phase main circuit breaker sized for the total load of the event with a 20% margin

  • Individual circuit breaker for each circuit — sound, lighting, video, support, dressing room, emergency generator

  • 30mA Residual Current Device (RCD) on ALL circuits — without exception

  • Grounding busbar (PE) connected to the event's grounding system — with documented resistance measurement

  • Clear and permanent identification of all circuits — non-removable labels

  • Closure with a padlock — preventing unauthorized access by untrained workers and the public

  • Visible safety signaling: "DANGER — ELECTRICAL HAZARD — RESTRICTED ACCESS"

  • Positioning in a location protected from rain (additional cover for outdoor), dry, and inaccessible to the public

  • ART of the licensed professional affixed to the distribution board or available on site


16.3 Cabling — Types and Requirements

Cable Type

Correct Application at Events

Prohibitions

Indicative Minimum Cross-Section

PP Cable (Parallel Polychloroprene)

Powering equipment in indoor and outdoor environments — flexible, with a weather-resistant rubber sheath

NEVER use rigid cable (NYM or Solid type) in temporary event installations — inflexible, tears with movement

Check the NBR 5410 table: minimum 4mm² for circuits of 25A+; 10mm² for 40A+; 70mm² for 100A+

Flexible Cable (Parallel Wire)

Powering small equipment and musical instruments on stage

NEVER in power circuits above 20A — insufficient cross-section and insulation inadequate for heavy use

Maximum 2.5mm² — only for light equipment (passive microphones, DI boxes, small mic stands)

Multi-strand Copper Cable (Cord)

Internal connections of distribution boards and equipment racks

NEVER use in exposed installations — fragile insulation, unsuitable for the events environment

Diameter defined by the licensed professional — calculated from the circuit's current

Industrial Plugs and Sockets (CEE/Steck)

MANDATORY for loads above 16A at professional events

PROHIBITED use of residential sockets (NBR 14136) for professional PA, lighting and video loads — inadequate current capacity and sealing

Choose the correct rated current: 16A / 32A / 63A / 125A


16.4 Grounding at Events — Technical Rules

  • EVERY metal structure (truss, stage, PA tower, barrier, walkway) that receives electrical equipment MUST be grounded to the PE busbar of the distribution board

  • The grounding resistance must be measured with an earth tester before energization — maximum acceptable value: 10 Ω

  • The grounding rod must be driven into moist soil to a minimum depth of 1.5m — in very dry soil, use a deeper rod or add bentonite to the soil

  • Grounding cable: bare copper or with green-yellow insulation, minimum cross-section of 1/3 of the phase conductor, never less than 4mm²

  • At outdoor events with a risk of lightning — install a temporary LPS per NBR 5419 and a lightning evacuation protocol


16.5 Electrical Safety Checklist — Pre-Energization
