35d38e02-0c4c-4158-ad41-78c6dbd81bd2.conteudo_rico:
SMU

PRODUCTIONS

Professional Training in Occupational Safety

TECHNICAL HANDBOOK

NR-18

Assembly of Structures and Temporary Works

STAGES · TRUSSES · SCAFFOLDING · BLEACHERS · ROOFING


Module aimed at the Live Events Sector

Course Load: 4 hours  |  Level: Advanced Operational

In accordance with NR-18 (MTE Ordinance) · NR-35 · NR-01 · ABNT NBR 8681

"A safe structure is no accident — it is design, inspection and technical responsibility."

  01    NR-18 AND THE EVENTS SECTOR — LEGAL BASIS AND SCOPE

Fundamentals of NR-18 Applied to Temporary Assembly

Regulatory Standard No. 18 — Working Conditions and Environment in the Construction Industry — governs the minimum conditions of safety, health and working environment in all works of construction, renovation, expansion, installation, maintenance or assembly. Although originally conceived for the civil construction sector, its application to the world of live events is not only possible but legally unavoidable: the assembly of a stage, of a set of bleachers, of a structural tent or of a truss tower constitutes, technically and legally, a temporary work subject to the requirements of NR-18.

This equivalence is not arbitrary. The box-truss aluminum structure that supports a lighting system twelve meters above the ground presents the same risks of structural collapse, worker fall and anchorage failure as a civil-construction scaffold. The worker assembling the stage is exposed to falls from height, burial under structural collapse, crushing by loads and electric shock — all the risk vectors addressed by the provisions of NR-18. To disregard the standard because it is an "event" and not a "construction site" constitutes not only technical negligence, but explicit exposure to civil, criminal and administrative liability in the event of an accident.

The application of NR-18 to the live events sector must be understood in conjunction with NR-01 (GRO/PGR), NR-35 (Work at Height), NR-06 (PPE) and NR-26 (Signage). The Program of Working Conditions and Environment — PCMAT — required by NR-18 for construction sites with twenty or more workers finds its functional equivalent in the PGR (Risk Management Program) of NR-01, which must incorporate the specific features of the temporary structures assembled at each event.



Legal basis

NR-18 — MTb Ordinance No. 3,214/1978, with successive updates. Last structural revision: MTE Ordinance No. 3,733/2020.

Field of application

Every activity of assembly, disassembly and maintenance of temporary structures with workers exposed to construction risks

Planning instrument

PCMAT (works ≥ 20 workers) or PGR/NR-01 for operations with smaller teams

Technical officer

Civil, mechanical or structural engineer with ART registered at CREA

Minimum documentation

Structural design, ART, calculation report, inspection report, training records

Relationship with NR-35

All work on a structure above 2.00 m in height is simultaneously governed by NR-35


⚠️  LEGAL BASIS

The absence of an ART (Technical Responsibility Note) for temporary structures at events is not merely a labor irregularity — it is a crime provided for in Article 65 of Law No. 5,194/1966, which governs the professional practice of engineers. The event producer, the party contracting the assembly company and the engineer themselves may be held jointly liable in the event of structural collapse.


  02    TYPOLOGY OF TEMPORARY STRUCTURES

Technical Classification of Structures in Live Events

To correctly apply the provisions of NR-18, the events safety professional needs to master the typology of the temporary structures used in the sector. Each type presents specific risks, distinct design requirements and particular inspection requirements. The absence of correct technical classification is one of the main causes of under-sizing of safety measures.

2.1 Stage Structures (Box Truss and Tubular)

The stage is the central structure of any event with a live performance. In the Brazilian industry, two construction systems predominate: aluminum box truss (square or rectangular structural aluminum profiles with internal diagonals, welded or bolted in modules of 0.5 to 3.0 meters) and galvanized steel tube (a circular structure, more robust and used in large-scale events or in adverse weather conditions).

The stage is composed of hierarchically interdependent elements: work floor (platform on bases), support tower for the roofing (upstage towers), roofing protected against wind and rain (roof structure), suspension beam for lighting and equipment (front truss, mid truss and back truss), load grid for motorized rigging, and backdrop structure. Each element is sized by specific loads — self-weight, occupancy load, wind load and dynamic equipment load — and must be calculated separately by a qualified professional.

2.2 Bleachers and VIP Boxes

Bleachers and VIP boxes are structures intended for the public and, therefore, subjected to occupancy loads significantly greater than those of stages. The reference technical standard is ABNT NBR 8681, which establishes the actions and safety of steel structures. For occupancy calculation, the convention of 4.0 kN/m² (approximately 400 kgf/m²) is used as the variable floor load for bleachers, plus the dynamic amplification coefficients when rhythmic movement of the public is anticipated — the so-called "crowd surfing load" or "sway load," a structural resonance phenomenon documented in collapses such as that of the bleachers at Madonna's show in Portugal (2006).

The maximum capacity of each bleacher module must be indicated by a sign fixed in a visible location, in accordance with Technical Instruction No. 01/2013 of the DF Fire Department and equivalent state regulations. Occupancy control is the responsibility of the event promoter and must be an integral part of the safety plan.

2.3 Structural Tents and Roofing

Tents and membrane roofing are structures highly sensitive to wind action. The calculation of wind loads follows ABNT NBR 6118 and ABNT NBR 6120, using the climatological data of the event region and the aerodynamic profile of the roofing. The greatest risk associated with tents is collapse due to lateral wind (overturning) or due to water accumulation on the membrane (when the inclination angle is insufficient for drainage), which generates concentrated loads capable of collapsing the entire structure.

Any tent with an area greater than 100 m² or a height greater than 4.5 m requires a structural design with an ART, regardless of the municipality. In cities with specific regulations for events — such as São Paulo (Law No. 16,748/2017) and Rio de Janeiro — the thresholds may be lower. The event safety coordinator must consult municipal legislation before each operation.

2.4 PA and Lighting Towers

Independent towers for supporting sound reinforcement systems (PA towers) and lighting (lighting towers) are vertical truss structures, generally with a height between 8 and 20 meters, founded on ballasted metal bases or anchored to the ground with foundation pins. Their predominant risk is overturning due to wind, which is a function of the exposure area of the hanging equipment (loudspeakers, light fixtures) and the wind speed at the location.

Correct sizing requires analysis of the exposed surface of all equipment fixed to the tower — not only the weight, but the area projected onto the plane perpendicular to the prevailing wind. Large-format loudspeakers can have a wind exposure area equivalent to a 2 m² billboard, generating overturning moments that the tower base must absorb. This calculation is frequently neglected by companies that size only by the vertical load capacity of the structure.


  03    STRUCTURAL DESIGN — LEGAL AND TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

Design as the Non-Negotiable Foundation of Structural Safety

NR-18 establishes unequivocally that the assembly activities of suspended scaffolding, cantilever structures and support structures with a specific design must be preceded by a design drawn up by a legally qualified professional. This requirement extends, by technical analogy and the force of the general principles of occupational safety, to every temporary structure for events that bears the load of workers, equipment or the public.

The structural design of a temporary structure for an event is not limited to the strength calculation of the metal profiles. It is a multidisciplinary technical document that covers: calculation report (verification of ultimate and serviceability limit states), assembly drawings and details, specification of materials and connectors, load table with all considered actions, hoisting and sequential assembly plan, and inspection and maintenance criteria. The ART — Technical Responsibility Note — is the legal instrument that links the professional to the design and transfers the technical responsibility to the signatory engineer.

3.1 Design Loads — What Must Be Calculated

The calculation report of a temporary event structure must quantify and combine the following actions, in accordance with the relevant ABNT standards:

  • Self-weight of the structure (profiles, connections, bracing, platforms)

  • Permanent load of fixed equipment (lighting systems, PA, video walls, generators on the stage)

  • Variable occupancy load (musicians, technicians, mobile equipment on the stage)

  • Wind load — basic speed according to climatic zone (ABNT NBR 6118, isopleth map)

  • Load of rainwater accumulated on roofing with insufficient inclination

  • Dynamic impact loads on bleachers (crowd resonance)

  • Load of special effects and pyrotechnics (when applicable)

The combination of these actions follows the weighting coefficients of ABNT NBR 8681 and must be verified for the ultimate limit states (collapse) and serviceability limit states (excessive deformation, vibration perceived by the public). Excessive deformation of a stage, even without collapse, can compromise the stability of the equipment fixed to the structure and generate fall risks.

3.2 The ART — Technical Responsibility Note

The ART is the document issued by the responsible engineer and registered at the Regional Council of Engineering and Agronomy (CREA) of the federative unit where the work is carried out. It describes the services provided, the technical standards applied and the professional's registration number, and it has probative value in civil, labor and criminal proceedings. The absence of an ART for a temporary structure that comes to collapse exposes the contracting party to strict liability, since the obligation to hire a qualified engineer belongs to the event organizer — not only to the assembly company.

From a practical standpoint, the event safety coordinator must require, before the start of assembly, the presentation of the registered ART for each main structure: main stage, bleachers (per module or per typology), tents above the regulatory limit, independent PA towers and rigging grids. The ART must remain available at the event site throughout the entire operation, for immediate presentation during inspection by the MTE, CREA, Fire Department or Civil Defense.


Design ART

Registered before the start of assembly — links the engineer to the structural calculation

Execution ART

When the engineer physically oversees the assembly — recommended for large events

Inspection report

Issued after complete assembly, before opening to the public — attests to conformity with the design

Registration at the local CREA

The ART must be registered at the CREA of the state where the event takes place, not in the company's home state

Who can issue it

Civil, mechanical or structural engineer with specific qualification — verify attributions at CREA


💡  BEST PRACTICE — PRE-ASSEMBLY CHECKLIST

Before any hoisting or assembly at height: verify whether the ART is registered at the local CREA, whether the design includes the specific configuration of this event (distances, loads, equipment), whether the ground at the site has been assessed to support the foundations, and whether all assembly workers hold documented NR-35 training. This verification takes less than 30 minutes and can prevent a tragedy.


  04    SCAFFOLDING AND WORK PLATFORMS — NR-18, ITEM 18.15

Scaffolding at Events: Regulation and Technical Application

Item 18.15 of NR-18 is the densest and most technically specific part of the standard with respect to structural safety during assembly. Although written with reference to the civil construction sector, its provisions are directly applicable to structure assembly operations at events, constituting the set of minimum requirements that the safety officer must know and enforce.

4.1 Design and Qualification Requirements

Item 18.15.2.4 of NR-18 establishes that the assembly of façade, suspended and cantilever scaffolding must be preceded by a design drawn up by a legally qualified professional. For the events sector, this requirement applies directly to stage support towers over 4 meters high, to rigging grid structures and to bleachers with more than two steps in height. The design must be carried out before the materials arrive at the site — not after the start of assembly.

Item 18.15.2.7 determines that, in the assembly and disassembly activities of scaffolding, all workers must be qualified and receive specific training for the type of scaffolding in question. This training is distinct from NR-35 (Work at Height) training, although complementary to it. The worker needs to master the assembly sequences of the specific system used — whether box truss, tubular tower or modular grid — since the assembly order is decisive for the progressive stability of the structure.

4.2 Mandatory PPE During Assembly

Item 18.15.2.7 of NR-18 is explicit: the use of a full-body (parachute-type) safety harness with a double lanyard featuring hooks with a minimum opening of fifty millimeters and a double lock is mandatory throughout the entire process of assembly and disassembly of scaffolding. This requirement is absolute — it admits no exceptions for "short duration of the task" or "worker comfort." Additionally, the item determines that the tools used must be exclusively manual and secured in a way that prevents their accidental fall, and that workers must carry an identification and qualification badge with the date of the last medical examination.

The prohibition of power tools (screwdrivers, drills) in work at height during scaffolding assembly stems from the risk of accidental fall of the tool — a 2 kg object falling from 8 meters high reaches kinetic energy sufficient to cause a fatal injury to the worker below. Manual tools must be tied to the harness or to the operator's wrist with certified load-retention cords (tool lanyards).

4.3 Express Prohibitions of NR-18 — Applied to Events

NR-18 Prohibition

Equivalent at Events

Consequence of Non-Compliance

Scaffolding built of wood (except when metal is not possible)

Stage or work platform built with wood not structurally certified

Risk of collapse due to excessive deflection, hidden rot or improvised joints

Removing or disabling safety devices

Removing guardrails, closures, connection locks or safety pins from the trusses

Immediate instability of the module — risk of progressive overturning of the structure

Using ladders to access an upper level from the scaffolding floor

Using step ladders resting on the stage platform to access the rigging grid

Fall from height — a ladder on an elevated surface multiplies the kinetic risk of the fall

Suspended scaffolding without an identification and load-capacity sign

Lighting or PA towers without a visible maximum-load sign

Impossibility of operational load control — risk of structural overload

Work surfaces without locking against displacement

Stage platform panels without a side lock or fixing pin

Displacement of the work floor under dynamic load — worker fall


🔴  CRITICAL RISK — ASSEMBLY SEQUENCE

The greatest risk during the assembly of large temporary structures is not the finished structure — it is the partially assembled structure. A stage tower with 70% of the modules installed and without complete horizontal bracing is in a state of instability that no final-structure calculation considers. The assembly sequence prescribed by the manufacturer is a safety document, not an operational suggestion.


  05    FOUNDATIONS AND SOIL ANALYSIS

The Soil as Part of the Structure: Foundations of Temporary Structures

One of the most neglected dimensions in the safety of temporary structures for events is soil analysis and foundation design. Most of the documented collapses of structures at events — including the collapse of the stage roof at the Roskilde Festival (Belgium, 2000) and the collapse of the main stage at the Indiana State Fair Festival (USA, 2011) — did not occur due to failure of the metal profiles, but due to failure in the foundations or due to wind action on a structure with a foundation insufficient for the resulting overturning moment.

The transmission of loads from a metal structure to the soil occurs through the bases (metal footings, pile pins or ballast concrete blocks). At outdoor events on grassy fields, the soil frequently presents a bearing capacity lower than that required for wind loads under adverse conditions. The soil bearing capacity must be verified by a CPT (cone penetration test) or SPT (standard penetration test) at the foundation positions of the main structures, especially at new sites or after periods of rain.

5.1 Types of Foundation at Events

The most widely used foundation solutions in temporary event structures are: a metal plate base with anchor bolts into existing concrete (for events in pavilions and arenas), pile pins driven into the ground with a hydraulic hammer (for events in open fields with firm soil), concrete blocks as compression ballast (for PA towers with predominant vertical load), and diagonal anchor tie-rods (for tents with membrane prestressing loads). Each solution has a specific load-transmission capacity that must be calculated in relation to the expected actions.

5.2 Field Soil Verification Protocol

In the absence of laboratory tests — which is frequent at small and medium-sized events — the responsible engineer can perform a visual and tactile estimate of the soil bearing capacity, complemented by a proof-load check: install the base and gradually apply the design load, monitoring the settlement (sinking) of the base. Settlement greater than 10 mm under the design load is a warning sign of soil with insufficient capacity, requiring foundation reinforcement or relocation of the structure.

At events held during periods of heavy rain or on soils of low consistency (soft clays, loose sands), the bearing capacity can be reduced by up to 60% compared to dry soil. This seasonal factor must be part of the event safety planning and may require the preventive suspension of assembly or the emergency reinforcement of the foundations. The decision must be made by the responsible engineer, documented and formally communicated to the event producer.


  06    RIGGING — HOISTING AND LOAD-SUSPENSION SYSTEMS

Rigging at Events: Technique, Standard and Responsibility

The term rigging designates the set of techniques, equipment and procedures used to suspend and position loads at height by means of hoisting systems. In the context of live events, rigging comprises the attachment of electric chain hoists, manual hoists, steel cables, textile slings, shackles, cable clips and other lifting accessories to the structural grid of the stage, with the objective of suspending and holding in position lighting systems, PA, video walls, curtains and other scenic equipment.

Event rigging operates at the intersection of three regulatory domains: NR-18 (structure assembly), NR-35 (work at height) and the ABNT technical standards for load-lifting equipment (ABNT NBR ISO 4301, ABNT NBR 11900). Responsibility for the safe sizing and execution of rigging is shared between the structural engineer responsible for the grid (who must calculate the hoisting points and their maximum allowable loads) and the certified rigger (a worker qualified to operate the hoisting systems).

6.1 Calculation of Rigging Loads

The load applied at each hoisting point of the grid must be calculated considering: the declared weight of each suspended piece of equipment, the dynamic amplification factor (which considers the impact of the motors' operation and equipment vibration), the distribution of loads along the grid crossbars, and the minimum safety coefficient of 7:1 (WLL — Working Load Limit) for textile slings and 5:1 for steel cables, in accordance with international rigging standards (ESTA and ANSI E1.6).

Load distribution on the grid is frequently misunderstood by assemblers without technical training. A 500 kg load suspended at the center of a 12-meter beam with supports at the ends does not generate 250 kg at each support — it generates 250 kg of vertical reaction at each end plus the bending moments in the beam, which must be absorbed by the bending strength of the profile. The simplification of calculating only the vertical load without verifying the bending capacity of the beam is one of the most common causes of collapse of rigging grids.

6.2 Rigging Equipment and Accessories — Mandatory Inspection

All rigging equipment — electric motor, hoist, steel cable, textile sling, shackle, swivel, beam clamp — must be inspected before each use. NR-18, complemented by the technical standards, establishes that lifting equipment without manufacturer identification, without a declared load capacity or with visible defects must be immediately removed from use.

Equipment

Criterion for Removal from Service

Inspection Frequency

Electric chain hoist

Deformation of the hook, chain with deformed links, brake with slippage, abnormal noise, missing identification plate

Visual inspection before each use + technical inspection every 12 months with a report

Steel cable

Breakage of more than 10% of the wires in any length of 10 diameters, corkscrew deformation, corrosion with deep pitting

Visual inspection before each use + immediate disposal at the first sign of damage

Textile sling (flat sling)

Cut, wear of 30% of the thickness, chemical burn, illegible label, broken stitching

Visual inspection before each use — disposal at the slightest sign of compromise

Shackle

Pin without a locking wire, deformation, structural corrosion, illegible capacity

Visual inspection before each use — pin torque verified at each assembly

Beam clamp

Deformation of the fixing bolt, crack in the casting, excessive play in the fit to the beam

Visual inspection before each use + verification of fixing torque


⚠️  THE 5:1 AND 7:1 RULE — WORKING LOAD VS. BREAKING LOAD

The load capacity declared on a piece of rigging equipment (WLL — Working Load Limit) already accounts for the safety factor. DO NOT add the WLL of two shackles in series to double the capacity — the load in a series configuration is not divided, it remains full at both fixing points. Rigging calculation requires an understanding of force vectors, not just the addition of weights.


  07    PRE-EVENT STRUCTURAL INSPECTION — TECHNICAL PROTOCOL

From Assembly to Release: The Inspection Report as a Safety Instrument

The assembly of a temporary structure for an event does not end with the last bolt tightened. The structural safety process requires, before any occupancy — whether by workers in their final positions or by the public — a complete technical inspection, with the issuance of a conformity report by the responsible engineer. This report is the document that operationally authorizes the use of the structure and that, in the event of an accident, legally defines the state of the art of safety at the moment of release.

7.1 Structural Inspection Items

The pre-event inspection of a temporary structure must systematically verify, with photographic and documentary records of each item:

  1. Conformity of the assembled geometry with the approved design — verification of spans, heights, arrangement of modules and bracing

  2. Integrity of all connectors: pins, bolts, cotter pins, clamps and shackles — verification of torque and safety lock

  3. Absence of visible deformations in the structural elements: warping, excessive curvatures, cracks in welds

  4. Conformity of the foundations: leveled base, torqued anchor bolts, absence of differential settlement

  5. Integrity of the guardrails and closures: minimum height of 1.20 m, resistance to 150 kgf horizontally

  6. Signage of maximum load capacity on all bleacher modules and public platforms

  7. Functional verification of the rigging motors: brake operation, operation at slow speed with nominal load, alarms

  8. Verification of collective protection systems: safety nets below work at height, edge protection

7.2 Staged Release Protocol

The release of complex structures must follow a staged sequence: first the technical release by the engineer (structural inspection report), then the operational release by the safety coordinator (verification of PPE, training and signage), and finally the release to the public by the event producer (after confirmation of occupancy capacity, emergency routes and an active evacuation plan).

This staged sequence is not bureaucracy — it is the model that prevents commercial pressure (the public arriving, the show starting) from replacing technical judgment. The engineer cannot be pressured to sign the report before concluding the inspection. The premature release of a structure with a non-conformity is a responsibility shared between the professional who signed and the producer who applied the pressure.


  08    RISK MANAGEMENT IN ASSEMBLY — INTEGRATION WITH THE PGR

Assembly as a Risk Process: Planning through the GRO/PGR

NR-18 requires, for works with twenty or more workers, the preparation of the PCMAT — Program of Working Conditions and Environment in the Construction Industry. The PCMAT is the sectoral equivalent of the PGR (Risk Management Program) of NR-01, with specific content aimed at construction risks. For live events with smaller teams, the PGR of NR-01 must incorporate the elements of the PCMAT applicable to assembly, constituting the risk-management document of the operation.

The risk inventory of the assembly of a large event structure is significantly denser than that of a typical office or retail operation. It must identify, for each phase of assembly (unloading of materials, base assembly, vertical structure assembly, roofing installation, equipment hoisting, electrical installation, disassembly), the hazards present, the workers exposed, the severity and probability of the risk event, and the control measures implemented.

8.1 Main Risks by Assembly Phase

Assembly Phase

Main Hazards

Priority Control Measures

Unloading and handling of materials

Crushing by load, low-back injury from exertion, being run over by a vehicle

Area signage, manual load limit (23 kg per individual), segregation of vehicles and pedestrians

Assembly of bases and foundations

Cut with a tool, impact from a falling part, forced posture

Cut-resistant gloves, helmet, safety footwear, weight limitation for manual lifting

Assembly of vertical structure (above 2 m)

Fall from height, fall of a part/tool onto a worker below

Full-body harness + double lanyard, isolation of the area below the hoisting, tool tethering

Installation of roofing and membrane

Fall from height on an inclined surface, wind, sun exposure

Horizontal anchorage, work stoppage in wind > 45 km/h, sun PPE

Hoisting of equipment (rigging)

Failure of hoisting equipment, swinging load, falling shackle

Pre-use inspection, isolate the hoisting area, rigger credential, verify WLL

Temporary electrical installations

Electric shock, electric arc, fire from overload

Electrical design with ART, NR-10 qualified worker, RCD protection, grounding

Disassembly

Worker fatigue, deadline urgency, night work, unstable reversed sequence

Team rotation, limit the shift, adequate lighting, prescribed disassembly sequence

8.2 Risk Factors Specific to the Events Sector

The assembly of structures for events presents risk factors that are not present in conventional civil construction works and that need to be explicitly addressed in the PGR:

  • Compressed deadline: the assembly of a large-scale show frequently occurs in 24 to 48 hours, generating time pressure that leads to unsafe operational decisions — skipping inspection steps, not installing collective protections due to "lack of time," assembling in adverse weather conditions

  • Simultaneous multi-supplier teams: stage, sound, lighting, structure, electrical and generator are frequently installed by different companies, with deficient coordination between the activities — generating interface risks that no individual PGR addresses in isolation

  • Night work and fatigue: the post-show disassembly frequently occurs between midnight and 6 a.m., with workers who have accumulated 16-20 hours of activity — the peak of accidents in the sector coincides with this period

  • Variation of sites: unlike a fixed construction site, each event takes place at a different location, with distinct soil, electrical conditions, lighting and wind exposure — requiring a risk assessment per site, not just per type of structure


📋  ESSENTIAL INSTRUMENT — ASSEMBLY RISK MAP

Before the start of each assembly, the safety coordinator must prepare a site-specific Risk Map: a simplified floor plan with the location of the structures, the risks by zone, the control measures implemented, the position of the fire extinguishers and the evacuation route. This map must be presented at the pre-assembly briefing and remain available at the site throughout the entire operation.


  09    TEMPORARY ELECTRICAL INSTALLATIONS — NR-18 AND NR-10

Temporary Electricity at Events: Requirements and Risks

NR-18 dedicates a specific section to temporary electrical installations at construction sites, establishing that these must be executed and maintained in accordance with an electrical design drawn up by a legally qualified professional. In the events sector, the temporary electrical installation covers: connection of the public grid to the event's entry panel, distribution to generators, sub-distribution to the stage, lighting, sound and suppliers, grounding and residual current protection (RCD).

The most frequent error in electrical installations at events is the under-sizing of the circuits. A PA system of 50,000 watts (50 kW) in continuous-use operation demands, at 220V three-phase, a current of approximately 132 amperes — a current that requires a cable with a minimum cross-section of 70 mm² for a run of 30 meters, with 160A protection. Using 16 mm² cables (common at poorly planned events) results in progressive heating of the conductor, melting of the insulation and an electrical fire.

NR-18 determines that electrical installations must have an electrical grounding protection system and must undergo periodic electrical inspections and measurements, with the issuance of the respective reports by a legally qualified professional. For events, the grounding report must be issued for each installation — it is not possible to reuse the report from a previous event at a different location.

9.1 The RCD (Residual Current Device)

NR-18 makes the use of the Residual Current Device (RCD) mandatory as an additional safety measure in electrical installations. The RCD is a protection device that continuously monitors the difference in current between the phase and neutral conductors: when the leakage current exceeds the sensitivity threshold (typically 30 mA for human protection), the RCD switches off the circuit in less than 40 ms, before the current is sufficient to cause ventricular fibrillation in the worker. At outdoor events, where humidity, the presence of water on the floor and cables with worn insulation create conditions conducive to leakage currents, the RCD is the most important collective protection equipment of the electrical installation.


  10    WIND ACTION ON TEMPORARY STRUCTURES — WEATHER PROTOCOL

Wind: The Invisible Risk of Open Structures

Wind is the main agent of collapse of temporary structures at outdoor events. The most frequent collapse mechanism is not the rupture of the metal profiles by tension or compression — it is the overturning of the structure due to a wind moment greater than the resisting moment of the foundations. This distinction is fundamental: a structure may statically resist the design wind calculated with the structure complete and empty of equipment, but collapse during assembly, when the roofing is already installed but the bracing and definitive ballasts have not yet been applied.

The event safety plan must include a formal weather protocol, defining: the threshold wind speed for suspending operations at height (typically 45 km/h for load hoisting and 60 km/h for workers remaining on structures), the meteorological information source used (local weather station, professional forecasting service), and the person responsible for the stoppage decision. The decision to halt operations due to wind cannot belong to the event producer — it belongs technically to the responsible engineer and operationally to the safety coordinator.

10.1 Action Table by Wind Speed

Wind Speed

Beaufort Scale

Condition

Mandatory Action

< 30 km/h

F1-F2

Light wind

Normal operation — continuous monitoring

30 to 45 km/h

F3

Moderate wind

Suspend load hoisting — workers on the structure with restriction

45 to 60 km/h

F4

Strong wind

Suspend work at height — remove workers from elevated structures

60 to 75 km/h

F5

Very strong wind

Suspend the entire operation — evacuation of the area near the structures

> 75 km/h

F6+

Storm

Total evacuation — no worker or member of the public near the structure


🔴  REAL CASE — LESSON FROM THE SECTOR

The collapse of the main stage roof at the Hillsong UK Festival (2013) occurred with wind measured at 68 km/h — within the "strong wind" range. The structure had been sized for 80 km/h wind, but the assembly sequence was incomplete: the lateral wind guys had not yet been tensioned. The lesson: the wind resistance of a partially assembled structure is significantly lower than the resistance of the finished structure.


  11    LEGAL RESPONSIBILITIES — THE CHAIN OF RESPONSIBILITY

Who Answers for What: The Chain of Responsibility in Event Structures

The assembly of temporary structures for live events involves a chain of responsibility distributed among multiple agents: the event organizer/producer, the structure assembly company, the responsible engineer (ART), the rigger and the assembly workers. Understanding this chain is fundamental for the safety professional, both to perform their role with clarity of attributions and to advise the producer about their legal responsibilities.

11.1 Responsibilities by Agent

Organizer/Producer

Legal contracting party — responsible for requiring the ART, structural design and worker qualification before the start of assembly; responsible for releasing the event to the public only after the structural conformity report

Assembly Company

Employer of the assemblers — responsible for the assembly PGR, provision of PPE, NR-35 training, qualification of the riggers and compliance with the NR-18 procedures

Responsible Engineer (ART)

Technical responsibility for the conformity of the design and, when present at the execution, for the conformity of the executed work; cannot be pressured to sign a report without a complete inspection

Certified Rigger

Responsible for the technical execution of the hoisting points — cannot operate without verifying the WLL of each piece of equipment and without confirming the maximum load of the hoisting point on the grid

Safety Coordinator (OSH)

Responsible for the integrated PGR of the operation, for the safety briefing, for enforcing the use of PPE and for communicating non-conformities to the producer and the engineer

11.2 Joint Liability

The legal concept of joint liability is extremely relevant in the events sector: when an accident occurs due to the failure of a contracted company (the assembly company, for example), the event organizer may be held jointly liable if it is demonstrated that they did not require the minimum safety conditions provided for by law. Outsourcing the assembly does not transfer full responsibility for the accident to the assembler — it shares it, in proportion to the extent of the control exercised by the contracting party.

The argument that "the assembly company is responsible for its own workers" does not protect the producer in the event of a structural collapse that also affects the public or third parties. In this case, the principle of strict liability applies: once the damage and the causal link with the event structure are demonstrated, the obligation to indemnify does not require proof of fault — it is enough to demonstrate that the damage occurred by reason of the risk activity (structure assembly).


  12    INSPECTION AND COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST

NR-18 Compliance Protocol for Live Events

12.1 What the MTE Inspector Verifies at an Event Assembly

  • Existence and validity of the design and execution ART of the main structures

  • Qualification and training of the assembly workers: NR-35, NR-06, specific scaffolding/rigging training

  • PGR records with a risk inventory specific to the assembly

  • Effective use of PPE: full-body harness, helmet, gloves, safety footwear

  • Collective protections installed: guardrails, safety nets, isolation signage

  • Electrical design with ART and grounding report for the event installation

  • RCD devices installed in the distribution panels

  • Working conditions of the workers: shift, access to drinking water, nearby sanitary facilities

  13    CONCLUSION — A SAFE STRUCTURE AS A TECHNICAL AND ETHICAL OBLIGATION

Closing: The Responsibility that Sustains the Structure

NR-18 applied to the live events sector represents the materialization of a principle that the entire production chain must internalize: the structure of an event is not scenography — it is engineering. The stage that supports a band and the equipment on it, the bleachers that accommodate the public, the roofing that protects from wind and rain are engineering works subject to the same laws of physics and the same regulatory requirements as any permanent structure.

The difference between a permanent structure and a temporary structure does not lie in the requirement of technical quality — it lies in the frequency with which it is assembled, inspected and disassembled. This frequency, far from reducing the safety requirements, amplifies them: each assembly is a new work, with new soil risks, a new weather history, a new load configuration. The standardization of procedures, the rigorous maintenance of the rigging and scaffolding equipment, and the presence of a technical officer at each operation are the pillars that transform event assembly from an improvised activity into a professional engineering activity.

The events safety professional who masters the principles of NR-18 not only reduces accidents — they raise the technical standard of the industry, contribute to the economic sustainability of the companies (which no longer bear the costs of accidents and legal liabilities) and, fundamentally, fulfill their social role: ensuring that the workers who raise the spectacle return to their homes at the end of each operation.

"A safe structure is no accident — it is design, inspection and technical responsibility."


NR-18 module completed. Course load: 4 hours.

⚠️  MANDATORY REQUIREMENT — WHO NEEDS NR-35?

Every events professional who performs activities above 2.00 meters in height — including technicians who climb stage structures, riggers who install equipment on the grid, aerial work platform operators and any assembler who accesses trusses, towers or roofs — MUST hold complete and valid NR-35 training BEFORE beginning any activity. The fine per untrained worker can reach R$ 6,708.08.

  01    INTRODUCTION AND LEGAL BASIS — NR-35 IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 1 — Fundamentals: what Work at Height is and why it Kills

Regulatory Standard No. 35 — Work at Height — establishes the minimum requirements and protective measures for the planning, organization and execution of work-at-height activities. Published by SIT Ordinance No. 313 of March 23, 2012, and updated by MTP Ordinance No. 4,218/2022 and MTE Ordinance No. 1,680/2025, it is the standard that most directly affects the survival of the live-events worker — because in the events sector, work at height is not the exception: it is the daily routine of setup.

Falls from height are one of the leading causes of fatal workplace accidents in Brazil. In the events sector, the risk is systematically amplified by factors that rarely coexist in other sectors: compressed setup deadlines (often at night), temporary structures that vary with each production, production pressure that encourages safety shortcuts, and crews of variable composition who often do not know one another and are working together for the first time. This combination of factors makes the events professional working at height one of the highest-risk workers in Brazil.

🔴  OFFICIAL DEFINITION — NR-35, ITEM 35.1.2

Work at height is defined as any activity performed above 2.00m (two meters) from the lower level, where there is a risk of falling. It is not necessary for the worker to be "at the top of a tall structure" — climbing two steps onto a raised stage or accessing the truss of a small indoor rig already constitutes work at height, with all the legal requirements of NR-35.


1.1 Event activities that constitute work at height

  • Assembly and dismantling of metal stage structures — ground support, box truss, roof pillars

  • Installation of lighting equipment on light bars, overhead rigs and rigging points at 6-15 meters

  • Hoisting and positioning of line arrays, clusters and suspended PA systems

  • Operation and maintenance of aerial work platforms (scissor, articulating, telescopic)

  • Installation of banners, LED panels and decorative elements on elevated structures

  • Access to tent roofs, warehouse roofs and arena roofs to install rigging points

  • Assembly of delay towers, PA towers and standalone video structures

  • Any scaffold work during scenery or structure assembly



Legal definition

Any activity above 2.00m with a risk of falling — NR-35, item 35.1.2

Establishing ordinance

SIT Ordinance No. 313 of March 23, 2012

Latest update

MTE Ordinance No. 1,680/2025 — new Annex III (individual-use ladders)

Minimum workload

8 hours — 4h theory + 4h practical | In-person for the practical portion

Validity

2 years — mandatory biennial refresher

Trained worker

Passed theory and practical training + OHC of physical and mental fitness + formal company authorization

Fine per untrained worker

Up to R$ 6,708.08 per worker + civil and criminal liability in the event of an accident

Complementary standards

NR-18 (Construction) | NR-06 (PPE) | NR-10 (Electrical) | NR-11 (Materials Handling)


  02    RESPONSIBILITIES AND THE TRAINED WORKER

Module 2 — Who Answers for What: the Chain of Responsibility

NR-35 distributes responsibilities between employer and worker so that neither can be exempt in the event of an accident. The employer is responsible for the protection system — structure, PPE, training, procedures. The worker is responsible for correctly applying the procedures and for the effective use of PPE. Joint liability means that both may be held accountable when an accident occurs.

2.1 Responsibilities of the Employer/Producer

  • Ensure the implementation of all protective measures established in NR-35 before any work at height

  • Ensure the training of ALL workers BEFORE activities begin — with no exception for urgency or deadline

  • Provide free of charge all appropriate PPE, certified (valid CA) and in perfect condition

  • Conduct a risk analysis (PRA) and issue a Work Permit for each work-at-height task

  • Ensure emergency and rescue procedures are defined and tested BEFORE work begins

  • Immediately suspend activities when unsafe or prohibitive conditions are identified

  • Provide an OHC (Occupational Health Certificate) attesting to the worker's physical and mental fitness for work at height

2.2 Responsibilities of the Worker

  • Strictly comply with all legal and regulatory provisions on work at height

  • Cooperate with the employer in implementing protective measures — safety is a shared responsibility

  • Correctly use all PPE provided — harness connected, lanyard tensioned, helmet with chin strap fastened

  • Inspect all PPE before each use — defective PPE must be refused and reported immediately

  • Report to the immediate supervisor any risk situations identified at the workplace

  • Exercise the right of refusal when identifying serious and imminent risk — this right is provided by law and cannot result in punishment


💡  RIGHT OF REFUSAL — NO DEADLINE JUSTIFIES THE RISK

The right of refusal is provided in NR-01 and reinforced in NR-35: every worker may stop their activities when they observe a serious and imminent risk to their safety and health, immediately notifying the hierarchical supervisor. This right cannot result in penalty, deduction or dismissal. In the events sector, deadline pressure ("hurry up, we're already behind") is the documented root cause of several fatal accidents. No show is more important than a life.


  03    RISK ANALYSIS AND PROHIBITIVE CONDITIONS

Module 3 — PRA: Plan before You Climb

The Preliminary Risk Analysis (PRA) is mandatory under NR-35 before any work at height. It is the technical instrument that forces the systematic planning of each stage of the work, identifying the specific risks of the location, the activity and the conditions of the day. In the events sector, where each production is a new scenario with unique variables — location, structure, crew, weather conditions — the PRA cannot be a generic document copied from the previous event: it must be specific to the event and to the setup shift.

3.1 Mandatory content of the PRA for work at height at events

  1. Identification of the location and the task — event name, date, specific location, type of structure

  2. Mapping of all work-at-height points — heights, type of structure, available anchorage points

  3. Verification of overhead power lines — especially critical for ground support assembly with a crane

  4. Assessment of weather conditions — wind speed, rain forecast, extreme temperature

  5. Verification of the ground or floor beneath the structures — firmness for aerial work platforms, leveling

  6. Identification of simultaneous activities that create a risk of interference — rigging and electrical assembly in the same area

  7. Definition of the system for isolating the area below the work at height

  8. Identification of authorized workers and their roles in the operation

  9. Definition of the emergency and rescue plan specific to the event


3.2 Prohibitive Conditions — when work MUST be suspended

Prohibitive Condition

Reference Parameter

Mandatory Action

Excessive wind

Above 46 km/h (Force 6 on the Beaufort scale)

Suspend immediately — do not resume until the wind drops for a sustained period

Electrical storm / lightning

Any visible lightning or audible thunder on the horizon

Descend from metal structures immediately — safe distance: 30 meters from trusses during a storm

Rain with wind

The combination of wet surfaces + wind raises the risk exponentially

Suspend — resume only after inspecting all work surfaces

Insufficient lighting

Below 150 lux in the work-at-height area (reference NBR 5413)

Wait for adequate lighting — night work without sufficient lighting is common and extremely dangerous at events

Damaged or expired PPE

Any visual non-conformity or expired CA

Remove from service immediately — do not replace with another without a valid CA

Worker with symptoms

Dizziness, extreme fatigue, malaise, use of alcohol/drugs, medication that causes drowsiness

Remove immediately from work at height — the OHC does not cover momentary conditions

Absence of a signed WP

No Work Permit issued and signed before the start

Do not start the work under any circumstances

No defined emergency plan

Rescue not planned or rescue team not available

Do not authorize the start of work — the emergency plan is a condition, not an add-on


  04    POTENTIAL RISKS IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 4 — Risk Map: the Specific Hazards of Event Setup

The events sector presents a work-at-height risk profile that differs substantially from the risks of other industries such as civil construction or manufacturing. The main factors that make the risk especially high at events are: the variability of structures (each event is different), extreme deadline pressure (the show has a set start time), crew turnover (technicians who do not know one another working together), and the normalization of unsafe behaviors as a cultural practice of the sector.

4.1 Risk categories — technical analysis

Category

Specific Hazard at Events

Frequent Root Cause

Maximum Consequence

Fall of the worker

Fall of a technician while positioning a moving head on the grid at 10m

Lanyard not connected — "it would be quick"

Death or severe permanent injury (spinal fracture, TBI)

Falling equipment

A 12kg LED PAR fixture comes loose from the rig during the show

Absence of a safety cable — clamp only

Death or severe injury of a worker or the audience below

Partial structural collapse

A ground support truss collapses during hoisting

Overload, incorrect assembly, unstable ground

Death of workers in the area, injury to the audience

Electric shock at height

A lighting technician at height touches live dimmer wiring

Unprotected electrical installation + simultaneous work

Shock + fall — a double death factor (NR-10 + NR-35)

Suspension syndrome

Unplanned rescue + delayed response

Death within 15-30 minutes from circulatory collapse

Fall from a portable ladder

A ladder slides while accessing a structure on sandy ground

Absence of base anchoring, wrong angle

Limb fractures, TBI, death at greater heights


⚠️  THE FACTOR THAT AMPLIFIES ALL RISKS — DEADLINE PRESSURE

Research on live-event accidents consistently documents that deadline pressure is the main organizational factor leading to the suppression of safety measures. "Hurry up, we're already behind," "you don't need a harness for a quick job," "don't stop now to sign paperwork" are phrases that precede fatal accidents. NR-35 has no urgency clause — the safety requirements apply regardless of the event schedule.


  05    COLLECTIVE PROTECTION SYSTEMS

Module 5 — Protect Everyone Before Protecting the Individual

NR-35 is explicit about the hierarchy of protection: collective protective measures take priority over individual ones. Collective protections eliminate or reduce the risk for all workers in the area — regardless of their individual action. PPE, by contrast, depends on the worker's correct behavior to be effective. In the events sector, the consistent implementation of collective protections is what distinguishes a professional operation from a high-risk one.

5.1 Guardrail and Toe Board

The guardrail is the edge protection system par excellence: a physical barrier that prevents the fall of workers and materials from elevated surfaces. At events, it must be installed on the edges of stages more than 2.00m high, work platforms on temporary structures, assembly scaffolds and access walkways between structures.

  • Minimum height of the top rail: 1.20m — resistance of 150 kgf applied horizontally

  • Intermediate rail at 0.70m from the surface

  • Toe board at least 20cm high — prevents the fall of tools and smaller materials

  • Maximum opening between elements: 60cm — prevents the passage of a human body

  • Materials: steel, aluminum, certified wood or a combination — no improvised electrical conduit pipes

5.2 Area Isolation and Signage

The entire vertical projection area below the work at height must be isolated and marked to prevent access by unauthorized people. At events, this measure is often neglected because the work areas are the same areas that will be used by the audience hours later — which creates the false impression that isolating is unnecessary or impractical. It is absolutely necessary and practical.

  • Cones, hazard tape and barriers forming a safety perimeter around the projection of the load or the worker at height

  • Spotters positioned at access points to prevent people from entering — with radio communication to the worker at height

  • Visible signage: "DANGER — WORK AT HEIGHT — ACCESS PROHIBITED" on all sides of the perimeter

  • The minimum isolation radius must take the working height into account: for work at 10 meters, isolate at least a 10-meter horizontal radius

5.3 Aerial Work Platforms (MEWP)

Aerial Work Platforms — scissor, articulating and telescopic — are the most effective collective protection equipment for positioning equipment at height at events, because they eliminate the need for the worker to move over structures without guardrails. The correct use of MEWPs in place of manual climbing on trusses is the most significant engineering measure for reducing accidents in event setup.

  06    PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE)

Module 6 — PPE for Height: Each Component has a Life-Saving Function

PPE for work at height forms an integrated system: each component has a specific function and depends on the others. The harness arrests the falling worker; the lanyard connects the harness to the anchorage point; the energy absorber reduces the impact load on the body; the fall arrester limits the fall distance during vertical movement; the helmet protects the head from impacts. No component works properly if any other fails. That is why inspecting each item before every use is not bureaucracy — it is the guarantee that the system works as designed.

6.1 Full-Body (Parachute-Type) Safety Harness

The full-body (parachute-type) harness is the only harness accepted for work at height by NR-35. The waist belt — used for positioning and static support — is expressly PROHIBITED for work at height because it does not adequately distribute the impact force in the event of a fall: all the arresting energy is absorbed by the waist, causing serious internal injuries.

  • Minimum of 5 adjustment points: shoulders (2), chest (1) and thighs (2)

  • Dorsal D-ring centered on the back — the lanyard connection point

  • Individual adjustment mandatory — no excessive slack at the torso or thighs

  • Valid CA from INMETRO — check the internal label of the harness

  • Immediate disposal after any fall — even if the harness appears intact, the impact load damages the fibers internally

6.2 Lanyard with Energy Absorber

The lanyard is the connecting element between the harness and the anchorage point. The energy absorber is the device attached to the lanyard that deforms in a controlled way during fall arrest, reducing the impact load transmitted to the body from up to 1,200 kg to a maximum of 600 kg — the human body's tolerance limit for a controlled fall.

  • Use of the energy absorber: mandatory when the lanyard length exceeds 0.9m or when the fall factor is greater than 1

  • Double (Y-type) lanyard: allows staying connected at all times during lateral movement on structures — connect the second leg before disconnecting the first

  • Maximum lanyard length: 1.80m — always calculate the Fall Clearance Zone (FCZ) before use

  • After the absorber is deployed (fall): replace immediately — the absorber is a single-use consumable

6.3 Fall Arrester — for Vertical Movement

The fall arrester is the fall protection device for vertical movement — access to delay towers, ladders built into stage structures, access to the top of the ground support. In the event of a fall, it locks automatically, limiting the maximum fall distance to 15-30 cm (with a retractable fall arrester) or to 1 meter (with a guided-type fall arrester on a cable).

6.4 Mandatory Pre-Use Inspection Procedure

Item to Inspect

What to check

Rejection Criterion

Full-body harness

Stitching: cuts, abrasion, fraying. Buckles: deformation, corrosion, correct locking. Label: valid CA and manufacturing date.

Any cut in the stitching. Buckle that does not lock correctly. Expired CA. Chemical burn. History of a fall.

Lanyard with absorber

Webbing: cut, abrasion, chemical burn, heavy moisture. Absorber: intact casing, intact seal. Hooks: deformation, working gate, corrosion.

Absorber with a broken seal (already deployed). Hook without a gate or with a jammed gate. Visible cut in the webbing.

Fall arrester

Locking mechanism: operate manually and check that it locks. Steel cable: broken strands, kink (corkscrew). Housing: deformation.

Mechanism that does not lock immediately when pulled. More than 2 broken strands in the cable. Deformation of the housing.

Helmet

Shell: cracks, deformation, perforations. Cradle: stitching, adjustment, positioning. Chin strap: buckle locking.

Any crack in the shell. Loose or torn cradle. Chin strap with no working buckle. Mark of a previous impact.

Carabiner / Connector

Deformation of the frame. Working double-locking mechanism. Structural corrosion.

Deformed frame. Gate that does not close completely. Corrosion that compromises integrity.


  07    ANCHORAGE SYSTEMS — THE POINT THAT CANNOT FAIL

Module 7 — Anchorage: if the Point Fails, the Whole System Fails

The anchorage point is the most critical component of the entire fall protection system. It is the element to which the worker is connected through the lanyard — and it is the only one that must absorb the full load of the fall without failing. A perfect harness and a flawless lanyard protect no one if the anchorage point is a truss not calculated for fall loads, a rope tied to a scenery element, or a point not approved by an engineer.

🔴  FUNDAMENTAL RULE — ANCHORAGE MUST WITHSTAND 15 kN

Every anchorage point for work at height must withstand a minimum load of 15 kN (equivalent to approximately 1,500 kgf) per connected worker. At events, many improvised "anchorage points" — scenery hooks, decorative elements, banner supports — were not sized for this load and can fail catastrophically when the fall load is applied. Never use anchorage points not approved by a qualified engineer.


7.1 Types of Anchorage at Live Events

Type of Anchorage

Description

Application at Events

Technical Requirement

Fixed structural anchorage

Eye bolts, rings or points permanently welded to the stage structure or building

Rigging points on the beams of arenas and theaters; eye bolts welded to the top of ground support pillars

Structural design with an ART; documented allowable load; periodic inspection

Horizontal lifeline

Steel cable (minimum 12mm) or tensioned webbing between two fixed anchorage points

Grid walkways for lighting and rigging technicians; the top of open stages

Engineer's design; correct tensioning; verification of the end anchorages; fall arrester specific for use on a horizontal line

Vertical lifeline

Steel cable or static rope fixed vertically for access to tall structures

Access to the top of PA towers, delay towers and ground support more than 5m high

Steel cable min. 10mm or static rope 11mm; top anchorage with 15 kN; guided-type fall arrester specific for a vertical line

Portable anchorage (anchor sling)

High-strength textile sling that wraps around a structural element (truss, pillar)

When no fixed point is available — a temporary solution approved for the situation

Minimum WLL 15 kN; verify that the structural element supports the load; correct angle of the sling


7.2 Fall Factor (FF) — an essential concept

The fall factor is the ratio between the free-fall distance and the length of the lanyard. It is the parameter that determines the impact force the protection system must absorb — and, therefore, the correct sizing of the energy absorber.

Fall Factor

Situation

Free-fall distance

Impact force

Absorber mandatory?

FF 0

Anchorage above the head, no free fall — the worker slips but does not fall

0 cm

Not mandatory, but recommended

FF 0.5

~50 cm

  08    EMERGENCY, RESCUE AND FIRST AID

Module 8 — When the System Fails: Rescue Must Be Planned in Advance

NR-35 requires the emergency and rescue plan to be defined BEFORE any work at height begins. This requirement is not a formality — it is recognition that rescuing a worker suspended at height is a technical operation that requires planning, specific equipment and trained people. Improvising the rescue at the moment of the accident costs precious time that can determine the difference between life and death.

🔴  SUSPENSION TRAUMA — RISK OF DEATH WITHIN MINUTES

Suspension trauma (also called harness hang syndrome or orthostatic intolerance) occurs when a worker hangs motionless in the full-body harness after a fall. The thigh straps of the harness compress the femoral blood vessels, causing venous pooling in the lower limbs, reduced venous return to the heart and progressive circulatory collapse. It can cause death within 15 to 30 minutes. The rescue must be immediate — any delay is potentially fatal.


8.1 Post-fall procedure — suspended worker

  1. Immediately alert the person in charge of the worksite and the emergency service (SAMU 192 / Fire Department 193)

  2. Start the rescue using the method planned in the WP — an aerial work platform is the safest and fastest method

  3. Maintain verbal communication with the suspended worker — instruct them to move their legs continuously if possible (this activates the venous muscle pump)

  4. Get the worker out of suspension as quickly as possible — every minute suspended increases the risk

  5. AFTER THE RESCUE: do NOT lay the victim down immediately — place them in a semi-seated position (semi-seated with knees bent) for 20-30 minutes to prevent a sudden return of blood to the heart

  6. Monitor vital signs continuously — pulse, breathing, level of consciousness

  7. Call SAMU (192) even if the worker appears fine — internal injuries and delayed cardiac arrhythmias are possible

8.2 Rescue Methods at Events

Rescue Method

When to use

Advantages

Limitations

Aerial Work Platform (MEWP)

Whenever available on site — it is the PREFERRED method

Safest; allows a controlled approach to the suspended worker; load capacity adequate for two workers

Requires an available MEWP and a trained operator; the ground must allow positioning

Ladder with an assistant

Worker at low height (up to 5m) with viable ladder access

Fast when no MEWP is available and the height is low

Limited by height; requires coordination; risk to the rescuer

Rope system — controlled descent

Worker at any height, with no MEWP access

Can reach positions a MEWP cannot

Requires a team trained in vertical techniques; more complex; not recommended without specific training

Fire Department team

Specialized equipment; trained team

Arrival time — suspension trauma does not wait; the in-house rescue must take place while waiting


  09    WORK PERMIT — MANDATORY DOCUMENTATION

Module 9 — WP: the Document that Organizes and Authorizes each Work at Height

The Work Permit (WP) is the formal document that authorizes the execution of work at height after verifying that all safety measures are in place. It is issued before activities begin, for each work-at-height operation, and must be signed by the person responsible for issuing it and by the authorized workers. The WP is not paperwork — it is the documented record that the planning was done, the risks were assessed and the safety conditions were verified.

9.1 Mandatory content of the Work Permit

  • Identification: date, start time and expected finish time, specific work location, event

  • Detailed description of the activity to be performed and the structures involved

  • Names and roles of the workers authorized for this specific job

  • Risks identified in the PRA and the control measures adopted for each risk

  • List of the PPE each worker is using — with verification of a valid CA

  • Description of the collective protection systems installed (guardrail, isolation, signage)

  • Anchorage points identified and approved — with reference to the engineering design

  • Emergency and rescue plan: method, responsible team, available equipment

  • Emergency contacts: SAMU (192), Fire Department (193), reference hospital, person in charge of the worksite

  • Signature of the person responsible for issuing the WP and of the authorized workers

  • Closing field: time of completion, observations, closing signature


9.2 Complete operational procedure for events

  1. DSD (Daily Safety Dialogue): a safety briefing before each setup shift — communicate the day's risks, special conditions, schedule changes

  2. PRA: carrying out the Preliminary Risk Analysis specific to the event and the shift

  3. WP: issuing and signing the Work Permit — all authorized workers sign

  4. PPE inspection: each worker inspects and signs off on the conformity of their PPE

  5. Anchorage check: all anchorage points verified before the start

  6. Area isolation: safety perimeter installed and spotters positioned

  7. Communication: radio tested between workers at height and the ground team

  8. Execution: work carried out per the procedure, with continuous communication

  9. Closing: tools collected, PPE collected for inspection, formal closeout of the WP


  10    SPECIFIC PRACTICES — STAGE ASSEMBLY, RIGGING AND MEWP

Module 10 — Technical Guide by Type of Work-at-Height Activity at Events

This module integrates the concepts of the previous modules into specific technical guides for the main work-at-height activities in the events sector. Each type of activity presents specific risks, anchorage requirements and procedures that the trained worker needs to master.

10.1 Stage and Ground Support Assembly

The assembly of a stage with ground support (a self-standing metal structure that supports the lighting grid) is the most complex and highest-risk work-at-height operation at events. It involves work at multiple heights simultaneously, hoisting of heavy structures and dependence on precise communication between teams.

  • Phase 1 — Pillar assembly (0-5m): single lanyard with absorber, anchorage on the pillars themselves via a portable sling; the area below isolated from the start

  • Phase 2 — Connecting the grid trusses (5-12m): DOUBLE (Y) lanyard mandatory — keeps the connection during lateral movement across the grid; tools tethered with a safety lanyard

  • Phase 3 — Hoisting the grid: the ENTIRE area below the grid must be completely clear of people; continuous communication with the operator of the electric hoists; attention to the maximum load capacity of the system

  • Phase 4 — Positioning equipment on the grid: double lanyard, safety cable on each piece of equipment; a complete visual inspection before clearing the hoist

10.2 Rigging — Hoisting Suspended Equipment

Rigging is the activity of suspending and positioning heavy equipment at height. Line arrays can weigh 500 kg per side; complete grids with lighting can weigh tons. Professional rigging requires not only NR-35 training but also specific knowledge of hoisting equipment, load calculation and rigging techniques — training that goes beyond the scope of this basic NR-35 course.

  • Every suspension point must have a structural design with an engineer's ART — no informal sizing

  • Minimum safety factor: 8:1 for steel cables; 5:1 for textile rigging slings

  • Safety cable mandatory on EACH suspended piece of equipment — independent of the main cable or the hoist chain

  • Inspection of all accessories (shackles, slings, cables, chains) before each hoist

  • Total isolation of the area below the hoisting path — spotters positioned at each access point

  • Never stay under a suspended load — not even for "a second" to position it


10.3 Operation of Aerial Work Platforms (MEWP) at Events

  • Check the platform's load capacity versus the total weight of the occupants + equipment to be carried

  • Level the ground under the outriggers — use support boards on uneven or wet ground

  • Deploy ALL outriggers before raising the platform — never operate without outriggers

  • Keep the safety harness connected to the platform's anchorage point (if available) throughout the elevated operation

  • Do not exceed the maximum number of occupants indicated on the platform's capacity plate

  • Do not operate a MEWP with wind above 46 km/h — even platforms rated for use in higher wind

  • Keep a minimum distance of 3 meters from any overhead power line — check for lines in the area before positioning

  • Never use the platform as a hoisting platform (moving loads with the platform elevated without an approved hoisting system)


10.4 Portable Ladders — New Rules (NR-35, Annex III — 2025)

The 2025 update to NR-35 added the new Annex III with specific requirements for individual-use ladders in work at height. For events, the main requirements are:

  • Portable ladders: use only when aerial work platforms are not technically viable — the MEWP is always preferable

  • Correct angle: 75° from the horizontal (4:1 ratio — for every 4m of height, 1m of base)

  • Usage limit: never go above the second-to-last rung — the last rung does not offer a safe support point

  • Mandatory stabilization: a person holding the base of the ladder throughout use — or securing the base with an anti-slip device

  • Prohibition of painted wood: wooden ladders must have a transparent finish that allows visual inspection of the fibers

  • On unstable surfaces (sand, grass, wet asphalt): use an anti-slip base or secure with stakes when possible


  11    CASE STUDIES — REAL ACCIDENTS AND LESSONS LEARNED

Module 11 — Learning from What Went Wrong

The study of real work-at-height accidents at events has an educational and technical purpose: understanding the mechanisms of systemic failure that lead to the accident makes it possible to identify and eliminate those same risk vectors in the operation of the trained professional. Each case presented is a sequence of decisions and failures that, analyzed in hindsight, reveal where preventive intervention would have been possible and effective.

Case 1 — Fall while positioning equipment on the rig

Scenario: A lighting technician climbs onto the grid truss 8 meters high to position a moving head. He is wearing a full-body safety harness on his body but did not connect the lanyard to the anchorage point because "it would be a quick 2-minute job." As he reaches out to reach the equipment's fixing point, he loses his balance and falls. Fracture of the pelvis, ribs and cranial trauma. Eight months off work.

Analysis: The harness was present but was PPE with no effect — a harness that is not connected does not protect. The phrase "it would be quick" is the documented normalization of unsafe behavior. Absence of effective oversight by the safety officer at the time of the activity.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

A full-body harness with no lanyard connected is merely a clothing accessory. The connection to the anchorage point is the action that creates protection. Establish it as a non-negotiable cultural rule: do not take a single step above 2 meters without being connected. Double lanyard — connect leg 2 before disconnecting leg 1.


Case 2 — Fixture falling onto the audience area during the show

Scenario: During the second set of a show, a 12 kg LED PAR fixture comes loose from the rig and falls onto the audience area 10 meters below. The equipment was held only by its clamp (the hook that fixes it to the truss). No safety cable had been installed. Two spectators injured, one with moderate TBI. The show was halted by order of the Fire Department.

Analysis: The absence of a safety cable is the single failure that turned an incident (a defective clamp) into an accident with a casualty. The clamp fails through fatigue, thermal overload or incorrect installation — the safety cable ensures the equipment does not fall even when the main fixing element fails.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

The safety cable (secondary safety cable) is MANDATORY on all suspended equipment at events with an audience below. It is not optional and cannot be replaced by the "quality of the clamp." A post-assembly visual inspection must verify the safety cable on 100% of suspended equipment before releasing the area to the audience.


Case 3 — Delay tower collapse during an event

Scenario: An aluminum PA delay tower 8 meters high, positioned on a sandy field, with the manufacturer's standard base and no additional anchoring to the ground. On the third day of the festival, a 55 km/h wind (above the 46 km/h limit for work at height) causes the tower to topple during the show. The tower falls onto the audience protection barrier; three people injured, one with a broken arm.

Analysis: Wind speed above the prohibitive limit. Absence of an anemometer or a formal weather protocol for continuous monitoring. The tower's base was not anchored to the ground despite the unstable sandy soil. The presence of the audience during extreme wind conditions is the consequence of a nonexistent weather protocol.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 3

A formal weather protocol is mandatory for outdoor events with self-standing structures. Install an anemometer, define operation-stop limits (46 km/h for work at height / 60 km/h for the audience remaining under structures) and designate someone responsible for the decision to stop.


  12    NR-35 COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST — LIVE EVENTS

📋  CLASSIFICATION OF YOUR EVENT

Most events (concerts, festivals, corporate) fall under NR-20 CLASS I, requiring the 8-hour Basic Course with a validity of 3 years. Large festivals with multiple generators, significant fuel stock and professional pyrotechnics may fall under CLASS II, requiring 14-hour Intermediate training.

  01    FLAMMABLES AND COMBUSTIBLES IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 1 — Introduction: NR-20 and the World of Events

Regulatory Standard No. 20 (NR-20) sets the minimum requirements for managing occupational health and safety against accident risk factors arising from the extraction, production, storage, transfer, handling and manipulation of flammables and combustible liquids. It was completely overhauled by SEPRT Ordinance No. 1,360/2019 and applies to all workers who, directly or indirectly, interact with flammables and combustibles — even if that is not their main activity.

The events sector deals daily with flammable and combustible substances, often without proper awareness of the risks involved. A typical mid-sized concert may have: two 200kVA diesel generators consuming 40 liters per hour, three propane cylinders feeding fire machines on stage, two CO₂ cylinders for cryo jet effects, fog machines and haze machines running continuously, and indoor pyrotechnic products positioned on stage. This scenario concentrates, in a single temporary space, a volume of flammables equivalent to that of a small gas station — with the difference of having hundreds or thousands of people nearby.



1.1 Flammables and Combustibles Found at Events

Combustible Liquids

  • Diesel: the main fuel for large electric generators (50kVA to 500kVA+). Flash point: ~52°C. It does not ignite easily at room temperature, but its vapors are flammable.

  • Gasoline: fuel for smaller portable generators and support vehicles. Flash point: -42°C. EXTREMELY flammable — its vapors ignite at temperatures far below ambient.

  • Ethyl alcohol (ethanol): present in bars, dressing rooms, cleaning areas. Flash point: 13°C. Highly flammable.

Flammable and Special Gases

  • LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas): used in catering kitchens, food trucks and gas generators. Heavier than air — it accumulates in low points and confined spaces.

  • Propane: fuel for fire machines and fire effects at concerts. Highly flammable and stored under pressure.

  • CO₂ (carbon dioxide): although not flammable, it is stored under high pressure (50-60 bar) and can cause asphyxiation in confined environments. Used in cryo jet effects (CO₂ cannons, cryo jets).

  • Acetylene: used in welding and cutting metal structures during stage assembly. Extremely flammable and explosive — explosive range of 2.5% to 81%.

Pyrotechnic Products

  • Outdoor fireworks: they contain black powder and pyrotechnic compositions — they are EXPLOSIVES regulated by the Brazilian Army (R-105 and REG/T 02). PROHIBITED for use indoors.

  • Indoor fireworks (Gerbs, Flames, Airburst, Silver Jets, Micro Mines, Micro Comets): low-power pyrotechnic devices designed for indoor use, with a controlled effect and a smaller range of action.

  • Sparkular (Cold Spark / Ti Powder): a machine that generates cold sparks using titanium powder. Titanium powder is a flammable metallic powder — fire category D.


⚠️  APPLICABILITY OF NR-20 TO EVENTS

Even though the events professional does not work at a gas station or refinery, the presence of diesel generators, propane cylinders for fire machines, LPG in food trucks and pyrotechnic products makes NR-20 directly applicable. Ignorance of the standard does not exempt anyone from civil and criminal liability in the event of an accident.


  02    CHARACTERISTICS, PROPERTIES, HAZARDS AND RISKS

Module 2 — The Science of Flammables: What the Professional Needs to Know

Understanding the physical-chemical properties of flammables is not an academic exercise — it is operational knowledge that determines the difference between a safe decision and an accident. The events professional who knows what flash point, explosive range and vapor density are makes better decisions in the field, even under deadline pressure.

2.1 Fundamental Concepts

Flash Point

The minimum temperature at which a liquid releases enough vapors to form a flammable mixture with air. The LOWER the flash point, the MORE DANGEROUS. Gasoline: -42°C | Alcohol: 13°C | Diesel: ~52°C

LEL — Lower Explosive Limit

The minimum concentration of vapor in air for combustion to occur. Below the LEL, the mixture is too lean to ignite.

UEL — Upper Explosive Limit

The maximum concentration. Above the UEL, the mixture is too rich (little oxygen). The danger is the range BETWEEN the LEL and UEL.

Explosive Range

LPG: 2.1% to 9.5% — narrow. Gasoline: 1.4% to 7.6%. Acetylene: 2.5% to 81% — huge, extremely dangerous.

Auto-Ignition Temperature

The temperature at which the material ignites spontaneously, without the need for an external spark or flame.

Vapor Density

Vapors HEAVIER than air (LPG, gasoline): accumulate in low points — pits, basements, understage. LIGHTER vapors (acetylene): rise and accumulate at the ceiling.


2.2 Main Hazards at Events

Hazard

Cause at Events

Consequence

Prevention

Fire

Combustion of spilled diesel, leaked gasoline or gas

Destruction of equipment, structures, burns, deaths

Eliminate ignition sources near flammables

Explosion

Ignition of vapors accumulated in a confined area (LPG under the stage)

Lethal pressure wave, structural destruction

Ventilation, gas detector, never LPG in a confined space
