  03    CLASSIFICATION OF FACILITIES — CLASSES I, II AND III

Module 3 — Event Class and Required Training Level

NR-20 classifies facilities where flammables are present into three classes, based on the nature of the activity and the volume of flammable substances handled. This classification directly determines the level of training required of workers and the validity of certificates.


Class

💡  PRACTICAL CLASSIFICATION

Most live events (concerts, weddings, graduations, corporate events) fall under CLASS I. Large music festivals with multiple high-power generators, diesel stocks above 1,000 liters and professional pyrotechnic operations may fall under CLASS II. Consult the local Fire Department and the OHS professional to define the correct classification for each event.


  04    COLLECTIVE AND INDIVIDUAL CONTROLS

Module 4 — CPE and PPE for Work with Flammables

In accordance with Annex II of NR-20, the second mandatory training topic is: collective and individual controls for work with flammables. The hierarchy of controls established by NR-01 applies in full: collective measures (CPE) take priority over individual measures (PPE). PPE is the last layer of protection — not the first.

4.1 Collective Controls (CPE)

  • Adequate ventilation in storage and generator-operation areas — to prevent the buildup of flammable vapors and carbon monoxide

  • Isolation and signage of areas with flammables: cones, tapes, signs reading "NO SMOKING", "FLAMMABLE", "RESTRICTED ACCESS"

  • Grounding and bonding of metal tanks and containers — to prevent static electricity discharge during refueling

  • Containment trays under generators to retain fuel and lubricating-oil leaks

  • Fire extinguishers suited to the type of material: dry chemical powder (DCP) for flammable liquids (Class B), CO₂ for electrical equipment (Class C)

  • Gas detectors (LPG, CO) in hazard areas with audible and visual alarms

  • Emergency shower and eyewash station where large volumes of fuel are handled

  • Explosion-proof lighting in classified areas with flammable vapors


4.2 Individual Controls (PPE)

PPE

Purpose

When to Use

PVC or nitrile gloves

Protection against fuels, solvents and chemical fluids

Generator refueling, handling of special-effects fluids

Safety glasses

Protection against splashes and flying particles

Refueling, cylinder handling, pyrotechnics operation

FR (Flame Resistant) clothing

Primary protection against flames and radiant heat

Pyrotechnics and fire-machine operators

Mask with organic-vapor filter

Respiratory protection against fuel vapors

Prolonged refueling, area with a leak

Antistatic footwear

Prevent static electrical discharge that can ignite vapors

Every refueling operation and handling of flammables

Ear protection

Noise attenuation — generators produce > 85 dB continuously

Operation near running generators

Helmet with chin strap

Head protection in pyrotechnics assembly areas

Assembly and disassembly of pyrotechnic equipment


  05    IGNITION SOURCES AND THEIR CONTROL AT EVENTS

Module 5 — Identify and Eliminate What Ignites the Fire

An ignition source is any element capable of supplying enough energy to start the combustion of flammable vapors or gases. At live events, ignition sources are spread throughout the operating area and often go unnoticed in the heat of setup. Identifying and controlling ignition sources in the vicinity of flammables is the central preventive strategy of NR-20.

The fundamental strategic concept is SEPARATION: keeping ignition sources and flammable materials apart by distances that ensure any released flammable vapor dilutes below the Lower Explosive Limit before reaching the heat source. This separation must be physical (distance, barriers) and administrative (prohibitions, signage, procedures).

5.1 Most Common Ignition Sources at Events

  • Open flames: cigarettes, lighters, birthday candles, barbecue grills, catering stoves — absolutely prohibited in flammables areas

  • Electrical sparks: short circuits in undersized temporary installations, poorly made connections in distribution boards

  • Mechanical sparks: metal tools striking metal surfaces — use spark-free tools in hazard areas

  • Static electricity: discharge when touching ungrounded metal tanks during refueling — ALWAYS ground before refueling

  • Hot surfaces: generator exhausts, dimmer racks, high-power amplifiers, heating elements

  • Cell phones and radios: electronic devices can theoretically generate sparks — as a precaution, keep them out of refueling areas

  • Pyrotechnics itself: when poorly positioned or fired with an unfavorable wind, it can be an ignition source for nearby combustible materials

5.2 Ignition-Source Control Measures

  1. Strictly prohibit smoking in ALL areas with flammables — mark with visible signs at all entrances

  2. Ground fuel tanks and metal containers to the generator BEFORE refueling

  3. Keep a minimum distance of 3 meters between operating generators and any combustible material

  4. Turn off electrical equipment before connecting or disconnecting generator power cables

  5. Use spark-free tools (bronze or aluminum alloys) in classified hazard areas

  6. Verify minimum safety distances between pyrotechnics and combustible materials — in accordance with the local Fire Department's Technical Instruction


  06    ELECTRIC GENERATORS: SAFE OPERATION AND REFUELING

Module 6 — The Energy Heart of the Event and Its Risks

Electric generators are the machines that concentrate the most flammables-related risks in the events sector. They can range from small portable gasoline generators (2-5kVA) to large industrial diesel generators (100-500kVA+) mounted in refrigerated containers. Incorrect operation of a generator is the most common cause of flammables accidents at events — and most of these accidents occur during refueling.

6.1 Generator Positioning

  • VENTILATED location: never in an enclosed environment or understage without adequate ventilation — risk of carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning, odorless and lethal within minutes

  • Away from combustible materials: minimum 3 meters from curtains, carpets, scenery, tent structures and canvas

  • Away from public circulation areas: noise above 85 dB + exhaust gases + radiated heat

  • On a firm, level surface — instability can cause leaks

  • Rain protection with a cover that maintains side ventilation — never seal it completely

  • Metal containment tray under the generator to retain fuel and oil leaks

  • Mandatory grounding of the metal housing — ground wire connected to the system's grounding point

  • DCP or CO₂ extinguisher positioned no more than 5 meters away — accessible and unobstructed




🔴  CRITICAL REFUELING PROCEDURE

NEVER refuel a generator with a hot or running engine. Wait a minimum of 10 minutes after shutting down — hot surfaces can ignite gasoline or diesel vapors immediately. Isolate the area, put up signage, ground the container to the generator, refuel slowly. If there was a spill: clean it with absorbent material BEFORE restarting.


6.2 Safe Refueling Sequence — Step by Step

  1. TURN OFF the generator and wait a minimum cooling time of 10 minutes

  2. Isolate the area with cones and barrier tape at a minimum radius of 3 meters

  3. Post signs: "NO SMOKING", "NO CELL PHONES", "NO OPEN FLAME"

  4. GROUND the fuel container to the generator with a bonding cable

  5. Use an approved, sealed and labeled container — never improvised jerry cans

  6. Refuel slowly, avoiding overflow — never fill to the brim (thermal expansion can cause leaks)

  7. If there was a spill: clean it with absorbent material (sand, blanket) BEFORE restarting

  8. Store spare fuel in a ventilated, signposted location away from the generator

  9. After refueling: check that the tank cap is closed, that there are no leaks, and remove the barrier

6.3 Monitoring During Operation

  • Check voltage and frequency of the power before connecting sensitive equipment

  • Monitor engine temperature and fuel level during the event — assign a person in charge

  • Do not overload the generator beyond its rated capacity (kVA — check the data sheet)

  • In case of mechanical failure with a leak: SHUT DOWN immediately, isolate the area, call the fire brigade

  • Monitor CO with a portable detector if the generator is near confined work areas


  07    PYROTECHNICS AND SPECIAL EFFECTS AT EVENTS

Module 7 — Pyrotechnics: Beauty Controlled by Regulation and Certification

Pyrotechnics at events is the point of greatest risk concentration and the strictest regulatory requirement of NR-20. It is governed by multiple layered laws: NR-20 (flammables), NR-19 (explosives), the Brazilian Army's Regulation R-105 (control of controlled products), Technical Regulations REG/T 02 (fireworks) and REG/T 03 (pyrotechnic shows), and the Technical Instructions of the state Fire Departments. Failure to comply with any of these regulatory layers constitutes a criminal offense.

🔴  ABSOLUTE RULE — BLASTER CERTIFICATION

Professional-use pyrotechnic products at events may only be handled by a PYROTECHNIC BLASTER — a professional holding a Pyrotechnics Technician License and a Certificate of Registration (CR) from the Brazilian Army. The show must have a Technical Responsibility Note (ART) signed by the blaster. It is a CRIME to allow any events technician, roadie or producer to handle professional pyrotechnic products without this specific certification.


7.1 Classification of Pyrotechnic Products for Events

Category

Characteristics

Use Environment

Specific Requirements

OUTDOOR fireworks

Larger caliber, higher temperature, range of 50 to 200+ meters depending on caliber

ONLY open areas with safety distances

Prohibited for use indoors or near the public without a minimum distance

INDOOR fireworks

Controlled effect: Gerbs, Flames, Airburst, Silver Jets, Micro Mines, Micro Comets

Enclosed and semi-enclosed stages, near the public

When an open area does not allow outdoor distances — check the Fire Department's Technical Instruction

Sparkular / Cold Spark

Titanium sparks at ~70-100°C. Classification: flammable metallic powder (Class D)

Indoor and outdoor stages

Minimum distance: 2m from the public, 3m from combustibles

Fire Machines / Flame FX

Real controlled flames using propane/LPG. Higher thermal risk

Stages with a controlled area

Minimum distance: 3-5m from the public and performers, 5m from combustibles


7.2 Mandatory Documentation for Pyrotechnic Shows

  • Safety Plan drawn up by the blaster, submitted to and approved by the local Fire Department

  • ART (Technical Responsibility Note) of the pyrotechnic blaster

  • Authorization from the state Military Fire Department for the pyrotechnic show

  • Certificate of Registration (CR) from the Brazilian Army for the blaster

  • Purchase invoice for the pyrotechnic products (traceability is mandatory)

  • After the show: inspection of the firing area to identify undetonated remnants (dud rounds) — dispose of them in accordance with Army regulations


7.3 Safety Procedures for the Show

  1. Prior inspection of the site: distances, combustible materials, emergency exits, ventilation, ground

  2. Products firmly secured on stable mounts and correctly aimed, according to the blaster's design

  3. Firing test (dry run) without a pyrotechnic charge before the event, with the area isolated and the fire brigade present

  4. DCP extinguishers and firefighting materials positioned in the firing area

  5. Identified fire-brigade members positioned at strategic points during the show

  6. Radio communication between the blaster, production coordination and the fire brigade

  7. Complete inspection of the firing area after the show — collect all dud rounds before releasing the area


  08    SPECIAL EFFECTS: FOG, HAZE, CO₂, SPARKULAR AND FIRE MACHINES

Module 8 — The Effects That Enchant and the Risks That Must Be Managed

Special effects are an essential part of the professional-concert experience. Each of them combines technology, art and specific technical risk. The events professional must master the risks of each type of effect and the corresponding safety procedures — not to eliminate the effect, but to operate it safely.

8.1 Smoke (Fog) and Haze Machines

Fog machines vaporize fluid (glycol- or mineral-oil-based) through a block internally heated to ~300°C, generating dense clouds of white smoke. Haze machines atomize the fluid into finer particles, creating a subtle haze that highlights lighting beams.

  • Use ONLY the fluid recommended by the manufacturer — never improvise with other liquids (risk of overheating and internal fire)

  • Do not block the vapor outlets — risk of overheating the heating block

  • Keep it away from the venue's smoke detectors (or deactivate them with formal authorization from the Fire Department)

  • Ensure adequate ventilation — excessive smoke indoors can cause respiratory irritation and reduce the visibility of emergency exits

8.2 CO₂ Jets (CO₂ Cannons / Cryo Jets)

CO₂ jet effects use carbon dioxide cylinders under high pressure (50-60 bar). Liquid CO₂, when released, expands rapidly, forming a freezing white cloud (-78°C). Although it is not flammable, it poses serious risks:

  • ASPHYXIATION: CO₂ displaces the oxygen in the air. Understage or in a pit, it can reduce O₂ to dangerous levels within seconds — risk of death

  • COLD BURNS: direct contact with the jet causes severe burns from extreme cold — keep a distance from the public in accordance with the manufacturer's specifications

  • PRESSURE: high-pressure cylinders (50-60 bar) can explode if damaged, exposed to excessive heat or with a faulty valve

  • Cylinders must be secured VERTICALLY on a suitable stand — never lying down or loose

  • Check the validity of the cylinder's hydrostatic test (mandatory every 5 years per INMETRO)

8.3 Sparkular (Cold Spark / Ti Powder)

The Sparkular generates "cold" sparks using heated titanium granules. Although the spark temperature is lower (~70-100°C) than traditional pyrotechnics (300-1000°C), they still pose risks that require a safety protocol:

  • Titanium powder is a FLAMMABLE metallic powder (Class D) — store it in a dry, ventilated place, away from sources of moisture and heat

  • The sparks can damage delicate fabrics and cause minor burns on prolonged contact with skin

  • Minimum distance of 2 meters from the public and 3 meters from highly combustible materials

  • Never operate near smoke detectors, gas cylinders or exposed fuels

  • Use only the granules specified by the manufacturer — NEVER improvise with other metallic powders

8.4 Fire Machines

Fire machines (wave flame, U-flame, dragon) produce real controlled flames using propane or LPG under pressure. They are the highest-risk effect at live events — real flames + flammable gas under pressure + proximity to the public and performers.

  • Fed by propane/LPG cylinders — a highly flammable gas stored under pressure

  • Minimum distance of 3-5 meters from the public and performers (in accordance with the manufacturer's specifications)

  • Minimum distance of 5 meters from combustible materials (curtains, scenery, tent structure)

  • Operation ONLY by a technician trained by the manufacturer with specific documentation

  • Mandatory test (dry run) before the event with the area isolated and an extinguisher available

  • Inspection of hoses, connections and valves before EACH use — check for wear and integrity

  • Check for the absence of gas leaks with a soap-and-water solution on all connections

  • In case of a leak: close the cylinder IMMEDIATELY, ventilate the area, isolate it, do not trigger ignition


⚠️  GOLDEN RULE — SPECIAL EFFECTS

Every special effect that uses a flammable, pressurized or high-temperature substance must go through: (1) Equipment inspection before each event; (2) Operation test (dry run) without an audience; (3) Verification of safety distances; (4) An extinguisher positioned within reach; (5) The fire brigade informed of the position and type of effect. No step is optional.


  09    FIRE PROTECTION AND FIREFIGHTING WITH FLAMMABLES

Module 9 — When the Fire Was Never Meant to Exist: Correct Response

The first response to any flammables fire at a live event is ALWAYS to assess: is this fire controllable with the available extinguisher, within the next 30 seconds, without risk of explosion? If the answer is no — evacuate and call the Fire Department. Fighting a fire with a portable extinguisher should only be attempted on fires in their early stages, by a trained person, with a safe escape route behind them.

9.1 Fire Classes and Correct Extinguishers

Class

Material

Correct Extinguisher

NEVER use

Occurrence at Events

A

Solids: wood, fabric, paper, rubber

Pressurized water or ABC DCP

—

Stage timber, curtains, carpets, tent canvas

B

Flammable liquids: gasoline, diesel, alcohol, liquid propane

BC DCP or ABC DCP or mechanical foam

NEVER water (it splashes the burning liquid)

Generator fuel, leaking propane cylinders

C

ENERGIZED electrical equipment

CO₂ or BC/ABC DCP

NEVER water (it conducts electricity, fatal shock)

Distribution boards, amplifier racks, generators

D

Special Class D chemical powder — dry sand

NEVER water, CO₂ or conventional DCP

Sparkular titanium powder — an unusual but possible fire

K

Food court, food trucks, catering kitchens


9.2 Positioning of Extinguishers at Events with Flammables

  • ABC DCP extinguisher (6 kg minimum) near generators, refueling area, gas cylinders, fire machines

  • CO₂ extinguisher (6 kg) near distribution boards, electrical-equipment racks

  • Position it with the top no more than 1.60 m from the floor — ergonomic access

  • Floor beneath the extinguisher painted red — never block it with equipment or boxes

  • Extinguisher inspected monthly — check the pressure gauge, seal and expiry label

9.3 Extinguisher Use Technique — PASS Method

Every worker in contact with flammables must master the PASS extinguisher technique:

  • P — PULL the safety pin (removes the seal that prevents activation)

  • A — AIM the nozzle at the BASE of the flames, not at the top

  • S — SQUEEZE the trigger with steady pressure

  • S — SWEEP horizontally at the base of the fire, covering its entire width


  10    EMERGENCY PLAN AND EVACUATION PROCEDURES

Module 10 — Organizing the Chaos Before It Happens

Every event with flammables must have a documented Emergency Plan before setup begins. The plan is not bureaucracy — it is the script that transforms impulsive reactions into coordinated actions in the situations of greatest psychological pressure that an events professional can face.

10.1 Minimum Structure of the Emergency Plan

  1. Identification of emergency scenarios: generator fire, gas leak, pyrotechnic accident, CO poisoning, CO₂ cold burn

  2. Alarm and communication procedure: who alerts whom, emergency radio channel, phone number of each person in charge

  3. Escape routes signposted and unobstructed — check before each event

  4. A meeting point defined and signposted outside the venue

  5. Fire brigade assigned, identified (orange vest or armband) and with equipment available

  6. External contacts: Fire Department (193), SAMU emergency medical service (192), Civil Defense, reference hospital

  7. General power-cutoff procedure: location of the boards, shutdown sequence

  8. Gas-valve shutoff procedure: location of each cylinder and the person in charge

10.2 In Case of a Fuel Leak

  • Isolate the area immediately — minimum 15 meters of distance

  • Eliminate ALL ignition sources: turn off equipment, ban cell phones, put out cigarettes

  • Ventilate the area: open doors and windows, use spark-free fans if available

  • Contain the leak with absorbent material (blankets, sand) — NEVER wash it away with water (it spreads the contaminated area)

  • Call the fire brigade and, if necessary, the Fire Department

  • If the leak is gas (LPG/propane): evacuate the area and do NOT try to close the valve if there is a flame — risk of explosion

10.3 In Case of a Flammables Fire

  • Trigger the emergency alarm and communicate by radio immediately

  • Evacuate people from the immediate area — priority over any equipment

  • If the fire is small and controllable: fight it with a DCP extinguisher (Class B) — NEVER water on flammable liquids

  • If the fire is out of control: evacuate completely and call the Fire Department (193)

  • Cut the main power (generators, distribution boards)

  • Close the gas valves if it is safe — never if there is a risk of exposure to the fire


  11    FIRST AID IN FLAMMABLES ACCIDENTS

Module 11 — First Aid: Reducing the Harm Before the Ambulance Arrives

First aid in flammables accidents does not replace professional medical care — it reduces the harm in the first decisive minutes. In all cases, CALLING SAMU (192) is the first priority, simultaneous with any first-aid action.

11.1 Thermal Burns

  1. Move the victim away from the heat source or flame — ensure the rescuer's safety first

  2. Put out the fire on the clothes: roll on the ground, cover with a blanket, use an extinguisher — never pull off fabric stuck to the skin

  3. Cool the burn with clean, cold running water for 10 to 20 minutes — never ice

  4. Do NOT apply toothpaste, butter, oils, creams — they worsen the tissue damage

  5. Do NOT pop blisters — risk of severe infection

  6. Cover with a clean, moist cloth — never cotton (its fibers stick to the burn)

  7. Call SAMU (192), stating the extent and location of the burn

11.2 Carbon Monoxide (CO) Poisoning

CO is produced by the incomplete combustion of diesel and gasoline in generators. It is ODORLESS and COLORLESS — the victim does not notice the progressive poisoning. Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, loss of consciousness. At high concentrations: death within minutes.

  • Move the victim IMMEDIATELY to a place with fresh air — the rescuer must protect their own breathing when entering the area

  • If unconscious: check breathing and pulse — start CPR if necessary

  • Call SAMU (192), reporting a suspected carbon monoxide poisoning

  • Keep the victim lying down and warm — do not let them walk (exertion increases O₂ consumption)

  • Do NOT return to the contaminated area without an adequate respiratory protective mask

⚠️  HIDDEN DANGER — CO UNDERSTAGE

Carbon monoxide is responsible for silent deaths at events. Technicians working understage or in areas near generators without adequate ventilation can be progressively poisoned without realizing it — the initial symptoms (headache, drowsiness) are easily mistaken for fatigue. Install a portable CO detector in every work area near generators.


11.3 Cold Burns from Contact with CO₂

A direct CO₂ jet at -78°C causes cold burns as severe as thermal burns. The tissue freezes rapidly and can develop necrosis if the treatment is incorrect.

  • Warm the affected area gradually with lukewarm water (between 37°C and 42°C) — NEVER hot water

  • Do not rub the injured area — frozen tissue is extremely fragile

  • Cover with a clean, dry cloth — do not pop any blisters that form

  • Call SAMU (192) and take the victim to a hospital


  12    LEGAL RESPONSIBILITIES, DOCUMENTATION AND PENALTIES

Module 12 — Legal Compliance: Documentation That Protects and Proves

12.1 Mandatory Documentation for Events with Flammables

  • NR-20 training certificates for all workers involved (valid and available on site)

  • Emergency Plan documented, signed and communicated to all coordinators

  • Risk Analysis specific to flammables (SRA — Simplified Risk Analysis or PRA — Preliminary Risk Analysis)

  • For pyrotechnics: blaster's ART + Fire Department authorization + Army CR + pyrotechnic Safety Plan

  • For generators: inspection report, grounding-resistance measurement (report issued by a qualified technician)

  • SDS (Safety Data Sheet) for all fuels and fluids used

  • Monthly extinguisher inspection records — available for inspection by authorities


12.2 Chain of Responsibilities

Employer / Producer

Ensure NR-20 training for all workers; provide PPE; draw up the Emergency Plan; hire a qualified blaster for pyrotechnics; obtain Fire Department authorizations; make suitable extinguishers available

Worker

Follow all safety procedures; use the PPE provided; report identified risks to their supervisor; exercise the right of refusal in a situation of serious and imminent risk

Pyrotechnic Blaster

Full technical responsibility for the pyrotechnic show; issue the ART; calculate and ensure safety distances; carry out the post-firing inspection; dispose of dud rounds in accordance with regulations

OHS Coordinator

Draw up and implement the Emergency Plan; supervise refueling procedures; verify the pre-event checklist; train the fire brigade; oversee the use of PPE


12.3 Penalties for Non-Compliance

  • Non-compliance with NR-20: progressive administrative fines under NR-28

  • Flammables accident due to negligence: civil liability (compensation) and criminal liability (negligent bodily harm, negligent homicide)

  • Pyrotechnic show without authorization: a crime under Law No. 10,826/2003 (Disarmament Statute — the article concerning explosives)

  • Use of pyrotechnics by an unqualified person: the crime of illegal use of explosives, punishable by 1 to 5 years' imprisonment

📌  HISTORICAL LESSON — KISS NIGHTCLUB (2013)

The Kiss Nightclub fire in Santa Maria (RS) on January 27, 2013: 242 deaths caused by indoor fireworks (a Gerb) set off by a band without authorization, which struck highly flammable polyurethane acoustic foam on the ceiling — with no adequate emergency exits, no fire brigade, no Fire Department authorization. It is the most painful and definitive example of the legal, human and irreversible consequences of failing to comply with NR-20 and NR-23.


  13    CASE STUDIES — FLAMMABLES ACCIDENTS AT EVENTS

Module 13 — Learning from What Went Wrong

Studying real cases of flammables accidents at events is not intended to be sensationalist — it is technical and educational in purpose. Each case represents a set of systemic failures which, understood correctly, allow the professional to identify and eliminate the same risk vectors in their own operation.

Case 1 — Fire During the Refueling of a Gasoline Generator

Scenario: A roadie refuels a portable gasoline generator during the event break, with the engine off but still hot. When gasoline overflows at the filler neck, gasoline vapors are ignited by the heat of the exhaust, still at ~200°C. The fire reaches the reserve jerry can positioned 1 meter away. In 45 seconds, the fire reaches the stage backdrop curtains.

What went wrong: Engine still hot (did not wait for it to cool); reserve jerry can placed less than 3 meters away; area not isolated; extinguisher placed inside the hazard area, inaccessible during the fire.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

Wait a MINIMUM of 10 minutes after shutdown; store reserve fuel outside the generator's operating area; position the extinguisher within reach BEFORE starting to refuel — not inside the hazard area.


Case 2 — CO Poisoning Under the Stage

Scenario: A diesel generator positioned partially under the stage to reduce the noise perceived by production. Exhaust gases accumulate in the confined understage space (insufficient ventilation). Technicians making cabling adjustments begin to experience headaches and dizziness. One technician loses consciousness. Taken into fresh air, two recover — one is hospitalized with severe CO poisoning.

What went wrong: Generator in a confined area without cross ventilation; no portable CO detector; lack of training on recognizing the symptoms of CO poisoning.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

NEVER position a generator in a confined area or understage. Install a portable CO detector in every work area near generators. Train the entire technical team to recognize the symptoms of CO poisoning.


Case 3 — Pyrotechnic Accident at an Indoor Concert

Scenario: A production company hires pyrotechnic effects for a concert in an enclosed venue. The technician in charge is not a qualified blaster — he is a general lighting technician. He uses outdoor fireworks (35mm caliber) indoors out of ignorance of the difference. The sparks strike stage fabric with no flame-retardant treatment. A fire breaks out on stage during the show with an audience present. Chaotic evacuation — three people injured by trampling.

What went wrong: No qualified blaster; use of outdoor fireworks indoors (PROHIBITED); stage materials with no flame-retardant treatment; no safety plan; no Fire Department authorization.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 3

ONLY a qualified blaster operates pyrotechnics. ONLY indoor fireworks in enclosed environments. Require a flame-retardancy certificate for all stage materials. Fire Department authorization is non-negotiable.

"Fire gives no warning — prevention makes no compromises."

  01    FUNDAMENTALS OF NR-23 IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

NR-23 and the Real Fire Risk at Live Events

Regulatory Standard No. 23 — Fire Protection — establishes the minimum measures for fire prevention and firefighting in workplaces. Originally published in 1978 and formally revised in 2022 by MTE Ordinance No. 2,770, it defines the fundamental principles of protection, referring to the state legislation of the Military Fire Brigades and to the ABNT technical standards for the technical specifics of each type of installation and activity.

In the live events sector, the fire risk takes on extraordinarily serious dimensions due to a combination of factors that rarely coexist in other workplaces: a high load of combustible materials in temporary spaces (curtains, tarpaulins, scenic coverings, carpets, stage timber), the simultaneous presence of high-power ignition sources (temporary electrical installations, generators, special effects, pyrotechnics), a concentration of audiences that can reach tens of thousands of people, and evacuation routes that are frequently narrow, improvised, or obstructed by the event's own logistics.

The fire at the Kiss Nightclub, in Santa Maria (RS), on January 27, 2013, which claimed 242 lives, is the historical landmark that precipitated the enactment of Federal Law No. 13,425/2017 — known as the Kiss Law — and represents the extreme case of what happens when all these factors combine with no operative preventive system in place. The Kiss Law establishes national guidelines for fire and panic safety in establishments and public-assembly events, and must be known and applied by the safety coordinator of any live event in Brazil.




Creation ordinance

MTb No. 3,214/1978 — one of the original NRs

Last formal update

MTE Ordinance No. 2,770/2022 — harmonization of structure; addition of Objective and Scope of application

Mandatory complementary basis

State legislation of the Fire Brigades (state NPT/IT) + ABNT standards

Kiss Law

Federal Law No. 13,425/2017 — national guidelines for public assembly

Fire brigade

ABNT NBR 14276:2020 — Fire and emergency brigade — Requirements and procedures

Emergency plan

ABNT NBR 15219 — Emergency fire plan — Requirements

Extinguishers

ABNT NBR 12693 (extinguisher systems) + NBR 12962 (inspection, maintenance, and recharging)


⚠️  LEGAL BASIS

Failure to comply with NR-23 at public-assembly events may constitute a criminal offense for exposing third parties to risk of life (Art. 132 of the Penal Code) and strict civil liability in the event of an accident. Since the Kiss Law, event organizers are personally liable for the safety of the public, regardless of having delegated the implementation of the measures to third parties.


  02    FIRE CLASSES AND EXTINGUISHING AGENTS

The Code of the Flames: Technical Classification of Fire

The classification of fires by class is the technical foundation of the entire fire protection strategy. It determines which extinguishing agent must be used, which portable extinguisher must be available in each zone of the event, and what conduct the brigade member must adopt when facing each type of fire outbreak. Using the wrong extinguishing agent not only fails to put out the fire — it can intensify it or create additional risks to the firefighter.

2.1 The Five Fire Classes

NR-23, in line with the ABNT standards, recognizes five fire classes, each associated with specific combustion materials and conditions:

Class

Combustible Material

Characteristics

Recommended Extinguishing Agent

Occurrence at Events

A

Common solids: wood, paper, fabric, rubber

Burns forming embers — leaves solid residue

Pressurized water (better penetration) or ABC powder

Stage timber, curtains, scenic covering, tent tarpaulin, packaging

B

Flammable liquids: gasoline, alcohol, diesel, paint, solvent

Burns at the surface — no solid residue

BC powder, ABC powder, CO₂, mechanical foam — NEVER water (splashes the liquid)

Generator fuel, maintenance solvent, sanitizing alcohol, scenography paints

C

Energized electrical equipment

Risk of shock — a conductive agent cannot be used

CO₂ or BC/ABC powder — NEVER water or foam (they conduct electricity)

Electrical panels, generators, PA system, signal-processing racks

D

Combustible metals: magnesium, titanium, sodium

Extremely high combustion temperature — reacts with water

Specific D powder or dry sand — never water or CO₂

Rarely at conventional events — may occur in industrial scenarios

K

Vegetable/animal fats and oils at high temperature

Common in industrial kitchens — easy reignition

Specific class K wet agent

Food court of large festivals, industrial kitchens of events


🔴  FATAL ERROR — WATER ON AN ELECTRICAL FIRE

Using water on a Class C fire (energized electrical equipment) conducts the electric current through the water jet to the firefighter, causing a potentially fatal electric shock. At live events, where the entire stage and backstage area is permeated by temporary electrical installations, the presence of CO₂ and ABC powder extinguishers in critical zones is not optional — it is a matter of operational survival.

  03    FIRE EXTINGUISHERS — NR-23 REQUIREMENTS

Extinguishers: Acquisition, Positioning, Inspection, and Maintenance

The portable extinguisher is the most present and most frequently mismanaged firefighting equipment at live events. NR-23 dedicates extensive regulation to the acquisition, positioning, periodic inspection, and maintenance of extinguishers — requirements that the event's safety coordinator must verify before opening any area to the public and to workers.

3.1 Acquisition — What NR-23 Requires

Item 23.11.1 of NR-23 determines that only extinguishers complying with Brazilian standards or INMETRO technical regulations may be used, with such conformity guaranteed by affixing to the device a conformity identification from an accredited certification body. In practice, every extinguisher must have a visible and legible INMETRO seal. Extinguishers without this seal must not be used, regardless of external appearance or the supplier's claim.

Every extinguisher must have an identification label attached to its body, indicating: date of the last recharge, date of the next charging or inspection, and the identification number of the person responsible for the recharge. Visual inspection must be carried out monthly (item 23.14.2), checking external appearance, intact seals, the pressure gauge within the green range (for pressurized extinguishers), and the absence of obstruction in the nozzle.



3.2 Positioning — Technical Rules of NR-23

  • The top of the extinguisher may not be more than 1.60 m above the floor — ensuring ergonomic access by any worker

  • The places designated for extinguishers must be marked by a red circle or a wide red arrow visible from multiple angles

  • A wide area of the floor beneath the extinguisher must be painted red — and may not be obstructed by equipment, boxes, or cables

  • Wheeled extinguishers (over 40 kg) must have free access to any point of the area, ensuring unrestricted mobility

  • At least 2 extinguishers per floor, regardless of the total area

  • At outdoor events without floor divisions, distribute extinguishers every 15 m in the zones of greatest fire load and every 25 m in the remaining areas

3.3 Maintenance and Validity — Mandatory Schedule

Frequency

Action

Responsible Party

Document Generated

Monthly

Visual inspection — external appearance, seals, pressure gauge, unobstructed nozzle

Trained brigade member or OSH technician

Record on the monthly inspection sheet

Biannual

Weighing the cylinder of injected-pressure extinguishers — loss > 10% requires immediate recharge

Extinguisher maintenance company

  04    EMERGENCY EXITS AND EVACUATION ROUTES

Emergency Exits: the Last Frontier Between the Fire and Life

NR-23 is explicit and absolute in item 23.2: workplaces must have exits in sufficient number and arranged so that those present in these places can leave them quickly and safely in the event of an emergency. Item 23.4 goes further: no emergency exit shall be locked or fastened shut during the work shift.

These two provisions, seemingly simple, encapsulate the most costly lesson learned from the Kiss Nightclub fire: the emergency exits were obstructed and the security staff prevented people from leaving during the first and decisive minutes of the fire. The result was catastrophic. In the context of live events, these requirements take on an even more critical dimension because emergency exits frequently compete with service entrances, access control points, and logistics corridors — and tend to become obstructed by equipment throughout the setup and operation.

4.1 Technical Requirements of Emergency Exits

Emergency exits at events must simultaneously meet the requirements of NR-23 and the state Fire Brigade standards (ABNT NBR 9077 — Emergency exits in buildings, applicable by analogy to temporary structures). The most relevant requirements for the events sector are:

  1. Sufficient number of exits — calculated as a function of the maximum capacity of the venue and the maximum travel distance (walking distance), which must not exceed 45 m in environments without sprinkler protection

  2. Minimum width of exits — calculated by the evacuation capacity: 1 module of 0.55 m for every 60 people/minute of expected flow; at large-audience events, the exit width is calculated by occupancy and projected evacuation speed

  3. Photoluminescent signaling — emergency exit signs that glow in the dark, visible even with a power cut and dense smoke

  4. Emergency lighting — an autonomous system that comes into operation automatically in the event of failure of the main lighting

  5. Doors — must open in the direction of the evacuation flow (outward), never inward; doors opening inward are prohibited on public evacuation routes

  6. Permanent clearance — no equipment, box, cable, or stage element may reduce the width of the exit route below the calculated minimum, at any moment of the operation


🔴  CRITICAL RISK — EXITS OBSTRUCTED DURING OPERATION

During the operation of an event, emergency exits are systematically threatened by: PA power cables crossing corridors, scenography equipment repositioned during the show, security personnel blocking emergency doors by mistaking them for service entrances, and crowd-control barriers that accidentally close off evacuation routes. The on-duty brigade member must inspect the exit routes every 30 minutes during operation with an audience.


4.2 Evacuation Map — Mandatory Document of the Emergency Plan

ABNT NBR 15219 (Emergency fire plan — Requirements) requires that the Emergency Plan include evacuation plans of the venue indicating the escape routes, the position of extinguishers, hydrants, meeting points, and first-aid locations. At temporary events, this map must be prepared specifically for the venue and configuration of the event, and may not be reused from a previous edition without verifying compliance with the current layout.

The evacuation map must be posted in visible locations at all access points of the event — worker entrances, public gates, backstage entrance — and must be distributed in printed form to all sector coordinators at the pre-event briefing. Digital distribution (via app or WhatsApp group) is complementary, never a substitute for the printed version.


  05    FIRE ALARM AND DETECTION SYSTEM

Detect Before Fighting: Alarm Systems at Events

Item 23.18 of NR-23 determines that in establishments of high or medium risk there must be an alarm system capable of giving perceptible signals at all points of the establishment, with bells or sirens that emit a sound distinct from all other acoustic devices. In the live events sector, this requirement presents an operational paradox: the sound environment of a show is, by definition, of very high intensity — a standard alarm system would be inaudible to the audience and to the workers in the PA area.

The technical solution to this paradox is the integration of the fire alarm system into the event's PA system, configuring a protocol for signal interruption and the broadcast of an evacuation message through the event's own sound system. This protocol must be programmed in advance with the sound engineer and tested before opening, defining: the activation signal (who may activate it), the pre-recorded evacuation message (in which language and with what content), the procedure for the gradual cutting of the show (controlled fade-out to avoid panic), and the stage communication script (host or master of ceremonies announcing the evacuation).

⚠️  FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLE — FIRE BRIGADES

The Fire Brigade must be called IMMEDIATELY at the first sign of fire, even if the brigade is attempting to control the outbreak. Firefighting by the brigade is an initial containment measure — it never replaces the professional call. An outbreak that seems controllable in 60 seconds may be beyond any control in 3 minutes. Call the Fire Brigade before, not after.


  06    FIRE BRIGADE — NBR 14276

Fire Brigade at Events: Training, Sizing, and Action

The fire brigade is the organized group of people trained and qualified to act in prevention, building evacuation, fighting incipient fires, and providing first aid. Regulated by ABNT NBR 14276:2020, the brigade is the line of immediate response in emergency situations — the interval between the detection of the outbreak and the arrival of the Fire Brigade, which at outdoor events can be 10 to 20 minutes, is the period of greatest criticality, and it is precisely this interval that the well-trained brigade is able to manage.

For temporary events, NBR 14276 establishes specific sizing rules. Events with a population of fewer than 500 people are exempt from the requirement of a formal brigade, but NR-23 maintains the obligation of basic information and training for all workers. For events with more than 500 people in the audience, a professional brigade is mandatory and must be sized as a function of the number of participants and the risk level of the event — medium risk for events with PA and lighting, high risk for events with pyrotechnics or special effects.

6.2 Content of the Brigade Training — NBR 14276

The training of the fire brigade must cover, at a minimum:

  • Basic concepts of fire prevention and firefighting — fire theory, classes, and extinguishing agents

  • Correct use of portable extinguishers — practical demonstration with real activation

  • Building evacuation techniques — flow control, crowd guidance technique, panic prevention

  • Basic first aid — recognition of a smoke-inhalation victim, CPR, recovery position

  • Emergency communication — activation protocol, use of the emergency radio, contact with the Fire Brigade

  • Event-specific Emergency Plan — reading and understanding the evacuation map, positions of each brigade member

  • Recognition of escape routes at the physical site — guided visit to the venue before opening


  07    EMERGENCY PLAN — NBR 15219 AND THE KISS LAW

Emergency Plan: The Document That Organizes the Chaos

The Emergency Plan is the technical document that systematically describes the actions to be taken by each agent (brigade, coordination, production, private security) in the event of a fire or other emergency. Regulated by ABNT NBR 15219 and required implicitly by NR-23 and explicitly by the Kiss Law (Law No. 13,425/2017), the Emergency Plan is the instrument that converts individual training into coordinated collective action.

The absence of a documented and tested Emergency Plan at a public-assembly event is not merely a technical irregularity — it is the root cause of practically every live-event fire tragedy. In the Kiss Nightclub fire, none of the staff present knew what to do, where the extinguishers were, or what the escape route was. The Emergency Plan is precisely the instrument that eliminates this fatal ignorance.

7.1 Minimum Structure of the Emergency Plan — NBR 15219

  1. Event identification — name, venue, date, maximum capacity of audience and workers

  2. Emergency organizational chart — chain of command with the name, role, and contact of each agent

  3. Evacuation map — floor plan of the venue with routes, exits, position of extinguishers, and meeting points

  4. Identified emergency scenarios — fire, structural collapse, mass panic, medical emergency

  5. Actions by scenario — specific protocol for each type of emergency with a responsible party and action deadline

  6. Available resources — list of firefighting equipment, first-aid brigade, AED (if required)

  7. External contacts — Fire Brigade, SAMU, Civil Defense, Military Police, reference hospital

  8. Drill procedure — frequency, evaluation criteria, and recording of results

7.2 The Drill — Real Emergency Training

The drill is the practical test of the Emergency Plan: a controlled simulation of an emergency scenario with real activation of the brigade, the alarm system, and the evacuation protocols. NR-23 calls it an "alert exercise" (item 23.8) and establishes that it must be carried out periodically, preferably without prior notice to the participants, coming as close as possible to real conditions.

For recurring events — such as annual festivals, monthly shows, or periodic events — the drill must be carried out at least annually, with recording of results and adjustment of the Emergency Plan based on the identified failures. For large-scale events exceeding 20,000 people, the state Fire Brigades frequently require a drill as a condition for issuing the Inspection Certificate (AVCB or state equivalent).



  08    FIRE PREVENTION — CONTROL OF IGNITION SOURCES

Prevent Before Fighting: Elimination of Ignition Sources

NR-23 is clear in its fundamental principle: the priority is to prevent the fire, not to fight it. Firefighting is the last line of defense — prevention is the complete system of ignition-source control, combustible-material management, and space organization that prevents the fire triangle from completing. In the live events sector, where the density of ignition sources and combustible materials is exceptionally high, preventive management is at once more necessary and more challenging than in conventional industrial installations.

8.1 Main Ignition Sources at Events and Control Measures

Ignition Source

Risk

Mandatory Control Measure

Undersized temporary electrical installation

Progressive heating of the conductor → melting of the insulation → electric arc → electrical fire

Electrical design with ART, conductor cross-section compatible with the load, RCD protection installed

Diesel generator

Fuel leak near the hot containment basin; sparks from the engine

Metal base with containment tray, minimum distance of 3 m from combustible materials, external BC extinguisher

Special effects — flames, sparks, CO₂ cryo

Projection of incandescent particles onto curtains or scenic covering

Exclusion radius for combustible materials; Fire Brigade approval; qualified operator

Pyrotechnics

High combustion temperature; projection of incandescent fragments

Specific operator qualification; calculated safety distances; isolated staging area; dedicated brigade

Fog machine

Overheating of the fluid; accumulation of flammable fluid on a heated floor

Check the fluid temperature (< 200°C), do not use near heat sources, ventilation of the area

Cigarettes and birthday candles

Improper disposal onto combustible material

Clear and signposted prohibition + patrol brigade member in the audience area during the show's closing

Heaters and heat lamps backstage

Contact with curtains or textile materials

Minimum distance of 1.5 m from any combustible material; never leave switched on unsupervised


8.2 Management of Combustible Materials

The fire load of an event — the set of combustible materials present — is decisive for the speed of propagation of any outbreak. Scenography elaborated with polyurethane foam, synthetic textile coverings, PVC tarpaulins, and dry timber can dramatically raise the fire load and the rate of flame propagation. The safety coordinator must require, for every scenography material used at indoor events, the manufacturer's flame-retardancy certificate — a specification that determines the material's time of resistance to ignition and the speed of flame propagation after the start of combustion.

  • Scenography materials in indoor environments must have a flame-retardancy certificate (ABNT NBR 9177 standard or equivalent)

  • Tent tarpaulins must have M2 or higher fire-resistance certification in accordance with NBR 7480

  • Stage curtains and drapes: prefer natural materials (treated cotton) or synthetics with FR (flame retardant) certification

  • Setup residues — wood, cardboard, foam — must be removed from the event area before opening to the public

  • Fuels (diesel, alcohol) must be stored in approved, labeled containers, in an area isolated from ignition sources


  09    PASSIVE FIRE PROTECTION AT EVENTS

Passive Protection: The Structure That Buys Time for Evacuation

Passive fire protection comprises the set of constructive solutions that delay the spread of fire and smoke without the need for human intervention or energization. In permanent buildings, it is composed of compartmentation, fire walls and fire doors, sealing of penetrations, and thermal coatings. At events with temporary structures, the passive protection options are more limited, but no less important.

Item 23.7.3 of NR-23 provides that for certain types of industry or activity with high fire risk, special construction requirements may be demanded, such as fire doors and fire walls or dikes around reservoirs. In the context of live events, functional compartmentation — physical separation between the generator zone, the electrical area, the special-effects zone, and the audience areas — is the main passive protection instrument available and must be implemented with metal fencing, rigid partitions, and access control by function.

9.1 Passive Protection Strategies Applicable to Events

  • Generator isolation — metal fence with a restricted-access gate, minimum distance of 5 m from combustible materials and from any structure of the audience area

  • Compartmentation of the electrical area — a closed room or cabinet for the main distribution panels, limiting the spread of an electrical fire

  • Physical separation of pyrotechnics — a staging area isolated from the entire rest of the operation, with no access by unqualified personnel

  • Flame retardant applied to existing materials — chemical retardant spray applicable to wood, fabrics, and tarpaulins that lack original certification

  • Controlled ventilation — in enclosed tents, ensure adequate ventilation that prevents the accumulation of gases produced by generators or special effects, but that does not create air currents feeding any fire outbreak


  10    KISS LAW (No. 13,425/2017) — REGULATORY LANDMARK FOR EVENTS

Kiss Law: The Normative Legacy of a Tragedy

Federal Law No. 13,425, of March 30, 2017, known as the Kiss Law because it was motivated by the Kiss Nightclub fire, represents the most important regulatory advance in fire safety at public assemblies in Brazilian history. It establishes national guidelines on measures for the prevention and fighting of fire and disasters in establishments, buildings, and public-assembly areas.

The Kiss Law is not a technical standard — it is a federal law that delegates to technical standards and state legislation the definition of the specific criteria, but imposes direct legal obligations on the promoters and organizers of public-assembly events. Its main provisions of interest to the live events sector are:

  • Mandatory operating permit and Fire Brigade Inspection Certificate (AVCB) for establishments and public-assembly events — without an AVCB, the event cannot legally take place

  • Strict liability of the owner or legal representative of the establishment or event for the safety conditions

  • Prohibition of materials without fire-safety certification in public-assembly places

  • Mandatory fire brigade sized in accordance with a technical standard (NBR 14276)

  • Requirement for fire detection and alarm systems as a function of the capacity and risk of the venue

  • Maximum venue capacity determined by the safety design — overcrowding prohibited

10.1 The AVCB — Fire Brigade Inspection Certificate

The AVCB is the document issued by the state Military Fire Brigade attesting that the analyzed venue or structure meets the fire and panic safety requirements established by state legislation. For events at permanent venues (pavilions, arenas, gymnasiums), the venue's AVCB must be valid. For events at temporary or outdoor venues, state legislation may require an equivalent document (temporary use license, event-specific inspection) that must be requested from the state Fire Brigade at least 30 to 60 days before the event.

⚠️  CRIMINAL LIABILITY — KISS LAW

Art. 7 of the Kiss Law establishes that the party responsible for the establishment or event that causes death or serious injury through non-observance of safety standards is liable for the crimes of manslaughter or negligent bodily injury with the penalty increased by 1/3. The STJ (Superior Court of Justice) confirmed in 2023 the criminal liability of the partners of the Kiss Nightclub, setting the precedent that omission of safety measures constitutes contingent intent (dolus eventualis) — intent, not mere negligence.


  11    INTEGRATION OF NR-23 WITH THE OSH SYSTEM

NR-23 in the RMP: Fire as a Managed Occupational Risk

NR-23 does not operate as an isolated standard — it is a specific layer of the occupational risk management system established by NR-01 (ORM/RMP). Fire is an occupational risk like any other: it must be identified in the Risk Inventory, assessed in terms of probability and severity, and controlled by hierarchical measures ranging from the elimination of the ignition source (elimination) to the positioning of extinguishers (administrative control) and the training of the brigade (administrative control).

11.1 Integration with Other NRs

NR-01 (ORM/RMP)

Fire as a risk in the Risk Inventory; the control measures of NR-23 integrate the Action Plan; the Emergency Plan makes up the RMP

NR-06 (PPE)

Brigade members must have specific PPE: respiratory protection mask (smoke), thermal protection gloves, helmet; brigade PPE documented on the sheet

NR-10 (Electrical)

Deficient electrical installations are the main cause of fire at events; compliance with NR-10 is the primary preventive measure for class C fire

NR-18 (Assembly)

Temporary construction materials with flame-retardancy certification; compartmentation as passive protection

NR-23 (Fire)

Specific standard: fire classes, extinguishers, emergency exits, alarm, brigade

NR-26 (Signaling)

Signaling of escape routes, extinguishers, and emergency exits must comply with NR-26 (red for firefighting equipment, green for exits)

Kiss Law (13,425/2017)

Strict liability of the organizer; mandatory AVCB; declared maximum capacity; sized brigade

  13    CONCLUSION — FIRE PROTECTION AS AN ETHICAL COMMITMENT

Closing: Prevention Is Responsibility, Not Bureaucracy

NR-23 applied to the live events sector is not a formal compliance standard — it is a system for protecting human life in high-risk contexts. Fire in an environment with a large concentration of public is one of the few occupational and public risks that has the capacity to escalate from a controllable outbreak to an irreversible catastrophe in less than two minutes. This characteristic places it in a category of risk that demands systematic prevention, immediate response, and robust emergency planning — not last-minute measures improvised on the day of the event.

The events safety professional who masters NR-23 — its requirements on extinguishers, emergency exits, alarm, brigade, and emergency plan — and who integrates these elements into the NR-01 RMP and the regulatory framework of the Kiss Law, does not merely comply with the legislation. Event after event, they build the safety culture that transforms the sector: from tolerating risk as a cost of business to managing risk as a non-negotiable condition of operation.

Every event that closes without a fire accident is not luck — it is the result of correct equipment, trained people, unobstructed exits, and an Emergency Plan that no one had to use because prevention worked. This is the objective of NR-23, and this is the standard that the SMU Profissionalizante professional is committed to maintaining.

"Fire gives no warning — prevention makes no compromises."


NR-23 module completed. Workload: 4 hours.

  01    NR-11 AND THE LIVE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 1 — Fundamentals: Load Handling as a Critical Activity

Regulatory Standard No. 11 — Transport, Handling, Storage and Manipulation of Materials — establishes the safety requirements for all operations involving the transport, hoisting, handling, storage and manipulation of materials, whether performed manually or with motorized equipment. Originally published by Ordinance MTb No. 3,214/1978 and updated by Ordinance MTPS No. 505/2016, it is defined as a Special Standard — applicable to all sectors of the economy without restriction of economic activity.

In the live events sector, load handling is one of the highest-volume and highest-risk activities of the operation. At a medium-sized show, dozens of tons of equipment are moved: large-format loudspeakers weighing 150 to 400 kg each, lighting grid systems, modular LED screens, generators, stage structures and backline equipment. This handling takes place in temporary environments, on floors of variable capacity, under deadline pressure and often with workers from multiple companies operating simultaneously in the same space.

The combination of these factors — heavy loads, temporary space, compressed deadlines and a multiplicity of teams — creates a risk environment that demands rigorous application of the provisions of NR-11 and the associated technical standards. Accidents with suspended loads at events have the potential to cause fatalities among workers and, in cases of rigging collapse during the show, among the audience.



Legal basis

NR-11 — Ordinance MTb No. 3,214/1978 | Last update: Ordinance MTPS No. 505/2016

Classification

Special Standard — applicable to all sectors without restriction of economic activity

Complementary standards

⚠️  APPLICABILITY IN THE EVENTS SECTOR

NR-11 applies fully to truck loading and unloading operations, the hoisting of PA and lighting systems, the movement of generators with forklifts, the displacement of flight cases and every rigging operation at live events. Ignorance of the standard does not exempt the producer, the assembly company or the operator from liability in the event of an accident.


  02    HOISTING AND HANDLING EQUIPMENT

Module 2 — Classification and Requirements of Equipment

NR-11 determines that all material transport and handling equipment must be calculated and built so as to offer the necessary guarantees of strength and safety, kept in perfect working condition and operated by a qualified person. This requirement is the premise of all equipment management — it is not enough for the equipment to exist; it must be technically approved, inspected and operated by a competent person.

2.1 Main Equipment at Live Events

5 to 500 tons

Rigging Plan with ART (technical responsibility record); qualified operator; anemometer; technical report

2.2 Universal Requirements for All Equipment — NR-11

  • Maximum working load indicated in a visible and indelible location — permanent plate or label on the equipment

  • Permanent periodic inspection — defective or deficient parts replaced immediately

  • Specific inspection of: steel cables, synthetic ropes, chains, pulleys and hooks — immediate replacement at the slightest sign of defect

  • Safety device on the hook (closing latch or safety catch) — prevents accidental release of the load

  • Qualified operator carrying an identification card with name, photo and expiration date in a visible place

  • Express prohibition of transporting people on equipment intended for material handling


🔴  ABSOLUTE PROHIBITION — PEOPLE ON LOADS

NR-11 expressly prohibits transporting people on suspended loads or on hoisting accessories (slings, hooks, shackles). At events, it is common to see technicians trying to "hitch a ride" on a hoisted load to reach the grid — this practice is illegal, extremely dangerous and may result in criminal liability for the operator and the company.


  03    HOISTING ACCESSORIES — INSPECTION AND SELECTION

Module 3 — Slings, Cables, Shackles and Chains: the Safety Chain

Hoisting accessories are the elements that make the connection between the lifting equipment and the load. They are frequently the weakest link in the chain — and the most neglected in the daily operation of events. A shackle with a loose pin, a textile sling with an invisible cut or a steel cable with deformed strands can cause sudden collapse of the load, with fatal consequences for workers below.

NR-11 requires that slings, strops and other accessories have their load capacity marked in a visible and indelible manner, and that they be inspected before each use. This is not a recommendation — it is a legal requirement with direct implication of liability in the event of an accident caused by an uninspected accessory failure.

3.1 Types of Accessories and Removal-from-Service Criteria

Accessory

Use at Events

Removal-from-Service Criterion

Mandatory Verification

Flat textile sling (flat sling)

Slinging of loudspeakers, aluminum structures, flight cases

3.2 Effect of the Hoisting Angle on the Load on the Accessories

One of the most critical and least understood concepts in load handling at events is the effect of the hoisting angle on the actual tension in the accessories. When a load is hoisted by two sling legs, the angle formed between them does not simply divide the load in two — it increases the tension in each leg as the angle grows.

NR-11 (item 11.4.3) establishes that the angle formed by the sling/strop legs must NOT exceed 120 degrees, except in the case of a design produced by a legally qualified professional. This limit is not arbitrary — it is the point beyond which the tension in the legs exceeds the total hoisted load, approaching the WLL of the accessories dangerously.

⚠️  PRACTICAL RULE — MAXIMUM ANGLE 120°

Never position hoisting slings or cables at an angle greater than 120° between the legs without a design by a qualified engineer. At events, when slinging loudspeakers or structures with two suspension points, keep the angle as close as possible to 0° (parallel vertical legs); never let the slings "open into a V" excessively.


  04    THE SIGNALER-RIGGER — AN ESSENTIAL TECHNICAL ROLE

Module 4 — Signaler-Rigger: the Coordinator of the Suspended Load

The signaler-rigger is the professional responsible for securing the load and guiding the operator of the lifting equipment during the movement. NR-11 (item 11.4.4) determines that the suspended transport and movement of materials must be guided by a trained signaler-rigger. This role is not hierarchically inferior to that of the operator — it is technically complementary and equally critical: the operator moves the load, the signaler ensures it is moved safely.

The professional hierarchy in large-scale hoisting operations is defined by three levels of competence, each with a distinct scope of action:

4.1 Hierarchy of Competence in Rigging

Role

Main Responsibility

Minimum Training

Documentation

RIGGER

Preparation of the Rigging Plan — sizing of equipment, load calculation, selection of accessories, design of the operation. Technical officer for the hoisting.

Engineer or Technician with active CREA registration — Rigger course (40h)

ART for each Rigging Plan — mandatory by law

RIGGING SUPERVISOR

Interpretation and execution of the Rigging Plan in the field. Management of the signaler team. Authority to halt the operation in case of risk.

Rigging Supervisor course (specific) + proven experience

Recognized certificate; employer authorization

SIGNALER-RIGGER

Execution of load securing. Guiding the operator by hand signals or radio. Isolation and signaling of the area. Reporting deviations to the supervisor.

Signaler-Rigger course NR-11/NR-18 (16h min.) + elementary education

Identification card with photo — valid for 2 years (NR-18) or per the applicable standard


4.2 Communication by Hand Signals — Operational Code

When visual contact between the signaler and the operator is not possible, radio communication is used (dedicated exclusively to this purpose). When there is visual contact, standardized hand signals must be used. The signaler is the ONLY worker authorized to give signals to the operator during load movement — other workers must not interfere with the communication.

Signal

Arm extended upward, index finger pointing up, circular motion with the wrist

  05    RIGGING PLAN — TECHNICAL PLANNING OF THE HOISTING

Module 5 — The Rigging Plan: No Improvising Suspended Loads

The Rigging Plan is the technical document that plans and formalizes a hoisting or movement operation with lifting equipment. NR-11 determines that equipment offer "guarantees of strength and safety" — in complex hoisting operations, such as those performed at large events, that guarantee is only possible with documented planning. The Rigging Plan is not a bureaucratic requirement: it is the instrument that converts technical knowledge into verifiable operational instruction.

For the technical and legal validity of the Rigging Plan, it must be prepared by a qualified professional (Engineer or Technician with active CREA registration) and have an ART registered with the CREA of the state where the operation takes place. At events, the Rigging Plan is especially critical for the hoisting of large-format PA systems, stage roof structures and movements with a mobile crane.

5.1 Minimum Content of the Rigging Plan

  1. Identification of the operation — event, location, date, technical officer (Rigger) and ART number

  2. Description of the load — total weight, dimensions, center of gravity, available hoisting points

  3. Selected hoisting equipment — model, capacity, working radius, conditions of use

  4. Hoisting accessories — type, capacity (WLL), angle of use, applied safety factor

  5. Sketch or floor plan of the operation — position of the equipment, load trajectory, risk areas

  6. Indication of isolation measures — minimum isolation distance, signaling, responsible persons

  7. Limiting weather conditions — maximum wind speed for suspending the operation

  8. Step-by-step procedure — hoisting sequence, worker positions, verification points

  9. Emergency procedure — action in case of equipment failure or load drop


5.2 Express Prohibitions of NR-11 — Hoisting

  • Prohibited to hoist a load that is not fully detached from the support surface and free of any interference — never pull a stuck or snagged load

  • Prohibited to use natural fiber ropes (sisal, manila) as load-hoisting elements — only synthetic fiber ropes provided for in technical standards

  • Prohibited to work in adverse weather conditions that affect safety — wind, heavy rain, lightning

  • Prohibited to hoist simultaneously more loads than the equipment is designed to support

  • Prohibited to remain under a suspended load — the area below the trajectory must be isolated throughout the entire operation

  • Prohibited to use the hook block in such a way that the steel cable forms an excessive angle with the vertical of the hook

  • Prohibited to place advertising boards on the crane structure, except when specified by the manufacturer — it alters the aerodynamic profile and can cause tipping in the wind


  06    MANUAL HANDLING AND ERGONOMICS

Module 6 — Manual Handling of Loads: Limits and Worker Protection

Not all load handling at events uses motorized equipment. Manual transport of equipment — pushing flight cases, carrying boxes, positioning monitors, moving racks — represents one of the greatest sources of musculoskeletal injuries in the sector. Low back pain, herniated discs, muscle strains and repetitive strain injuries (RSI) are documented consequences of inadequate manual handling of heavy loads.

NR-11 establishes specific limits and conditions for manual transport. Manual transport of sacks over planks across spans greater than 1 meter is prohibited, and planks must have a minimum width of 50 cm. For loading and unloading trucks, the worker must have a helper. NR-17 (Ergonomics) complements NR-11 by establishing weight limits and postural conditions for manual work.

6.1 Technical Limits for Manual Transport

Individual weight limit (NR-17)

Up to 23 kg for occasional lifting by an adult worker. Loads above this limit require two workers or the use of auxiliary equipment.

Manual transport distance

NR-11 limits transport on foot to up to 60 meters. Greater distances require auxiliary equipment (trolleys, hand carts, pallet trucks).

Planks over spans

Manual transport over planks across spans greater than 1 meter is prohibited. Minimum plank width: 50 cm.

Loading and unloading trucks

A helper is mandatory during manual loading and unloading operations — never a single worker alone.

Flight cases with casters

Check the condition of the casters before pushing — a locked caster on an uneven surface can cause the equipment to tip over onto the worker.

Footwear and PPE

Safety footwear with steel toe and non-slip sole — mandatory throughout the load handling area.


6.2 Safe Manual Lifting Technique

The correct technique for manual lifting of loads is taught in NR-11 and NR-17 training, but frequently ignored in the fast pace of event assembly. Incorrect lifting is the most common cause of acute low back pain in the sector.

  1. Approach the load — position the feet close to the object, at shoulder width

  2. Squat down by bending the knees — keep the spine straight, never curve the lower back

  3. Grip the load firmly — use both hands, distributing the weight evenly

  4. Lift with the strength of the legs — extension of the knees and hips, not the lower back

  5. Keep the load close to the body — never extend the arms with weight

  6. Rotate the whole body (pivot on the feet) — never twist the spine with a load

  7. To lower: reverse the process — bend the knees, keep the spine straight


  07    FORKLIFT OPERATION AT EVENTS

Module 7 — Forklifts: Power and Responsibility in the Loading Area

The counterbalanced forklift is the most versatile and most widely used equipment for handling heavy loads at live events — from unloading trucks to positioning generators and transporting large flight cases. Its operation by an unqualified professional is one of the most common violations found in MTE inspections at events.

NR-11 requires that operators of motorized equipment receive specific training provided by the company and carry an identification card with name, photo and expiration date in a visible place throughout the operation. For forklifts, the minimum training is 40 hours (theory + practice) per the Brazilian Program for Quality and Productivity in Packaging (PBQP-E) and the specific requirements of each manufacturer.

7.1 Rules for Safe Forklift Operation at Events

  • Check the condition of the floor before operating — forklifts require a firm, level surface without holes or obstacles

  • Strictly respect the rated capacity of the equipment — the load plate on the forklift mast is an irreducible reference

  • Keep the forks tilted backward while moving with a load — prevents the load from falling during braking

  • Lower the forks to floor level before any horizontal movement — an elevated load multiplies the risk of tipping

  • Maximum speed of 10 km/h in the internal event area — reduce to walking pace (< 5 km/h) in areas with workers on foot

  • A signaler must accompany the operation when the operator does not have full visibility of the route

  • The forklift's work area must be signaled and with controlled pedestrian access

  • Prohibited to lift people on the forks or on pallets on the forks — no exceptions


🔴  FREQUENT ACCIDENT — FORKLIFT TIPPING

The tipping of a forklift with an elevated load is the most serious accident involving this equipment. The most common causes at events: uneven floor with a hole or step, turning at speed with a high load, unbalanced load exceeding the capacity diagram limit, collision with an unknown obstacle. The operator must always check the forklift's load diagram before each operation with a new load.


  08    SAFE STORAGE OF EQUIPMENT AT EVENTS

Module 8 — Storage: Organization that Prevents Accidents

The storage of equipment at live events — stacked flight cases, stored loudspeakers, stage structures awaiting hoisting, generators at the loading dock — represents a frequently underestimated risk. NR-11 dedicates specific provisions to the safe storage of materials, which apply directly to the events context.

8.1 Storage Requirements — NR-11

  • Stored materials must not obstruct emergency exits, doors, windows or firefighting equipment — free access at all times

  • Stacks of equipment must maintain a minimum distance of 50 cm from lateral structures — to prevent excessive pressure on walls and allow circulation

  • Stacked materials must have guaranteed stability — avoid conical or unstable stacks that could topple

  • Handling aisles must be kept clear, illuminated and clearly marked

  • Floors of storage areas must be flat, clean and dry — risk of falling on a wet floor with a load

  • Stacks of flight cases must have assured stability — verify that the casters are locked and that the stack does not have a displaced center of gravity

  • Heavy materials must be stored at the bottom of the stacks — never light equipment beneath heavy items



  09    RISK MANAGEMENT — NR-11 IN THE PGR

Module 9 — Integration with the GRO/PGR System of NR-01

Load handling must be treated as an occupational risk managed within the GRO/PGR system established by NR-01. The Risk Inventory must identify each load handling operation, assess the severity and probability of risk events, and define the corresponding control measures. The Rigging Plan is one of the control measures of the PGR — not a separate, standalone document.

  10    INSPECTION, RESPONSIBILITIES AND CHECKLIST

Module 10 — Legal Compliance and Verification Protocol

MTE inspection at live events includes verifying compliance with NR-11. Labor inspectors check the qualification of operators, the condition of equipment, the existence of a Rigging Plan for complex hoisting, and storage compliance. Accidents with loads at events give rise to civil liability and, depending on the circumstances, criminal liability for the event organizer, the assembly company and the operator.

10.1 What the Inspector Checks in NR-11 at Events

  • Identification card of the operators of motorized equipment (forklift, crane) — name, photo, validity

  • Maximum load plate visible and legible on all hoisting equipment

  • Records of periodic inspection of equipment and accessories — maintenance sheets

  • Rigging Plan with ART for hoisting with lifting equipment

  • Isolation of areas under suspended loads — no one passing beneath an elevated load

  • Training certificates of signaler-riggers — specific training documented

  • Storage conditions — clear aisles, stable stacks, no obstruction of emergency routes

  • Use of PPE by load handling workers
