  11    CASE STUDIES — LOAD HANDLING ACCIDENTS

Module 11 — Learning from What Went Wrong

Case 1 — Loudspeaker Drop Due to a Shackle with a Loose Pin

Scenario: During the load-in of a show in an arena, a 180 kg loudspeaker is hoisted to the grid. The signaler did not inspect the accessories before hoisting. The shackle used had a loose threaded pin — it was not fully inserted and had no locking wire. At 4 meters high, the pin comes loose and the box falls onto the stage floor. Luckily, the area below was not isolated, but no one was directly beneath at the moment. Total damage to the equipment: R$ 85,000.

What went wrong: Lack of pre-use inspection of the accessories; shackle without a safety lock on the pin; hoisting area not isolated; signaler without a specific training certificate.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

Inspection of the accessories is mandatory before EACH hoisting — not once a day. The shackle pin must be fully threaded and with the locking wire installed. The area below the load must be isolated before starting the hoisting — not after.


Case 2 — Forklift Tipping on an Arena Floor

Scenario: A forklift operator without specific qualification moves a subwoofer flight case (weight 420 kg) during assembly. When making a sharp turn with the load elevated to 1.5 meters, the forklift loses lateral stability and tips over. The operator is thrown from the cab and suffers an arm fracture and head trauma. The flight case falls onto the equipment and is destroyed.

What went wrong: Operator without specific qualification for the forklift; load elevated during movement in a turn; excessive speed in the turn; absence of a signaler to accompany.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

Only a qualified operator with a valid card may operate a forklift. The load must be low (close to the floor) during movement. Reduced speed in turns. A signaler is mandatory when the operator does not have full visibility.


Case 3 — Crushing by a Stack of Flight Cases

Scenario: A backstage storage area with flight cases stacked 2.2 meters high on an uneven floor. A caster on one of the upper cases was not locked. Vibration caused by a forklift passing nearby unbalances the stack. The stack topples onto a technician who was checking the stored equipment. Fracture of three ribs and pulmonary contusion — 90 days of leave.

What went wrong: Casters without brakes; unstable stack on an uneven floor; worker moving through the storage area without prior stability verification; absence of controlled-access signaling.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 3

Always lock the casters of stacked flight cases. Verify the stability of the stack before allowing access. Signal and control access to the storage area during assembly. An uneven floor is incompatible with a tall stack.


  12    CONCLUSION — LOAD HANDLING AS ENGINEERING, NOT IMPROVISATION

Closing: The Load that Falls Does Not Come Back

NR-11 applied to the live events sector is the standard that translates the simplest — and most forgotten — maxim of occupational safety: what is in the air can fall. A 400 kg load suspended at 8 meters high contains enough potential energy to destroy any structure or kill any person at the point of impact. There is no "bad luck" in this context — there is a lack of planning, a lack of inspection and a lack of adequate technical training.

The events safety professional who masters NR-11 understands that each hoisting is an engineering operation: it requires load calculation, technical selection of accessories, a qualified professional in the field and isolation of the risk area. Each forklift operation requires a certified operator, floor verification and respect for the load diagram. Each storage area requires organization that guarantees the accessibility of emergency routes and the stability of the stacks.

None of these requirements is bureaucracy — all are a direct consequence of the laws of physics and the lessons learned from real accidents that cost lives and property. The SMU PRO professional who completes this module is committed to a standard: at your event, no load falls due to a lack of inspection, no worker is crushed due to a lack of isolation, and no operator moves heavy equipment without proven qualification.

"No load is worth a life — planning and inspection are non-negotiable."




NR-11 module completed. Workload: 4 hours.

References: NR-11 (Ordinance MTPS No. 505/2016) · NR-18 · NR-35 · NR-01 · ABNT NBR 17089 · ABNT NBR 11436 · ABNT NBR 8400.

SMU

PRODUCTIONS

Professional Training in Occupational Safety

TECHNICAL HANDBOOK

NR-12

Occupational Safety in Machines and Equipment

DANGER ZONES · GUARDS · LOTO · EMERGENCY STOP · SAFE MAINTENANCE


Module focused on the Live Events Sector

Workload: 4 hours  |  Level: Advanced Operational

In accordance with NR-12 (SIT Ordinance No. 197/2010 and updates) · NBR ISO 12100 · ABNT NBR ISO 13850

"A safe machine is not one that doesn't break down — it's one that doesn't hurt."

  01    NR-12 AND THE LIVE EVENTS SECTOR

Module 1 — Fundamentals: Machines and Equipment as Risk Vectors

Regulatory Standard No. 12 — Occupational Safety in Machines and Equipment — establishes technical references, fundamental principles and protective measures to ensure the health and physical integrity of workers who operate, maintain, inspect or interact in any way with machines and equipment. Originally published in 1978 and profoundly reformulated by SIT Ordinance No. 197/2010 with subsequent updates, it covers the entire life cycle of machines: design, manufacture, import, marketing, installation, operation, maintenance, cleaning, inspection, decommissioning, dismantling and disposal.

In the live events sector, NR-12 applies to a broad category of equipment that often operates under adverse conditions — temporary environments, workers from multiple companies, deadline pressure, nighttime operation and the absence of easily accessible operating manuals. Motorized stage lifts, load cranes, automated stages with movement mechanisms, generators, stage crushers, motorized towers, forklifts and motorized rigging systems are all subject to NR-12 in the events context.

The standard is not restricted to the machine's direct operator — it covers every person who may be affected by the equipment's operation, including workers circulating nearby, technicians performing maintenance and riggers operating in the same area. This scope is especially relevant at events, where dozens of workers with different roles share the same physical space with operating equipment.




Legal basis

NR-12 — SIT Ordinance No. 197/2010 | Updates: SEPRT Ordinance No. 916/2019 | MTP Ordinance No. 4,219/2022

Scope of application

New and used machines and equipment in operation — with no "automatic exemption" for age or continuous use

Relevant exceptions

Equipment displayed at fairs/events for HISTORICAL and non-productive purposes — with safety measures for the public

Protection hierarchy

1st Safe design and construction measures | 2nd Collective protection (physical guards, devices) | 3rd Administrative measures | 4th PPE

Risk assessment

Base document — prepared by a qualified professional in accordance with NBR ISO 12100, with a Technical Responsibility Note (ART)

Machine inventory

List of all machines with name, location, power, safety systems and risk category

Documented maintenance

Preventive with a schedule | Predictive with analysis techniques | Mandatory records

Training

Mandatory, specific to the machine type, funded by the employer, with records


⚠️  CENTRAL PRINCIPLE OF NR-12

NR-12 does not accept PPE as the first line of protection against machine hazards. Protection must be installed at the source — on the machine itself — before resorting to administrative or individual measures. A helmet does not protect against the risk of being crushed by a lift that lacks a safety barrier.


  02    DANGER ZONES AND PROTECTION SYSTEMS

Module 2 — Danger Zones: Identify to Protect

The danger zone is the central concept of NR-12: it is the space around or inside a machine where there is a risk to the worker's safety or health. Every guard installed on a machine aims to prevent unauthorized access to the danger zone, or to detect human presence in the danger zone and trigger a safe shutdown of the equipment before contact occurs.

In the events sector, danger zones are frequently underestimated because the equipment seems less "industrial" than a press or a milling machine. A stage lift, however, creates a danger zone in the space between the platform and the fixed structure — capable of fatal crushing if a worker's limb is caught in the movement. A motorized rigging system creates danger zones below the descent path. A forklift creates a danger zone in its turning radius area and at the lifting height of its forks.

2.1 Types of Guards — NR-12 Hierarchy

Type of Guard

Technical Description

Example at Events

Advantage

Fixed guard

Permanent physical barrier that physically prevents access to the danger zone — removable only with tools

Fixed metal grille around the lifting mechanism of a stage platform

Maximum reliability — does not depend on human action

Movable guard with interlock

Barrier that, when opened/removed, triggers the automatic shutdown of the machine. Cannot be bypassed

Cover of the lift's drive mechanism — opening it interrupts the movement

Allows access for maintenance with automatic protection

Guard with hold-to-run device

Allows keeping the guard open for short-duration intervention, with reduced speed and limited force

Slow maintenance mode (crawl mode) on a motorized rigging system during position adjustments

Operational flexibility with controlled safety

Safety devices (without a physical guard)

Presence sensors, light curtains, pressure-sensitive mats, laser scanners — detect presence and stop the machine

Light curtain at the edges of the stage lift — stops if someone is in the movement space

Does not obstruct visibility and access; requires rigorous maintenance


2.2 Safety Distances — NR-12 and NBR ISO 13857

NR-12 defines a safety distance as one that protects people from reaching the danger zones. The technical standards NBR ISO 13857 (safety distances for upper and lower limbs) and NBR ISO 13855 (positioning of electro-sensitive devices) provide the specific calculations. For the events sector, the critical safety-distance points are:

  • Minimum space between a moving lift and the fixed structure — to prevent crushing between parts

  • Distance between the protective grille and the equipment's moving part — a hand/arm must not be able to reach the danger zone through the grille opening

  • Isolation radius around operating forklifts — pedestrian workers kept outside the turning radius

  • Area below a load suspended by motorized rigging — total isolation of the load's projection on the ground


  03    EMERGENCY STOP — NR-12 REQUIREMENTS

Module 3 — The Emergency Stop as the Last Line of Protection

The emergency stop is the safety device of last resort: when all guards have failed or when a worker identifies an imminent risk, activating the emergency stop interrupts the machine's dangerous movement before harm occurs. NR-12 dedicates specific and rigorous requirements to these devices, which must be present on every machine that poses a risk to the operator or third parties.

The standard requires emergency-stop devices to be positioned in easily accessible locations, visible and identifiable by the operator and by workers nearby. The standard color is red on a yellow background — a standardization that ensures immediate recognition even under stress or in low-light conditions. This visual standard is defined by ABNT NBR ISO 13850.

3.1 Technical Requirements of the Emergency Stop — NR-12

  • Red color on a yellow background — mandatory standard for immediate visual identification

  • Positioned in a location easily accessible by the operator and by other people in the machine area

  • Activated by pressing a palm button or by a pull cord (tensioned) — an intuitive action under stress

  • After activation: the machine remains stopped (de-energized state) until deliberate manual reset

  • The reset can only be performed after identifying and correcting the cause that triggered the stop

  • The reset CANNOT be performed remotely or automatically — it requires the operator's physical presence on site

  • Pull-cord emergency stop: any break or slackening of the cord automatically triggers the stop

  • NR-12 prohibits using the emergency stop as a routine stop — it is an emergency device, not an operating switch




3.2 Emergency Stop on Event Equipment — Practical Application

Equipment

Type of Emergency Stop

Location

Who Can Reset It

Stage lift

Red/yellow palm button + pressure bar on the sides of the platform

On the control panel + on the side edges of the platform

Qualified operator after verifying the absence of people in the danger zone

Motorized rigging system (chain hoist)

Palm button on the controller + wireless emergency stop

Stage controller + second point at floor level

Qualified operator after verifying that the load is stabilized

Electric forklift

Emergency-stop button on the operator's panel

To the left of the steering column — within natural reach of the seated operator

Operator after a complete stop and identification of the cause

Automatic electric generator

Emergency shutdown button on the generator panel

On the generator's front panel — restricted-access area

Authorized generator technician — after cooling and verification

Stage / bench saw

Palm button + pull cord (tensioned) along the bench

On the panel + every 2 m along the equipment

Operator after verifying the absence of tools/hands in the cutting zone


  04    LOTO SYSTEM — LOCKOUT AND TAGOUT

Module 4 — LOTO: Zero Energy Is the Only Real Protection During Maintenance

The LOTO system (Lockout/Tagout) is the most critical safety procedure at the interface between machine operation and maintenance. Statistics from the U.S. government (OSHA) reveal that when an accident occurs during machine maintenance: in 80% of cases, the machine was not shut down; in 10% of cases, the machine was activated by another person; in 5% of cases, it was not possible to control the accumulated potential energy. These figures are not specific to the U.S. — they are universal human behaviors that repeat in any industry, including the events sector.

LOTO addresses a precise technical need: to ensure that no energy — electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, gravitational, thermal or chemical — can manifest in an uncontrolled manner while a worker performs an intervention on a machine or equipment. Switching off at the button is not enough: the button can be switched back on by another person, by an electrical fault, by power returning to the grid, or by the release of stored energy (capacitors, tensioned springs, suspended load).

4.1 The Seven Types of Hazardous Energy — LOTO

Type of Energy

Source at Events

Risk if not locked out

Lockout Device

Electrical

Distribution boards, generators, amplifiers, digital consoles

Electric shock, arc flash, unintended motor activation

Lockout padlock on the circuit breaker; plug lockout; tagout on the board

Mechanical (kinetic)

Rotating parts: motors, fans, lift servos

Entrapment, crushing, cutting by a moving part that keeps spinning after shutdown

Wait for a complete stop; physical chock to prevent rotation; mechanical lockout

Gravitational (potential)

Load suspended on a hoist, a raised platform, a crane arm

Sudden descent of the load or structure — lethal crushing

Chock beneath the structure; mechanical position lock; retention cable

Pneumatic

Compressed-air systems, pneumatic cylinders of scenic mechanisms

Sudden cylinder movement from residual pressure; hose whip

Valve locked with a padlock; bleeding of residual pressure before intervention

Hydraulic

Hydraulic systems of lifts and cranes

Uncontrolled movement from residual pressure in the hydraulic circuit

Valve locked; relief of circuit pressure; mechanical locking of the structure

Thermal

Hot surfaces of generators, amplifiers, lighting ballasts

Burn from contact with a surface still hot after shutdown

Wait for cooling (check with a thermometer); thermal PPE during intervention

Chemical

Acid from UPS batteries, hydraulic fluids, equipment cleaning products

Chemical burn, inhalation of vapors

Chemical PPE; ventilation; spill containment before intervention


4.2 The Eight Steps of the LOTO Procedure

  1. COMMUNICATION: Inform everyone involved that the equipment will be locked out for intervention — define the extent and duration of the lockout

  2. IDENTIFICATION: Identify ALL of the equipment's hazardous energy sources — electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, gravitational, thermal and chemical

  3. SHUTDOWN: Shut down the equipment using the normal operating controls

  4. ISOLATION: Isolate each identified energy source — open circuit breakers, close valves, lower platforms, disconnect plugs

  5. LOCKOUT: Apply a personal lockout padlock at each isolation point — EACH worker applies THEIR OWN padlock

  6. TAGOUT: Place a tag (tagout) at each lockout point with: the responsible person's name, date/time, contact phone number and reason

  7. STORED ENERGY CONTROL: Dissipate all residual energy — discharge capacitors, bleed circuit pressure, chock gravitational parts, wait for cooling

  8. VERIFICATION: Test the machine's activation to confirm that it does not respond — a "try-to-start" test — and verify the absence of electrical voltage with a multimeter


🔴  FUNDAMENTAL LOTO RULE

Each worker performing an intervention applies THEIR OWN padlock at the lockout point. If five technicians are working on the same equipment, five padlocks are applied. The equipment can only be switched back on when ALL padlocks have been removed by their respective owners. No supervisor or manager may remove another worker's padlock — this is the principle that protects lives.



  05    MACHINE INVENTORY AND RISK ASSESSMENT

Module 5 — Technical Documentation: the Machine Logbook

NR-12 requires companies to maintain structured technical documentation for each machine in operation. This documentation is not bureaucracy — it is the repository of technical knowledge that enables the operator to understand the risks, the maintenance technician to plan safe interventions, and the OSH officer to audit compliance. In the events sector, where the same equipment is used in different locations and configurations for each operation, technical documentation is even more critical.

5.1 Documents Required by NR-12

Machine Inventory

List of all machines and equipment subject to NR-12, with: name/model, location, power or capacity, installed safety systems and risk category

Floor Plan

Graphical representation of each machine's location in the facility layout — facilitates identification and inspection

Risk Assessment (RA)

Central document — prepared by a qualified professional in accordance with NBR ISO 12100, with an ART. Identifies all hazards, estimates risks and defines control measures per machine

Compliance Diagnosis

Checklist comparing the current situation of each machine with NR-12 requirements — identifies non-conformities and priorities

Action Plan

Planning of the necessary corrective and preventive measures, with deadlines and responsible parties

Operation and maintenance manual

In Brazilian Portuguese, easy to understand, available at the machine location — mandatory for all equipment

Maintenance logbook

Record of all maintenance performed — preventive (with a schedule) and corrective (with a description) — filed per machine

Training records

Evidence that all operators received training specific to the machine type — with content, workload and signature


5.2 Risk Assessment — NBR ISO 12100

The Risk Assessment (RA) is the most technical document required by NR-12. Based on NBR ISO 12100, it follows a systematic process of: (1) determining the machine's limits — intended uses, spatial and time limits; (2) identifying the hazards — mechanical, electrical, thermal, noise, ergonomic; (3) estimating the risk — severity of harm × probability of occurrence; (4) evaluating the risk — comparison with an acceptable level; (5) reducing the risk — selection and application of protective measures per the NR-12 hierarchy.

For the events sector, the Risk Assessment of a stage lift must consider all usage scenarios: normal raising/lowering operation with performers, height adjustment during setup with technicians on the platform, mechanism maintenance with the platform raised, and operation in emergency mode. Each scenario presents distinct hazards and control measures.


  06    SAFE MACHINE MAINTENANCE AT EVENTS

Module 6 — Maintenance: When a Stopped Machine Is More Dangerous Than a Moving One

The maintenance of machines and equipment at live events concentrates the greatest risks foreseen in NR-12, for three converging reasons: the machine is stopped but not necessarily de-energized; deadline pressure leads to "shortcuts" that compromise the safety procedure; and maintenance technicians often work alone or in confined spaces, far from supervision.

NR-12 distinguishes two maintenance regimes with distinct requirements: planned maintenance (preventive and predictive), which has a schedule, documented procedures and execution conditions known in advance; and corrective (unplanned) maintenance, which occurs after equipment failure, often under time pressure, and which demands special attention to safety procedures precisely because the risk factors are less controllable.

6.1 Safe Maintenance Procedure — Mandatory Sequence

  1. AUTHORIZATION: Issue a Work Permit documenting: description of the service, the machine involved, identified risks, control measures and the person responsible for authorization

  2. COMMUNICATION: Inform operators and workers in the area about the intervention — the area isolated and signposted

  3. APPLYING LOTO: Follow the eight steps of the Lockout/Tagout procedure for all energy sources

  4. ZERO-ENERGY VERIFICATION: Confirm with a measuring instrument (multimeter, voltage detector) that there is no residual energy

  5. PERFORMING MAINTENANCE: Carry out the service with appropriate tools and PPE specific to the type of intervention

  6. POST-MAINTENANCE INSPECTION: Verify that no tool or material was left inside the machine — a "foreign object debris check"

  7. LOTO REMOVAL: Each technician removes their own padlock — verify that no person is in a danger zone before switching back on

  8. FUNCTION TEST: Turn the machine on in test mode, verify normal operation and the absence of abnormalities before releasing it for operation

  9. DOCUMENTATION: Record in the maintenance logbook: date, responsible technician, service performed and test result


🔴  EXPRESS PROHIBITION — MAINTENANCE ON AN ENERGIZED MACHINE

NR-12 strictly prohibits performing adjustments, cleaning, lubrication or any intervention on an energized machine, except when intervention with the machine de-energized is technically impossible, and only under the specific conditions foreseen in the standard (maintenance mode with reduced speed, limited force and additional protection). This exception never applies to electrical work — which is governed by NR-10 and requires specific qualification.


  07    SAFETY SIGNAGE AND COMMUNICATION ON MACHINES

Module 7 — Signage: the Visual Language of Machines

NR-12 requires machines to have adequate signage and clear information about risks, controls and operating procedures. This signage is not merely decorative — it is an integral part of the safety system, forming part of the set of administrative protective measures. The standard determines that the markings must clearly indicate the risk AND the part of the machine they refer to, and that the generic marking "DANGER" alone must not be used — the specific risk must be identified.

For machines manufactured from December 24, 2011 onward, NR-12 requires the following to be visible in a permanent location: the manufacturer's or importer's company name, tax ID (CNPJ) and address; type, model and load capacity; serial number and year of manufacture; the registration number of the manufacturer or of a legally qualified professional at the engineering council (CREA); and, for large machines, the specifications of the engineering services needed for installation.

7.1 Types of Signage Required by NR-12

  • Danger-zone signage — physical demarcation or floor marking delimiting the risk area around moving parts

  • Hazardous-parts signage — labels on the specific parts of the machine identifying the type of risk (cutting, crushing, electric shock, temperature)

  • Load-capacity signage — in a visible and indelible location on all material-handling equipment

  • Active warning signals — intermittent light and sound signals that indicate the machine's start, stop or abnormal condition before the dangerous movement occurs

  • Control-device signage — buttons and controls labeled with a clear function in Brazilian Portuguese

  • Lockout (LOTO) signage — tagout labels with information on the person responsible for the lockout

  • Access-restriction signage — "RESTRICTED ACCESS: AUTHORIZED OPERATORS ONLY" at all entrances to machine areas




  08    TRAINING AND QUALIFICATION — NR-12

Module 8 — Training: Machine-Specific, Mandatory and Documented

NR-12 establishes that all workers who operate, maintain, inspect, clean or interact in any way with machines and equipment must receive specific qualification. The training must be fully funded by the employer, carried out within working hours, and have technical content adapted to the specific type of machine the worker will operate or maintain — it is not possible to substitute the specific training for a lift with the training for a forklift.

8.1 Minimum Content of NR-12 Training

  • Operating principles of the specific machine — mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic — depending on the equipment

  • Identification of the danger zones and the installed safety devices

  • Safe operation of the controls — the normal start, operation and stop sequence

  • Identification and location of the emergency stop — periodic functional testing

  • Lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedure for the specific equipment

  • Recognition of anomalies — sounds, vibrations, odors or behaviors that indicate imminent failure

  • Routine preventive-maintenance procedures — lubrication, visual inspection, verification of safety devices

  • Accident history for the machine type — lessons learned

  • The operator's responsibilities — the right of refusal, reporting of faults, prohibition of bypassing safety devices


8.2 Training Responsibilities

Employer / Producer

Ensure specific training before operation; fund it fully; conduct it within working hours; document it with an attendance list, content and workload; offer refresher training when there is a modification to the equipment or after an accident

Worker

Attend the training sessions; apply the knowledge during operation; not operate machines for which they were not trained; immediately report any fault in a safety device

OSH officer

Identify which workers need NR-12 training and for which machines; oversee the quality of the training; keep records up to date; include NR-12 in the Risk Management Program (PGR)


💡  MANDATORY REFRESHER TRAINING

NR-12 training must be refreshed whenever: there is a significant modification to the machine (a change of control system, a new safety device, a process change); the worker is assigned to operate a machine different from the one they were trained on; or after an accident involving the machine. The refresher has no fixed term in years — it is triggered by specific technical events.


  09    MACHINES SPECIFIC TO THE EVENTS SECTOR — NR-12 APPLICATION

Module 9 — Practical Guide by Equipment Type

The live events sector uses a variety of equipment that, although not "industrial" in the conventional sense, is fully subject to NR-12 when used for productive purposes. Knowledge of the specific requirements for each type of equipment is what sets the events OSH professional apart from the generalist.

9.1 Motorized Stage Lift

Motorized lifts are used at shows to raise performers, instruments and equipment. They constitute the highest-risk equipment under NR-12 in the events sector, due to the combination of: high load capacity (300 to 2,000 kg), vertical movement at a controlled speed, the space between the moving platform and the fixed structure (crushing zone), and the presence of performers and technicians on the platform during operation.

  • Protection of the crushing zones between the platform and the fixed structure — a grille, sensor or safety edge that stops the movement when an obstruction is detected

  • Automatic leveling device — the platform must stop automatically if it detects excessive tilt (an unbalanced load)

  • Travel limiter — prevents the platform from exceeding the extreme positions foreseen in the design

  • Maintenance mode — reduced speed (crawl mode) for inspection and adjustment with the guards open

  • Audible and visual start signaling — a warning before any movement, especially when there are people on the platform

  • Visible and up-to-date load capacity — never operate beyond the rated load


9.2 Motorized Rigging System (Chain Hoists)

Electric chain hoists are used to lift and position PA systems, lighting, video walls and stage structures. Subject to NR-12 (for the equipment) and NR-11 (for the lifting operation), this equipment presents specific risks:

  • Load limiter — interrupts the lift when the load exceeds the safety limit; cannot be disabled

  • Upper and lower travel limiter — prevents the hoist from driving the hook to the point of collapse (top) or the chain from running out (bottom)

  • Safety brake — keeps the load suspended even in the event of a power or control failure

  • Speed control — reduced speed available for fine positioning; maximum speed according to the structure's capacity

  • Real-time load-monitoring system — especially on computerized motorized grids


9.3 Stage Equipment with Automated Movement

Large shows use scenic equipment with automated movement: horizontally translating carts (Stage Wagons), scenic robots, instrument rotation systems and mechanical effects. For this equipment, NR-12 requires:

  • A safety control system with a Performance Level (PL) appropriate to the risk — calculated in accordance with NBR ISO 13849

  • Protection by a laser scanner or light curtain that detects the presence of a person in the equipment's path and stops the movement automatically

  • An exclusion zone clearly demarcated and kept clear during operation

  • A rehearsal mode with reduced speed for programming and adjustment with the technical team present

  • Synchronization with the lighting system to warn in advance of the start of movement


  10    INTEGRATION OF NR-12 WITH THE OSH SYSTEM

Module 10 — NR-12 in the PGR: Machines as Managed Risks

NR-12 is not an isolated standard — it links directly to the GRO/PGR system of NR-01 and to the other NRs applicable to the events sector. The NR-12 Machine Inventory feeds the Risk Inventory of the NR-01 PGR; the NR-12 Risk Assessment (RA) constitutes the specialized technical evaluation that underpins the control measures in the Action Plan.

10.1 Integration with Other NRs

NR-01 (GRO/PGR)

The Machine Inventory feeds the Risk Inventory; the RA underpins the control measures of the Action Plan; training documented in the PGR records

NR-06 (PPE)

PPE for machine operation and maintenance: cut-resistant gloves, safety glasses, ear protectors, safety footwear, FR clothing for thermal risk

NR-10 (Electrical)

The machines' boards and control panels are subject to NR-10; electrical LOTO must follow NR-10; an NR-10-qualified worker is required for the electrical maintenance of the equipment

NR-11 (Material Handling)

Electric hoists and cranes: NR-12 governs the equipment itself; NR-11 governs the lifting operation and the qualification of the signaler

NR-18 (Structures)

Lifts and stage-handling equipment during the structure assembly phase

NR-35 (Work at Height)

Maintenance of equipment at height (rigging grid, lighting towers): NR-12 for the equipment + NR-35 for the worker at height

NR-26 (Signage)

Signage for danger zones, load capacity, emergency stop and LOTO in accordance with the NBR 7195 color standard

  11    CASE STUDIES — MACHINE ACCIDENTS AT EVENTS

Module 11 — Learning from What Went Wrong

Case 1 — Crushing on a Stage Lift

Scenario: A stage technician checks the fastening of an equipment cable at the lower level while the platform is raised. Without communicating with the operator, he places his arm between the edge of the platform and the stop of the fixed structure to reach the cable. The operator, unable to see the technician (no signaler and no presence-detection system), activates the platform's descent. The technician's arm is crushed between the platform and the stop. Result: an open fracture, partial amputation of the forearm.

What went wrong: No protection in the crushing zone; the operator had no visibility of the platform's surroundings; the technician did not communicate with the operator before entering the danger zone; no "technician in danger zone" signaling; the platform had no presence sensor.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 1

Never enter the danger zone of a lift without communicating with and confirming with the operator. The operator must have full visibility before any activation. Protection in the crushing zone is a mandatory engineering measure — it cannot be replaced by an administrative procedure.


Case 2 — Accidental Restart During Electric Hoist Maintenance

Scenario: A rigging technician is inspecting the chain of an electric hoist with the equipment stopped. The technician switched the equipment off at the remote-control button but did NOT apply LOTO. Another technician on the ground, believing the system was free, activates the positioning remote control from another point on the grid. The hoist is activated with the technician holding the chain. Result: fingers trapped in the teeth of the chain mechanism — amputation of two fingers.

What went wrong: No LOTO procedure — switching off at the remote control does not lock out the equipment; no communication between technicians about who was inspecting; the hoist chain accessible without protection during inspection.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 2

Switching off at the remote control or operating button is NOT a safety lockout. LOTO requires physical isolation of the energy source with a padlock and a tag. Never inspect the chain, hooks or hoist mechanism without physical lockout of the electrical source.


Case 3 — Bypassing a Guard on Automated Scenic Equipment

Scenario: During the dress rehearsal, the automated scenic equipment (stage wagon) stops repeatedly due to unintended triggering of the safety sensor (a laser scanner detects workers in its path). The technician in charge, under pressure from the production director to "not delay the show", disables the safety scanner so that "the equipment stops interrupting the rehearsal". The Stage Wagon continues the rehearsal without protection. A dancer who was not informed about the equipment's path is struck by the wagon at speed. Result: two fractured ribs and a pulmonary contusion.

What went wrong: Intentional bypassing of the safety system (a practice prohibited by NR-12 and criminal); inadequate communication of the equipment's path to all the performers; production pressure overriding safety.

💡  LESSON FROM CASE 3

Bypassing any machine safety device is expressly prohibited by NR-12 and may constitute a willful crime in the event of an accident. The technician who disables a laser scanner "to not delay the show" is personally responsible for the resulting accidents. The scanner stopped because there were people in its path — it was working correctly.


  12    CONCLUSION — A SAFE MACHINE IS A MACHINE WITH PROTECTION AT THE SOURCE

Closing: Safety Lies in Engineering, Not in Individual Care

NR-12 starts from a principle that runs counter to the common sense of many events professionals: machine safety cannot depend on the operator's care, attention or experience. Human beings get distracted, get tired, make mistakes — especially at events with 20 hours of setup, under deadline pressure and in a low-light environment at 3 a.m. The standard requires protection to be on the machine, not in the constant vigilance of the worker.

Physical protection in the danger zones, an accessible and functional emergency stop, a LOTO procedure rigorously applied during maintenance, documented specific training, and a risk assessment prepared by a qualified professional — these are the five pillars of NR-12 that transform a dangerous piece of equipment into a managed one. None of them is optional; none can be replaced by another.

The SMU PRO professional who completes this module understands that a lift without protection in the crushing zone is not an "old" piece of equipment that needs special care — it is a non-compliant piece of equipment that cannot operate. That a hoist without LOTO during maintenance is not a "quick job" — it is exposure to the risk of amputation. That bypassing a safety scanner is not a "creative solution for the rehearsal" — it is a crime. This is the NR-12 standard.


"A safe machine is not one that doesn't break down — it's one that doesn't hurt."


NR-12 module completed. Workload: 4 hours.

References: NR-12 (SIT Ordinance No. 197/2010 and updates) · NBR ISO 12100 · ABNT NBR ISO 13850 · ABNT NBR ISO 13857 · ABNT NBR ISO 13849 · NR-01 · NR-10 · NR-11.

528fcd63-a268-468b-b52f-6446f6878ed4.explicacao: The risk is assessed by combining the probability of occurrence with the severity of the harm, resulting, in this case, in a high risk due to prolonged exposure to intense noise.

0ba5f57b-d5ab-4e58-975c-c4d42e6767cc.pergunta: Why is the decibel (dB) scale used to measure sound pressure level logarithmic?
0ba5f57b-d5ab-4e58-975c-c4d42e6767cc.explicacao: Because human hearing perception increases logarithmically, each 10 dB represents a doubling of perceived loudness, making it easier to represent a vast range of sound intensities.

opcoes.f1d53adf-e0ff-4f63-ad06-578fa7ce1d6f: To adequately represent the large variation in perceived acoustic power.
opcoes.7634f21b-21cb-4457-b3bc-f0e9d8211e32: So that measurement is more precise at low frequencies.
opcoes.4b9f6a51-bf6a-4c9f-9f3c-bd614d76876d: To simplify the calculation of sound pressure in Pascals.
opcoes.0dadbc2e-9eec-4fe4-b0f5-003ce11a42ce: To prevent high levels from being recorded as dangerous.

118a8e36-40f1-4f9b-90f3-b554b8f1e288.pergunta: In the GRO hierarchy of control measures, what is the correct sequence from most effective to least effective?
118a8e36-40f1-4f9b-90f3-b554b8f1e288.explicacao: The correct sequence follows the hierarchy: elimination of the risk, substitution, use of Collective Protection Equipment (EPC), administrative measures and, lastly, Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).

5de35a48-7d2b-46bf-bb9d-98c2147fb0cf.pergunta: Considering the physics of sound, why can subwoofers with frequencies below 100 Hz pose a structural risk at live events?
5de35a48-7d2b-46bf-bb9d-98c2147fb0cf.explicacao: Low frequencies have long wavelengths that can cause significant vibrations in temporary structures, increasing the risk of damage or accidents.

opcoes.95706328-f70c-4182-9b32-1c4e2243f331: Because high-frequency waves penetrate structures more.
opcoes.7fd11750-65a6-4056-a121-5a1c1d9a2735: Because subwoofers produce sound pressure levels below the hearing threshold.

opcoes.cf61398f-d94e-49eb-8ad7-431de285e72f: Because these frequencies are inaudible and are not controlled.

2b604086-7321-4820-bf0a-c8ca6bc398e6.explicacao: The classification of risks is done to prioritize actions according to the degree of severity and probability, facilitating appropriate control.

916a01df-457d-4e59-b0f7-5b99ed3be17b.pergunta: Considering the noise exposure limit under NR-15 and NR-01, what is the correct interpretation of exposure to 100 dB for 8 hours?
916a01df-457d-4e59-b0f7-5b99ed3be17b.explicacao: Exposure to 100 dB for 8 hours is considered high-risk, requiring strict control measures to prevent hearing damage.

dbaecb77-244d-44fb-aad5-0d44d710f77f.pergunta: Why is the decibel scale (dB SPL) used to measure sound pressure considered logarithmic, and what is the impact of this on sound reinforcement?
dbaecb77-244d-44fb-aad5-0d44d710f77f.explicacao: Because each 10 dB increase represents a 10-fold increase in sound power, which means doubling the loudspeakers does not double the perceived volume.

opcoes.308c76b9-36ad-4a72-b4e2-fe6ebaa6433c: Because dB SPL measures only the frequency and not the intensity of the sound.

3c03660d-36d2-4d4b-92ae-1f284beee329.explicacao: The use of auxiliary equipment such as pallet jacks reduces physical effort and the risk of lower back injuries, as recommended by the GRO.

31c3fb77-77f8-40cf-afce-450ed39b9f57.pergunta: Why is the decibel (dB) scale used to measure Sound Pressure Level logarithmic at live events?
31c3fb77-77f8-40cf-afce-450ed39b9f57.explicacao: Because a 10 dB increase represents a doubling of perceived loudness and 10 times the acoustic power, which is essential for understanding the impact of sound and sizing PA systems.

opcoes.aa5b8b62-eed4-48c1-9bb6-d6cd65d47d2a: To make measurement easier with simple instruments.

opcoes.07624c47-03da-4293-aae4-16f7ad4d64e5: A valid Certificate of Approval (CA) issued by the MTE.

8a7d5946-da1e-4cc8-932d-8ea9b4f11629.explicacao: NR-06 establishes that PPE is the last line of defense, used only after the possibilities of collective protection and improvements in work organization have been exhausted.

f35edfe0-a2cc-4349-968b-7e5657f40b2f.explicacao: NR-06 complements NR-01, which defines the residual risks, and must be integrated into the PGR to ensure the proper selection, use and control of PPE according to the identified risks.

bb3d1479-b609-4aba-be84-685af1f1bc23.pergunta: What is the position of NR-06 in the occupational safety and health (OSH) system applied to the live events sector?
bb3d1479-b609-4aba-be84-685af1f1bc23.explicacao: NR-06 complements NR-01 and other specific standards, defining the requirements for the use and control of PPE, and is part of the OSH management system.

427de66f-72d5-456d-a26d-4bb2b8708339.pergunta: Why should the supply of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) be the last stage in risk prevention, according to NR-06?
427de66f-72d5-456d-a26d-4bb2b8708339.explicacao: NR-06 reinforces the hierarchy of controls of NR-01, in which collective protection must be prioritized before the use of PPE, which is the last line of defense.
